blocks:
Ayahs

بَرَآءَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّم مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١)
[This is] a [declaration of] repudiation by Allah and His Apostle [addressed] to the polytheists with whom you had made a treaty: (1)
فَسِيحُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِى ٱللَّهِ ۙ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْزِى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢)
Travel [unmolested] in the land for four months, but know that you cannot frustrate Allah, and that Allah shall disgrace the faithless. (2)
وَأَذَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلنَّاسِ يَوْمَ ٱلْحَجِّ ٱلْأَكْبَرِ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ۙ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ فَإِن تُبْتُمْ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِى ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٣)
[This is] an announcement from Allah and His Apostle to all the people on the day of the greater Hajj: that Allah and His Apostle repudiate the polytheists: If you repent that is better for you; but if you turn your backs [on Allah], know that you cannot frustrate Allah, and inform the faithless of a painful punishment (3)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّم مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَنقُصُوكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَمْ يُظَـٰهِرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَدًا فَأَتِمُّوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلَىٰ مُدَّتِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤)
(barring the polytheists with whom you have made a treaty, and who did not violate any [of its terms] with you, nor backed anyone against you. So fulfill the treaty with them until [the end of] its term. Indeed Allah loves the Godwary). (4)
فَإِذَا ٱنسَلَخَ ٱلْأَشْهُرُ ٱلْحُرُمُ فَٱقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ وَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱحْصُرُوهُمْ وَٱقْعُدُوا۟ لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍ ۚ فَإِن تَابُوا۟ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَخَلُّوا۟ سَبِيلَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥)
Then, when the sacred months have passed, kill the polytheists wherever you find them, capture them and besiege them, and lie in wait for them at every ambush. But if they repent, and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, then let them alone. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)
وَإِنْ أَحَدٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ٱسْتَجَارَكَ فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّىٰ يَسْمَعَ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ أَبْلِغْهُ مَأْمَنَهُۥ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦)
If any of the polytheists seeks asylum from you, grant him asylum until he hears the Word of Allah. Then convey him to his place of safety. That is because they are a people who do not know. (6)
كَيْفَ يَكُونُ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ عَهْدٌ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعِندَ رَسُولِهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّمْ عِندَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ فَمَا ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱسْتَقِيمُوا۟ لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٧)
How shall the polytheists have any [valid] treaty with Allah and His Apostle?! (Barring those with whom you made a treaty at the Holy Mosque; so long as they are steadfast with you, be steadfast with them. Indeed Allah loves the Godwary.) (7)
كَيْفَ وَإِن يَظْهَرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ لَا يَرْقُبُوا۟ فِيكُمْ إِلًّا وَلَا ذِمَّةً ۚ يُرْضُونَكُم بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَتَأْبَىٰ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٨)
How? For if, they get the better of you, they will observe toward you neither kinship nor covenant. They please you with their mouths while their hearts spurn you; and most of them are transgressors. (8)
ٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٩)
They have sold the signs of Allah for a paltry gain, and have barred [the people] from His way. Evil indeed is what they have been doing. (9)
لَا يَرْقُبُونَ فِى مُؤْمِنٍ إِلًّا وَلَا ذِمَّةً ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُعْتَدُونَ (١٠)
They observe toward a believer neither kinship nor covenant, and it is they who are the transgressors. (10)
فَإِن تَابُوا۟ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ ۗ وَنُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (١١)
Yet if they repent and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, then they are your brethren in faith. We elaborate the signs for a people who have knowledge. (11)
وَإِن نَّكَثُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ عَهْدِهِمْ وَطَعَنُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ فَقَـٰتِلُوٓا۟ أَئِمَّةَ ٱلْكُفْرِ ۙ إِنَّهُمْ لَآ أَيْمَـٰنَ لَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَنتَهُونَ (١٢)
But if they break their pledges after their having made a treaty and revile your religion, then fight the leaders of unfaith—indeed, they have no [commitment to] pledges,—maybe they will desist. (12)
أَلَا تُقَـٰتِلُونَ قَوْمًا نَّكَثُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ وَهَمُّوا۟ بِإِخْرَاجِ ٱلرَّسُولِ وَهُم بَدَءُوكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ۚ أَتَخْشَوْنَهُمْ ۚ فَٱللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَن تَخْشَوْهُ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٣)
Will you not make war on a people who broke their pledges and resolved to expel the Apostle, and opened [hostilities] against you initially? Do you fear them? But Allah is worthier of being feared by you, should you be faithful. (13)
قَـٰتِلُوهُمْ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ وَيُخْزِهِمْ وَيَنصُرْكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَيَشْفِ صُدُورَ قَوْمٍ مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٤)
Make war on them so that Allah may punish them by your hands and humiliate them, and help you against them, and heal the hearts of a faithful folk, (14)
وَيُذْهِبْ غَيْظَ قُلُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَيَتُوبُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٥)
and remove rage from their hearts, and Allah turns clemently to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise (15)
أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تُتْرَكُوا۟ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا رَسُولِهِۦ وَلَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلِيجَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٦)
Do you suppose that you will be let off while Allah has not yet ascertained those of you who wage jihad and those who do not take anyone as [their] confidant besides Allah and His Apostle and the faithful? Allah is well aware of what you do. (16)

(Commentary)

These verses are the opening of a group of verses named Surah al-Tawbah (Repentance) or Surah al-Bara’ah (Disavowal). Scholars have differed as to whether it is an independent surah or a part of Surah al-Anfal. The disagreement among exegetes on this matter traces back to the disagreement among the Companions (الصحابة) and then the Successors (التابعين). Similarly, the narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) differ on this point, though the most preponderant view according to the technical requirements of the discipline is what their narrations indicate: that it is appended to Surah al-Anfal.

An investigation into the meanings of its verses and the themes they contain does not lead to a single, determined objective in the same manner as other surahs, which possess distinct objectives aimed at by their beginnings and returned to by their endings. Its beginning consists of verses announcing disavowal (البراءة), and it contains verses regarding fighting the polytheists (المشركين) and fighting the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). A significant portion of it discusses the matter of the hypocrites (المنافقين), as well as verses urging mobilization for combat, those addressing the condition of those who stayed behind, verses regarding the guardianship (ولاية) of the disbelievers, verses on zakat (الزكاة), and other matters. Most of it pertains to fighting the disbelievers and the affairs of the hypocrites.

In any case, no significant benefit follows from this investigation in terms of exegesis (التفسير), even if it were possible from the perspective of jurisprudential (الفقهي) inquiry, which lies outside our objective.

His saying, the Exalted: بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ إِلَى الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — [This is] a repudiation by Allah and His Apostle [addressed] to the polytheists with whom you had made a treaty. (9:1). Al-Raghib stated: "The root of al-bur’ (البرء), al-bara’ (البراء), and al-tabarri (التبري) is to be free from that whose proximity is disliked. Thus, it is said: 'I recovered (bara’tu) from the illness,' 'I am free (bari’tu) from so-and-so,' and 'I disassociated myself (tabarra’tu).' Also, 'I cleared him (abra’tuhu) of such-and-such' and 'I acquitted him (barra’tuhu).' A person is bari’ (بريء) [free/innocent], and a group is bara’ (براء) or bari’un (بريئون). The Exalted said: A repudiation (براءة) from Allah and His Apostle." End quote.

This verse stands in relation to the subsequent verses like a title prefixed to a discourse, indicating the summary of the matter, following the method of other detailed surahs where the first verse or two briefly indicate the objective for whose explanation the verses are narrated.

The address in the verse is to the believers or to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and them, as indicated by His saying: عاهَدْتُمْ (you had made a treaty). Allah, the Exalted—from Whom the address originates—and His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—who is the intermediary—have been mentioned, as well as the polytheists, who are those from whom disavowal is intended. The address is directed so as to reach them all in the third person (الغيبة). This method in decrees and edicts intended to reach the people is a form of honoring the possessor of the decree and command.

The verse entails the enactment of a judgment and a decree of disavowal (البراءة) from these polytheists; it is not merely a legislative act (تشريع محض), as evidenced by His joining the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the disavowal. It is the custom of the Quran to attribute purely legislative judgments to Allah, the Glorified, alone, as the Exalted said: وَلا يُشْرِكُ فِي حُكْمِهِ أَحَداً — and He does not let anyone share in His judgment (18:26). Nothing is attributed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) except judgment in the sense found in authority (الولاية), governance (السياسة), and the settling of disputes.

Thus, what is meant by the verse is the decree to revoke the protection (الأمان) from those polytheists with whom they had made a treaty. This was not a haphazard revocation or an invalidation of the treaty without a cause that permits it. Indeed, Allah, the Exalted, will mention after several verses that there is no trust in the treaty they made, as most of them have been transgressors (فسق), failed to observe the sanctity of the treaty, and violated their covenant. At that point, the Exalted permitted the invalidation of the treaty as a reciprocal measure—violation for violation—where He said: وَإِمَّا تَخافَنَّ مِنْ قَوْمٍ خِيانَةً فَانْبِذْ إِلَيْهِمْ عَلى سَواءٍ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْخائِنِينَ — And if you fear treachery from a people, break off [the treaty] with them in a like manner. Indeed Allah does not like the treacherous (8:58). He permitted the invalidation of the treaty upon the fear of treachery, yet He did not approve of it except by notifying them of the breach, so they would not be taken unawares, which would constitute prohibited treachery.

Had it been an invalidation of their treaty without a justifying cause from the side of the polytheists, He would not have distinguished between those who remained faithful to their treaty and those who did not. The Exalted said, making an exception: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَنْقُصُوكُمْ شَيْئاً وَلَمْ يُظاهِرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَداً فَأَتِمُّوا إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلى مُدَّتِهِمْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ — excepting those of the polytheists with whom you have made a treaty, and who have not since then curtailed anything [of your rights] nor supported anyone against you. So fulfill their treaty with them until [the end of] their term. Indeed Allah loves the God-conscious (9:4).

Furthermore, the Exalted did not approve of breaking the treaty of these violators without setting a term for them to reflect on their affair and consider their position, so they would not be taken by surprise or suddenness.

The summary of the verse is the judgment of the invalidity of the treaty and the revocation of protection from a group of polytheists who had made a treaty with the Muslims, most of whom then violated it, such that no trust remained in those who were left—a trust that would provide peace of mind regarding their treaty, reliance on their oath, and safety from their evil and various deceptions.

His saying, the Exalted: فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللهِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ مُخْزِي الْكافِرِينَ — Travel [freely] in the land for four months, and know that you cannot thwart Allah, and that Allah shall disgrace the faithless (9:2). Al-siyahah (السياحة) is to travel and move about the land; hence, water that flows continuously in an area (sahah) is called sa’ih.

Commanding them to travel for four months is a metaphor for granting them security during this period of time and leaving them such that no human interferes with them, until they choose what they see as more beneficial for their state—whether survival or destruction. This is coupled with what is in His saying: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللهِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ مُخْزِي الْكافِرِينَ (and know that you cannot thwart Allah, and that Allah shall disgrace the faithless), which informs them that what is best for their state is to reject polytheism (الشرك) and turn toward the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. It is an admonition to them not to destroy themselves through arrogance and exposure to divine disgrace.

In the verse, the address is directed to them by shifting from the third person to the second person (الالتفات). This is because directing a decisive address and a firm will toward an adversary indicates the extension of dominance and triumph over him, as well as the humiliation and belittling of whatever strength or intensity he possesses.

The exegetes have differed regarding what is meant by the أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ (four months). What the context indicates and what is supported by the consideration of issuing a judgment and setting a term—so they may have the latitude to choose what they find more beneficial for their state, life or death—is that the four months begin from the Day of the Greater Hajj (يوم الحج الأكبر), which Allah mentions in the following verse. For the Day of the Greater Hajj is the day of proclamation and notification. It is most appropriate for the setting of a term—which involves a kind of leniency for those judged and the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة)—to begin from the time of the announcement and notification.

The transmitters of reports are in consensus that the verses were revealed in the ninth year of the Hijrah. If we assume that the Day of the Greater Hajj is the Day of Sacrifice (يوم النحر), the tenth of Dhu al-Hijjah, then the four months would be: twenty days of Dhu al-Hijjah, al-Muharram, Safar, Rabi‘ al-Awwal, and ten days of Rabi‘ al-Akhir.

According to some, the four months begin from the twentieth of Dhu al-Qa‘dah, which is the Day of the Greater Hajj according to them. Thus, the four months would be: ten days of Dhu al-Qa‘dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, al-Muharram, Safar, and twenty days of Rabi‘ al-Awwal. What this view entails will be discussed later.

Others mentioned that the verses were revealed at the beginning of Shawwal in the ninth year of the Hijrah. Thus, the four months would be Shawwal, Dhu al-Qa‘dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, and al-Muharram, ending with the conclusion of the sacred months (الأشهر الحرم). They were led to this by the view that what is meant by His saying later: فَإِذَا انْسَلَخَ الْأَشْهُرُ الْحُرُمُ فَاقْتُلُوا (Then, when the sacred months have passed, slay...) refers to the well-known sacred months: Dhu al-Qa‘dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, and al-Muharram. Thus, the passing of the sacred months would coincide with the end of the four months. This view is far from correct and is not supported by the context or the circumstantial evidence of the situation, as you have seen.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَذانٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ إِلَى النَّاسِ يَوْمَ الْحَجِّ الْأَكْبَرِ أَنَّ اللهَ بَرِيءٌ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ وَرَسُولُهُ — And an announcement from Allah and His Apostle to all the people on the day of the greater hajj: that Allah and His Apostle repudiate the polytheists (9:3). Al-adhan (الأذان) is the announcement (الاعلام). This verse is not a repetition of His previous saying: بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (A repudiation from Allah and His Apostle). Although the two sentences return to a single meaning—which is the disavowal of the polytheists—the first verse is the notification of the disavowal and its delivery to the polytheists, as evidenced by His saying at the end of the verse: إِلَى الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (to the polytheists with whom you had made a treaty). This is in contrast to the second verse, where the address is directed to the people (إلى الناس) so they may know of the disavowal of Allah and His Messenger from the polytheists, and so they may prepare and ready themselves to execute Allah's command regarding them after the passing of the sacred months. This is evidenced by His saying: إِلَى النَّاسِ (to all the people) and His subsequent saying: فَإِذَا انْسَلَخَ الْأَشْهُرُ الْحُرُمُ فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ (Then, when the sacred months have passed, slay the polytheists wherever you find them), to the end of the verse.

Scholars have differed in determining what is meant by the Day of the Greater Hajj (يوم الحج الأكبر) according to several views:

Among them: That it is the Day of Sacrifice (يوم النحر) of the ninth year of the Hijrah, because it was a day in which the Muslims and the polytheists gathered, and no polytheist performed Hajj after that year. This is supported by the narrations related from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) and is most appropriate for the announcement of disavowal. Reason also supports this, as it was the greatest day in which the Muslims and the polytheists from among all the pilgrims gathered at Mina. Narrations to this effect have been reported through Sunni channels, though the implication of most of them is that "the Greater Hajj" is the name for the Day of Sacrifice, and thus it would recur every year. However, such a naming has not been established through transmission in this manner.

Among them: That it is the Day of ‘Arafah, because the standing (الوقوف) occurs on it, and the "Lesser Hajj" (الحج الأصغر) is that which has no standing, namely the ‘Umrah. This is a preference (istihsan) for which there is no evidence, and there is no way to verify its correctness.

Among them: That it is the second day after the Day of Sacrifice, because the Imam delivers the sermon on it. The weakness of this view is apparent.

Among them: That it refers to all the days of the Hajj, just as it is said: "the Day of the Camel," "the Day of Siffin," and "the Day of Bu‘ath," meaning the time and period. This view does not entirely oppose the other views; it merely explains that what is meant by "the day" is all the days of the Hajj. As for the reason for naming this Hajj "the Greater Hajj," it can be explained by some of what was in the previous views, such as the first view.

In any case, reason does not support this view, because the existence of a specific day among the days of Hajj in which all the pilgrims gather—where the announcement of disavowal can be fully realized, such as the Day of Sacrifice—diverts His saying: يَوْمَ الْحَجِّ الْأَكْبَرِ (the day of the greater hajj) to that specific day itself, and prevents it from including the other days of Hajj in which the people do not gather in such a manner.

Then He, the Glorified, turned to the polytheists a second time and reminded them that they cannot thwart Allah, so they might have insight into their affair, just as He reminded them of that in the previous verse by His saying: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللهِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ مُخْزِي الْكافِرِينَ (and know that you cannot thwart Allah, and that Allah shall disgrace the faithless). However, He added to it in this verse His saying: فَإِنْ تُبْتُمْ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ (So if you repent, it will be better for you), to be an explicit statement of what was hinted at in the previous verse. For the reminder that they cannot thwart Allah was in the position of an admonition and the offering of sincere advice to them, so they would not cast themselves into destruction by choosing to remain in polytheism and turning away from entering the religion of monotheism. Thus, in the alternative presented, there is a threat, a piece of advice, and an admonition.

Then He, the Glorified, turned to His Messenger and addressed him to give the faithless the news of a painful punishment, saying: وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ (and inform the faithless of a painful punishment). The reason for the shift (الالتفات) in His saying: فَإِنْ تُبْتُمْ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ (So if you repent, it will be better for you) etc., is what was mentioned regarding His saying: فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ (Travel [freely] in the land) etc. As for the shift in His saying: وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (and inform the faithless) etc., it is because it is a message that is only completed through the addressing of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَنْقُصُوكُمْ شَيْئاً وَلَمْ يُظاهِرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَداً (excepting those of the polytheists with whom you have made a treaty, and who have not since then curtailed anything [of your rights] nor supported anyone against you) etc., is an exception from the general disavowal of the polytheists. Those excepted are the polytheists who have a treaty that they have not violated, neither directly nor indirectly. Thus, it is obligatory to fulfill their covenant and complete their treaty until their term.

From this, it becomes clear that the purpose of adding His saying: وَلَمْ يُظاهِرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَداً (nor supported anyone against you) to His saying: لَمْ يَنْقُصُوكُمْ شَيْئاً (have not since then curtailed anything [of your rights]) is to cover both types of violation: direct violation, such as their killing some of the Muslims, and indirect violation, such as their supporting some of the enemies of the Muslims against them. An example of this is the polytheists of Mecca providing weapons to the Banu Bakr against the Khuza‘ah. The Banu Bakr were in a treaty with the Quraysh, and the Khuza‘ah were in a treaty with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). They fought, and the Quraysh aided the Banu Bakr against the Khuza‘ah, thereby violating the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah which they had concluded between themselves and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). This was among the causes for the Conquest of Mecca in the eighth year.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ (Indeed Allah loves the God-conscious) is in the position of providing the rationale for the obligation of fulfilling the treaty as long as the polytheist treaty-partner has not violated it. This makes respecting the treaty and preserving the covenant one of the instances of absolute godwariness (التقوى المطلق) which the Quran continuously commands. He has stated this explicitly in similar instances, such as His saying: وَلا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَآنُ قَوْمٍ عَلى أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا اعْدِلُوا هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوى — and ill-feeling for a people should never lead you to be unfair. Be fair; that is nearer to God-consciousness (5:8), and His saying: وَلا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَآنُ قَوْمٍ أَنْ صَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ أَنْ تَعْتَدُوا ، وَتَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوى وَلا تَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوانِ وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ — and ill-feeling for a people because they barred you from the Holy Mosque should not lead you to transgress. Cooperate in piety and God-consciousness, and do not cooperate in sin and aggression, and be conscious of Allah (5:2).

By this, the error in the statement of some becomes apparent: that what is meant by "the God-conscious" (المتقين) are those who guard against violating the treaty without cause. This is because taqwa (التقوى) in the sense of refraining from the prohibitions of Allah is generally like a secondary literal meaning in the Quran; thus, intending something else would require a diverting context.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا انْسَلَخَ الْأَشْهُرُ الْحُرُمُ فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ وَخُذُوهُمْ وَاحْصُرُوهُمْ وَاقْعُدُوا لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍ — Then, when the sacred months have passed, slay the polytheists wherever you find them, and seize them, and besiege them, and lie in wait for them at every ambush (9:5). The root of al-insilakh (الانسلاخ) is from the skinning of a sheep (salkh al-shah), which is the removal of its skin. The "passing" (insilakh) of the month is a kind of metaphor for its departure. Al-hasr (الحصر) is to prevent someone from leaving an area. Al-marsad (المرصد) is a noun of place from al-rasd (الرصد), meaning the preparation for watching.

Al-Raghib said: "Al-rasd (الرصد) is the preparation for watching. It is said: 'He watched (rasada) for him,' 'He lay in wait (tarassada),' and 'I prepared it (arsadtuhu) for him.' Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَإِرْصاداً لِمَنْ حارَبَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ — and as an ambush for those who had fought Allah and His Apostle before (9:107). And His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصادِ — Indeed your Lord is in ambush (89:14), as a warning that there is no refuge and no escape. Al-rasd is used for a single watcher, a group of watchers, and for the one being watched, whether one or many. His saying, the Exalted: يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً — He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him (72:27) can carry all these meanings. Al-marsad is the place of watching." End quote.

What is meant by the sacred months (الأشهر الحرم) are the four months: the months of travel (أشهر السياحة) which Allah, the Glorified, mentioned in His saying: فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ (Travel [freely] in the land for four months) and made them a set term for the polytheists during which their condition would not be interfered with. As for the well-known sacred months—namely Dhu al-Qa‘dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, and al-Muharram—they do not coincide with the announcement of disavowal that occurred on the Day of Sacrifice, the tenth of Dhu al-Hijjah, in any way, as previously indicated.

Accordingly, the definite article (al-) in "the sacred months" (الأشهر الحرم) is for mental reference (العهد الذكري), meaning: when these months have passed which We have mentioned and made sacred for the polytheists such that their condition is not interfered with during them, then slay the polytheists, etc.

From this, it appears that there is no basis for interpreting His saying: فَإِذَا انْسَلَخَ الْأَشْهُرُ الْحُرُمُ (Then, when the sacred months have passed) as the passing of Dhu al-Qa‘dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, and al-Muharram, such that the passing of the four months would coincide with the passing of these three months, or that the passing of the sacred months is taken as an allusion to the conclusion of the four months even if the months do not coincide. All of this is impossible according to the context, even if the term "the sacred months" (الأشهر الحرم) in itself is apparent in the months of Rajab, Dhu al-Qa‘dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, and al-Muharram.

His saying: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ (slay the polytheists wherever you find them) realizes the disavowal from them and the removal of the sanctity of their lives by making their blood lawful. Thus, there is no barrier against any calamity that befalls them. In His saying: حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ (wherever you find them), there is a generalization of the judgment; there is no barrier preventing the obligation of killing them wherever they are found, whether in the non-sacred area (al-hill) or the Sanctuary (al-haram). Indeed, even if they are caught in a sacred month—based on the generalization of "wherever" (haythu) to both time and place—it is obligatory for the Muslims, whoever they may be, if they catch them, to kill them, whether that be in the non-sacred area or the Sanctuary, in a sacred month or otherwise.

They were only commanded to be killed wherever they are found as a means to bring them to the point of annihilation and extinction, to cleanse the earth of them, and to save the people from mixing and socializing with them, after they had been allowed and permitted to travel in His saying: فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ (Travel [freely] in the land for four months).

The implication of this is that each of His sayings: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ (slay the polytheists wherever you find them), وَخُذُوهُمْ (and seize them), وَاحْصُرُوهُمْ (and besiege them), and وَاقْعُدُوا لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍ (and lie in wait for them at every ambush) is an explanation of a type of means toward annihilating their collective and exhausting their numbers, so that society may be free from their evil.

If they are caught and it is possible to kill them, they are killed. If that is not possible, they are captured and seized. If seizing them is not possible, they are besieged and confined in their strongholds and prevented from going out to the people and mixing with them. If their location is unknown, one lies in wait for them at every ambush to catch them so they may be killed or seized.

Perhaps this meaning is what was intended by those who said: what is meant is "slay the polytheists wherever you find them OR seize them and besiege them" by way of choice (التخيير) in considering the more beneficial of the two matters. However, this is not free from affectation in terms of considering seizing, besieging, and lying in wait at every ambush as a single matter in contrast to killing. In any case, the context only accords with the meaning we have presented.

As for the statement of those who said that in His saying: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ وَخُذُوهُمْ (slay the polytheists wherever you find them, and seize them) there is a transposition (تقديماً وتأخيراً), and the intended meaning is: "seize the polytheists wherever you find them and slay them," this is an alteration of the meaning of the verse without a justifying proof. The verse, and especially its conclusion, refutes that through its context.

The meaning of the verse is: when the sacred months have passed and the four months have concluded with which We granted them respite in Our saying: فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ (Travel [freely] in the land for four months), then annihilate the polytheists by any possible means you see as closer and more conducive to annihilating their collective and erasing their trace—whether by killing them wherever you find them in the non-sacred area or the Sanctuary, and whenever you catch them in a sacred month or otherwise, or by seizing them, besieging them, or lying in wait for them at every ambush until they are annihilated to the last of them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ تابُوا وَأَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتَوُا الزَّكاةَ فَخَلُّوا سَبِيلَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — But if they repent and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, then let them go their way. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful (9:5). This is a condition in the sense of a limit (الغاية) for the previous judgment. What is meant by repentance (التوبة) is its linguistic meaning, which is "returning" (الرجوع); that is, if they return from polytheism to monotheism through faith (الإيمان) and establish a proof for that from their deeds—which are the prayer and zakat—and commit to the laws of your religion that pertain to the Creator and the creation together, then let them go their way.

"Letting them go their way" (تخلية السبيل) is a metaphor for not interfering with those who walk upon it. Even if it became commonplace through frequent use, their way was blocked and occupied by the interference of those who would interfere; so when it is cleared, that is concomitant with or identical to not interfering with them.

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful) is the rationale for His saying: فَخَلُّوا سَبِيلَهُمْ (then let them go their way), either from the perspective of the command (الأمر) which it indicates by its form, or from the perspective of the thing commanded (المأمور به) which it indicates by its substance—namely, letting them go their way.

The meaning according to the first: Allah only commanded letting them go their way because He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful; He forgives whoever repents to Him and has mercy on him.

According to the second: Let them go their way because your letting them go their way is from forgiveness and mercy, and these are among the supreme attributes of Allah; thus, you characterize yourselves with the attribute of your Lord. The more apparent of the two views is the first.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ أَحَدٌ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ اسْتَجارَكَ فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّى يَسْمَعَ كَلامَ اللهِ (If any of the polytheists seeks asylum from you, grant him asylum until he hears the Word of Allah) to the end of the verse. The verse contains the judgment of granting asylum (الإجارة) to whoever seeks it from the polytheists in order to hear the Word of Allah. Although the judgment it contains is parenthetical—or like a parenthesis—between what indicates disavowal and the revocation of protection from the polytheists, it is in the position of addressing a necessary objection that cannot be neglected. For the foundation of this true call (الدعوة الحقة) and what accompanies it of promise and threat, glad tidings and warning, and what follows from it of concluding contracts and firming covenants or revocation and disavowal and the laws of combat—all of that is only to turn people from the path of error and misguidance to the path of rectitude and guidance, and to save them from the misery of polytheism to the happiness of monotheism.

The implication of this is to pay complete attention to every path in which there is hope of reaching the guidance of one who is lost and succeeding in reviving a right, even if it be small. For the truth is truth even if it be small. Although Allah has disavowed the polytheist who has no treaty, made his blood lawful, and removed the sanctity from all that pertains to him of wealth and honor, He, the Exalted, only did that to him to revive a right and nullify a falsehood. Thus, if good is hoped for from him, that prevents any evil intent directed at him until despair of his guidance and salvation is reached.

Therefore, if a polytheist seeks asylum to look into what the true call invites to and to follow it if it becomes clear to him, it is obligatory to grant him asylum until he hears the Word of Allah, the veil of ignorance is lifted from him, and the proof is completed against him. If he persists after that in his misguidance and remains stubborn in his arrogance, he becomes one from whom protection has been removed, from whom disavowal has been declared, and it becomes obligatory to cleanse the earth of the filth of his existence by any possible means and whichever path is closer and easier. This is what is provided by His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ أَحَدٌ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ اسْتَجارَكَ فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّى يَسْمَعَ كَلامَ اللهِ ثُمَّ أَبْلِغْهُ مَأْمَنَهُ ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — If any of the polytheists seeks asylum from you, grant him asylum until he hears the Word of Allah, then convey him to his place of safety. That is because they are a people who do not know (9:6), along with the verses that surround it.

The meaning of the verse is: if one of these polytheists from whom protection has been removed asks you to secure him in your protection so he may attend your presence and speak with you regarding what you call to of the truth contained in the Word of Allah, then grant him asylum until he hears the Word of Allah and the veil of ignorance is lifted from him. Then convey him to his place of safety so he may possess from you a complete and perfect security. Allah only legislated this judgment and granted them this complete security because they are an ignorant people, and there is no harm upon an ignorant person if good is hoped from him by accepting the truth should it become clear to him.

This is the utmost that can be observed of the principles of virtue, the preservation of dignity, the spreading of mercy and kindness, and the nobility of humanity, which the Noble Quran has considered and the upright religion has invited to.

From what we have presented, several points have become clear:

First: The verse specifies (مخصصة) the generality of His saying in the previous verse: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ (slay the polytheists wherever you find them...).

Second: His saying: حَتَّى يَسْمَعَ كَلامَ اللهِ (until he hears the Word of Allah) is the limit (غاية) for the seeking of asylum and the granting of it; thus, the judgment is limited by it. The seeking of security was only for hearing the Word of Allah and inquiring about the materials of the message with the Messenger. Thus, the security given to the one seeking asylum and security is measured by it. When he has heard from the Word of Allah that by which rectitude is distinguished from error and guidance is distinguished from misguidance, the period of asylum has ended, and it is time for the seeker of asylum to be returned to his place of safety and his specific location where he is secure—where the swords of the Muslims do not threaten him—so he may return to the state he left and choose for himself what he wishes with freedom of will and intention.

Third: What is meant by the Word of Allah (كلام الله) is the verses of the Noble Quran in general. Yes, it is restricted to the verses that benefit the seeker of asylum, which clarify for him the principles of divine knowledge (أصول المعارف الإلهية), the landmarks of the religion, and the answers to the doubts that occur in his breast—all of this by the indication of the situation and the context.

By this, the invalidity of what was said becomes apparent: that what is meant by the Word of Allah are the verses of monotheism from the Quran. Similarly, what was said: that what is meant by it is Surah al-Bara’ah or specifically what they were notified of during the season from the verses at the beginning of the surah. All of that is specification without a specifier.

Fourth: What is meant by "hearing the Word of Allah" is becoming acquainted with the principles of the religion and its landmarks. Indeed, it can be said that the listening to the Word of Allah itself—in the case where the seeker of asylum is an Arab who understands the divine speech—has a role in that. However, if he is a non-Arab and does not understand the Arabic speech, what is understood from the context is that the limit in his case is merely the understanding (تفقه) of the principles of the religion and its landmarks.

Fifth: The verse is decisive (محكمة), not abrogated (منسوخة) nor susceptible to it. For it is clearly necessary from the taste of the religion and the outward meanings of the Book and the Sunnah that there is no punishment before the completion of the proof, and no severity—whatever it may be—except after the explanation. Thus, the ignorant person walking the path of investigation or the one seeking to know the truth and inquiring about reality is not turned away disappointed nor taken unawares. It is upon Islam and the Muslims to give full security to whoever seeks security from them to be present with the knowledge of the religion and to inquire about the principles of the call, so he may follow it if the signs of truthfulness appear to him therein. This is a principle that accepts neither invalidation nor change as long as Islam is Islam. Thus, the verse is decisive and not susceptible to abrogation until the Day of Resurrection.

From here, the invalidity of the statement of those who said that His saying: وَإِنْ أَحَدٌ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ اسْتَجارَكَ فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّى يَسْمَعَ كَلامَ اللهِ (If any of the polytheists seeks asylum from you, grant him asylum until he hears the Word of Allah) is abrogated by the following verse: وَقاتِلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ كَافَّةً كَما يُقاتِلُونَكُمْ كَافَّةً — and fight the polytheists all together as they fight you all together (9:36).

Sixth: The verse only necessitates granting asylum to the seeker of asylum if he seeks it for a religious matter in which good for the religion is hoped. As for absolute seeking of asylum not for a religious purpose nor a benefit returning to it, the verse has no indication for it at all; rather, the previous verses commanding severity toward them remain in place.

Seventh: His saying in completing the command of granting asylum: ثُمَّ أَبْلِغْهُ مَأْمَنَهُ (then convey him to his place of safety), along with the completion of His saying: فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّى يَسْمَعَ (grant him asylum until he hears) without it in indicating the objective, shows the complete concern for opening the door of guidance (الهداية) to the people, the preservation of people's freedom in their lives and vital activities, and the overlooking on its path of every final judgment and decisive resolution, so that "he who perished might perish by a clear proof and he who lived might live by a clear proof," and so that the people may have no argument against Allah after the messengers.

Eighth: The verse—as has been said—indicates that belief in the foundation of the religion must be from certain knowledge (علم يقيني) that is not entered by doubt nor mixed with suspicion, and nothing else suffices for it, even if it be a preponderant conjecture (الظن الراجح). Allah, the Exalted, has censured the following of conjecture and invited to the following of knowledge in many verses, such as His saying: وَلا تَقْفُ ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ — Do not follow that of which you have no knowledge (17:36), and His saying: إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَإِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً — They follow nothing but conjectures, and conjecture is no substitute for the truth (53:28), and His saying: ما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ — They have no knowledge of that; they do nothing but surmise (43:20).

If traditional belief (الاعتقاد التقليدي) sufficed for the foundation of the religion, the judgment of granting asylum to whoever seeks it to understand the principles of the religion and its knowledge would not be sound, for it would be permissible that he be tasked with imitation (التقليد) and refraining from investigating whether it is true or false.

However, the obligatory amount in that is that it be from certain knowledge (علم قطعي), whether it is obtained through deduction by technical methods or by other ways that provide knowledge, even if by chance. This is different from the statement that deduction regarding the principles of knowledge is only valid through the way of the intellect (العقل). For the correctness of deduction is one matter, and the permissibility of relying on knowledge through whatever way it is obtained is another matter.

His saying, the Exalted: كَيْفَ يَكُونُ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ عَهْدٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَعِنْدَ رَسُولِهِ (How can the polytheists have a treaty with Allah and His Apostle?) to the end of the verse. This is an explanation and clarification of what passed in summary regarding the judgment of violating the treaty of the polytheists from whom there is no trust in fulfilling their treaty, and killing them until they believe in Allah and submit to the religion of monotheism, and the exception of those who did not violate the treaty and remained faithful to the covenant until the period of their treaty concludes.

Thus, the verse and those that follow it until the completion of six verses explain that and clarify the judgment, the exception from it, the limit, and the limited together.

His saying: كَيْفَ يَكُونُ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ عَهْدٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَعِنْدَ رَسُولِهِ (How can the polytheists have a treaty with Allah and His Apostle?) is an interrogation in the position of denial (الإنكار). The verse hastened to except those with whom they had made a treaty at the Holy Mosque (المسجد الحرام) because they had not violated a treaty nor been negligent in what they had covenanted, as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: فَمَا اسْتَقامُوا لَكُمْ فَاسْتَقِيمُوا لَهُمْ — so as long as they are true to you, be true to them (9:7). This is because being true to those who are true and peace for those who are peaceful are among the requirements of religious godwariness (التقوى الديني). Therefore, He rationalized that saying of His by His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ (Indeed Allah loves the God-conscious), just as the exact same came in the previous verse: فَأَتِمُّوا إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلى مُدَّتِهِمْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ (So fulfill their treaty with them until [the end of] their term. Indeed Allah loves the God-conscious).

His saying, the Exalted: كَيْفَ وَإِنْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ لا يَرْقُبُوا فِيكُمْ إِلًّا وَلا ذِمَّةً (How? For if they get the better of you, they will observe toward you neither kinship nor treaty) to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Al-ill (الإل) is every manifest state of a sworn covenant and kinship that ta’illu (تئل): shines, such that it cannot be denied. The Exalted said: لا يَرْقُبُونَ فِي مُؤْمِنٍ إِلًّا وَلا ذِمَّةً (they observe toward a believer neither kinship nor treaty). And ala al-faras (آل الفرس): the horse quickened; its reality is that it shone, and that is a metaphor in the chapter of quickening like baraqa (flashed) and tara (flew)." End quote.

He also said: "Al-dhimam (الذمام)—with a kasrah on the dhal—is that for whose neglect a man is blamed (yudhammu), such as a treaty. Similarly, al-dhimmah (الذمة) and al-madhammah (المذمة). It is said: 'I have a madhammah, so do not violate it.' And 'I removed their madhammah with something,' meaning: I gave them something because of the dhimam they have." End quote. This is apparent in that al-dhimmah is taken from al-dhamm (blame) in the sense that is opposite to al-madh (praise).

Perhaps the placement of the contrast in the verse between al-ill and al-dhimmah is to indicate that they do not preserve regarding the believers any of the covenants that must be observed and kept, whether they are based on real, ontological foundations such as kinship (القرابة) which necessitates in a way that the relative observe the state of his relative, or based on convention and terminology such as treaties and covenants concluded by oath and the like.

The word "How" (kayfa) has been repeated for emphasis and to remove the ambiguity in the explanation arising from the intervention of His saying: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ (excepting those with whom you have made a treaty) in its full length between His saying: كَيْفَ يَكُونُ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ (How can the polytheists have...) and His saying: وَإِنْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ (For if they get the better of you...).

The meaning of the verse is: How can the polytheists have a treaty with Allah and His Apostle while the state is that if they get the better of you and overcome you in the matter, they will not preserve nor observe regarding you kinship nor any of the treaties? They please you with deceptive speech and embellished words, while their hearts refuse that, and most of them are transgressors (فاسقون).

From here, it appears that His saying: يُرْضُونَكُمْ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ (They please you with their mouths) is from rational metaphor (المجاز العقلي) in which the pleasing is attributed to the mouths, while it is in reality attributed to the speech and words coming out of the mouths formed within them.

His saying: يُرْضُونَكُمْ (They please you) etc., is the rationale for the denial of the existence of a treaty for the polytheists; therefore, it was brought with disjunction (الفصل). The implied meaning is: How can they have a treaty while they please you with their mouths and their hearts refuse and most of them are transgressors?

As for His saying: وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ فاسِقُونَ (and most of them are transgressors), it contains an explanation that most of them are violators of the treaty and covenant in actuality, without waiting for all of them to get the better of you. Thus, the verse clarifies the state of their individuals and their collective: that most of them are transgressors by violating the treaty without observing toward a believer kinship nor treaty, and had they all gotten the better of you, they would not have observed toward you kinship nor treaty.

His saying, the Exalted: اشْتَرَوْا بِآياتِ اللهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلاً (They have sold Allah’s signs for a paltry price) to the end of the two verses. This is an explanation and interpretation of His saying in the previous verse: وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ فاسِقُونَ (and most of them are transgressors). His saying: اشْتَرَوْا بِآياتِ اللهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلاً (They have sold Allah’s signs for a paltry price) to the end of the verse was a preparation and preamble for His saying in the second verse: لا يَرْقُبُونَ فِي مُؤْمِنٍ إِلًّا وَلا ذِمَّةً (They observe toward a believer neither kinship nor treaty).

By this, it appears that the closer view is that what is meant by transgression (الفسق) is the exiting from the treaty and protection, rather than transgression in the sense of exiting from the form of servitude to Allah, the Glorified, even if that is also the case.

His saying: وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُعْتَدُونَ (and it is they who are the transgressors) is like an interpretation for all that has passed of their spiritual states and physical deeds. The sentence further provides an answer to a presumed question or what stands in its place. The meaning is: since this is their state and these are their actions, do not think that if you violated their treaty you would be transgressing against them; rather, it is they who are the transgressors against you because of the enmity and hatred they harbored and because of what most of them manifested in the realm of action of barring from the way of Allah and not observing kinship nor treaty regarding the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ تابُوا وَأَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ (Yet if they repent and maintain the prayer) to the end of the two verses. The two verses are a detailed explanation of His previous saying: فَإِنْ تُبْتُمْ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ وَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللهِ (So if you repent, it will be better for you, and if you turn away, then know that you cannot thwart Allah).

What is meant by repentance (التوبة), by the indication of the context, is returning to faith in Allah and His signs. Therefore, He did not restrict it to repentance only, but conjoined to it the maintaining of the prayer—which is the most manifest of the manifestations of worshiping Allah—and the giving of zakat—which is the strongest pillar of the religious society. Through these two, an allusion was made to the types of religious duties by whose performance faith in the signs of Allah is completed after faith in Allah, exalted is His Name. This is the meaning of His saying: تابُوا وَأَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتَوُا الزَّكاةَ (repent and maintain the prayer and give the zakat).

As for His saying: فَإِخْوانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ (then they are your brethren in faith), what is meant by it is the explanation of equality between them and the rest of the believers in the rights that Islam considers in the Islamic society: they have what the Muslims have and they owe what the Muslims owe.

In the verse, He expressed that through brotherhood in religion (الأخوة في الدين), and He said in another place: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ — The faithful are indeed brothers (49:10), considering the equality between them in religious rights. For two brothers are siblings derived from a single substance, and they are therefore equal in the affairs pertaining to that in the society of the home under their father—who is the master of the house—and in the society of kinship among the relatives and the clan.

Since this meaning, called in the language of religion "brotherhood," has jurisprudential judgments and effects that the law of Islam has attended to, it is a real consideration of a type of brotherhood between the members of the Islamic society that has consequent effects, just as the natural brotherhood in what Islam has considered has consequent rational and religious effects. It is not merely a linguistic metaphor from a figurative concern. In what is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he said: "The believers are brothers; the lowest of them strives for their protection, and they are one hand against those besides them."

His saying: وَإِنْ نَكَثُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ عَهْدِهِمْ وَطَعَنُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ فَقاتِلُوا أَئِمَّةَ الْكُفْرِ إِنَّهُمْ لا أَيْمانَ لَهُمْ — But if they break their pledges after their covenant and revile your religion, then fight the leaders of unfaith—indeed they have no [binding] pledges (9:12). The context indicates that they are other than the polytheists whose treaty Allah, the Glorified, commanded to be violated in the previous verse and mentioned that they are the transgressors who observe toward a believer neither kinship nor treaty. For those were violators of oaths and breakers of the treaty; thus, the condition mentioned by Allah, the Glorified, in His saying: وَإِنْ نَكَثُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ (But if they break their pledges) etc., would not be sound regarding them.

Thus, these are another people who have treaties and oaths with the possessor of authority (ولي الأمر) from the Muslims, and they break their oaths after their covenant—that is, they violate their treaties after concluding them. So Allah, the Glorified, commanded to fight them, nullified their oaths, and named them "the leaders of unfaith" (أئمة الكفر) because they are the foremost in disbelief in the signs of Allah, followed by others who succeed them. They are all fought so that perhaps they may desist from breaking oaths and violating treaties.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلا تُقاتِلُونَ قَوْماً نَكَثُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ وَهَمُّوا بِإِخْراجِ الرَّسُولِ — Will you not fight a people who broke their pledges and resolved to expel the Apostle (9:13). This verse and those after it until the completion of four verses are an incitement of the believers and an agitation of them to fight the polytheists by explaining what they have committed of crimes against Allah and how they have betrayed the truth and reality, and by counting their sins and transgressions such as breaking oaths, resolving to expel the Messenger, and starting the fight the first time.

Then, by informing the believers that the requirement of their faith in Allah—Who possesses all good and evil, benefit and harm—is that they should fear none but Him if they are believers in Him. In that, there is a strengthening of their hearts and an encouragement of them against them. It concludes with the explanation that they are tested by Allah regarding the sincerity of faith for Him and the cutting off from the polytheists, so they may be rewarded with what the believer who is realized in his faith is rewarded.

His saying, the Exalted: قاتِلُوهُمْ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ اللهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ — Fight them: Allah will punish them by your hands (9:14) to the end of the two verses. He repeated the command to fight because, from the perspective of the preceding incitement and provocation, it became more likely to be accepted. For the first command was initial, not preceded by preparation and preamble, unlike the second command which came after the intensification of readiness and the completion of preparation by those commanded.

Furthermore, what followed the command of His saying: يُعَذِّبْهُمُ اللهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ وَيُخْزِهِمْ (Allah will punish them by your hands and disgrace them) to His saying: وَيُذْهِبْ غَيْظَ قُلُوبِهِمْ (and remove the rage of their hearts) emphasizes the command and entices those commanded to obey it and execute it upon the polytheists. For their remembering that the killing of the polytheists is a divine punishment for them by the hands of the believers, and that the believers are the executing hands for Allah, the Glorified, and that in that is a disgrace for the polytheists, a victory from Allah for the believers over them, a healing for the breasts of a believing people, and a removal of the rage of their hearts—all this emboldens them for the work, activates them, and purifies their will.

His saying: وَيَتُوبُ اللهُ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ — and Allah turns clemently to whomever He wishes (9:15) is in the position of an exception so that the judgment of combat does not proceed in its absolute sense.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تُتْرَكُوا وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللهُ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا مِنْكُمْ — Do you suppose that you will be let off while Allah has not yet known those of you who have waged jihad (9:16) to the end of the verse. This is in the position of another rationale for the obligation of fighting them, to result in their incitement to combat. It contains an explanation of the reality of the matter, the summary of which is that the world is the abode of trial and tribulation (الامتحان والابتلاء). For human souls accept good and evil, happiness and misery; they are in their first existence simple and indeterminate. The ranks of proximity and closeness are only granted in exchange for sincere faith in Allah and His signs. The purity of faith only appears through the trial that brings the believer to the station of action, so that Allah may thereby distinguish the good from the wicked, and the one of pure faith from the one who has nothing but a mere claim or pretension.

Thus, it is obligatory that these claimants—who say they have sold their souls and wealth to Allah in exchange for Paradise—be tested and tried by something like combat, by which the truthful is distinguished from the liar, and the one who has cut the ties of love and connection with the enemies of Allah, the Glorified, is separated from the one in whose heart remain remnants of their guardianship (ولاية) and affection, so that these may live and those may perish.

Therefore, it is upon the believers to obey the command of combat; indeed, they should hasten to it and race in it to manifest thereby the purity of their essence and the reality of their faith, and to argue with it before their Lord on a day when there is no success except by the argument of truth.

So His saying: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تُتْرَكُوا (Do you suppose that you will be let off) means: rather, did you think that you would be left as you are in your state while the reality of your truthfulness in the claim of faith in Allah and His signs has not yet appeared?

His saying: وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللهُ (while Allah has not yet known) etc., means: while your jihad and your not taking any intimate (وليجة) besides Allah, His Messenger, and the believers has not yet appeared in the external world. For the actualization of things is the "knowing" of them by Him, the Exalted. A similar discourse has passed with the expansion of what is in the exegesis of His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللهُ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا مِنْكُمْ — Do you suppose that you will enter Paradise while Allah has not yet known those of you who have waged jihad (3:142) in the fourth volume of the book. Among the proofs for what we have mentioned regarding the meaning of "knowing" is His saying at the end of the verse: وَاللهُ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ — and Allah is well aware of what you do.

The walijah (الوليجة)—according to what is in al-Raghib's al-Mufradat—is everything that a person takes as a support while not being of his people.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (A repudiation from Allah and His Apostle): My father narrated to me from Muhammad ibn al-Fadl from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from Abi al-Sabbah al-Kinani from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "This verse was revealed after the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) returned from the expedition of Tabuk—in the ninth year of the Hijrah."

He said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) conquered Mecca, he did not prevent the polytheists from the Hajj in that year. It was a custom of the Arabs in the Hajj that whoever entered Mecca and circumambulated the House in his clothes, it was not permissible for him to keep them; they would give them in charity and not wear them after the circumambulation (الطواف). Thus, whoever arrived at Mecca would borrow a garment and circumambulate in it, then return it. Whoever did not find one for loan or rent—and had only one garment—would circumambulate the House naked."

"Then a woman from the Arabs came, handsome and beautiful. She sought a garment for loan or rent but did not find one. They said to her: 'If you circumambulate in your clothes, you will need to give them in charity.' She said: 'How can I give them in charity when I have no others?' So she circumambulated the House naked, and the people looked at her. She placed one of her hands over her front and the other over her back and recited poetry:

Today some of it appears, or all of it, And what appears of it, I do not make lawful.

When she finished the circumambulation, a group proposed to her. She said: 'I have a husband.'"

"The practice of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) before the revelation of Surah al-Bara’ah was that he would not fight except those who fought him, and would not wage war except against those who waged war against him and sought him. Regarding that, there had been revealed to him: فَإِنِ اعْتَزَلُوكُمْ فَلَمْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ وَأَلْقَوْا إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلَمَ ـ فَما جَعَلَ اللهُ لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سَبِيلاً — So if they keep out of your way and do not fight you, and offer you peace, then Allah does not allow you any way [to harm] them (4:90). Thus, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would not fight anyone who had withdrawn from him and kept out of his way, until Surah al-Bara’ah was revealed to him, and he was commanded to kill the polytheists—those who withdrew from him and those who did not—except those with whom the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had made a treaty on the day of the Conquest of Mecca for a term. Among them were Safwan ibn Umayyah and Suhayl ibn ‘Amr. So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ـ إِلَى الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ ـ فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ (A repudiation from Allah and His Apostle... Travel [freely] in the land for four months), then they are killed wherever they are found afterward. These months of travel are: twenty days of Dhu al-Hijjah, al-Muharram, Safar, the month of Rabi‘ al-Awwal, and ten days of Rabi‘ al-Akhir."

"When the verses of Surah al-Bara’ah were revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) gave them to Abu Bakr and commanded him to go out to Mecca and read them to the people at Mina on the Day of Sacrifice. When Abu Bakr went out, Gabriel descended to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Muhammad, none shall deliver on your behalf except a man from you.'"

"So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) in pursuit of Abu Bakr. He caught up with him at al-Rawha’ and took the verses from him. Abu Bakr returned to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, has Allah revealed something regarding me?' He replied: 'No, but Allah commanded me that none shall deliver on my behalf except myself or a man from me.'"

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Harir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah sent Abu Bakr with Bara’ah to the season [of Hajj] to read it to the people. Then Gabriel descended and said: 'None shall convey on your behalf except ‘Ali.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called ‘Ali and commanded him to ride his she-camel, al-‘Adba’, and commanded him to catch up with Abu Bakr and take Bara’ah from him and read it to the people at Mecca. Abu Bakr said: 'Is there displeasure?' He replied: 'No, except that it was revealed to him that none shall convey except a man from you.'"

When he arrived at Mecca on the Day of Sacrifice (يوم النحر) after noon—which is the Day of the Great Pilgrimage (يوم الحج الأكبر)—he stood and said: "I am the messenger of the Messenger of Allah to you." Then he recited to them: [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 9, Ayah 1-2] — [the four months being] twenty days of Dhu al-Hijjah, al-Muharram, Safar, Rabi‘ al-Awwal, and ten days of Rabi‘ al-Akhar. He said: "No naked man or woman shall circumambulate the House, nor shall any polytheist (مشرك) perform the pilgrimage after this year. And whoever has a treaty with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, its term is extended until these four months."

I say: The intent is to specify the duration for treaties that had no fixed term, as evidenced by the narrations that follow. As for treaties with a specified term, their validity until their expiration is indicated by the noble verses themselves.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi and al-Majma‘, narrated from Abu Basir from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, he said: "Ali عليه‌السلام addressed the people while unsheathing his sword and said: 'No naked person shall circumambulate the House, no polytheist shall perform the pilgrimage to the House, and whoever has a fixed term [in a treaty], it remains until its term; whoever has no fixed term, his term is four months.'" He delivered this sermon on the Day of Sacrifice, and the period consisted of twenty days of Dhu al-Hijjah, al-Muharram, Safar, Rabi‘ al-Awwal, and ten days of Rabi‘ al-Akhar. He also said: "The Day of Sacrifice is the Day of the Great Pilgrimage (يوم الحج الأكبر)."

I say: The narrations from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام regarding these meanings are beyond counting.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd Allah ibn Ahmad ibn Hanbal recorded in Zawa’id al-Musnad, as did Abu al-Shaykh and Ibn Mardawayh, from Ali رضي الله عنه, who said: "When ten verses of Bara’ah were revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he called Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه to recite them to the people of Mecca. Then he called me and said: 'Overtake Abu Bakr; wherever you meet him, take the document from him.'"

Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه returned and said: "O Messenger of Allah, has something [revelation] been sent down regarding me?" He replied: "No, but Gabriel came to me and said: 'None shall discharge [this duty] on your behalf except you or a man from you.'"

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Sa‘d ibn Abi Waqqas رضي الله عنه: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه with Bara’ah to the people of Mecca. Then he sent Ali رضي الله عنه after him and he took it from him. Abu Bakr felt some distress within himself, so the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'O Abu Bakr, indeed, none shall discharge [this duty] on my behalf except I or a man from me.'"

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Abu Rafi‘ رضي الله عنه, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه with Bara’ah to the season [of pilgrimage]. Then Gabriel عليه‌السلام came and said: 'Indeed, none shall discharge it except you or a man from you.' So he sent Ali رضي الله عنه after him until he caught up with him between Mecca and Medina, took it, and recited it to the people during the season."

In the same source, Ibn Hibban and Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه to discharge Bara’ah on his behalf. After sending him, he sent for Ali رضي الله عنه and said: 'O Ali, none shall discharge on my behalf except I or you.' He mounted him on his she-camel, al-Adhba’, and he traveled until he caught up with Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه and took Bara’ah from him."

Abu Bakr came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, having been overcome by fear that something [revelation] had been sent down regarding him. When he reached him, he said: "What is the matter with me, O Messenger of Allah?" He replied: "It is well; you are my brother and my companion in the cave, and you are with me at the Pond (الحوض). However, none shall convey (يبلغ) on my behalf except a man from me."

I say: There are other narrations with the same meaning as what has preceded. It is narrated in Tafsir al-Burhan from Ibn Shahr Ashub that it was narrated by al-Tabarsi, al-Baladhuri, al-Tirmidhi, al-Waqidi, al-Sha‘bi, al-Suddi, al-Tha‘labi, al-Wahidi, al-Qurtubi, al-Qushayri, al-Sam‘ani, Ahmad ibn Hanbal, Ibn Battah, Muhammad ibn Ishaq, Abu Ya‘la al-Mawsili, al-A‘mash, and Simak ibn Harb in their books from Urwah ibn al-Zubayr, Abu Hurayrah, Anas, Abu Rafi‘, Zayd ibn Nufay‘, Ibn Umar, and Ibn Abbas—and the wording is his: "When بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (A repudiation from Allah and His Messenger) was revealed up to nine verses, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله dispatched Abu Bakr to Mecca to discharge it. Then Gabriel descended and said: 'Indeed, none shall discharge it except you or a man from you.' So the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to the Commander of the Faithful: 'Mount my she-camel, al-Adhba’, overtake Abu Bakr, and take Bara’ah from his hand.'"

He said: "When Abu Bakr returned to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he was distressed and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, you deemed me worthy of a matter for which necks were craned [in anticipation]; yet when I set out for it, you turned me back from it?' He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: 'The Trustworthy One [Gabriel] descended to me from Allah the Exalted [saying]: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you"; and Ali is from me, and none shall discharge on my behalf except Ali.'"

In the narrations we have cited, and those we have omitted—which are more numerous—and in what is to come in this chapter, there are two fundamental points (نكتتان أصليتان).

First: The Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sending of Ali with Bara’ah and his removal of Abu Bakr was solely by the command of his Lord through the descent of Gabriel: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you." This ruling was not restricted in any of the narrations to Bara’ah or the breaking of the treaty (نقض العهد). It was not stated in any of them: "None shall discharge Bara’ah or break the treaty except you or a man from you." Thus, there is no evidence for restricting it to Bara’ah as occurs in many commentaries; the absolute nature (الإطلاق) of the statement is supported by what follows.

Second: Just as Ali عليه‌السلام was proclaiming Bara’ah, he was also proclaiming another ruling: that whoever has a fixed term [in a treaty], it remains until its term, and whoever has no fixed term, his term is four months. This is also indicated by the verses of Bara’ah.

He also proclaimed another ruling: that no naked person shall circumambulate the House. This is also a divine ruling indicated by His saying, the Exalted: [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 7, Ayah 31]. Some narrations mention the verse along with the ruling, as will be seen.

Another ruling was that no polytheist shall circumambulate or perform pilgrimage to the House after this year, which is the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 9, Ayah 28].

There is a fifth matter mentioned in some narrations of this chapter that he عليه‌السلام was proclaiming: "None shall enter Paradise except a believer." Although this is not mentioned in other narrations, and rational consideration (الاعتبار) does not support it—since many Meccan and Medinan verses had been revealed regarding this years prior, and it is historically improbable that this matter remained hidden from the polytheists until the ninth year of the Hijrah—it is nonetheless indicated by the noble verses. In any case, Ali's عليه‌السلام mission was not limited to delivering the verses of Bara’ah; rather, it was for that and for the communication (تبليغ) of three or four other Quranic rulings. All of these are included in what Gabriel brought down from Allah, the Glorified, to His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "None shall discharge on your behalf except you or a man from you," as there is no evidence to restrict the speech from its absolute sense at all.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it hasan), Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who graded it sahih), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il recorded from Ibn Abbas رضي الله عنه: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه and commanded him to proclaim these words. Then he sent Ali رضي الله عنه after him and commanded him to proclaim them. They set out and performed the pilgrimage. Ali رضي الله عنه stood during the Days of Tashriq (أيام التشريق) and proclaimed: 'Allah and His Messenger are free from the polytheists; so travel the land for four months. No polytheist shall perform the pilgrimage after this year, no naked person shall circumambulate the House, and none shall enter Paradise except a believer.' Ali رضي الله عنه continued to proclaim these."

I say: This report is close in content to what we have derived from the narrations.

In the same source, Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim recorded via Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib from Abu Hurayrah: "Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه commanded him to announce Bara’ah during the pilgrimage of Abu Bakr." Abu Hurayrah said: "Then the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Ali رضي الله عنه after us, commanding him to announce Bara’ah, while Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه remained in charge of the season [of pilgrimage] as he was."

I say: It has been reported through several Sunni paths that the Prophet appointed Abu Bakr over the pilgrimage that year, so he was the leader of the pilgrims (أمير الحاج) while Ali proclaimed Bara’ah. The Shi‘a have narrated that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله appointed Ali for the leadership just as he charged him with discharging the verses of Bara’ah. Al-Tabarsi mentioned this in Majma‘ al-Bayan, and al-Ayyashi narrated it from Zurarah from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام. This may be supported by reports that Ali was acting as a judge (يقضي) during that journey and that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله prayed for him in that regard; for it is well known that a mere mission to deliver Bara’ah does not include the authority to judge between people, and such authority is most consistent during those days with the leadership (الإمارة). The narration is as follows:

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from al-Hasan from Ali عليه‌السلام: "When the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent him with Bara’ah, he said: 'O Prophet of Allah, I am not eloquent (لسن) nor a public speaker (خطيب).' He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: 'Allah refuses [any other way]; either I must go with it or you must go.' He said: 'If it must be so, then I shall go.' He said: 'Then go, for Allah will steady your tongue and guide your heart.' Then he placed his hand over his [Ali's] mouth and said: 'Go and recite it to the people.' And he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'The people will bring their disputes to you; so when two litigants come to you, do not judge for one until you hear the other, for it is more likely that you will know the truth.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated through Sunni paths as well, as in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abu al-Shaykh from Ali رضي الله عنه, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent me to Yemen with Bara’ah. I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, you send me while I am a youth of young age, and I will be asked about judgment (القضاء) and will not know how to answer?' He said: 'There is no choice but that I go with it or you go with it.' I said: 'If it must be so, I will go.' He said: 'Go, for Allah will steady your tongue and guide your heart.' Then he said: 'Go and recite it to the people.'"

However, the inclusion of the word "Yemen" in the narration raises suspicion, as it is clear from the wording of the verses of Bara’ah that they were to be proclaimed to the people of Mecca on the Day of the Great Pilgrimage in Mecca. What does that have to do with Yemen and its people? It is as if the wording of the narration was "to Mecca," and "to Yemen" was substituted to justify the mention of the discourse on judgment.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, al-Nasa’i, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "I was with Ali رضي الله عنه when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent him. He sent Ali with four [commands]: No naked person shall circumambulate the House; the Muslims and polytheists shall not gather after their year; whoever has a treaty between him and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, it remains until its term; and Allah and His Messenger are free from the polytheists."

I say: This meaning is narrated from Abu Hurayrah through several paths with different wordings that are not free from issues in their content—as will be seen—and the most robust of these narrations in content is the one we have cited.

In the same source, Ahmad, al-Nasa’i, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "I was with Ali when the Messenger of Allah sent him to the people of Mecca with Bara’ah. We were proclaiming that none shall enter Paradise except a believer, no naked person shall circumambulate the House, and whoever has a treaty between him and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, his matter or term is until four months; when the four months pass, then Allah and His Messenger are free from the polytheists, and no polytheist shall perform pilgrimage to this House after the year."

I say: There is clear inconsistency (اضطراب) in the content of this narration. First: because it includes the proclamation that "none shall enter Paradise except a believer," and it has already been mentioned that many Meccan and Medinan verses had been revealed regarding this years prior, heard by city-dwellers, Bedouins, polytheists, and believers alike; so what conceivable need was there to convey this to the gathered people?

Second: because the second proclamation—meaning his saying: "whoever has a treaty between him and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله..."—does not align with the contents of the verses nor with the contents of the numerous previous narrations. Furthermore, it places the repudiation (البراءة) after the passing of four months.

Third: for reasons we shall mention in the following.

In the same source, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il recorded from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "Abu Bakr رضي الله عنه sent me during that pilgrimage among the announcers (مؤذنين) he sent on the Day of Sacrifice to announce at Mina: 'No polytheist shall perform pilgrimage after this year, and no naked person shall circumambulate the House.' Then the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Ali ibn Abi Talib رضي الله عنه after him and commanded him to announce Bara’ah. So Ali announced Bara’ah with us to the people of Mina on the Day of Sacrifice, and that no polytheist shall perform pilgrimage after the year, and no naked person shall circumambulate the House."

In Tafsir al-Manar, from al-Tirmidhi from Ibn Abbas: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Abu Bakr...
