Its objective is as significant as, if not more than, His saying: «قُلْ إِنْ كانَ آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ». Thus, there is no reason for it to be subordinate to it or coupled with it in meaning.

Furthermore, if it were part of what the people must be addressed with, it would have been necessary to say: "And say to them: Allah has certainly helped you in many situations..." as is the Quranic style in similar instances, such as His saying: «قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ» — Say, ‘I am only a mortal like you. It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God’ — until He said — «قُلْ أَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَكْفُرُونَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَرْضَ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ» — Say, ‘Do you really disbelieve in Him who created the earth in two days...’ (Fussilat: 9), and other instances.

Moreover, the context of the verses and what they must contain of shifts in address (الالتفات) and other elements—were they part of the quoted speech—do not align with them being part of the previous quoted speech.

The address in His saying: «لَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللهُ» — Allah has certainly helped you — and what follows in His saying: «إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ» — when your great number impressed you — is to the Muslims, who constitute a single Islamic society that attended, in this unified capacity, the events of Badr, Uhud, al-Khandaq, Khaybar, Hunayn, and others.

Among them were hypocrites (المنافقون), those weak in faith, and the sincerely faithful, despite their differing ranks. However, the address is directed to all of them because it includes those to whom it is appropriate to say: «إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ» — when your great number impressed you — until the end of the verse.

His saying: «وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ» — and on the day of Hunayn — means: and on the day the battle occurred between you and your enemies in the valley of Hunayn. Attributing the "day" to the locations of great events is common in usage, as one says: the Day of Badr, the Day of Uhud, and the Day of al-Khandaq, similar to attributing it to the groups involved, like the Day of the Confederates (الأحزاب) or the Day of Tamim, or to the event itself, like the Day of the Conquest of Mecca.

His saying: «إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ» — when your great number impressed you — means: the multitude you observed in yourselves delighted you, causing you to cease relying on Allah and trusting in His support and power. You leaned upon your numbers, hoping they would repel the enemy's plot and defeat their gathering. Yet, it is but one of the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) which has no effect except by the will of Allah, to Whom belongs the causation of all causes.

In view of this meaning, He followed His saying: «إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ» with His saying: «فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنْكُمْ شَيْئاً» — but it did not avail you anything. That is, you took it as an independent cause besides Allah, which made you forget reliance on Allah. You leaned upon it, and thus the reality of this illusory cause became clear to you: that it possesses no sufficiency to suffice you. So, it did not avail you anything—neither victory nor anything else.

His saying: «وَضاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ» — and the earth became narrow for you despite its expanse. This is a metaphor for the enemy surrounding them so completely that they could find no place of safety on earth to settle, nor a cave to take refuge in to protect them from the enemy. That is, you fled a flight in which you would not turn back for anything.

This is close in meaning to His saying regarding the story of the Confederates (الأحزاب): «إِذْ جاؤُكُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ وَمِنْ أَسْفَلَ مِنْكُمْ وَإِذْ زاغَتِ الْأَبْصارُ وَبَلَغَتِ الْقُلُوبُ الْحَناجِرَ وَتَظُنُّونَ بِاللهِ الظُّنُونَا» — When they came at you from above you and from below you, and when the eyes rolled [with fear] and the hearts reached the throats, and you entertained [various] thoughts about Allah. (Al-Ahzab: 10).

The statement of some that it means: "the earth became narrow for you so you found no place to flee to," is incorrect.

His saying: «ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ» — then you turned back, retreating — means: you made the enemy follow your backs, which is a metaphor for defeat. This was the flight from the battlefield (الفرار من الزحف) to which they were led by their complacency in their numbers and their disconnection from their Lord. The Exalted said: «يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا زَحْفاً فَلا تُوَلُّوهُمُ الْأَدْبارَ وَمَنْ يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ» — O you who have faith! When you encounter the faithless on the battlefield, do not turn your backs to them. Whoever turns his back [to them] on such a day... — until He said — «فَقَدْ باءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَمَأْواهُ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ» — ...shall certainly incur Allah’s wrath, and his abode shall be hell, and it is an evil destination. (Al-Anfal: 15-16). And He said: «وَلَقَدْ كانُوا عاهَدُوا اللهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ لا يُوَلُّونَ الْأَدْبارَ وَكانَ عَهْدُ اللهِ مَسْؤُلاً» — though they had certainly pledged to Allah before that they would not turn their backs [to the enemy], and pledges to Allah are accountable. (Al-Ahzab: 15).

All of this—namely, the earth becoming narrow for them despite its expanse, then their defeat and flight from the battlefield despite the great sin it entails, and their standing in this position that warrants reproach from their Lord—was only brought upon them by their reliance and complacency in these mirage-like causes that avail them nothing.

Yet Allah, in the vastness of His mercy and the greatness of His favor, bestowed upon them His victory, the descent of His tranquility (السكينة), and the sending of hosts they did not see, and the punishment of the disbelievers. He gave a general promise of His forgiveness—a promise whose occurrence is not so certain as to eliminate the quality of fear from their hearts, nor so certain of its absence as to remove the quality of hope from their souls. Rather, it is a promise that maintains in them balance and moderation between fear and hope, and nurtures them with a fine upbringing that prepares them for true felicity.

One of the commentators has offered a strange interpretation of the verse, attempting to reconcile various narrations. He insisted on a summary that the Muslims did not flee out of cowardice, but rather they were displaced from their positions when they were surprised by the intense charge of the battalions of Thaqif and Hawazin as one man. They were shaken by a disturbance that unsettled them and displaced them from their positions all at once. This is a natural human reaction when danger surprises one and calamity strikes suddenly without delay; the soul is agitated and leaves its place.

He argues that the descent of tranquility (السكينة) upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and upon all of them testifies to this, as the agitation had encompassed him and them all. However, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was affected by agitation and anxiety out of grief and regret for what had occurred, while the Muslims were affected by it because they were surprised by the charge of the battalions as one man.

Among the evidences he cites is that as soon as they heard the call of the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the call of al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, they returned immediately and defeated the disbelievers through the tranquility (السكينة) sent down upon them from Allah the Exalted.

Then he mentioned the verses revealed in praise of the Companions, such as the verse of the Pledge of Ridwan (بيعة الرضوان), and His saying: «مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ» — Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless..., and His saying: «إِنَّ اللهَ اشْتَرى مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوالَهُمْ بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ» — Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful their souls and their possessions for paradise being theirs..., and what has been narrated in praise of the Prophet's (ص) companions. [End of his summary].

What he has presented is a conflation between exegetical inquiry (البحث التفسيري)—which is concerned solely with uncovering what the noble verses indicate—and theological inquiry (البحث الكلامي)—which seeks to prove the claims of a theologian in a particular school of thought by any means possible, whether through reason, the Book, the Sunnah, consensus, or a mixture thereof. Exegetical inquiry does not permit the researcher any of that, nor the imposition of any scientific view upon the Book that Allah sent down as an exposition (تبيانا).

As for his claim that they did not flee out of cowardice or to desert the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), but rather it was a displacement due to a surprise that agitated and unsettled them, causing them to flee and then return—this does not negate the explicit wording of His saying: «ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ» — then you turned back, retreating. This act of theirs falls under the universal scope of His saying in the verse prohibiting flight from the battlefield: «فَلا تُوَلُّوهُمُ الْأَدْبارَ وَمَنْ يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ» — do not turn your backs to them. Whoever turns his back [to them] on such a day... — until He said — «فَقَدْ باءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللهِ» — ...shall certainly incur Allah’s wrath.

The Exalted did not restrict the prohibition of turning one's back to it being out of cowardice or for the purpose of desertion. Nor did He exempt from the ruling of prohibition a flight caused by a sudden agitation. He mentioned no exception except what He stated in His saying: «إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفاً لِقِتالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى فِئَةٍ» — unless it be for a tactical maneuver or to join [another] company. These two exceptions are not, in reality, flight from the battlefield.

Furthermore, the Exalted did not mention any exception in what He recounted of their pledge when He said: «وَلَقَدْ كانُوا عاهَدُوا اللهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ لا يُوَلُّونَ الْأَدْبارَ وَكانَ عَهْدُ اللهِ مَسْؤُلاً» — though they had certainly pledged to Allah before that they would not turn their backs [to the enemy], and pledges to Allah are accountable. (Al-Ahzab: 15).

As for his inference that the agitation was shared between them and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and his deduction from His saying: «ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — Then Allah sent down His composure (السكينة) upon His Apostle and upon the faithful — where the descent of tranquility (السكينة) occurred some time after their displacement (as indicated by the word thumma [then])—this implies the descent of agitation upon the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at that time, even if it were out of grief and regret, since it is inconceivable for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to waver in his steadfastness and courage.

Let us examine the grief and regret he attributed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Was it grief and regret over what had occurred of the Muslims' defeat and the trial and tribulation Allah afflicted them with as a consequence of their pride in their numbers—in short, a grief disliked by Allah? Allah has purified him from that and disciplined him with what He revealed of His Book and taught him of His knowledge. He revealed to him such as His saying: «لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ» — You have no hand in the matter... (Al-Imran: 128), and He said: «سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى» — We shall have you recite [the Quran] and then you will not forget. (Al-A'la: 6).

Moreover, it is not mentioned in any of the narrations of the story that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) moved from his place that day or was agitated by the weakness and defeat that befell the Muslims.

If that grief and regret were for the Muslims because of what befell them due to their error in relying on other than Allah, leaning on the mirage of apparent causes, and neglecting to hold fast to Allah—to the point that it led them into the sin of fleeing from the battlefield—then this is something Allah loves, and He has praised His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for it when He said: «بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ» — ...kind and merciful to the faithful. (At-Tawbah: 128).

Such regret and grief do not vanish with the descent of tranquility (السكينة) upon him. Nor is the tranquility (السكينة)—if we assume its descent was for that reason—something that occurred only after the defeat so that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would have been devoid of it before that. Rather, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was upon a clear proof from his Lord from the time Allah commissioned him until He took him to Himself, and tranquility (السكينة) in this sense was descending upon him time after time.

Furthermore, what is this tranquility (السكينة) that descended upon the believers? And what does he consider it to be? Was it the psychological state resulting from stillness and composure, as he interpreted it, citing the author of al-Misbah that it is applied to gravity, dignity, and solemnity, such that the steadfastness and stillness of the disbelievers in their military positions was due to a tranquility (السكينة) descending upon them? If tranquility (السكينة) were this, then at the beginning of the encounter, it was with the disbelievers of Hawazin and Thaqif, the opponents of the Muslims. Then it left them and descended upon the general Muslim army—including the believer who stood firm with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the believer who did not stand firm and chose flight over staying, the hypocrite, and the one weak in faith with a diseased heart. For they all returned a second time to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and stood firm with him until they defeated the enemy. Thus, they would all be the recipients of the tranquility (السكينة) Allah sent down to them. Why then does the Exalted restrict the descent of tranquility (السكينة) to His Messenger and the believers when He says: «ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ»?

Moreover, if tranquility (السكينة) were this common state, granted to every believer and disbeliever, what is the meaning of Allah favoring the believers with it in a way that suggests it is a special, non-common gift? He has only mentioned it in His speech in a few instances—about half a dozen—not reaching ten.

By this, it becomes clear that tranquility (السكينة) is something beyond mere stillness and steadfastness. It is not that it has a meaning in language or custom beyond the concept of the psychological state resulting from stillness and composure, but rather that what the Exalted intends by tranquility (السكينة) in His speech has an instance (مصداق) other than the instance we find in every brave hero who has a calm soul and a steady heart. Rather, it is a specific type of psychological composure with a particular character and quality.

How could it be otherwise? Whenever Allah mentions it in His speech as a favor upon His Messenger and the believers, He specifies its descent from Him. Thus, it is a divine state (حالة إلهية) in which the servant does not forget the station of his Lord, unlike the general state of brave men of strength and valor who are impressed by their own bravery and rely on themselves.

In His speech, it is surrounded by descriptions and effects that do not encompass every solemnity and psychological composure. As He said regarding His Messenger: «إِذ يَقُولُ لِصاحِبِهِ لا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَنا فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ وَأَيَّدَهُ بِجُنُودٍ لَمْ تَرَوْها» — ...when he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us.’ Then Allah sent down His composure (السكينة) upon him and strengthened him with hosts you did not see... (At-Tawbah: 40). And He said regarding the believers: «لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ فَعَلِمَ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ» — Allah was certainly pleased with the faithful when they swore allegiance to you under the tree. He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down composure (السكينة) on them... (Al-Fath: 18). He mentioned that He only sent down tranquility (السكينة) upon them because of what He knew was in their hearts. Thus, its descent requires a prior pure heart-state, which the context indicates is sincerity (الصدق) and the heart's purity from harboring intentions of opposition.

He also said: «هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ» — It is He who sent down composure (السكينة) into the hearts of the faithful that they might enhance their faith with [further] faith. To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth... (Al-Fath: 4). He mentioned that among its effects is the increase of faith upon faith. He also said: «إِذْ جَعَلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْحَمِيَّةَ حَمِيَّةَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ التَّقْوى وَكانُوا أَحَقَّ بِها وَأَهْلَها» — When the faithless nourished bigotry in their hearts, the bigotry of pagan ignorance, Allah sent down His composure (السكينة) upon His Apostle and upon the faithful, and made them abide by the word of Godwariness (كلمة التقوى), and they were worthier of it and deserved it. (Al-Fath: 26).

The verse—as you see—mentions that the descent of tranquility (السكينة) from Him is preceded by a prior readiness, worthiness, and deservingness, which was alluded to in the previous verse by His saying: «فَعَلِمَ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ». It also mentions that among its effects is the adherence to the word of Godwariness (كلمة التقوى) and the purification of the person from opposing Allah and His Messenger by committing prohibitions and falling into sins.

This serves as an explanation for His saying in the other verse: «لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ». The increase of faith upon faith through the descent of tranquility (السكينة) means that the person is under divine protection from committing sins and violating prohibitions, alongside a sincere faith in the essence of the true call.

This is an excellent witness that testifies, firstly: that the intended "believers" in His saying in the verse under discussion — «ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — are other than the hypocrites, those with diseased hearts, and those weak in faith. There remain only those believers who stood firm with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—who were three, four, nine, ten, eighty, or less than a hundred according to the varying narrations—and those who fled and were displaced from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) initially, then returned and fought a second time, among whom were the majority of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) companions and several of their elite.

So, is the intended meaning of the believers upon whom it descended all those who stood firm with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and those who fled initially then returned, or is it only those who stood firm with him from among the believers until victory descended?

What is gathered from the verses of tranquility (السكينة) is that its descent is contingent upon a prior heart-purity and spiritual clarity so that Allah may establish it with tranquility (السكينة). These others had committed the major sin of fleeing from the battlefield, their hearts being sinful. There is no place for the descent of tranquility (السكينة) upon one in such a state. If they were among those upon whom tranquility (السكينة) descended, it would have been necessary for them to regret what they did and repent to their Lord with a sincere repentance (توبة نصوحا) with truthful hearts, so that Allah would know what was in their hearts and send down tranquility (السكينة) upon them. Thus, they would have sinned first, then repented and returned, and then Allah sent down His tranquility (السكينة) upon them and aided them against their enemy. Perhaps this is what is alluded to by the delay (التراخي) understood from His saying: «ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ», where He expressed it with thumma (then).

However, there remain two issues. First: it would have been necessary for the text to mention their repentance. In that case, His saying: «ثُمَّ يَتُوبُ اللهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ» — Then Allah will accept the repentance of whomever He wishes after that, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful — would be restricted to the disbelievers who embraced Islam afterward. Yet there is no trace of this in the speech, nor any context that restricts His saying: «ثُمَّ يَتُوبُ اللهُ» etc., to the disbelievers who embraced Islam later. Understand this.

Second: this would involve overlooking the beautiful effort and the fine trial (المحنة الحسنة) undergone by those few individuals who stood firm with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) when the masses of Muslims left him among the enemies and fled, retreating and not turning back for anything. It is unlikely for the Quranic method to neglect the matter of one who bore a trial for the sake of Allah and threw himself into the most difficult perils seeking His pleasure—while He is Appreciative and All-Knowing—and not praise him or thank his effort.

The established Quranic method is that if He encompasses a people with reproach, censure, or blame, and among them is one who is innocent of deserving blame or reproach, or pure from the stain of sin and error, He exempts him from them and singles him out with beautiful mention, praising him for his deed and excellence. We see this frequently in the addresses that encompass the Jews or Christians with reproach, blame, or censure; He addresses them as He does, censuring them and attributing to them disbelief in His signs and deviation from His commands and prohibitions, then He praises the few among them who believed in Him and His signs and obeyed Him in what He desired of them.

Clearer than that are the verses concerning the Battle of Uhud, where He favors the believers with the victory and honor Allah sent down upon them, and reproaches them for the weakness and failure they displayed, then He exempts the steadfast among them upon the feet of sincerity, and promises them a good promise, saying time after time: «وَسَيَجْزِي اللهُ الشَّاكِرِينَ» — ...and Allah will soon reward the grateful (Al-Imran: 144), and «وَسَنَجْزِي الشَّاكِرِينَ» — ...and We will soon reward the grateful (Al-Imran: 145).

We find the same in what Allah the Exalted mentions regarding the Battle of the Confederates (الأحزاب). In His speech, there is severe reproach for a group of the believers, and censure and blame for the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a disease, until He said among what He said: «وَلَقَدْ كانُوا عاهَدُوا اللهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ لا يُوَلُّونَ الْأَدْبارَ وَكانَ عَهْدُ اللهِ مَسْؤُلاً» — ...though they had certainly pledged to Allah before that they would not turn their backs [to the enemy], and pledges to Allah are accountable. (Al-Ahzab: 15). Then He concluded the story with such as His saying: «مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ رِجالٌ صَدَقُوا ما عاهَدُوا اللهَ عَلَيْهِ فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ قَضى نَحْبَهُ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَنْتَظِرُ وَما بَدَّلُوا تَبْدِيلاً» — Among the faithful are men who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah: of them are some who have fulfilled their pledge, and of them are some who still wait, and they have not changed in the least. (Al-Ahzab: 23).

Why then did He not address their state in the story of Hunayn, which was no less significant than others? Why did He not single them out with any gratitude, nor praise them with what they might favor themselves with of His subtle praise, as He did for others elsewhere?

Thus, what we have mentioned makes it more plausible that the intended "believers" mentioned in the descent of tranquility (السكينة) are those who stood firm with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). As for the rest of the believers who returned after the displacement, they fall under the scope of His saying: «ثُمَّ يَتُوبُ اللهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ», which includes those among them encompassed by divine care, just as it includes those encompassed by care and success from the disbelievers of Hawazin and Thaqif, the freedmen (الطلقاء), and those in whose hearts is a disease. This is what exegetical inquiry leads to; as for the narrations, they have their own status, and some of them will follow.

As for what he mentioned regarding the evidence of their immediate return when they heard the call of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the call of al-Abbas, that does not invalidate what we presented regarding the apparent meaning of His saying: «ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ» when joined with His saying: «إِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا زَحْفاً فَلا تُوَلُّوهُمُ الْأَدْبارَ», in that what they displayed in the encounter was flight from the battlefield, whether they did it out of cowardice, intentional desertion, or out of anxiety, agitation, and unsettling.

As for the verses he mentioned that praise them and mention the Lord's pleasure with them and their deserving of a great reward from their Lord—the response is that these praises are restricted by conditions such that the matter is not absolute for them. The verses only praise those among them whom they praise for their attributes of servitude, such as faith, sincerity, truthfulness, counsel, and religious struggle. Thus, the praise remains as long as the attributes remain, and the good promise is valid as long as the attributes and states necessitating it persist in them. If they vanish due to an event or a sin, the promise vanishes accordingly.

The principles of goodness and blessings they possess are not greater or more important than the attribute of infallibility (العصمة) possessed by the Prophets, with which the commission of sin is impossible. Yet Allah the Exalted said after a long praise of them: «وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ» — ...and had they ascribed partners [to Him], what they used to do would have come to nothing. (Al-An'am: 88).

And He said, in response to what they thought—that they were protected from what they disliked of types of punishment out of honor for their Islam, just as the People of the Book thought similarly: «لَيْسَ بِأَمانِيِّكُمْ وَلا أَمانِيِّ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً يُجْزَ بِهِ» — It is not by your fancies, nor by the fancies of the People of the Book: whoever commits evil shall be requited for it... (An-Nisa: 123).

What was mentioned regarding the Pledge of Ridwan (بيعة الرضوان) in His saying: «لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ» — Allah was certainly pleased... — His pleasure is among His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية), which are identical to His external acts, abstracted from them. Thus, it is identical to the pure psychological states He bestowed upon them, which naturally result in great reward and the best recompense if their deeds remain as they are. If they change, the pleasure changes to wrath and the blessing to vengeance. No one has taken a pledge from Him that He will not break His promise, such that He would lead them to felicity and honor whether they did well or ill, obeyed or disobeyed, believed or disbelieved.

The pleasure of the Lord is not among His essential attributes (صفاته الذاتية) with which He is described in His essence, such that it would not be subject to change or alteration, nor would it be overtaken by cessation or disappearance.

His saying, the Exalted: «ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — Then Allah sent down His composure (السكينة) upon His Apostle and upon the faithful — until the end of the verse. Tranquility (السكينة)—as previously mentioned—is a heart-state that necessitates the soul's stillness and the heart's steadfastness, inseparable from the increase of faith upon faith and the word of Godwariness (كلمة التقوى) which leads to piety (الورع) regarding Allah's prohibitions, as the verses interpret it.

It is different from justice (العدالة), which is a psychological faculty (ملكة نفسانية) that restrains one from committing major sins and persisting in minor ones; for tranquility (السكينة) restrains from both minor and major sins alike.

Allah has attributed tranquility (السكينة) in His Book to Himself in a way that suggests a type of exclusivity, just as He attributed the Spirit (الروح) to Himself, unlike justice. He described it as being "sent down" (الإنزال), so it has a special connection to Him in my view. Indeed, some verses suggest that He counted it among His hosts, as in His saying: «هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ» — It is He who sent down composure (السكينة) into the hearts of the faithful that they might enhance their faith with [further] faith. To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth. (Al-Fath: 4).

In more than one verse containing the mention of tranquility (السكينة), the hosts (الجنود) are mentioned, as in His saying: «فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ وَأَيَّدَهُ بِجُنُودٍ لَمْ تَرَوْها» — ...Then Allah sent down His composure (السكينة) upon him and strengthened him with hosts you did not see... (At-Tawbah: 40), and as in the verse under discussion: «ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ جُنُوداً لَمْ تَرَوْها».

What is understood from the context is that these hosts are the angels descending to the battlefield, or it may be said that the descending angels are among them. What is attributed to tranquility (السكينة) and the angels is that the disbelievers are punished by them, and the believers are guided and made felicitous by them, as contained in the verses of Al-Imran recounting the story of Uhud, and verses at the beginning of Surah al-Fath. Refer to them so that the reality of the matter becomes clear to you, if Allah wills.

Some discussion regarding divine tranquility (السكينة) has already been presented under His saying: «فِيهِ سَكِينَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ» — ...in which there is composure (السكينة) from your Lord... (Al-Baqarah: 248) in the second volume of this book, which is not without benefit in this context.

His saying, the Exalted: «ثُمَّ يَتُوبُ اللهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ» — Then Allah will accept the repentance of whomever He wishes after that, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. It has been mentioned repeatedly that repentance (التوبة) from Allah the Glorified is His returning to His servant with care and success first, then with pardon and forgiveness second. From the servant, it is the return to his Lord with regret and seeking forgiveness. Allah does not accept the repentance of one who does not repent to Him.

The reference in His saying: «مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ» — after that — according to what the context provides, is to what He mentioned in the two previous verses of their error in leaning on other than Allah the Glorified and their disobedience in fleeing and retreating, then the descent of tranquility (السكينة), the sending of the hosts, and the punishment of those who disbelieved.

It is appropriate that the relative pronoun in «مَنْ يَشاءُ» — whomever He wishes — includes both the Muslims and the disbelievers. He mentioned from both groups what makes it appropriate for Allah to accept their repentance if they repent: from the disbelievers, their disbelief, and from the Muslims, their error and disobedience. There is no reason to restrict repentance to some of them, given the generality and breadth of the ruling in the verses of repentance. In this verse under discussion, it is not restricted by anything that necessitates its exclusivity to one of the two groups—Muslims or disbelievers—while the occasion for it exists in both.

From what we have mentioned, the invalidity of how some have interpreted the verse becomes clear, where they restricted the reference to the punishment, saying: "Its meaning is that Allah will accept the repentance, after this punishment in this world, of whomever He wishes among the disbelievers, guiding them to Islam—those who were not encompassed by the errors of the ignorance of polytheism and its superstitions from all sides of their souls, and whose souls were not sealed by persistence in denial and rejection or rigidity upon what they were accustomed to by mere imitation." [End of his quote].

You have seen that restricting the verse to what was mentioned and manipulating its other constraints—such as restricting the reference to the punishment and so on—is something for which there is absolutely no evidence.

The reason for using the future tense (الاستقبال) in His saying: «ثُمَّ يَتُوبُ اللهُ» — Then Allah will accept the repentance — is to indicate that the door of repentance is always open, and that divine care and the flow of pardon and forgiveness are continuous, unlike what is indicated by His saying: «ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ», for those are limited, non-continuous matters.

His saying, the Exalted: «يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلا يَقْرَبُوا الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ بَعْدَ عامِهِمْ هذا» — O you who have faith! Indeed the polytheists are impure (نجس). So let them not approach the Sacred Mosque after this year of theirs. It is said in al-Majma': "Everything loathsome is najas (نجس). One says: a najas man, a najas woman, and najas people, because it is a verbal noun (مصدر). When this word is used with rijs (filth), it is said: rijsun najisun—with a kasrah on the nun." He said: "al-'aylah (العيلة) is poverty; it is said 'āla ya'īlu when one becomes poor." [End of his quote].

The prohibition of the polytheists entering the Sacred Mosque, according to conventional understanding, implies commanding the believers to prevent them from entering the Sacred Mosque. In His justifying the prevention of their entry by their being najas (impure), there is an attribution of a type of filthiness to them, just as there is an attribution of a type of purity and sanctity to the Sacred Mosque. Whatever it may be, it is something beyond the ruling of avoiding contact with them in the presence of moisture and so on.

The meaning of His saying: «عامِهِمْ هذا» — this year of theirs — is the ninth year of the Hijrah, which is the year in which Ali (عليه‌السلام) proclaimed the Declaration of Immunity (براءة), and prohibited circumambulating the House naked, and the pilgrimage of polytheists to the House.

His saying: «وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ عَيْلَةً» — And if you fear poverty... — the verse means: if you fear, in implementing this ruling, that they will cease coming for pilgrimage, your markets will stagnate, and your trade will vanish, leading you to become poor and needy, then do not fear, for Allah will soon enrich you from His grace and secure you from the poverty you fear.

This is a good promise from Him, the Exalted, in which there is a comforting of the souls of the people of Mecca and those who had trade there during the season. The present state of the Islamic world was then heralding the content of this promise; for Islam's word was rising and its fame was spreading time after time. The masses of polytheists were on the verge of eradication after the proclamation of Immunity (براءة)—only four months remained for them, except for a small group of Arabs with whom the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had made a treaty at the Sacred Mosque for a term beyond which there was no delay. Thus, all were in a position to accept Islam.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from some of his companions who mentioned it, he said: "When al-Mutawakkil was poisoned, he vowed that if he were cured, he would give a 'large amount' (مال كثير) of wealth in charity. When he was cured, he asked the jurists about the limit of a 'large amount' of wealth. They differed with him; some said: a hundred thousand, and some said: ten thousand. They gave various opinions, and the matter became confused for him.

One of his courtiers, called Safwan, said: 'Why do you not send for this black man and ask him about it?'

Al-Mutawakkil said to him: 'Woe to you, whom do you mean?' He said: 'The son of al-Rida.' He said to him: 'Does he know anything about this?' He said: 'If he gets you out of this, then I am entitled to such-and-such from you; otherwise, strike me with a hundred lashes.' Al-Mutawakkil said: 'I agree.' He said: 'O Ja'far ibn Mahmud, go to Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Muhammad and ask him about the limit of a large amount of wealth.' He asked him, and he said: 'The large amount is eighty.'

Ja'far ibn Mahmud said to him: 'O my master, he will ask me for the reason behind it.' Abu al-Hasan (عليه‌السلام) said to him: 'Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: «لَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللهُ فِي مَواطِنَ كَثِيرَةٍ» — Allah has certainly helped you in many (كثيرة) situations. We counted those situations, and they were eighty.'"

I say: Al-Qummi also narrated it in his Tafsir, and some of his companions mentioned in the narration that he named him Muhammad ibn Amr, according to what he mentioned in the Tafsir. The meaning of the narration is that eighty is among the instances (مصاديق) of "many" by the evidence of the Book, not that "many" means eighty, which is apparent.

In al-Majma', the commentators and biographers mentioned: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) conquered Mecca, he departed from it heading toward Hunayn to fight Hawazin and Thaqif, at the end of the month of Ramadan or in Shawwal in the eighth year of the Hijrah. The leaders of Hawazin had gathered under Malik ibn Awf al-Nasri, and they brought with them their wealth, women, and children, and settled at Awtas.

He said: Durayd ibn al-Simmah was among the people; he was the leader of Jusham and was a very old man who had lost his sight from old age. He said: 'In which valley are you?' They said: 'At Awtas.' He said: 'Yes, a field for horses; neither rugged and stony, nor soft and sandy. Why do I hear the braying of camels, the braying of donkeys, the lowing of cows, the bleating of sheep, and the crying of children?' They said: 'Malik ibn Awf has brought the people's children, wealth, and women so that each of them may fight for his family and wealth.' Durayd said: 'A shepherd of sheep, by the Lord of the Kaaba!'

Then he said: 'Bring Malik to me.' When he came to him, he said: 'O Malik, you have become the leader of your people, and this is a day that will be followed by others. Return your people to the high parts of their lands, and place the men on the backs of horses. For nothing will benefit you except a man with his sword and horse. If the victory is yours, those behind you will join you; and if it is against you, you will not have been disgraced in your family and children.' Malik said to him: 'You have grown old and your knowledge and intellect have departed.'

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) tied his greatest banner and gave it to Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه‌السلام). Everyone who had entered Mecca with a banner, he ordered him to carry it. He departed after staying in Mecca for fifteen days. He sent for Safwan ibn Umayyah and borrowed a hundred coats of mail from him. Safwan said: 'A loan or a seizure?' He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'A guaranteed loan to be returned.' So Safwan lent him a hundred coats of mail and went out with him. Two thousand men from the 'freedmen of the conquest' (مسلمة الفتح) went out, and he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had entered Mecca with ten thousand men and departed from it with twelve thousand.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent a man from his companions, who reached Malik ibn Awf while he was saying to his people: 'Let every man among you place his family and wealth behind his back, break the scabbards of your swords, and lie in ambush in the ravines of this valley. At dawn, when it is the grayness of morning, charge as one man and crush the people. For Muhammad has not met anyone who knows how to fight.'

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed the morning prayer with his companions, he descended into the valley of Hunayn. The battalions of Hawazin came out against them from every side. The Banu Sulaym, who were at the vanguard, were defeated, and those behind them were defeated. Allah left them to their enemy because of their pride in their numbers. Ali (عليه‌السلام) remained with the banner, fighting them with a few individuals. The defeated passed by the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), not turning back for anything.

Al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib was holding the bridle of the mule of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), with al-Fadl on his right, Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib on his left, Nawfal ibn al-Harith, and Rabi'ah ibn al-Harith among nine of the Banu Hashim, the tenth being Ayman ibn Umm Ayman. Regarding this, al-Abbas says:

We helped the Messenger of Allah in the war, nine of us, While those who fled from him had fled and dispersed. And my saying when al-Fadl charged with his sword Against the people again: 'O my sons, let them return!' And our tenth met death with his own soul, For what he attained for Allah, he does not grieve.

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw the people's defeat away from him, he said to al-Abbas—who was loud-voiced—'Ascend this hill and call out: O company of the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار)! O companions of Surah al-Baqarah! O people of the Pledge of the Tree (بيعة الشجرة)! Where are you fleeing? This is the Messenger of Allah!'

When the Muslims heard the voice of al-Abbas, they returned and said: 'At your service, at your service!' The Helpers (الأنصار) in particular rushed and fought the polytheists until the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Now the oven has heated up (حمى الوطيس). I am the Prophet, no lie; I am the son of Abd al-Muttalib.' Victory descended from Allah, and Hawazin were defeated a shameful defeat; they fled in every direction, and the Muslims remained in their pursuit.

Malik ibn Awf fled and entered the fortress of Ta'if. About a hundred of them were killed. Allah gave the Muslims their wealth, women, and children as booty. The Messenger of Allah ordered the children and wealth to be taken down to al-Ji'ranah, and he appointed Budayl ibn Warqa' al-Khuza'i over the spoils.

He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) proceeded in pursuit of the people and reached Ta'if seeking Malik ibn Awf. He besieged the people of Ta'if for the rest of the month. When Dhu al-Qa'dah began, he departed and came to al-Ji'ranah, where he divided the spoils of Hunayn and Awtas.

Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib said: 'A man who was among the polytheists on the day of Hunayn told me: When we and the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) met, they did not stand before us for the milking of a sheep. When we displaced them, we began to drive them until we reached the owner of the white mule—meaning the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Then men with white faces met us and said to us: "May the faces be shamed! Return!" So we returned, and they rode our shoulders—meaning the angels.'"

Al-Zuhri said: "It reached me that Shaybah ibn Uthman said: 'I came behind the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) intending to kill him for Talhah ibn Uthman and Uthman ibn Talhah—who had been killed on the day of Uhud. Allah informed His Messenger of what was in my soul. He turned to me, struck my chest, and said: "I seek refuge for you with Allah, O Shaybah!" My limbs trembled, and I looked at him, and he was more beloved to me than my hearing and sight. I said: "I bear witness that you are the Messenger of Allah, and that Allah informed you of what was in my soul."'"

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) divided the spoils at al-Ji'ranah. He had with him six thousand children and women from the captives of Hawazin, and of camels and sheep an unknown number.

Abu Sa'id al-Khudri said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) divided among the 'reconciled' (المتألفين) of Quraysh and other Arabs what he divided, and there was nothing for the Helpers (الأنصار) from it, neither little nor much. Sa'd ibn Ubadah walked to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, this tribe of the Helpers (الأنصار) have found grievance against you in your division of these spoils among your people and the rest of the Arabs, while there was nothing for them in that.' He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'And where do you stand in that, O Sa'd?' He said: 'I am but a man from my people.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Then gather your people for me in this enclosure.' He gathered them, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came out and stood among them as a speaker. He praised Allah and extolled Him, then said:

'O company of the Helpers (الأنصار)! Did I not come to you while you were astray and Allah guided you, and poor and Allah enriched you, and enemies and Allah reconciled your hearts?' They said: 'Yes, O Messenger of Allah.'

Then he said: 'Will you not answer me, O company of the Helpers (الأنصار)?' They said: 'What shall we say? And with what shall we answer you? Favor belongs to Allah and His Messenger.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'By Allah, if you wished, you could have said and been truthful: "You came to us as an outcast and we sheltered you, and poor and we consoled you, and fearful and we secured you, and forsaken and we helped you."' They said: 'Favor belongs to Allah and His Messenger.'

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Did you find grievance in yourselves, O company of the Helpers (الأنصار), over a trifle of this world with which I reconciled a people so they might embrace Islam, while I entrusted you to what Allah has apportioned for you of Islam? Are you not pleased, O company of the Helpers (الأنصار), that the people go to their dwellings with sheep and camels, while you go with the Messenger of Allah to your dwellings? By Him in Whose hand is my soul, if the people took a path and the Helpers (الأنصار) took a path, I would take the path of the Helpers (الأنصار). Were it not for the Hijrah, I would have been a man from the Helpers (الأنصار). O Allah, have mercy on the Helpers (الأنصار), the children of the Helpers (الأنصار), and the children of the children of the Helpers (الأنصار).' The people wept until their beards were soaked, and they said: 'We are pleased with Allah and His Messenger as our portion.' Then they dispersed."

Anas ibn Malik said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ordered a caller who called out on the day of Awtas: 'Behold, pregnant women are not to be approached until they give birth, nor others until they have been cleared by one menstruation.'"

Then the delegations of Hawazin came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at al-Ji'ranah as Muslims. Their speaker stood and said: "O Messenger of Allah, in the enclosures of captives are your paternal aunts and your foster-mothers who used to care for you. If we had favored Ibn Abi Shimr or al-Nu'man ibn al-Mundhir, and then what befell us from you befell us from them, we would have hoped for their return and kindness, and you are the best of those cared for." Then he recited some verses.

He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Which of the two matters is more beloved to you: the captives or the wealth?" They said: "O Messenger of Allah, you have given us a choice between our honor (الحسب) and the wealth; honor is more beloved to us, and we do not speak of a sheep or a camel." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "As for what belongs to the Banu Hashim, it is yours. I will speak to the Muslims for you and intercede for you. So speak to them and manifest your Islam."

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed the noon prayer, they stood and spoke. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I have returned what belongs to the Banu Hashim and what is in my hand to them. Whoever among you wishes to give without being compelled, let him do so; and whoever dislikes giving, let him take the ransom and I shall be responsible for their ransom." So the people gave what was in their hands of them, except for a few people who asked for ransom.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent to Malik ibn Awf and said: "If you come to me as a Muslim, I will return your family and wealth to you, and you shall have a hundred camels from me." So he came out to him from Ta'if, and he returned his family and wealth to him and gave him a hundred camels, and appointed him over those of his people who had embraced Islam.

I say: Al-Qummi narrated the like of it in his Tafsir, but he did not narrate the verses attributed to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), nor what he attributed to a specific narrator like al-Musayyib, al-Zuhri, Anas, and Abu Sa'id. These meanings have been narrated through many paths from the Sunni traditions.

In the narration of Ali ibn Ibrahim al-Qummi, there is a slight addition as follows:

Ali ibn Ibrahim said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw the defeat, he galloped around on his mule with his sword drawn and said: 'O Abbas, ascend this hill and call out: O companions of Surah al-Baqarah! O companions of the Tree! Where are you fleeing? This is the Messenger of Allah!'

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) raised his hand and said: 'O Allah, to You belongs all praise and to You belongs all thanks, and to You is the complaint, and You are the One whose help is sought.' Gabriel descended to him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, you have supplicated with what Moses ibn Imran supplicated when Allah parted the sea for him and saved him from Pharaoh.'

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith: 'Hand me a handful of pebbles.' He handed it to him, and he threw it in the faces of the polytheists, then said: 'May the faces be shamed!' Then he raised his head to the sky and said: 'O Allah, if this group is destroyed, You will not be worshipped; and if You wish not to be worshipped, You will not be worshipped.'

When the Helpers (الأنصار) heard the call of al-Abbas, they turned back and broke the scabbards of their swords while calling out: 'At your service!' They passed by the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and were ashamed to return to him, so they joined the banner. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to al-Abbas: 'Who are these, O Abu al-Fadl?' He said: 'O Messenger of Allah, these are the Helpers (الأنصار).' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Now the oven has heated up.' Victory descended from the sky and Hawazin were defeated."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Muhammad ibn ‘Ubaydullah ibn ‘Umayr al-Laythi, who said: "There were with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) four thousand from the Ansar (الأنصار) — the 'Helpers' from the inhabitants of Medina who supported the Prophet — and one thousand from Juhaynah, one thousand from Muzaynah, one thousand from Aslam, one thousand from Ghifar, one thousand from Ashja‘, and one thousand from the Muhajirun (المهاجرين) — the 'Emigrants' who migrated from Mecca to Medina — and others. Thus, there were ten thousand with him, and he set out with twelve thousand. Regarding this, Allah the Exalted said in His Book: وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنْكُمْ شَيْئاً 'and on the day of Hunayn, when your great number impressed you, but it did not avail you anything' (9:25)."

In the Sirah of Ibn Hisham, narrated from Ibn Ishaq, he said: "When the people were routed, and those among the uncouth people of Mecca who were with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw the defeat, some men among them spoke of the rancor (الضغن) they harbored in their souls.

Abu Sufyan ibn Harb said: 'Their flight will not end before they reach the sea.' He had the divining arrows (الأزلام) with him in his quiver. Jabalah ibn al-Hanbal — Ibn Hisham says it was Kaldah ibn al-Hanbal, who was with his brother Safwan ibn Umayyah as a polytheist (مشرك) during the period of respite granted to him by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) — shouted: 'Behold, the sorcery has been nullified today!' Safwan said to him: 'Be quiet! May Allah break your teeth! By Allah, that a man from Quraysh should be my master is more beloved to me than being mastered by a man from Hawazin.'"

Ibn Ishaq said: "Shaybah ibn ‘Uthman ibn Abi Talhah, the brother of Banu ‘Abd al-Dar, said: 'I said to myself: Today I shall take my revenge' — his father had been killed on the day of Uhud — 'Today I shall kill Muhammad.' He continued: 'So I circled around the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to kill him, but something approached until it overwhelmed my heart, and I could not endure it. Thus, I realized that he was protected (ممنوع) from me.'"

( Index of the Names of the Martyrs of Hunayn )

In the Sirah of Ibn Hisham, Ibn Ishaq said: "This is the naming of those among the Muslims who were martyred on the day of Hunayn:

From Quraysh, then from Banu Hashim: Ayman ibn ‘Ubayd. From Banu Asad ibn ‘Abd al-‘Uzza: Yazid ibn Zam‘ah ibn al-Aswad ibn al-Muttalib ibn Asad — he was bolted away by a horse called al-Janah and was killed.

From the Ansar: Suraqah ibn al-Harith ibn ‘Adi from Banu al-‘Ajlan. From the Ash‘arites: Abu ‘Amir al-Ash‘ari."

I say: As for those who remained steadfast (الثباة) with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), they have been counted in some narrations as three, in some as four, and in others as nine, the tenth being Ayman ibn ‘Ubayd — who was the son of Umm Ayman. In some reports, they are numbered at eighty, and in others, just under a hundred.

The most reliable among these is what was narrated from al-‘Abbas: that they were nine, the tenth being Ayman. He has poetry regarding this which was previously cited. This is because he [al-‘Abbas] was among those who remained steadfast with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) throughout the entire battle and witnessed the unfolding of events. He was the one who would call out to those in flight and summon them back by the command of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and he took pride in the poetry he composed.

It is possible that a group remained steadfast for a short while after the people fled and then joined those in retreat, or that a group returned before others and joined the banner, thus being counted among those who stood firm and fought. For a fierce war (الحرب العوان) does not proceed according to the same order (النظم) as a state of peace.

From this, one can understand the status of the claim made by some: that the most probable narration is that of eighty, as reported from ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud, and to which the report of Ibn ‘Umar—that they were under a hundred—returns. They argue that "the evidence of the one who remembers (حفظ) takes precedence over the one who does not." This is the end of the summary.

The reason [this is problematic] is that while it is true that the evidence of the one who remembers takes precedence over the one who does not, "remembering" during the state of war — with its rapid shifts in immediate circumstances — is not like remembering in other situations. Therefore, one should only rely on that which the surrounding indicators (القرائن) testify to be correct and which rational consideration (الاعتبار) supports through the reliability of its preservation. Al-‘Abbas was specifically commanded with a task that naturally involved monitoring these matters and what pertained to them.

Ayahs

قَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَلَا بِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ ٱلْحَقِّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ حَتَّىٰ يُعْطُوا۟ ٱلْجِزْيَةَ عَن يَدٍ وَهُمْ صَـٰغِرُونَ (٢٩)
Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day, nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, nor practise the true religion, from among those who were given the Book, until they pay the tribute out of hand, degraded. (29)
وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ٱبْنُ ٱللَّهِ وَقَالَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَى ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ ٱللَّهِ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ قَوْلُهُم بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ ۖ يُضَـٰهِـُٔونَ قَوْلَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ قَـٰتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٣٠)
The Jews say, ‘Ezra is the son of Allah,’ and the Christians say, ‘Christ is the son of Allah.’ That is an opinion that they mouth, imitating the opinions of the faithless of former times. May Allah assail them, where do they stray?! (30)
ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَحْبَارَهُمْ وَرُهْبَـٰنَهُمْ أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَسِيحَ ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَمَآ أُمِرُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا ۖ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٣١)
They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah, and also Christ, Mary’s son; though they were commanded to worship only the One God, there is no god except Him; He is far too immaculate to have any partners that they ascribe [to Him]! (31)
يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُطْفِـُٔوا۟ نُورَ ٱللَّهِ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَيَأْبَى ٱللَّهُ إِلَّآ أَن يُتِمَّ نُورَهُۥ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٢)
They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah is intent on perfecting His light though the faithless should be averse. (32)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ (٣٣)
It is He who has sent His Apostle with the guidance and the religion of truth that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse. (33)
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْأَحْبَارِ وَٱلرُّهْبَانِ لَيَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ ٱلذَّهَبَ وَٱلْفِضَّةَ وَلَا يُنفِقُونَهَا فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٣٤)
O you who have faith! Indeed many of the scribes and monks wrongfully eat up the people’s wealth, and bar [them] from the way of Allah. Those who treasure up gold and silver, and do not spend it in the way of Allah, inform them of a painful punishment (34)
يَوْمَ يُحْمَىٰ عَلَيْهَا فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ فَتُكْوَىٰ بِهَا جِبَاهُهُمْ وَجُنُوبُهُمْ وَظُهُورُهُمْ ۖ هَـٰذَا مَا كَنَزْتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ فَذُوقُوا۟ مَا كُنتُمْ تَكْنِزُونَ (٣٥)
on the day when these shall be heated in hellfire and therewith branded on their foreheads, their sides, and their backs [and told]: ‘This is what you treasured up for yourselves! So taste what you have treasured!’ (35)

(Commentary)

The verses command the fighting of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — those among them who may be spared through the payment of the tribute (الجزية) — and mention aspects of their deviation from the truth in both belief and action.

His saying, the Exalted: «Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day, nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, nor practise the true religion, from among those who were given the Book, until they pay the tribute out of hand, after being humbled.» (9:29). The People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) are the Jews and the Christians, as inferred from many verses of the Holy Quran, as well as the Magians (المجوس), as suggested or indicated by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالصَّابِئِينَ وَالنَّصارى وَالْمَجُوسَ وَالَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا إِنَّ اللهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ — "Indeed the faithful and the Jews, the Sabaeans, the Christians, the Magians and the polytheists—indeed Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed Allah is witness to all things." (Al-Hajj: 17). In this verse, they are enumerated alongside the other adherents of heavenly religions in contrast to the polytheists (الذين أشركوا). As for the Sabaeans (الصابئون), as previously mentioned, they are a group of Magians who leaned toward the religion of the Jews, thus adopting a path between the two.

The context indicates that the word "from" (مِنَ) in His saying: "from among those who were given the Book" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ) is explanatory (بيانية) rather than partitive (تبعيضية). This is because the Jews, the Christians, and the Magians each constitute a single community (أمة واحدة), much like the Muslims in their Islam, even if they have branched into different sects and dispersed into scattered factions that have intermingled. Had the intent been to fight only a portion of them while imposing the tribute (الجزية) on all, or on that specific portion alone, the context would have required a different explanation to achieve that purpose.

Since His saying: "from among those who were given the Book" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ) is an explanation of what preceded it—namely, His saying: "those who do not have faith" (الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ)—the mentioned attributes are general descriptions for all of them. These are three attributes with which Allah, the Glorified, has described them: the lack of faith in Allah and the Last Day, the failure to forbid what Allah and His Messenger have forbidden, and the failure to practice the true religion (دين الحق).

The first attribute He describes them with is His saying: "those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day" (الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَلا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ). Yet, the Exalted attributes to them in His speech that they affirm Him as God; and how could it be otherwise? He considers them "People of the Book," and that is nothing other than the Heavenly Book revealed from Allah to one of His messengers. He also recounts from them statements, or the necessary implications of statements, regarding His divinity in hundreds of verses of His Book.

Similarly, He attributes to them the belief in the Last Day (اليوم الآخر) in verses such as His saying: وَقالُوا لَنْ تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّاماً مَعْدُودَةً — "And they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days.’" (Al-Baqarah: 80), and His saying: وَقالُوا لَنْ يَدْخُلَ الْجَنَّةَ إِلَّا مَنْ كانَ هُوداً أَوْ نَصارى — "And they say, ‘No one will enter paradise except one who is a Jew or a Christian.’" (Al-Baqarah: 111).

However, the Exalted does not differentiate in His speech between faith in Him and faith in the Last Day; thus, disbelief in one of the two is disbelief in Allah, and disbelief in Allah is disbelief in both together. He has judged those who differentiate between Allah and His messengers—believing in some and disbelieving in others—as disbelievers, as He said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلاً أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ حَقًّا وَأَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذاباً مُهِيناً — "Those who deny Allah and His apostles and seek to separate Allah from His apostles, and say, ‘We believe in some and deny others,’ and seek to take a way in between—it is they who are truly faithless, and We have prepared a humiliating punishment for the faithless." (Al-Nisa: 150-151).

Thus, He counted the People of the Book who did not believe in the prophethood of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as true disbelievers (كفاراً حقاً), even if they possessed a form of faith in Allah and the Last Day. This is not merely in the sense that they disbelieved in one of the signs (آيات) of Allah—namely, the sign of prophethood—but rather in the sense that they disbelieved in the [true] faith in Allah. Thus, they did not truly believe in Allah or the Last Day, just as the polytheists (المشركون), the worshippers of idols, are disbelievers in Allah because they did not uphold His monotheism (التوحيد), even if they affirmed a God above all other deities.

Furthermore, they formulate the matter of the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد) in a manner that does not accord with the truth in any way. Examples include their claim that the Messiah is the son of Allah and that Ezra (عزير) is the son of Allah, thereby imitating (يضاهئون) the saying of those who disbelieved among the worshippers of idols and statues—that among the gods there is a father-god and a son-god—as well as the Jews' claim regarding the Return being based on [ethnic] nobility and the Christians' claim regarding the Atonement (التفدية).

Thus, it appears that the negation of faith in Allah and the Last Day from the People of the Book is because they do not uphold the truth regarding monotheism (التوحيد) and the Return (المعاد), even if they affirm the basic premise of divinity. It is not because some of them deny the existence of Allah, the Glorified, or deny the Return, for they do affirm these as the Quran recounts from them—even if the Torah present today contains no mention of the Return at all.

Secondly, He describes them with His saying: "nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden" (وَلا يُحَرِّمُونَ ما حَرَّمَ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ). This refers to instances such as the Jews' deeming permissible things that the Quran enumerated and mentioned to them in Surah al-Baqarah, al-Nisa, and elsewhere; and the Christians' deeming permissible wine and swine-flesh, despite their prohibition being established in the laws of Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (عليه السلام); and their consuming the wealth of people unjustly, as He will attribute to them in the subsequent verse: إِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْأَحْبارِ وَالرُّهْبانِ لَيَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ النَّاسِ بِالْباطِلِ — "Indeed many of the scribes and monks consume the people’s wealth wrongfully." (9:34).

The "Messenger" intended in His saying: "what Allah and His Messenger have forbidden" (ما حَرَّمَ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ) is either their own messenger whose prophethood they claim to follow—such as Moses (عليه السلام) for the Jews and Jesus (عليه السلام) for the Christians. In this case, the meaning is that each community among them does not forbid what was forbidden to them by their own messenger whose prophethood they profess and whose truthfulness they acknowledge. This represents the ultimate audacity against Allah and His messenger and a mockery of truth and reality.

Alternatively, it refers to the Prophet Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), whom they find written in their own Torah and Gospel, who makes lawful for them the good things and forbids them the corrupt things, and relieves them of their burdens and the shackles that were upon them. In this case, describing them as not forbidding what Allah and His Messenger have forbidden would be for the purpose of rebuking and disparaging them, and to incite and rouse the believers to fight them due to their disregard for what Allah and His Messenger have forbidden in their law, and their unbridled indulgence in the prohibitions of Allah and the violation of His sanctities.

This latter possibility may be supported by the fact that if the intent of "His Messenger" (ورسوله) were the messenger of each specific community—Moses for the Jews and Jesus for the Christians—the appropriate phrasing would have been "what Allah and His messengers (رسله) have forbidden," as is the custom of the Quran in similar instances to indicate the plurality of messengers. For example: وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ — "and seek to separate Allah from His apostles" (Al-Nisa: 150); قالَتْ رُسُلُهُمْ أَفِي اللهِ شَكٌّ — "Their apostles said, ‘Is there any doubt about Allah...?’" (Ibrahim: 10); and وَجاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ — "and their apostles had come to them with manifest proofs" (Yunus: 13).

Furthermore, the Christians rejected the prohibitions of the Torah and the Gospel, failing to forbid what Moses and Jesus (عليه السلام) had forbidden. In a case such as this, it would not be entirely accurate to say simply that they do not forbid what Allah and His Messenger have forbidden [if it referred only to their own].

Moreover, one who contemplates the general objectives of Islam will have no doubt that fighting the People of the Book until they pay the tribute (الجزية) is not for the purpose of the leaders of Islam or the Muslims enjoying the goods of this worldly life, nor for their indulgence and immersion in desires like the profligate kings and leaders or the extravagant among powerful nations.

Rather, the purpose of the religion in this is that the true religion (دين الحق), the way of justice (سنة العدل), and the word of God-consciousness (كلمة التقوى) should prevail over falsehood, injustice, and transgression. Thus, the path of religion should not be obstructed by play and caprice, ensuring that sound and reformative upbringing (التربية الصالحة المصلحة) is safe from the interference of corrupt and corruptive upbringing. This prevents a situation where society is pulled in opposing directions, leading to the disorder of the human system. However, if an individual or a group does not accept Islamic upbringing for themselves, they are free in what they choose for themselves of their own specific religious upbringing, provided they adhere to a form of monotheistic religion (دين التوحيد)—namely Judaism, Christianity, or Magianism—and that they do not openly engage in interference. This is the pinnacle of justice and fairness from the true religion that prevails over others.

As for the tribute (الجزية), it is a financial contribution taken from them and spent on their protection (حفظ ذمتهم) and the sound administration of their affairs. No government standing on its own feet—whether rightful or otherwise—can dispense with the like of it.

From this explanation, it becomes clear that the "prohibitions" intended here are the Islamic prohibitions that Allah has decreed shall not spread within the global Islamic society, just as the "true religion" (دين الحق) is that which He has decreed shall be followed in society.

The necessary implication of this is that the prohibitions intended are those forbidden by Allah and His Messenger Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), the proclaimer of the Islamic call. The three descriptions—"those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day..."—act as a rationale (تعليل) providing the wisdom behind the command to fight the People of the Book.

By all this, the flaw in the objection raised against this view becomes apparent—the objection being that it is inconceivable for the People of the Book to forbid upon themselves what Allah and His Messenger have forbidden upon us unless they embrace Islam, and the discussion here is about the People of the Book, not about disobedient Muslims.

The reason this objection is flawed is that it is not necessary for the purpose of fighting them to be that they forbid what Islam forbids while they remain People of the Book. Rather, the purpose is that the open display of prohibitions should not appear among the people without a deterrent to prevent their spread and indulgence—such as the drinking of wine, the eating of swine-flesh, and the unjust consumption of wealth in a public manner. Instead, they are fought so that they enter into the protection (الذمة) of the Islamic state, whereby they do not openly display corruption, and the evil is confined among themselves.

Perhaps this is what is alluded to by His saying: "after being humbled" (وَهُمْ صاغِرُونَ), as will be discussed in the commentary on the end of the verse.

Thirdly, He describes them with His saying: "nor practise the true religion" (وَلا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ الْحَقِّ) — meaning they do not adopt it as their religion and vital way of life.

The annexation of "religion" (الدين) to "truth" (الحق) is not the annexation of a noun to its adjective—in the sense of "the religion that is true"—but rather a real annexation (إضافة حقيقية). What is meant is the religion that is attributed to the Truth (الحق) because the Truth is what necessitates it for the human being and calls him toward it, and because this religion guides to the Truth and brings its followers to it. It is similar to our saying "the path of truth" (طريق الحق) and "the path of misguidance" (طريق الضلال), meaning the path that belongs to truth and the path that belongs to misguidance—i.e., its goal is truth or its goal is misguidance.

This is because what is inferred from verses such as His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّنِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ — "So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, the nature (الفطرة) of Allah on which He fashioned mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion." (Al-Rum: 30), and His saying: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللهِ الْإِسْلامُ — "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (Al-Imran: 19), and other similar verses, is that this religion has an origin in the cosmos, creation, and true reality. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) calls to it and invites people to Islam and submission to it. Adopting it as a way of life is called "Islam" (submission) to Allah the Exalted. Thus, he calls to that which the human being has no choice but to respond and submit to—which is submission to the conventional practical way (السنة العملية الاعتبارية) to which the real cosmic way (السنة الكونية الحقيقية) guides. In other words, it is submission to the legislative will (الإرادة التشريعية) of Allah, which emanates from His creative will (الإرادة التكوينية).

In short, for the Truth (الحق)—which is the fixed reality—there is a religion and a way that emanates from it, just as for misguidance and error there is a religion that calls to it. The former is the following of Truth, while the latter is the following of caprice (الهوى). The Exalted said: وَلَوِ اتَّبَعَ الْحَقُّ أَهْواءَهُمْ لَفَسَدَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ — "Had the Truth followed their desires, the heavens and the earth would have surely fallen into ruin." (Al-Mu'minun: 71).

Islam is the "true religion" (دين الحق) in the sense that it is the way of creation (سنة التكوين) and the path with which creation accords and to which the primordial nature (الفطرة) calls—the nature of Allah on which He fashioned mankind; there is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion.

From what has preceded, it can be summarized: First, that the meaning of the People of the Book's lack of faith in Allah and the Last Day is their lack of adherence to the faith that is acceptable to Allah. Their failure to forbid what Allah and His Messenger have forbidden means their lack of concern regarding the public commission of prohibited acts, the open display of which corrupts human society and thwarts the efforts of the rightful government operating within it. Their failure to practice the true religion means their failure to adopt the way of Truth that accords with creation and with which creation and the cosmos accord.

Second: That His saying: "those who do not have faith in Allah..." until the end of the three descriptions is intended to explain the wisdom behind the command to fight them, and it carries the benefit of inciting and urging toward it.

Third: That the intent is to fight all of the People of the Book, not just some of them, by treating "from" (مِنَ) in His saying: "from among those who were given the Book" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ) as partitive.

His saying, the Exalted: «until they pay the tribute out of hand, after being humbled.» (9:29). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The tribute (الجزية) is what is taken from the protected people (أهل الذمة), and it is so named because it suffices (الاجتزاء) in sparing their blood." End quote.

In al-Majma': "The tribute (الجزية) is a noun of the form fi'lah from jazā yajzī (to compensate/repay), like al-'uqdah and al-jilsah. It is a specific payment as a compensation (جزاء) to them for their adherence to disbelief, as a punishment for them." Narrated from Ali ibn Isa. End quote.

Reliance is upon what Al-Raghib mentioned, as it is supported by what we stated earlier: that this is a financial contribution spent on the preservation of their protection (حفظ ذمتهم), the sparing of their blood, and the sound administration of their affairs.

Al-Raghib also said: "Smallness (الصغر) and greatness (الكبر) are opposite nouns used when considering things in relation to one another; a thing may be small beside one thing and great beside another..." until he said: "It is said saghura ṣighran—with kasrah then fathah—as the opposite of great, and ṣaghura ṣighran and ṣaghāran—with two fathahs in both—in the sense of humiliation (الذلة). The humbled (الصاغر) is one who is content with a lowly position: 'until they pay the tribute out of hand, after being humbled (وَهُمْ صاغِرُونَ).'" End quote.

Considering the descriptions mentioned at the beginning of the verse that necessitate fighting them, followed by their paying the tribute to preserve their protection, implies that their "humbling" (صغارهم) means their submission to the Islamic way and the just religious government within Islamic society. Thus, they do not rival the Muslims nor challenge them with an independent, free persona in spreading what their souls desire and disseminating the beliefs and actions they have fabricated out of caprice, which corrupt human society—alongside the lowliness inherent in paying the wealth with their own hands.

Thus, the apparent meaning of the verse is that this is what is intended by their being "humbled," not that they should be insulted or mocked by the Muslims or the officials of the religious government, for this is something that Islamic dignity and composure (السكينة والوقار) would not tolerate, even if some commentators have mentioned it.

The "hand" (اليد) refers to the human limb, and it is also used to mean power (القدرة) and favor (النعمة). If the first meaning is intended in His saying: "until they pay the tribute out of hand" (حَتَّى يُعْطُوا الْجِزْيَةَ عَنْ يَدٍ), then the meaning is: until they pay the tribute, passing it from their hand to your hand. If the second meaning is intended, then the meaning is: until they pay the tribute out of your power and authority over them, while they are humbled, not seeking superiority over you nor being arrogant.

So the meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: Fight the People of the Book because they do not believe in Allah and the Last Day with an acceptable faith that does not deviate from the right path, nor do they forbid what Islam has forbidden of those things whose commission corrupts human society, nor do they practice a religion that accords with the divine creation. Fight them and continue to fight them until they are humbled before you and submit to your government, and pay in that regard a financial contribution imposed upon them that represents their humbling, and which is spent on the preservation of their protection, the sparing of their blood, and the needs of administering their affairs.

His saying, the Exalted: «The Jews say, ‘Ezra is the son of Allah,’ and the Christians say, ‘The Messiah is the son of Allah.’» (9:30) until the end of the verse. "Imitation" (المضاهاة) means resemblance (المشاكلة). "Falsehood" (الإفك), according to Al-Raghib, is everything turned away from the face it rightfully should be upon; thus, the meaning of "they are perverted" (يُؤْفَكُونَ) is that they are turned away in their beliefs from truth to falsehood.
