I say: The reports regarding the details of the stories of the migration are extremely numerous, narrated by the masters of transmission and the authors of biographies from both the Shi‘a and the Sunnis. Despite their number, they are conflicting and agitated; this book does not have the scope to critique them and extract the pure from them. For the indication of the general story, what we have provided is sufficient, and it is as if it is agreed upon among the reports of both groups.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Khaythama ibn Sulayman al-Tarabulsi in Fada’il al-Sahaba and Ibn Asakir narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: "Allah censured all the people and praised Abu Bakr, saying: «If you do not help him, Allah has already helped him—when the faithless expelled him, as one of two, when the two were in the cave—when he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us’»."

I say: A critical examination of the contents of the verses surrounding the story and what is joined to them of sound transmission necessitates a poor opinion of this narration. For the verses that censure the believers—or all the people as in the narration—and to which the verse of the Cave refers, including His saying: «If you do not help him,» are His saying, the Exalted: «O you who have faith! What is the matter with you that when you are told: ‘Go forth in the way of Allah,’ you sink heavily to the ground?» (9:38). Definitive transmission indicates that the mentioned sluggishness was not from the generality of the believers and all of them, and that many of them hastened to respond to the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in what he commanded of going forth. Rather, a group of people, both believers and hypocrites, were sluggish.

Thus, the address «O you who have faith!» which includes all the believers, and the censure following it, is only from the address to a group regarding some of them, like the address to the Jews in His saying: «Why then did you kill the prophets of Allah?» (2:91) and others. This is frequent in the Quran, except that the habit of the Quran in such instances is not to waste the right of the righteous nor the reward of the virtuous—meaning the few who are included in such general addresses of censure and reproach. So their matter is rectified, and they are excepted and mentioned with beauty, as He did in what will follow in this Surah of verses praising the believers and thanking their beautiful efforts, in His saying: «But the faithful, men and women, are comrades of one another» (9:71), and others.

If the verses—having been revealed regarding the Expedition of Tabuk—include all the believers, those hastening to go out and those sluggish in it without exception, then they include the generality of the companions and the believers, and among them was Abu Bakr himself, except that He, the Exalted, rectified what had attached to those hastening in obedience and response among them in subsequent verses and thanked their effort.

So if His saying in the verse: «If you do not help him» refers to what preceded of the account—

The reluctance to mobilize (التثاقل) and the allusion to it as a reproach to all the people would include a reproach of Abu Bakr just as it is a reproach of others for failing to aid the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or for their sluggishness in supporting him. Furthermore, the verse does not allow for an indication that Abu Bakr aided him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), given His saying: فَقَدْ نَصَرَهُ اللهُ إِذْ أَخْرَجَهُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا ثانِيَ اثْنَيْنِ إِذْ هُما فِي الْغارِ إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصاحِبِهِ لا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَنا — "Allah certainly helped him when the faithless expelled him, as one of two [refugees], when the two were in the cave, and he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us.’" (9:40). Rather, if it indicates anything, it indicates the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) aid to Abu Bakr, as he comforted his heart and consoled him by saying: "Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us."

Moreover, you have already recognized in the previous commentary (البيان) that the verse, by virtue of its context, only addresses the aid of Allah, the Glorified, alone to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) specifically in his own person, in contrast to the assumed lack of aid from the entirety of the believers and their desertion of him. Thus, the indication of the verse that on the day of the cave, none aided the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) except Allah, the Glorified, alone is a definitive indication (دلالة قطعية).

This meaning is itself the clearest witness that the pronouns in the remainder of the verse’s sentences: فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ وَأَيَّدَهُ بِجُنُودٍ لَمْ تَرَوْها وَجَعَلَ كَلِمَةَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا السُّفْلى وَكَلِمَةُ اللهِ هِيَ الْعُلْيا — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon him, and strengthened him with hosts you did not see, and He made the word of the faithless the lowest; and the word of Allah is the highest" (9:40), refer to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). These sentences are structured to explain His, the Exalted, sole undertaking of aiding him with a mighty, unseen aid in which no human had any part—namely, the sending down of composure (السكينة) upon him, strengthening him with hosts hidden from sight, making the word of the disbelievers the lowest, and exalting the word of Truth; and Allah is All-Mighty, All-Wise.

As for merits other than the aiding of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for which a person might be praised, if there were anything of that sort here, it would be in His saying: "one of two (ثانِيَ اثْنَيْنِ)" and in His saying: "to his companion (لِصاحِبِهِ)." Let us concede that a person being the second of two, one of whom is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and his being a companion to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) mentioned in the Quran with companionship (الصحبة), are among the honors that people aspire to. However, these are social merits (مناقب اجتماعية) whose value and preciousness are estimated within societies. As for the Noble Quran, it has another criterion for value, and in its logic, excellence and nobility have another meaning resting upon a reality higher than conventional social objectives—namely, the dignity of servitude (كرامة العبودية) and the degrees of proximity (القرب) and nearness (الزلفى).

Mere physical companionship (الصحابة الجسمانية) and inclusion in a count do not indicate any of that. It has been repeated in His, the Exalted, speech that being called by various names or being clothed in what the general public aspires to and what social perception deems great has no value with Allah, the Glorified. Rather, the reckoning is based on what is in the hearts, not what appears from outward deeds or the precedence of prestige (الأحساب) and lineage (الأنساب).

He has expressed this most eloquently regarding the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his close associates specifically, in His, the Exalted, saying: مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ تَراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً — "Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating..." until He says: وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً — "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward" (48:29). Look at the praise in the beginning of the verse and the qualification (القيد) at its end, and reflect.

This is a brief portion of what pertains to the verse and the narrations in this discussion; anything beyond this amount would take us out of exegetical discussion (البحث التفسيري) into theological discussion (البحث الكلامي), which is outside our purpose.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il, and Ibn Asakir in his Tarikh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon him" (9:40), that he said: "Upon Abu Bakr, because the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) always had composure with him."

In the same source, al-Khatib narrated in his Tarikh from Habib ibn Abi Thabit regarding: فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon him," that he said: "Upon Abu Bakr; as for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), composure was already upon him."

I say: It has been established previously that the pronoun refers back to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) according to what the context (السياق) guides to. The two narrations, despite their suspended chains (الوقف), are weak, and the word of Ibn Abbas or Habib is not a binding proof (حجية) for others.

As for the argument they presented in them—that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) always had composure with him—it is flawed (مدخولة) and refuted by His, the Exalted, saying in the story of Hunayn: ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the faithful" (9:26). Similar to it is the verse in Surah al-Fath referring to the story of al-Hudaybiyyah; both explicitly state the descent of composure upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in those specific instances. Thus, the matter should follow the same pattern in the cave.

Some of them (1) sensed the problem and interpreted their statement in the two narrations—that composure never left the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—to mean something else: that composure was inseparable from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the cave, which would then serve as a contextual clue that the composure which descended there only descended upon his companion and not him. Perhaps Habib’s narration is closer in its indication to what he mentioned.

He said, after citing the narration of Ibn Abbas and then the narration of Habib: "Some commentators of language and reason have adopted this narration and clarified the reasoning within it—that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not experience any agitation (اضطراب), fear, or grief at that time. Some strengthened it by the principle that the default for a pronoun is to return to the nearest mentioned noun (أقرب مذكور). But this is nothing."

Others held that the pronoun returns to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and that the descent of composure upon him does not necessitate that he was fearful, agitated, or distressed. This [view] is weak because the descent of composure is coordinated (عطف) with what preceded it, indicating its occurrence after it and its being consequential to it, and that its descent occurred after his saying to his companion: "Do not grieve." [End of quote].

As for what they mentioned regarding the absence of fear or agitation occurring to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at that time: if they derived this from the lack of mention of any such thing in the verse or in a reliable narration, then His, the Exalted, speech in the stories of Hunayn and al-Hudaybiyyah is also devoid of mentioning the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with fear, grief, or agitation, and no reliable narration has been transmitted indicating that. So how was the mention of the descent of composure upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sound in those two instances?

If they say that the descent of composure necessitates [prior] agitation, fear, and grief, this is rejected, as previously mentioned. How so? The descent of one of the divine blessings (النعم الإلهية) does not depend on being previously characterized by an opposite state or a corresponding affliction, just like the descent of mercy after mercy, blessing after blessing, and faith and guidance after faith and guidance, and so on. The Noble Quran has explicitly stated many matters of this kind.

As for his saying: "That the return of the pronoun to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is weak because the coordination of the descent of composure with what preceded it indicates its occurrence after it and its being consequential to it, and that its descent occurred after his saying to his companion: 'Do not grieve,'"

The response is: There is no doubt that the "fa" of derivation (فاء التفريع) indicates the consequence of what follows it upon what precedes it and its occurrence after it, but this is a sequentiality of rank (بعدية رتبية), not a sequentiality of time (بعدية زمانية), and no one has said it must always be temporal.

It is necessary in our case that His saying: فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ وَأَيَّدَهُ — "Then Allah sent down His composure upon him, and strengthened him" (9:40) be consequential to what preceded it of the discourse, not to what is nearest to it over others, except according to the view that the default for a pronoun is to return to the nearest mentioned noun—a view he himself weakened in his earlier remarks.

What is suitable from what preceded to be the basis for the aforementioned derivation is His saying: "Allah certainly helped him (فقد نصره الله)" in such-and-such a time. The derivation of these branches from it is like the derivation of the detailed (التفصيل) from the general (الإجمال). The context remains consistent: "Allah certainly helped him at such a time, so He sent down His composure upon him, strengthened him with hosts you did not see, and made the word of the faithless the lowest."

Thus, it becomes clear that what he finally answered with is the very thing he initially weakened regarding the principle of the proximity of the referent to the pronoun—that principle which has no basis—he repeated it a second time by merely changing the wording.

From here, the grounds for criticizing another narration become clear, which was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Anas ibn Malik: "He said: The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and Abu Bakr entered the cave of Hira—so Abu Bakr said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'If one of them were to look at the place of his feet, he would see me and you.' He said: 'What is your thought of two, of whom Allah is the third? Indeed, Allah has sent down His composure upon you and strengthened me with hosts you did not see.'"

Furthermore, the narration mentions the cave of Hira (غار حراء), while it has been established by widely transmitted and abundant reports (المستفيض المتكاثر) that the cave was the cave of Thawr (غار ثور), not the cave of Hira.

Moreover, the narration explicitly involves a fragmentation of the context (تفكيك السياق) by its saying: "sent down His composure upon you and strengthened me with hosts," etc.

Al-Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani cited the narration as follows: "Indeed, Allah sent down His composure upon you and strengthened you with hosts you did not see," thus returning both pronouns to Abu Bakr instead of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

We do not know which of the two wordings is the original and which is the distorted one, except that to the narration "and strengthened you with hosts you did not see," in addition to the aforementioned problems, other problems are added that were mentioned in the previous commentary, along with a new problem regarding His saying: "you did not see (لم تروها)" using the plural address (خطاب الجمع), while there was no plural audience at that time.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His, the Exalted, saying: لَوْ كانَ عَرَضاً قَرِيباً وَسَفَراً قاصِداً — "Had it been a near gain and a short journey" (9:42): In a narration by Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: لَوْ كانَ عَرَضاً قَرِيباً وَسَفَراً قاصِداً, he says: "A near spoil (غنيمة قريبة), لَاتَّبَعُوكَ — 'they would have surely followed you.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them) regarding the saying of Allah: لَوْ كانَ عَرَضاً قَرِيباً وَسَفَراً قاصِداً لَاتَّبَعُوكَ — "Had it been a near gain and a short journey, they would have surely followed you" (9:42). The verse [continues]—"They are able, and it was in the knowledge of Allah—that had it been a near gain and a short journey, they would have done so."

I say: Al-Saduq narrated it in al-Ma'ani with his chain from Abd al-Ala ibn A'yan from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) similarly.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His, the Exalted, saying: وَلكِنْ بَعُدَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الشُّقَّةُ — "but the distance seemed too far to them" (9:42), meaning to Tabuk. The reason for this was that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) never traveled a journey further than it nor more difficult than it.

The cause of this was that summer traders used to come to Medina from Syria with flour (الدرموك) and food. They were Nabataeans, and they spread rumors in Medina that the Romans had gathered, intending to raid the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with a massive army, and that Heraclius had marched with the collection of his troops, bringing with them Ghassan, Judham, Bahra, and Amilah. His vanguards had reached al-Balqa, while he himself had stopped at Homs.

So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent his companions to Tabuk, which is in the lands of al-Balqa, and sent word to the tribes around him, to Mecca, and to those who had accepted Islam from Khuza'ah, Muzaynah, and Juhaynah, urging them to Jihad.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ordered his camp to be pitched at Thaniyyat al-Wada', and he ordered the people of wealth (أهل الجدة) to assist those who had no strength. Whoever had anything brought it out; they carried [others], strengthened [them], and urged one another toward that.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) delivered a sermon and said, after praising Allah and extolling Him: "O people, indeed the most truthful of speech is the Book of Allah. The most worthy of words is the word of God-consciousness (كلمة التقوى). The best of religions is the religion of Abraham. The best of traditions is the tradition of Muhammad. The most noble of speech is the remembrance of Allah. The best of stories is this Quran. The best of matters are those determined with resolve (عزائمها), and the worst of matters are innovations (محدثاتها). The best of guidance is the guidance of the Prophets. The most noble of the slain are the martyrs. The blindest of blindness is wandering after guidance. The best of deeds is that which benefits. The best of guidance is that which is followed. The worst of blindness is the blindness of the heart. The upper hand is better than the lower hand. That which is little and sufficient is better than that which is much and distracting. The worst of excuses is at the presence of death. The worst of regrets is on the Day of Resurrection. Among the people is he who does not come to the Friday prayer except rarely (نزرا). Among them is he who does not remember Allah except with nonsensical speech (هجرا). Among the greatest of sins is the lying tongue. The best of wealth is the wealth of the soul (غنى النفس). The best of provisions is God-consciousness (التقوى). The head of wisdom is the fear of Allah. The best of what is cast into the heart is certainty (اليقين). Doubt (الارتياب) is from disbelief (الكفر). Distancing oneself from the deeds of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). Embezzlement (الغلول) is from the pus of Hell. Intoxication is a coal of the Fire. Poetry is from Iblis. Wine is the gatherer of sin. Women are the snares of Iblis. Youth is a branch of madness. The worst of earnings is the earning of usury (الربا). The worst of eating is eating the property of the orphan. The fortunate (السعيد) is he who takes warning from others. The wretched (الشقي) is he who is wretched in his mother's womb. Each of you shall eventually go to a place of four cubits. The matter is to its end, and the criterion of a matter is its conclusion. The most excessive usury is lying. Everything that is coming is near. Reviling a believer is transgression (فسوق). Fighting a believer is disbelief (كفر). Eating his flesh is from the disobedience of Allah. The sanctity of his property is like the sanctity of his blood. Whoever relies on Allah, He suffices him. Whoever is patient triumphs. Whoever pardons, Allah pardons him. Whoever restrains his rage, Allah rewards him. Whoever is patient in the face of calamity, Allah compensates him. Whoever seeks reputation (السمعة), Allah will expose him. Whoever fasts, Allah multiplies [the reward] for him. Whoever disobeys Allah, He punishes him. O Allah, forgive me and my nation. O Allah, forgive me and my nation. I seek Allah's forgiveness for me and for you."

He said: So the people desired Jihad when they heard this from the Messenger of Allah. The Arab tribes whom he had summoned arrived, while a group of hypocrites and others stayed behind. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) met al-Jadd ibn Qays and said to him: "O Abu Wahb, will you not march with us in this raid? Perhaps you might take some of the daughters of the 'Yellow Ones' (بنات الأصفر) [the Romans]." He said: "O Messenger of Allah, by Allah, my people know that there is no one among them more captivated by women than I. I fear that if I go out with you, I will not be able to remain patient when I see the daughters of the 'Yellow Ones.' So do not tempt me, and permit me to stay." And he said to a group of his people: "Do not go out in the heat."

His son said: "You talk back to the Messenger of Allah and say to him what you say, then you say to your people: 'Do not march in the heat'? By Allah, Allah will surely send down a Quran regarding this that people will read until the Day of Resurrection." So Allah sent down to His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding that: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ ائْذَنْ لِي وَلا تَفْتِنِّي ـ أَلا فِي الْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ — "Among them is he who says, ‘Give me leave, and do not put me into ordeal.’ Behold! They have already fallen into ordeal, and indeed hell besieges the faithless." (9:49).

Then al-Jadd ibn Qays said: "Does Muhammad hope that the war with the Romans is like the war with others? Not one of these will ever return."

I say: These meanings have been narrated in many other reports from both Shi'i and Sunni paths.

In al-Uyun, with its chain from Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm, who said: "I attended the assembly of al-Ma'mun while al-Rida Ali ibn Musa (peace be upon him) was with him. He said to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, is it not your view that the Prophets are infallible (معصومون)?' He said: 'Yes.' So al-Ma'mun said to him—among what he asked—'O Abu al-Hasan, tell me about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: عَفَا اللهُ عَنْكَ لِمَ أَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ — "May Allah excuse you! Why did you give them leave..." (9:43).'"

Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: "This is among what was revealed as: 'I mean you, but listen, O neighbor' (إياك أعني واسمعي يا جارة) — a rhetorical device where the speech is addressed to one person but intended for others. Allah, the Exalted, addressed His Prophet with that but intended his nation. Likewise is His, the Mighty and Majestic, saying: لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ — 'If you ascribe a partner [to Allah], your deeds will surely go for nothing, and you will surely be among the losers' (39:65), and His, the Exalted, saying: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَدَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً — 'Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly been on the verge of inclining toward them a little' (17:74)." He said: "You have spoken the truth, O son of the Messenger of Allah."

I say: The content of the narration aligns with what we presented in the explanation of the verse, contrary to what they mentioned—that his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) giving them leave to stay behind was of the nature of "leaving the better option" (ترك الأولى), for the verse would not be consistent with being of the type "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor" in that case.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd al-Razzaq narrated in al-Musannaf, and Ibn Jarir from Amr ibn Maymun al-Awdi, who said: "There were two things the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did regarding which he had not been commanded anything: his giving leave to the hypocrites, and his taking [ransom] from the captives. So Allah sent down: عَفَا اللهُ عَنْكَ لِمَ أَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ — 'May Allah excuse you! Why did you give them leave...'" (9:43).

I say: The discussion on the content of this narration has already preceded.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His, the Exalted, saying: وَلَوْ أَرادُوا الْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا لَهُ عُدَّةً — "Had they desired to go forth, they would have surely prepared some equipment for it" (9:46), and what follows it, he said: "There remained behind the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) people of [sincere] intentions and insights (بصائر) who were not overtaken by doubt or suspicion, but they said: 'We shall catch up with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).'"

Among them was Abu Khaythama, who was strong and had two wives and two shaded arbors (عريشان). His two wives had sprinkled his arbors [with water to cool them], cooled water for him, and prepared food for him. He looked out over his arbors, and when he saw them, he said: "No, by Allah, this is not fair! The Messenger of Allah—Allah has forgiven him his past and future sins—has gone out in the heat and wind, carrying weapons and striving in the way of Allah, while Abu Khaythama is strong, sitting in his arbor with two beautiful women? No, by Allah, this is not fair!"

Then he took his she-camel, fastened his saddle upon her, and caught up with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The people saw a rider on the road and informed the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of that. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Let it be Abu Khaythama." He approached and informed the Prophet of what had happened with him, so he rewarded him with good and prayed for him.

Abu Dharr had stayed behind the Messenger of Allah for three days because his camel was emaciated. He caught up after three days, but his camel stopped on the way. So he left it, carried his belongings on his back, and when the day brightened, the Muslims saw a person approaching. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Let it be Abu Dharr." They said: "It is Abu Dharr." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Reach him, for he is thirsty." So they reached him with water.

Abu Dharr reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while he had a water-skin (إداوة) containing water. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O Abu Dharr, you have water with you and yet you were thirsty?" He said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah—may my father and mother be sacrificed for you—I came upon a rock with rainwater on it. I tasted it and found it sweet and cool, so I said: 'I will not drink it until the Messenger of Allah drinks it.'"

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O Abu Dharr, may Allah have mercy on you. You live alone, you die alone, you are resurrected alone, and you enter Paradise alone. A group of people from the people of Iraq will be fortunate because of you—they will undertake your washing, your preparation, the prayer over you, and your burial."

Then he said: "There had stayed behind the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) a group of hypocrites, and a group of insightful believers (مؤمنين مستبصرين) in whom no hypocrisy was found: among them were Ka'b ibn Malik the poet, Murarah ibn al-Rabi', and Hilal ibn Umayyah al-Rafi'i. When Allah accepted their repentance, Ka'b said: 'I was never stronger than I was at that time when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out to Tabuk. I never had two mounts gathered for me except on that day. I used to say: I will go out tomorrow or the day after, for I am strong. I procrastinated and felt sluggish for days after the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had left. I would enter the market and not fulfill any need. Then I met Hilal ibn Umayyah and Murarah ibn al-Rabi', who had also stayed behind. We agreed to go early to the market, but we fulfilled no need. We kept saying: We will go out tomorrow and the day after, until news reached us of the Messenger of Allah's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) return, and we regretted it.'"

"When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrived, we met him to congratulate him on his safety. We greeted him, but he did not return our greeting and turned away from us. We greeted our brothers, but they did not return our greeting. News of that reached our families, so they cut off speech with us. We would attend the mosque, but no one would greet us or speak to us. Our wives came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'We have heard of your displeasure with our husbands; should we separate from them?' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Do not separate from them, but let them not come near you.'"

"When Ka'b ibn Malik and his two companions saw what had befallen them, they said: 'What keeps us in Medina while the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), our brothers, and our families do not speak to us? Come, let us go out to this mountain and remain there until Allah accepts our repentance or we die.'"

"So they went out to Dhubab—a mountain in Medina. They would fast, and their families would bring them food, placing it aside and then turning away from them without speaking to them."

"They remained like this for many days, weeping night and day and calling upon Allah to forgive them. When the matter became long for them, Ka'b said to them: 'O people, Allah and His Messenger are displeased with us, and our families and brothers are displeased with us and do not speak to us—so why are we not displeased with one another?' So they separated on the mountain and swore that none of them would speak to his companion until he died or Allah accepted his repentance. They remained like that for three days, each one of them in a part of the mountain, none of them seeing his companion or speaking to him."

"On the third night, while the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in the house of Umm Salamah, their repentance was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): لقد تاب الله بالنبي على المهاجرين والأنصار ـ الذين اتبعوه في ساعة العسرة — 'Allah has certainly turned [graciously] to the Prophet and the Emigrants and the Helpers who followed him in the hour of difficulty' (9:117). Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: 'This is how it was revealed, and they are Abu Dharr, Abu Khaythama, and Umayr ibn Wahb—those who stayed behind then caught up with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).'"

"Then He said regarding those three: وَعَلَى الثَّلاثَةِ الَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا — 'And to the three who were left behind' (9:118). The Knower (peace be upon him) said: 'It was only revealed: "upon the three who opposed (خالفوا)"; for if they had been "left behind" (خُلِّفُوا), there would have been no blame upon them. حَتَّى إِذا ضاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ — "until the earth became narrow for them despite its expanse" (9:118), as the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), their brothers, and their families would not speak to them, so Medina became narrow for them until they left it. وَضاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ — "and their own souls became narrow for them" (9:118), as they swore not to speak to one another, so they separated, and Allah accepted their repentance when He recognized the sincerity of their intentions.'"

I say: The discussion regarding these two verses and the narrations concerning them will follow.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from al-Mughirah, who said: "I heard him saying regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلَوْ أَرادُوا الْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا لَهُ عُدَّةً — 'Had they desired to go forth, they would have surely prepared some equipment for it' (9:46). He said: 'He means by equipment (العدة) the intention (النية); He says: "Had they an intention, they would have gone forth."'"

I say: This narration, despite its weakness, its being disconnected (إرسال), and its being attributed to an unnamed Imam (إضمار), does not align with the wording of the verse. Allah knows best.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Ishaq and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from al-Hasan al-Basri, who said: "Abdullah ibn Ubayy, Abdullah ibn Nabtal, and Rifa'ah ibn Zayd ibn Tabut were among the leaders of the hypocrites, and they were among those who plotted against Islam and its people. Regarding them, Allah revealed: لَقَدِ ابْتَغَوُا الْفِتْنَةَ مِنْ قَبْلُ ـ وَقَلَّبُوا لَكَ الْأُمُورَ — 'They have certainly sought to cause sedition before, and they upset the matters for you' (9:48) until the end of the verse."

Ayahs

وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ ٱئْذَن لِّى وَلَا تَفْتِنِّىٓ ۚ أَلَا فِى ٱلْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا۟ ۗ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌۢ بِٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٤٩)
Among them there are some who say, ‘Give me leave, and do not put me to temptation.’ Look! They have already fallen into temptation and indeed hell besieges the faithless. (49)
إِن تُصِبْكَ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْكَ مُصِيبَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ قَدْ أَخَذْنَآ أَمْرَنَا مِن قَبْلُ وَيَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُمْ فَرِحُونَ (٥٠)
If some good should befall you, it upsets them; but if an adversity befalls you, they say, ‘We had already taken our precautions in advance,’ and they go away boasting. (50)
قُل لَّن يُصِيبَنَآ إِلَّا مَا كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَنَا هُوَ مَوْلَىٰنَا ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (٥١)
Say, ‘Nothing will befall us except what Allah has ordained for us. He is our master, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust.’ (51)
قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنَآ إِلَّآ إِحْدَى ٱلْحُسْنَيَيْنِ ۖ وَنَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ أَن يُصِيبَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِعَذَابٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦٓ أَوْ بِأَيْدِينَا ۖ فَتَرَبَّصُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّتَرَبِّصُونَ (٥٢)
Say, ‘Do you not await one of the two excellent things to happen to us? But we await that Allah shall visit on you a punishment, from Him, or by our hands. So wait! We too are waiting along with you.’ (52)
قُلْ أَنفِقُوا۟ طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا لَّن يُتَقَبَّلَ مِنكُمْ ۖ إِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (٥٣)
Say, ‘Spend willingly or unwillingly, it shall never be accepted from you; for you are indeed a transgressing lot.’ (53)
وَمَا مَنَعَهُمْ أَن تُقْبَلَ مِنْهُمْ نَفَقَـٰتُهُمْ إِلَّآ أَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَبِرَسُولِهِۦ وَلَا يَأْتُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ إِلَّا وَهُمْ كُسَالَىٰ وَلَا يُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا وَهُمْ كَـٰرِهُونَ (٥٤)
Nothing stops their charities from being accepted except that they have no faith in Allah and His Apostle and do not perform the prayer but lazily, and do not spend but reluctantly. (54)
فَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٥٥)
So let not their wealth and children impress you: Allah only desires to punish them with these in the life of this world, and that their souls may depart while they are faithless. (55)
وَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ إِنَّهُمْ لَمِنكُمْ وَمَا هُم مِّنكُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ يَفْرَقُونَ (٥٦)
They swear by Allah that they belong to you, but they do not belong to you. Rather, they are a frightened lot. (56)
لَوْ يَجِدُونَ مَلْجَـًٔا أَوْ مَغَـٰرَٰتٍ أَوْ مُدَّخَلًا لَّوَلَّوْا۟ إِلَيْهِ وَهُمْ يَجْمَحُونَ (٥٧)
If they could find a refuge, or a hideout, or a hole [to creep into], they would turn to it in frantic haste. (57)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَلْمِزُكَ فِى ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ فَإِنْ أُعْطُوا۟ مِنْهَا رَضُوا۟ وَإِن لَّمْ يُعْطَوْا۟ مِنْهَآ إِذَا هُمْ يَسْخَطُونَ (٥٨)
There are some of them who blame you regarding [the distribution of] the charities: if they are given from them, they are pleased, but if they are not given from them, behold, they are displeased. (58)
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ رَضُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ سَيُؤْتِينَا ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ إِنَّآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ رَٰغِبُونَ (٥٩)
[It would have been better] if they had been pleased with what Allah and His Apostle gave them, and had said, ‘Allah is sufficient for us; Allah and His Apostle will give to us out of His grace. Indeed to Allah do we eagerly turn.’ (59)
۞ إِنَّمَا ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتُ لِلْفُقَرَآءِ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ عَلَيْهَا وَٱلْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَفِى ٱلرِّقَابِ وَٱلْغَـٰرِمِينَ وَفِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۖ فَرِيضَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٦٠)
The charities are only for the poor and the needy, and those employed to collect them, and those whose hearts are to be reconciled, and for [the freedom of] the slaves and the debtors, and in the way of Allah, and for the traveller. [This is] an ordinance from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (60)
وَمِنْهُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ ٱلنَّبِىَّ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ ۚ قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَّكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٦١)
Among them are those who torment the Prophet, and say, ‘He is an ear.’ Say, ‘An ear that is good for you. He has faith in Allah and trusts the faithful, and is a mercy for those of you who have faith.’ As for those who torment the Apostle of Allah, there is a painful punishment for them. (61)
يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ لِيُرْضُوكُمْ وَٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ أَحَقُّ أَن يُرْضُوهُ إِن كَانُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٦٢)
They swear to you by Allah, to please you; but Allah and His Apostle are worthier that they should please Him, should they be faithful. (62)
أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ مَن يُحَادِدِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَأَنَّ لَهُۥ نَارَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْخِزْىُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٦٣)
Do they not know that whoever opposes Allah and His Apostle, there awaits him the Fire of hell, to remain in it [forever]? That is the great disgrace. (63)

(Commentary)

The verses follow the discourse regarding the hypocrites (المنافقون) and the exposition of their state. They mention various sayings and actions of theirs, investigating what these reveal of their malicious internal traits and their beliefs founded upon delusion (الضلال).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ ائْذَنْ لِي وَلا تَفْتِنِّي أَلا فِي الْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا — Among them is he who says, ‘Give me leave [to stay at home] and do not put me into temptation.’ Look! They have already fallen into temptation (9:49). Temptation (الفتنة) — a trial or lure that leads one astray — here, according to what the context suggests, refers either to being cast into that which seduces and deceives, or being cast into widespread trial and calamity.

According to the first meaning, the intent is: "Give me leave to remain behind and not go out for Jihad (الجهاد) — striving in the way of Allah — and do not cast me into temptation by describing the precious spoils and desirable things of this expedition, lest I be seduced by them and feel compelled to go out." According to the second meaning: "Give me leave and do not cast me into the hardship, misfortune, and calamity present in this expedition."

Allah answered their claim with His saying: أَلا فِي الْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا (Look! They have already fallen into temptation). The meaning is that they are, according to their own claim, avoiding a temptation they anticipate from going out, yet they have erred. For the state they are currently in—of disbelief (الكفر), hypocrisy (النفاق), and evil intent, of which this very statement they uttered is a manifestation—is itself the temptation into which they have fallen. Satan (الشيطان) has already tempted them with delusion, and they have fallen into the peril of disbelief, misguidance, and its temptation.

This is their state in this worldly life (النشأة الدنيوية). As for the Hereafter, Hell (جهنم) encompasses the disbelievers just as temptation encompasses them in this world and just as they have fallen into it. Thus, His saying: أَلا فِي الْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا (Look! They have already fallen into temptation) and His saying: وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ (and indeed hell besieges the faithless) (9:49) together seem to convey a single meaning: that these people are perpetually situated in temptation and destruction in both this world and the Hereafter.

It is possible to understand from His saying: وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ (and indeed hell besieges the faithless) an actual, present encompassment rather than a future one, as suggested by the verses indicating the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال) — the manifestation of human actions in their true ontological forms in the afterlife.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ تُصِبْكَ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ وَإِنْ تُصِبْكَ مُصِيبَةٌ يَقُولُوا قَدْ أَخَذْنا أَمْرَنا مِنْ قَبْلُ — If some good should befall you, it upsets them, and if a misfortune befalls you, they say, ‘We had taken our precautions before,’ (9:50). By the evidence of the context, "good" (الحسنة) and "misfortune" (المصيبة) refer to what follows wars and expeditions for those involved: the good of victory, triumph, spoils, and captives; and the misfortune of being killed, wounded, or defeated.

His saying: يَقُولُوا قَدْ أَخَذْنا أَمْرَنا مِنْ قَبْلُ (they say, ‘We had taken our precautions before’) is a metaphor (كناية) for avoiding evil before it occurs. It is as if their affair was out of their hands, so they took hold of it, seized it, and gained mastery over it, not allowing it to become corrupted or lost.

The meaning of the verse is that these hypocrites' desires are against you: if you gain spoils and victory in this endeavor of yours, it upsets them; and if you are killed, wounded, or afflicted by any other misfortune, they say, "We have already guarded against evil beforehand," and they turn away while they are exultant.

Allah, the Glorified, responded to this with two answers in two verses: His saying: قُلْ لَنْ يُصِيبَنا (Say, ‘Nothing will befall us...’) and His saying: قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ (Say, ‘Do you await for us...’).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَنْ يُصِيبَنا إِلَّا ما كَتَبَ اللهُ لَنا هُوَ مَوْلانا وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ — Say, ‘Nothing will befall us except what Allah has ordained for us. He is our master, and in Allah let the faithful put their trust’ (9:51). The gist of this is that the guardianship (ولاية) of our affairs belongs solely to Allah, the Glorified—as indicated by the exclusivity in His saying: هُوَ مَوْلانا (He is our master)—not to ourselves nor to any of these apparent causes. Rather, the reality of the matter belongs to Allah alone. He has decreed with a final decree (كتابة حتم) whatever will befall us of good or evil, benefit or misfortune. Since this is the case, it is our duty to obey His command, strive to revive His cause, and perform Jihad in His way. The will (المشية) belongs to Allah regarding whatever befalls us therein of good or misfortune. The servants have no duty except to abandon their own contrivance (التدبير) and obey the command, which is trust (التوكل) — the total reliance upon Allah while resigning one's affairs to Him.

From this, it becomes clear that the intent of His saying: وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ (and in Allah let the faithful put their trust) is not a new sentence (كلام مستأنف) but is coordinated with what precedes it, completing it. The meaning is that the guardianship of our affairs belongs to Allah and we are believers in Him; the necessary consequence of this is that we trust in Him and refer the matter back to Him without choosing for ourselves anything of good or misfortune. If good befalls us, the favor is His; and if misfortune befalls us, the will and the choice are His. No blame attaches to us, no schadenfreude can affect us, and no grief or distress enters our hearts.

Indeed, the Exalted has said: ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَبْرَأَها إِنَّ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ. لِكَيْلا تَأْسَوْا عَلى ما فاتَكُمْ وَلا فَرحُوا بِما آتاكُمْ — No affliction visits the earth or your own selves but it is in a Book before We bring it about—that is indeed easy for Allah—so that you may not grieve for what escapes you, nor exult over what He has granted you (57:22-23). And He said: ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ وَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُ — No affliction visits [anyone] except by Allah’s leave. Whoever has faith in Allah, He guides his heart (64:11). And He said: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — That is because Allah is the protector of the faithful (47:11). And He said: وَاللهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — and Allah is the Protector of the faithful (3:68). And He said: فَاللهُ هُوَ الْوَلِيُّ — it is Allah who is the Protector (42:9).

These verses—as you see—contain the principles of this reality which the verse we are discussing announces as a response to the delusion of the hypocrites. This reality is that the true guardianship (الولاية) belongs to Allah, the Glorified; no one besides Him has any part in the command. When a human being believes in Him and recognizes the station of his Lord, he knows this, and it becomes his duty to trust in his Lord and return to Him the reality of will and choice. Thus, he does not exult over a good that befalls him, nor does he grieve over a misfortune that strikes him.

It is a mark of ignorance for a person to be upset by the good that befalls his enemy or to be pleased by the misfortune that strikes him, for he has no part in the command. This is the first answer regarding their distress at what befalls the believers of good and their joy at what befalls them of misfortune.

The apparent words of some exegetes suggest that "the Master" (المولى) in the verse means "the Helper" (الناصر). Similarly, the words of some suggest that His saying: وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ (and in Allah let the faithful put their trust) is a new sentence in which Allah commands the believers to trust in Him. However, the observed context of the two verses does not support this.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنا إِلَّا إِحْدَى الْحُسْنَيَيْنِ وَنَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ — Say, ‘Do you await for us anything except one of the two best outcomes? But we await for you...’ (9:52). The "two best outcomes" (الحسنيان) are the good and the misfortune, as indicated by the first verse which narrates that they are upset by the good that befalls the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and pleased by the misfortune that befalls him, saying, "We had taken our precautions before." Thus, they are in a state of awaiting (تربص), expecting what will occur to him and the believers of good or misfortune.

Both the good and the misfortune are "best outcomes" (حسنيان) according to the religious perspective. In the "good" (الحسنة), there is the good of this world and a great reward with Allah. In the "misfortune" (السيئة)—which is martyrdom (الشهادة) or any exhaustion and hardship that befalls them—there is the pleasure of Allah and an eternal, permanent reward.

The meaning of the verse is: "We and you are each awaiting something regarding the other. However, you await for us one of two traits, each of which is a 'best' trait: victory over the enemy accompanied by spoils, or martyrdom in the way of Allah. We, on the other hand, await for you that Allah should afflict you with a punishment from Himself—such as a heavenly punishment—or a punishment at our hands, such as Him commanding us to fight you and cleanse the earth of the filth of your existence. Thus, we are successful in any case. If what you await occurs, we are felicitous; and if what we await occurs, we are felicitous. So await; indeed, we are awaiting with you." This is a second answer to the hypocrites.

The first verse mentioned the befalling of good and misfortune upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, while in the position of answering in the second and third verses, it mentions them befalling the Prophet and the believers together, due to their adherence to him and their sharing in whatever good or misfortune befalls him.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَنْفِقُوا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً لَنْ يُتَقَبَّلَ مِنْكُمْ إِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ — Say, ‘Spend willingly or unwillingly, it shall never be accepted from you; for you are indeed a transgressing lot’ (9:53). The imperative (أمر) carries the meaning of a condition (شرط). The choice between two options (الترديد) is for generalization (التعميم). The use of the imperative in such instances is a metaphor for the absence of prohibition and the closing of the path, intimating that the action is futile (لغو) and no effect will result from it. His saying: لَنْ يُتَقَبَّلَ مِنْكُمْ (it shall never be accepted from you) is the justification for the command, just as His saying: إِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ (for you are indeed a transgressing lot) is the justification for the lack of acceptance.

The meaning of the verse is: "We do not prevent you from spending, whether willingly or unwillingly, for it is futile and unaccepted because you are transgressors (فاسقون), and the deeds of the transgressors are not accepted." The Exalted said: إِنَّما يَتَقَبَّلُ اللهُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ — Allah accepts only from the God-conscious (5:27). Acceptance (التقبل) is more emphatic than mere receiving (القبول).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما مَنَعَهُمْ أَنْ تُقْبَلَ مِنْهُمْ نَفَقاتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِاللهِ وَبِرَسُولِهِ — Nothing stops their charities from being accepted except that they have no faith in Allah and His Apostle... (9:54). The verse is a detailed justification for the non-acceptance of their spending; in other words, it serves as an explanation of their transgression (فسق). It enumerates disbelief in Allah and His Messenger, laziness in establishing prayer (الصلاة), and reluctance in spending as the pillars of their hypocrisy.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوالُهُمْ وَلا أَوْلادُهُمْ إِنَّما يُرِيدُ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ بِها — So let not their wealth or their children impress you: Allah only desires to punish them with these... (9:55). Impress (الإعجاب) with something is the joy found in observing its beauty, perfection, or the like. Departing (الزهوق) is the exit of something with difficulty; its root meaning is destruction (الهلاك), as has been said.

Allah, the Glorified, forbade His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from being impressed by the wealth and children of the hypocrites—meaning their abundance, as the context implies. He justified this by stating that this wealth and these children—which inevitably preoccupy a person—are not among the blessings that herald felicity for them, but are rather among the afflictions that drag them toward wretchedness (الشقاء). For Allah, who bestowed these upon them, only intended by them to punish them in the life of this world and that they should pass away while they are disbelievers.

For the life that a living being considers felicity for itself and rest for its essence is only a felicity containing rest and joy if it proceeds according to its true course: that the human being clothes himself with its real effects of beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), without being occupied by anything other than what contains his good and benefit. This is the life in which there is no death, the rest with which there is no exhaustion, and the pleasure beneath which there is no pain. It is the life within the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah. The Exalted said: أَلا إِنَّ أَوْلِياءَ اللهِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ — Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve (10:62).

As for one who is occupied with the world, whose heart is drawn to its ornaments of wealth and children, who is deceived by the false hopes and desires that appear to him therein, and who is seduced by the devils—he has fallen into the contradictions of bodily powers and the conflicts of material pleasures. He is punished with the severest punishment by the very things in which he sees his felicity and pleasure. It is an observed reality that the more the world turns toward a person and provides him with an abundance of wealth and children, the further it distances him from the station of servitude (العبودية) and the closer it brings him to destruction and spiritual torment. He remains perpetually fluctuating between these causes—agreeable and disagreeable—and these conditions—harmonious and conflicting. Thus, what these heedless people call "affluence" (سعة العيش) is in reality "distress" (ضنك), as the Exalted said: وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَعْمى. قالَ رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِي أَعْمى وَقَدْ كُنْتُ بَصِيراً. قالَ كَذلِكَ أَتَتْكَ آياتُنا فَنَسِيتَها وَكَذلِكَ الْيَوْمَ تُنْسى — But whoever turns away from My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection.’ He will say, ‘My Lord, why have You raised me blind? I was certainly observant.’ He will say, ‘So it is. Our signs came to you, but you forgot them, and thus you will be forgotten today’ (40:124-126).

The ultimate end of a person's turning away from the remembrance of his Lord and his devotion to the world—seeking thereby the felicity of life, the rest of the self, and the pleasure of the soul—is that he is punished amidst the layers of these temptations which he perceives as blessings, and he disbelieves in his Lord by exiting the garb of servitude. As He said: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ بِها فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَتَزْهَقَ أَنْفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كافِرُونَ (Allah only desires to punish them with these in the life of this world, and that their souls may depart while they are faithless). This is the "granting of respite" (الإملاء) and "gradual leading to ruin" (الاستدراج) which He mentions in His saying: سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ — We will draw them on [to their ruin] from whence they do not know. And I will grant them respite; indeed My stratagem is profound (7:182-183).

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَحْلِفُونَ بِاللهِ إِنَّهُمْ لَمِنْكُمْ وَما هُمْ مِنْكُمْ — They swear by Allah that they belong to you, but they do not belong to you... (9:56-57). Dread (الفرق) is the agitation of the soul due to an expected harm. Refuge (الملجأ) is the place to which one resorts and in which one is fortified. Caverns (المغار) is the place where a person goes deep to hide from sight; it is also applied to the cave (الغار), which is a hole in the mountains. A place to enter (المدخل) is the path into which one slips or enters stealthily. Bolting (الجماح) is the act of one passing by quickly, headlong, with nothing turning him away. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَلْمِزُكَ فِي الصَّدَقاتِ فَإِنْ أُعْطُوا مِنْها رَضُوا وَإِنْ لَمْ يُعْطَوْا مِنْها إِذا هُمْ يَسْخَطُونَ — There are some of them who blame you regarding the charities: if they are given from them, they are pleased, but if they are not given from them, behold, they are displeased (9:58). Blaming (اللمز) is finding fault. They only found fault with him regarding the charities when he did not give to them due to their lack of entitlement or for other reasons, as indicated by the end of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ رَضُوا ما آتاهُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ — Had they been pleased with what Allah and His Apostle gave them... (9:59). Had (لو) expresses a wish. His saying: رَضُوا ما آتاهُمُ اللهُ (pleased with what Allah gave them) is as if "pleasure" (الرضى) includes the meaning of "taking"; hence it is transitive by itself—meaning they took it while being pleased with it, or they were pleased while taking it. Bestowal (الإيتاء) is giving. Allah is sufficient for us (حسبنا الله) means He is enough for us in what we desire and hope for.

His saying: سَيُؤْتِينَا اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَرَسُولُهُ (Allah will give us out of His grace, and His Apostle [as well]) is an exposition of what is desired and craved, not a report of what will be. His saying: إِنَّا إِلَى اللهِ راغِبُونَ (Indeed we are suppliants to Allah) is like a justification for His saying: سَيُؤْتِينَا اللهُ (Allah will give us...).

The meaning is: It was among what was wished for them that they should have taken what Allah and His Messenger gave them by His command—from the wealth of charities or otherwise—and said: "Allah, the Glorified, is sufficient for us above all other causes; we are suppliants for His grace and we crave that He give us of His grace, and that His Messenger give us."

In the verse, there is a subtle exposition that is not hidden, where the "giving" (الإيتاء) is attributed to Allah and His Messenger, while "sufficiency" (الكفاية), "grace" (الفضل), and "supplication" (الرغبة) are reserved for Allah alone, as is necessitated by the religion of monotheism (التوحيد).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا الصَّدَقاتُ لِلْفُقَراءِ وَالْمَساكِينِ وَالْعامِلِينَ عَلَيْها وَالْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَفِي الرِّقابِ وَالْغارِمِينَ وَفِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ — The charities are only for the poor and the needy, and those employed to collect them, and those whose hearts are to be reconciled, and for [the freedom of] the slaves and the debtors, and in the way of Allah, and for the traveller... (9:60). The verse is an exposition of the categories to which the obligatory charities (الصدقات الواجبة)—which are the alms-taxes (الزكوات)—are to be directed, as evidenced by His saying at the end of the verse: فَرِيضَةً مِنَ اللهِ (an ordinance from Allah). These are eight categories, based on the apparent context of the verse, which necessitates that the "poor" (الفقير) and the "needy" (المسكين) are two distinct categories.

Scholars have differed as to whether the poor and the needy are one class or two. Then, assuming they are two, they differed on their meanings in many opinions, most of which do not lead to a clear proof. What the literal meaning of the two words provides is that the poor (الفقير) is one characterized by lack and the absence of wealth to meet his vital needs, in contrast to the rich (الغني), who is characterized by wealth and ease.

As for the needy (المسكين), he is one upon whom misery (المسكنة) and humiliation (الذلة) have descended in addition to the lack of wealth. This only occurs when his poverty reaches a point that humiliates him, such as one who finds no choice but to sacrifice his dignity and ask every noble and ignoble person due to the severity of poverty, or like the blind and the lame. Thus, the needy person is in a worse state than the poor person.

Although the poor and the needy are, in terms of ratio, general and specific—for every needy person is poor from the perspective of financial need, but not vice versa—custom (العرف) views them as two contrasting classes because of the difference between the two descriptions in themselves. Thus, it cannot be objected that mentioning the "poor" in this sense makes mentioning the "needy" redundant due to its generality. This is because "misery" (المسكنة) is a description of humiliation, like chronic illness, lameness, or blindness, even if some of its instances represent the ultimate humiliation due to lack of wealth.

As for those employed to collect them (العاملون عليها)—meaning the charities—they are those who strive to gather the alms-taxes and their collectors.

As for those whose hearts are to be reconciled (المؤلفة قلوبهم), they are those whose hearts are won over by being given a portion of the alms-tax so that they may embrace Islam, or so that the enemy may be repelled through them, or so that they may be assisted in the needs of the religion.

As for His saying: وَفِي الرِّقابِ (and for [the freedom of] the slaves), it is connected to an omitted word, the estimation being: "and the expenditure is for the slaves"—meaning for their liberation, as in the case of the contracted slave (المكاتب) who is unable to pay what he stipulated to his master for his freedom, or the slave who is in severe hardship.

His saying: وَالْغارِمِينَ (and the debtors)—meaning for expenditure upon the debtors (الغارمين) — those burdened by debts — whose debts are paid off with a portion of the alms-tax.

His saying: وَفِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ (and in the way of Allah)—meaning for expenditure in the way of Allah (سبيل الله), which is every public work whose benefit returns to Islam and the Muslims and by which the interest of the religion is preserved. Among its most prominent instances is Jihad in the way of Allah, and it includes other works of general benefit and widespread utility, such as repairing roads, building bridges, and the like.

His saying: وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ (and for the traveller)—meaning for expenditure upon the wayfarer (ابن السبيل) — one cut off from his homeland and lacking the means of subsistence — even if he is wealthy and possessed of ease in his own land; his need is met with a portion of the alms-tax.

The context of the enumeration has varied regarding the eight categories mentioned in the verse. The first four were mentioned with the preposition lām (لـ): لِلْفُقَراءِ وَالْمَساكِينِ وَالْعامِلِينَ عَلَيْها وَالْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ (for the poor and the needy, and those employed to collect them, and those whose hearts are to be reconciled). Then the context changed for the remaining four, where it was said: وَفِي الرِّقابِ وَالْغارِمِينَ وَفِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ (and for the slaves and the debtors, and in the way of Allah, and for the traveller). The apparent context specific to these four is that the estimation is: "and in the slaves, and in the debtors, and in the way of Allah, and in the traveller."

As for the first four, the lām (لـ) denotes ownership (الملك) in the sense of "exclusivity in disposal" (الاختصاص في التصرف). For the verse, according to the context, is like a response to the hypocrites who were craving the charities while not being entitled to them, and were blaming the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله for their deprivation. They were answered by the verse that the charities have specific places where they are spent and they do not exceed them. The verse is not apparent in more than this degree of exclusivity.

As for whether their ownership of the charities is ownership in its well-known jurisprudential sense, and the reality of this ownership given that the owners are classes defined by their categorical titles rather than specific individuals, and the ratio of each class's portion to the rest of the portions—these are jurisprudential (فقهية) issues outside our purpose. The opinions of the jurists (الفقهاء) have differed greatly regarding them, so one should refer to jurisprudence (الفقه).

As for the remaining four, several reasons have been suggested for the change in context and their being placed after the first four:

Among them: that the order is to explain the most entitled (الأحق فالأحق) among the categories. Thus, the most entitled of the categories to them are the poor, then the needy, and so on in order. Because the last four, according to the order of entitlement, fall in the last four ranks, each was placed in its specific position. Were it not for this order, it would have been more appropriate to mention the categories and then mention the areas of interest, saying: "for the poor, the needy, those employed to collect them, those whose hearts are to be reconciled, the debtors, and the traveller," and then say: "and in the slaves and the way of Allah."

The truth is that the indication of the order—including the precedence and delay—regarding the importance of the criterion (الملاك) and the strength of the interest (المصلحة) in the parts of the order is beyond doubt. If he meant by "the most entitled" the one with the most important criterion, then that is it. But if he meant precedence and delay in terms of giving and spending and the like, there is absolutely no indication from the perspective of the wording, as is not hidden. The reason he used to support this is of no avail.

Among them: that the shift from the lām (لـ) in the last four to "in" (في) is to intimate that they are more firmly rooted in the entitlement to have charity spent upon them than those previously mentioned. This is because "in" (في) denotes a vessel (الوعاء); thus, it draws attention to the fact that they are worthy of having the charities placed in them and being made a site and a destination for them. This is because of what is involved in the liberation of slaves—whether through contract, bondage, or captivity—and in the liberation of debtors from debt, release, and rescue; and because the poor warrior or the one cut off during Hajj (الحج) — the pilgrimage to Makkah — combines poverty with worship; and similarly, the wayfarer combines poverty with being a stranger away from family and wealth.

The repetition of "in" (في) in His saying: وَفِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ (and in the way of Allah, and for the traveller) contains a further preference for these two over the slaves and the debtors. This was mentioned in al-Kashshaf.

However, this is countered by the fact that the first four are preceded by the lām of ownership, and that which is owned is more closely and permanently connected to its owner than that which is contained is to its vessel. This is apparent.

Among them: that the first four categories are owners of what might be given to them; they take it as property, so the entry of the lām was suitable for them. As for the last four, they do not own what is spent toward them; rather, it is not even spent to them, but rather on interests (مصالح) related to them.

The wealth spent on slaves is received by the masters who have made contracts or the sellers; thus, their portion is not spent into their own hands such that it would be expressed with the lām which suggests their ownership of what is spent toward them. Rather, they are the "loci" (محال) for this expenditure and the interest related to it. Similarly, the portion of the debtors is spent to their creditors to clear their liabilities, not to them. As for the "way of Allah," this is clear. As for the wayfarer, it is as if he was included in the "way of Allah," and was mentioned separately only to draw attention to his specificity, despite being devoid of both particles; his coordination with what is governed by the lām is possible, but coordination with what is near to it is nearer.

This view is not without merit, except that applying it to the wayfarer is not without affectation. What was mentioned about his inclusion in the "way of Allah" is a reason shared between him and others.

If someone were to say that the debtors and the wayfarer are coordinated with what is governed by the lām, and then mentioned the first reason—in the sense we mentioned—as a reason for the order, and the last reason as a reason for the specificity of the slaves and the way of Allah with the entry of "in" (في), it would not be far from the truth.

His saying at the end of the verse: فَرِيضَةً مِنَ اللهِ وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (an ordinance from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise) is an indication that the alms-tax is an obligatory, legislated duty based on knowledge and wisdom, not accepting the change of any changer. It is not unlikely that the "ordinance" (الفرض) relates to its division among the eight categories, as the context may support. For the purpose in the verse is related to the exposition of the expenditures of the charities, not the obligation of their origin. Thus, it is more appropriate that His saying: فَرِيضَةً مِنَ اللهِ (an ordinance from Allah) be an indication that its division among the eight categories is an ordained matter from Allah that is not to be exceeded, contrary to what the hypocrites craved in their blaming of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

From here, it appears that the verse is not devoid of an intimation that the eight categories remain in their portion without being specific to one time over another, contrary to what some have mentioned: that "those whose hearts are to be reconciled" were a group of nobles in the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله whose hearts he won over by giving them a portion of the charities, but after him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, Islam became dominant over others and the need for this type of reconciliation ceased. This is a false view, and the cessation of the need is denied.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمُ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ النَّبِيَّ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ — Among them are those who molest the Prophet and say, ‘He is an ear.’ Say, ‘An ear of good for you: he has faith in Allah and trusts the faithful, and is a mercy for those of you who have faith’ (9:61). The ear (الأذن) is the well-known organ of hearing. They applied the term "ear" to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and called him by it to indicate that he listens to everything said to him and hearkens to everything mentioned to him; thus, he is an "ear."

His saying: قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَكُمْ (Say, ‘An ear of good for you’) is a "real genitive construction" (إضافة حقيقية)—meaning a hearing that hears what contains your good, as he hears the revelation (الوحي) from Allah, the Glorified, which contains good for you, and he hears advice from the believers, which contains good for you. It is also possible that it is the "addition of the described to the attribute" (إضافة الموصوف إلى الصفة)—meaning an ear that is good for you because he only hears what benefits you and does not harm you.

The difference between the two views is that, according to the first, it is necessary that what he hears be good for them, like the revelation from Allah and advice from the believers. According to the second, it is necessary that his listening be a "listening of good" even if what he hears is not good—as if he listens to some things that are not good for them, but he listens to them to respect the speaker, then he treats that speech with the "assumption of correctness" (حمل على الصحة). He does not violate the speaker's sanctity nor think ill of him, yet he does not apply the effect of a "truthful report matching reality" to it. Thus, he does not hold the person spoken about accountable for what was said about him. In this way, he has respected the faith of the one spoken about just as he respected the faith of the speaker who brought him the report.

From here, it appears that the second view is more appropriate to the context of the verse, because He followed it with His saying: يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (he has faith in Allah and trusts the faithful).

This is because faith (الإيمان) is "affirmation" (التصديق). The object of faith was mentioned in His saying: يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ (he has faith in Allah). As for His saying: وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and trusts the faithful), its object was not mentioned; rather, it was mentioned that this affirmation is for the benefit of the believers due to the presence of the lām (لـ). The affirmation that contains benefit for the believers—even in a report that includes what harms them—is affirmation in the sense of "granting subjective truthfulness" (إعطاء الصدق المخبري) rather than "objective truthfulness" (الصدق الخبري). That is, assuming the reporter is truthful in the sense that he believes in the truth of his report, even if it is objectively false and does not match reality.

This is as in His saying, the Exalted: إِذا جاءَكَ الْمُنافِقُونَ قالُوا نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ اللهِ وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُ وَاللهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لَكاذِبُونَ — When the hypocrites come to you they say, ‘We bear witness that you are indeed the Apostle of Allah.’ Allah knows that you are indeed His Apostle, and Allah bears witness that the hypocrites are indeed liars (63:1). Allah, the Glorified, deems the hypocrites liars not in terms of their report of the Prophet's messengership, but in terms of their reporting contrary to what they believe. This is unlike the saying of the believers in what Allah, the Glorified, narrated: وَلَمَّا رَأَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الْأَحْزابَ قالُوا هذا ما وَعَدَنَا اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَصَدَقَ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ — When the faithful saw the confederates, they said, ‘This is what Allah and His Apostle had promised us, and Allah and His Apostle were true’ (33:22). They affirm Allah and His Messenger in the report itself, not just in the belief.

In short, the apparent meaning of His saying: يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ is that he affirms Allah in the revelation He has informed him of, and he affirms for the benefit of the believers everyone among them who conveys a report to him, by treating their action with the assumption of correctness and not accusing them of lying or evil intent, without applying an effect to everything he hears and listens to. Otherwise, his affirmation would not be for the benefit of the believers and the matter would be disordered. This meaning, as you see, supports the second view mentioned.

It is as if the "believers" (المؤمنون) refers to the community attributed to them, even if it includes individuals other than them, like the hypocrites. Accordingly, "those who have faith from among you" (الذين آمنوا منكم) refers to those of their people who truly believe. Thus, the meaning of the speech is that he affirms his Lord and affirms every individual of your community out of respect for the apparent state of their attribution to the believers, and he is a mercy for those of you who truly believe because he guides them to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم).

If "those who have faith" refers to those who believed at the beginning of the mission before the conquest—as previously mentioned that "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) is an honorific name in the Qur'an for the early believers in Islam—then "the believers" in His saying: وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ refers to those among them who truly believe, as it was used in this sense in His saying: وَلَمَّا رَأَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الْأَحْزابَ قالُوا هذا ما وَعَدَنَا اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ (When the faithful saw the confederates, they said, ‘This is what Allah and His Apostle had promised us...’).

It has been said that the lām (لـ) in His saying: وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ is for transitivity (التعدية), just as in His saying: يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ. Thus, faith becomes transitive with both particles, as in His saying: فَآمَنَ لَهُ لُوطٌ — So Lot believed in him (29:26), and His saying: فَما آمَنَ لِمُوسى إِلَّا ذُرِّيَّةٌ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ — But none believed in Moses except some youths from his people (10:83), and His saying: أَنُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَاتَّبَعَكَ الْأَرْذَلُونَ — They said, ‘Shall we believe in you, when it is the lowliest who follow you?’ (26:111).

It has also been said that the wording follows the method of "incorporation" (التضمين) — the inclusion of the meaning of one verb within another — by incorporating into "faith" the meaning of "inclining" (الجنوح), which is transitive with the lām. The meaning would be: "he inclines toward the believers while believing in them" or "he believes while inclining toward them."

While these two views are not problematic in themselves, they are made unlikely by the necessity of separating the first "believes" from the second in His saying: يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ without a clear point, unless it is taken as a stylistic variation in expression. Even so, the result is the same as the previous result: that his faith in the believers is not specific to the reporters alone such that he would affirm their report and hold others accountable if they reported something harmful; rather, it is a faith that encompasses all believers. Thus, he affirms the reporter in his report in the sense of granting subjective truthfulness, and he affirms the one reported about by treating his action with the assumption of correctness. Understand this.

Allah's counting His Prophet in His saying: وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ (and is a mercy for those of you who have faith) as a mercy for a specific people in this verse, while counting him as a mercy for all people in His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ — We did not send you but as a mercy to all the nations (21:107), is due to the difference in the intended meaning of "mercy" in the two verses. What is intended here is "actual mercy" (الرحمة الفعلية), while there it is "potential mercy" (الرحمة الشأنية).

In other words, he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is a mercy for whoever truly believes in him, in the sense that Allah, the Glorified, saved him through him from misguidance and sealed his end with felicity and honor. He is also a mercy for all people—their believers and their disbelievers, from his contemporaries and those who come after him—in the sense that Allah sent him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with a clear creed and a good tradition (سنة). Thus, he transformed human society and turned it from its path that deviated from rectitude toward the path of wretchedness and destruction. He illuminated with his torch the path of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. Thus, some are riding upon the path, successful in reaching the desired goal; some have exited the path of ruin and destruction but have not yet ridden upon the primordial path; and some intend to exit and enter but have not yet exited. This is the state of the general human society after the rising of Islam, the spread of its knowledge among the people, its reaching the ears of every listener, and its influence on every social tradition to the extent that it could be influenced. This is something that no researcher into the nature of human society can doubt. This view is close to the previous view or returns to it in reality.

His saying, the Exalted: يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللهِ لَكُمْ لِيُرْضُوكُمْ وَاللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُرْضُوهُ إِنْ كانُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ — They swear to you by Allah to please you, but Allah and His Apostle are worthier that they should please Him, if they are faithful (9:62). It is said in al-Majma': "The difference between 'worthier' (الأحق) and 'more appropriate' (الأصلح) is that 'worthier' may be among the attributes of essence, as when you say: 'Zayd is worthier of the wealth,' while 'more appropriate' does not occur in this position because it is among the attributes of action. You say: 'Allah is worthier to be obeyed,' but you do not say 'more appropriate'." End quote.

The original reason for this is that "appropriateness" (الصلاحية) and "suitability" (الصلوح) carry the meaning of potential and preparation, while "right" (الحق) carries the meaning of stability and necessity. Allah, the Glorified, is not characterized by anything of the meaning of potential and receptivity, which would necessitate the influence of another upon Him or His being affected by another.

Allah has turned the address in the verse from His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to the believers as a "shift in address" (التفات). It seems the reason for this is to intimate to them the ruling contained in His saying: وَاللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُرْضُوهُ إِنْ كانُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ (but Allah and His Apostle are worthier that they should please Him, if they are faithful). This ruling is that it is obligatory upon every believer to please Allah and His Messenger, and not to oppose Allah and His Messenger, for in that is a great disgrace—the fire of Hell, abiding therein forever.
