Among the etiquette of monotheism (أدب التوحيد) in the verse is what is found in His saying: أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُرْضُوهُ (worthier that they should please Him) in the use of the singular pronoun, rather than saying "please them both" (يرضوهما), to safeguard His station, the Exalted, from having anyone equated with Him. For such rights, as well as attributes in which others share with Him, the Exalted, in terms of general application—belong to Him, the Exalted, by essence and for Himself, and to others by following or accidentally and from His side—such as the obligation of pleasing, honoring, obedience, and others, and like being characterized by knowledge, life, giving life, causing death, and others.

Similar etiquette has been observed in the Qur'an in many instances where the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله shares certain affairs with others of the community; the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is distinguished from among them and mentioned individually, as in His saying: يَوْمَ لا يُخْزِي اللهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — the day Allah will not let down the Prophet and those who have faith with him (66:8), and His saying: فَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the faithful (48:26), and His saying: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ — The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful [as well] (2:285), and others.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّهُ مَنْ يُحادِدِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَأَنَّ لَهُ نارَ جَهَنَّمَ — Do they not know that whoever opposes Allah and His Apostle, for him there shall be the fire of hell... (9:63). It is said in al-Majma': "Opposition (المحادة) is exceeding the limit through contention; it, along with disagreement, taking a side, and enmity, are synonyms. Its root is 'prevention' (المنع), and muhaddah is what afflicts a person of impetuosity because it prevents him from what is obligatory." He also said: "Disgrace (الخزي) is humiliation and that which one is ashamed of." End quote.

The interrogation in the verse is for "expressing wonder" (التعجيب). The speech is directed toward explaining that He, the Exalted, and His Messenger are worthier of being pleased. Its gist is that they know that opposing Allah and His Messenger, contention, enmity toward Allah and His Messenger, and causing displeasure necessitates eternal fire. If causing the displeasure of Allah and His Messenger is forbidden, then pleasing Him and pleasing His Messenger is obligatory upon whoever believes in Allah and His Messenger.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, narrated from Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: إِنْ تُصِبْكَ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ وَإِنْ تُصِبْكَ مُصِيبَةٌ (If some good should befall you, it upsets them, and if a misfortune befalls you...): "As for the 'good,' it is spoils and well-being; as for the 'misfortune,' it is trial and hardship."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah who said: "The hypocrites who remained behind in Madinah began to report evil news about the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, saying: 'Muhammad and his companions have been exhausted in their journey and have perished.' Then the news of the falsity of their talk reached them—along with the well-being of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his companions—and that upset them. So Allah, the Exalted, revealed: إِنْ تُصِبْكَ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ (If some good should befall you, it upsets them)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Hamzah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنا إِلَّا إِحْدَى الْحُسْنَيَيْنِ (Do you await for us anything except one of the two best outcomes?)' He said: 'Either death in obedience to the Imam or witnessing the appearance of an Imam.' 'وَنَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ (But we await for you)'—despite the hardship we are in—'أَنْ يُصِيبَكُمُ اللهُ بِعَذابٍ مِنْ عِنْدِهِ (that Allah should afflict you with a punishment from Himself).' He said: 'It is metamorphosis (المسخ).' 'أَوْ بِأَيْدِينا (or at our hands)'—which is killing. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said to His Prophet: 'فَتَرَبَّصُوا إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ مُتَرَبِّصُونَ (So await! We too are awaiting with you).'"

I say: This is of the nature of "conceptual application" (الجري) — the application of a verse to a specific instance that fits its general meaning — rather than literal exegesis (التفسير).

In al-Mahasin, with its chain of narration from Yusuf ibn Thabit from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "No deed harms with faith, and no deed benefits with disbelief." Then he said: "Do you not see that Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: وَما مَنَعَهُمْ أَنْ تُقْبَلَ مِنْهُمْ نَفَقاتُهُمْ ـ إِلَّا أَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِاللهِ وَبِرَسُولِهِ (Nothing stops their charities from being accepted except that they have no faith in Allah and His Apostle)?"

I say: This was narrated by al-Ayyashi and al-Qummi from him, as well as al-Kulayni in al-Kafi from him in a detailed hadith. The narration is explained by other verses and narrations: faith, as long as it remains, is not harmed by a sin in terms of necessitating eternal fire; and disbelief, as long as it is disbelief, is not benefited by a good deed.

In al-Majma': Regarding the saying of the Exalted: مُدَّخَلاً (a place to enter), he said: "A tunnel (سربا)," narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Ishaq ibn Ghalib, who said: "Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: 'O Ishaq, how many do you see are the people of this verse: (فَإِنْ أُعْطُوا مِنْها رَضُوا وَإِنْ لَمْ يُعْطَوْا مِنْها إِذا هُمْ يَسْخَطُونَ) (if they are given from them, they are pleased, but if they are not given from them, behold, they are displeased)?' I said: 'They are more than two-thirds of the people.'"

I say: This was narrated by al-Ayyashi in his Tafsir and al-Husayn ibn Sa'id in the Book of Asceticism from Ishaq from him (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bukhari, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "While the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was distributing a portion, Dhu al-Khuwaysirah al-Tamimi came to him and said: 'Be just, O Messenger of Allah!' He replied: 'Woe to you! Who will be just if I am not just?'

Umar ibn al-Khattab said: 'O Messenger of Allah, give me leave to strike his neck.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Leave him, for he has companions whose prayer one of you would look down upon compared to their prayer, and his fasting compared to their fasting. They pass through the religion as an arrow passes through the prey. One looks at its feathers and finds nothing; then one looks at its shaft and sees nothing; then one looks at its socket and sees nothing; then one looks at its head and finds nothing. It has preceded the offal and the blood. Their sign is a black man, one of whose breasts—or he said: his two breasts—is like the breast of a woman, or like a piece of flesh quivering. They will come out at a time of division among the people.' He said: 'So there was revealed concerning them: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَلْمِزُكَ فِي الصَّدَقاتِ (There are some of them who blame you regarding the charities).'"

Abu Sa'id said: "I bear witness that I heard this from the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and I bear witness that when Ali killed them—and I was with him—the man was brought according to the description that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had described."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding the verse: "It was revealed when the charities came and the wealthy came, thinking that the Messenger would distribute them among them. When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله placed them among the poor, they winked at the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and blamed him, saying: 'We are the ones who stand in war, go on expeditions with him, and strengthen his cause; then he gives the charities to these who do not assist him nor avail him anything.' So Allah revealed: وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ رَضُوا ما آتاهُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ ـ وَقالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللهُ سَيُؤْتِينَا اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَرَسُولُهُ ـ إِنَّا إِلَى اللهِ راغِبُونَ (Had they been pleased with what Allah and His Apostle gave them, and said, ‘Allah is sufficient for us. Allah will give us out of His grace, and His Apostle [as well]. Indeed we are suppliants to Allah’).

Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, explained for whom the charities are and upon whom they are obligatory. He said: إِنَّمَا الصَّدَقاتُ لِلْفُقَراءِ وَالْمَساكِينِ ـ وَالْعامِلِينَ عَلَيْها وَالْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَفِي الرِّقابِ وَالْغارِمِينَ ـ وَفِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ فَرِيضَةً مِنَ اللهِ وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (The charities are only for the poor and the needy, and those employed to collect them, and those whose hearts are to be reconciled, and for [the freedom of] the slaves and the debtors, and in the way of Allah, and for the traveller: [this is] an ordinance from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise). Thus, Allah excluded all people from the charities except these eight categories whom He named."

And al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) explained who they are. He said: "The poor (الفقراء) are those who do not ask, yet they have the expenses of their families upon them. The proof that they do not ask is the saying of Allah, the Exalted, in Surah al-Baqarah: لِلْفُقَراءِ الَّذِينَ أُحْصِرُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ضَرْباً فِي الْأَرْضِ يَحْسَبُهُمُ الْجاهِلُ أَغْنِياءَ مِنَ التَّعَفُّفِ تَعْرِفُهُمْ بِسِيماهُمْ لا يَسْئَلُونَ النَّاسَ إِلْحافاً ([The charities are] for the poor who are straitened in the way of Allah, not being able to move about in the land. The unaware consider them wealthy because of their abstinence [from begging]. You shall recognize them by their mark; they do not solicit the people importunately) (2:273).

The needy (المساكين) are the people of chronic illness—the blind, the lame, the lepers—and all categories of the chronically ill among men, women, and children.

Those employed to collect them (العاملين عليها) are the runners and collectors involved in taking, gathering, and guarding them until they deliver them to the one who distributes them.

Those whose hearts are to be reconciled (المؤلفة قلوبهم) are a people who have declared the oneness of Allah but knowledge has not entered their hearts that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah. So the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to win them over and teach them so that they might recognize. Thus, Allah made for them a portion in the charities so that they might recognize and be desirous."

I say: Many narrations have been reported supporting this which he has transmitted as a mursal narration from the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). In some narrations, there is some conflict; for the detail of the narrations in their abundance and the refinement of the matter, one should refer to the collections of hadith and books of jurisprudence.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bukhari, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "Ali ibn Abi Talib sent from Yemen to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله a piece of gold in its ore. He distributed it among four of those whose hearts were to be reconciled: al-Aqra' ibn Habis al-Hanzali, Alqamah ibn Ulathah al-Amiri, Uyaynah ibn Badr al-Fazari, and Zayd al-Khayl al-Ta'i. The Quraysh and the Ansar said: 'Do you distribute among the chiefs of the people of Najd and leave us?' The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'I am only winning them over.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Yahya ibn Abi Kathir, who said: "Those whose hearts were to be reconciled were: from Banu Hashim, Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib; from Banu Umayyah, Abu Sufyan ibn Harb; from Banu Makhzum, al-Harith ibn Hisham and Abd al-Rahman ibn Yarbu'; from Banu Asad, Hakim ibn Hizam; from Banu Amir, Suhayl ibn Amr and Huwaytib ibn Abd al-Uzza; from Banu Jumah, Safwan ibn Umayyah; from Banu Sahm, Adi ibn Qays; from Thaqif, al-Ala' ibn Jariyah—or Harithah; from Banu Fazarah, Uyaynah ibn Hisn; from Banu Tamim, al-Aqra' ibn Habis; from Banu Nasr, Malik ibn Awf; and from Banu Sulaym, al-Abbas ibn Mirdas.

The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله gave each man among them a hundred camels—except Abd al-Rahman ibn Yarbu' and Huwaytib ibn Abd al-Uzza, for he gave each of them fifty."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Those whose hearts were to be reconciled: Abu Sufyan ibn Harb ibn Umayyah; Suhayl ibn Amr, who is from Banu Amir ibn Lu'ayy; Hisham ibn Amr, his brother—the brother of Banu Amir ibn Lu'ayy; Safwan ibn Umayyah ibn Khalaf al-Qurashi, then al-Jumahi; al-Aqra' ibn Habis al-Tamimi, one of the Banu Hazim; Uyaynah ibn Hisn al-Fazari; Malik ibn Awf; and Alqamah ibn Ulathah.

It has reached me that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to give a man among them a hundred camels and their herders, and more than that or less."

I say: These are those whose hearts were to be reconciled whom the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله gave to win over their hearts. The intent is not to restrict "those whose hearts are to be reconciled"—who are one of the eight categories mentioned in the verse—to these specific individuals.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Ibn Ishaq from some of our companions from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "He was asked about a contracted slave (مكاتب) who was unable to fulfill his contract after having paid part of it. He said: 'It is paid from the wealth of charity; indeed, Allah says in His Book: وَفِي الرِّقابِ (and for the slaves).'"

In the same source, from Zurarah, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'A slave committed adultery?' He said: 'He is lashed half the legal punishment (الحد).' I said: 'What if he repeats it?' He said: 'He is struck the same.' I said: 'What if he repeats it?' He said: 'He is not increased beyond half the legal punishment.' I said: 'Is stoning (الرجم) obligatory upon him for any of his actions?' He said: 'Yes, he is killed in the eighth time if he does that eight times.'

I said: 'What is the difference between him and the free man when their action is one?' He said to him: 'Allah had mercy on him such that He would not combine upon him the yoke of slavery and the legal punishment of the free.' Then he said: 'And it is upon the Imam of the Muslims to pay his price to his master from the portion of the slaves (سهم الرقاب).'"

In the same source, from al-Sabbah ibn Siyabah, who said: "Any Muslim who dies and leaves a debt that was not in corruption or extravagance—it is upon the Imam to pay it. If he does not pay it, the sin of that is upon him. Indeed, Allah says: إِنَّمَا الصَّدَقاتُ لِلْفُقَراءِ وَالْمَساكِينِ ـ وَالْعامِلِينَ عَلَيْها وَالْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَفِي الرِّقابِ وَالْغارِمِينَ (The charities are only for the poor and the needy, and those employed to collect them, and those whose hearts are to be reconciled, and for [the freedom of] the slaves and the debtors). So he is among the debtors (الغارمين) and he has a portion with the Imam; if he withholds it, its sin is upon him."

And in the same source, narrated from Muhammad ibn al-Qasri, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: I asked him about charity (الصدقة), and he said: "Distribute it among those whom Allah has specified—but do not give from the share of the debtors (الغارمين) — those burdened by debt — to those who incur debt for the dowries of women, nor to those who call out with the call of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية)." I asked: "And what is the call of the Age of Ignorance?" He replied: "A man saying: 'O tribe of so-and-so!' such that killing breaks out between them—this shall not be paid from the share of the debtors (الغارمين), nor shall it be given to those who are indifferent to what they do with the wealth of others."

And in the same source, from al-Hasan ibn Muhammad, who said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: "A man made a bequest to me to spend 'in the way of Allah' (في سبيل الله)." He said to me: "Spend it on the Hajj." I said: "But he bequeathed it 'in the way'!" He replied: "Spend it on the Hajj, for I know of no way among His ways that is more excellent than the Hajj."

I say: The narrations in this chapter are too numerous to count; we have only cited these as a representative sample (الأنموذج).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمُ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ النَّبِيَّ "Among them are those who trouble the Prophet..." [9:61], Ibn Ishaq, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "Nabtal ibn al-Harith used to come to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and sit with him, listening to him, and then he would convey his speech to the hypocrites. It was he who said to them: 'Muhammad is only an ear (أذن); if anyone tells him something, he believes it.' So Allah sent down regarding him: وَمِنْهُمُ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ النَّبِيَّ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ 'Among them are those who trouble the Prophet and say, "He is an ear."'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the verse, it is stated: "The occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) was that ‘Abdullah ibn Nabtal was a hypocrite who used to sit with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, listening to his words and conveying them to the hypocrites, slandering him. Then Gabriel descended to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: 'O Muhammad, a man from the hypocrites slanders you and conveys your speech to the hypocrites.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله asked: 'Who is he?' He replied: 'The man with the dark face and thick hair on his head, who looks with two eyes like two pots and speaks with the tongue of a devil.'"

The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله summoned him and informed him of this, but he swore that he had not done it. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "I have accepted your word, so do not do it again." He returned to his companions and said: "Indeed, Muhammad is an ear (أذن). Allah informed him that I slander him and convey his news, and he accepted it; then I told him that I neither said nor did anything, and he accepted that too!"

So Allah sent down to His Prophet: وَمِنْهُمُ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ النَّبِيَّ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَّكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ "Among them are those who trouble the Prophet and say, ‘He is an ear.’ Say, ‘An ear of good for you: he believes in Allah and trusts the faithful...’" [9:61]. That is, He believes Allah in what He says to him, and he trusts you in the excuses you offer him, though he does not believe you in his heart; and "trusts the faithful" (يؤمن للمؤمنين) means those who profess faith without inner conviction.

I say: Something similar was narrated in Nahj al-Bayan from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Suddi, who said: "A group of hypocrites gathered, among them Jullas ibn Suwayd ibn Samit, Jahsh ibn Humayr, and Wadi‘ah ibn Thabit. they intended to disparage the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, but some of them forbade others, saying: 'We fear that it will reach Muhammad and he will fall upon you.' Some of them said: 'Indeed, Muhammad is an ear (أذن); we will swear to him and he will believe us.' Then there was revealed: وَمِنْهُمُ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ النَّبِيَّ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ 'Among them are those who trouble the Prophet and say, "He is an ear."'"

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Hammad ibn Sinan, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I intended to entrust a certain person with some goods for trade in Yemen, so I went to Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام and said: 'I wish to entrust so-and-so with goods.' He said to me: 'Do you not know that he drinks wine?' I said: 'I have heard that from the believers (المؤمنين), for they say so.' He said: 'Believe them, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ "...he believes in Allah and trusts the faithful."' He said: 'It means he believes Allah and trusts the believers—because he was compassionate and merciful toward the believers.'"

Ayahs

يَحْذَرُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ أَن تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ تُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۚ قُلِ ٱسْتَهْزِءُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْرِجٌ مَّا تَحْذَرُونَ (٦٤)
The hypocrites are apprehensive lest a surah should be sent down against them, informing them about what is in their hearts. Say, ‘Go on deriding. Allah will indeed bring out what you are apprehensive of.’ (64)
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّمَا كُنَّا نَخُوضُ وَنَلْعَبُ ۚ قُلْ أَبِٱللَّهِ وَءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَرَسُولِهِۦ كُنتُمْ تَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٦٥)
If you question them [regarding their conduct], they will surely say, ‘We were just gossiping and amusing ourselves.’ Say, ‘Were you deriding Allah, His signs, and His apostles? (65)
لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ قَدْ كَفَرْتُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ ۚ إِن نَّعْفُ عَن طَآئِفَةٍ مِّنكُمْ نُعَذِّبْ طَآئِفَةًۢ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (٦٦)
Do not make excuses. You have disbelieved after your faith.’ If We forgive a group among you, We will punish another group, for they have been guilty. (66)
ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتُ بَعْضُهُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ ۚ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَقْبِضُونَ أَيْدِيَهُمْ ۚ نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٦٧)
The hypocrites, men and women, are all alike: they bid what is wrong and forbid what is right; and are tight-fisted. They have forgotten Allah, so He has forgotten them. The hypocrites are indeed the transgressors. (67)
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْكُفَّارَ نَارَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ هِىَ حَسْبُهُمْ ۚ وَلَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٦٨)
Allah has promised the hypocrites, men and women, and the faithless, the Fire of hell, to remain in it [forever]. That suffices them. Allah has cursed them, and there is a lasting punishment for them. (68)
كَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ كَانُوٓا۟ أَشَدَّ مِنكُمْ قُوَّةً وَأَكْثَرَ أَمْوَٰلًا وَأَوْلَـٰدًا فَٱسْتَمْتَعُوا۟ بِخَلَـٰقِهِمْ فَٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِخَلَـٰقِكُمْ كَمَا ٱسْتَمْتَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُم بِخَلَـٰقِهِمْ وَخُضْتُمْ كَٱلَّذِى خَاضُوٓا۟ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٦٩)
[Hypocrites! Your case is] similar to those who were before you, who were more powerful than you and more abounding in wealth and children: they enjoyed their share [of worldly existence]; you too enjoy your share, just like those who were before you enjoyed their share, and you have gossiped [impiously] as they gossiped. They are the ones whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter; and it is they who are the losers. (69)
أَلَمْ يَأْتِهِمْ نَبَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ وَثَمُودَ وَقَوْمِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَأَصْحَـٰبِ مَدْيَنَ وَٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَـٰتِ ۚ أَتَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ۖ فَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٧٠)
Has there not come to them the account of those who were before them—the people of Noah, ‘Ad, and Tham£d, and the people of Abraham, the inhabitants of Midian, and the towns that were overturned? Their apostles brought them manifest proofs. So it was not Allah who wronged them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves. (70)
وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَيُطِيعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَيَرْحَمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٧١)
But the faithful, men and women, are comrades of one another: they bid what is right and forbid what is wrong and maintain the prayer, give the zakat, and obey Allah and His Apostle. It is they to whom Allah will soon grant His mercy. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (71)
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا وَمَسَـٰكِنَ طَيِّبَةً فِى جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ۚ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٧٢)
Allah has promised the faithful, men and women, gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and good dwellings in the Gardens of Eden. Yet Allah’s pleasure is greater [than all these]; that is the great success. (72)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ جَـٰهِدِ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٧٣)
O Prophet! Wage jihad against the faithless and the hypocrites, and be severe with them. Their refuge shall be hell, and it is an evil destination. (73)
يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ مَا قَالُوا۟ وَلَقَدْ قَالُوا۟ كَلِمَةَ ٱلْكُفْرِ وَكَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِسْلَـٰمِهِمْ وَهَمُّوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يَنَالُوا۟ ۚ وَمَا نَقَمُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ أَنْ أَغْنَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۚ فَإِن يَتُوبُوا۟ يَكُ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَتَوَلَّوْا۟ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَمَا لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٧٤)
They swear by Allah that they did not say it. But they certainly did utter the word of unfaith and renounced faith after their Islam. They contemplated what they could not achieve, and they were vindictive only because Allah and His Apostle had enriched them out of His grace. Yet if they repent, it will be better for them; but if they turn away, Allah shall punish them with a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter, and they shall not find on the earth any guardian or helper. (74)

(Commentary)

The verses mention another affair among the affairs of the hypocrites and expose another of their shameful deeds (سوأة) — an ignoble act or hidden vice — which they had concealed through hypocrisy (النفاق) — the outward profession of faith while concealing disbelief. They were wary that it might be exposed and that a Surah would be revealed recounting what they had intended therein.

The verses indicate that they were a group of significant number, as evidenced by His saying: "If We forgive a party of you, We will punish another party." It also shows that they had some connection and coordination with another group of hypocrites, as in His saying: "The hypocrites, men and women, are [all] of a piece" (التوبة: ٦٧). Furthermore, it indicates that they were outwardly adhering to Islam and faith until that day, and they only committed hypocrisy on that occasion—meaning they uttered the word of disbelief (كلمة الكفر) among themselves and kept it secret—as in His saying: "You have disbelieved after your belief."

It also shows that they conspired (تواطئوا) — agreed together in secret for a malicious purpose — regarding a matter they had plotted among themselves. At that point, they manifested the word of disbelief and intended a grave matter, but Allah intervened between them and their objective. Thus, their effort failed and their plot (كيدهم) — clandestine stratagem intended to cause harm — had no effect, as in His saying: "They have surely uttered the word of unfaith and disbelieved after their Islam, and they intended what they did not attain."

Furthermore, it appears that some signs and circumstantial evidence (قرائن) — indicators that lead to a logical conclusion — of what they intended became manifest. When they were questioned about it, they offered an excuse that was equally ugly and heinous, as in His saying: "And if you ask them, they will surely say, 'We were only idling and playing.'" The verses following these, in a cohesive and harmonious context, indicate that this incident—whatever it may have been—occurred after the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله set out for the expedition of Tabuk and before he returned to Medina, as evidenced by His saying: "So if Allah brings you back to a party of them" (التوبة: ٨٣), and His saying: "They will swear to you by Allah when you return to them" (التوبة: ٩٥).

In summary, the verses indicate that a group of those who went out with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله conspired to plot against him. At that time, they secretly uttered words of disbelief, thereby disbelieving after their Islam. Then they intended to carry out what they had agreed upon—an assassination or something similar—but Allah nullified their plot and disgraced them, exposing them. When they were questioned, they said: "We were only idling and playing." Thus, Allah rebuked them through the tongue of His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, stating that it was a mockery of Allah, His signs, and His Messenger. He threatened them with punishment if they did not repent and commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to wage jihad (يجاهدهم) — to strive with utmost effort in the cause of Allah — against them and against the disbelievers.

The verses—as you can see—apply more clearly to the "Hadith of al-Aqaba" than to any other accounts contained in other narrations regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) — the specific historical circumstances under which a verse was revealed. We shall cite most of them in the upcoming "Tradition Research," Allah the Exalted willing.

His saying, the Exalted: يَحْذَرُ الْمُنافِقُونَ أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ تُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ — The hypocrites fear lest a surah should be sent down against them, informing them of what is in their hearts. (التوبة: ٦٤) to the end of the verse. The hypocrites observed that most of what they kept secret regarding their hypocrisy—the words of disbelief they whispered to one another, and the various forms of insinuation, slandering, and mockery—was not hidden from the Messenger. It was being recited to the people in verses of the Quran which the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله stated were from the revelation (وحي) — the divine communication from Allah to His prophets — of Allah. Inevitably, they did not believe it was revelation brought down by the Trustworthy Spirit (الروح الأمين) upon the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. They presumed that it was something the believers spied out and reported to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who would then present it to them in the form of a heavenly book sent down to them. Despite this, they feared the exposure of their hypocrisy and the emergence of what they hid in their wicked inner selves (سرائرهم), because the authority (السلطنة) and dominance belonged to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله over them; he would execute among them whatever he commanded and judged.

Thus, they were wary of the revelation of a Surah that would expose the disbelief they harbored and the plot they intended—to subvert matters against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and target him with that which would nullify the success of his mission and the completion of his word. So Allah commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to inform them that Allah is Knower of what is in their breasts and will bring forth what they fear will emerge through the revelation of a Surah from Him; that is, He informs them that Allah is the sender of a Surah with this description.

Through this, the meaning of the verse becomes clear. His saying: يَحْذَرُ الْمُنافِقُونَ أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ — The hypocrites fear lest a surah should be sent down against them — is an address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the speech is directed toward him. He knows, through Allah's teaching, that this speech which he recites to the people is divine speech and a Quran revealed from Him. Thus, He, the Glorified, describes the speech that the hypocrites fear with the description it holds for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: that it is a Surah revealed by Allah upon the people, including the hypocrites—not according to the view of the hypocrites, who saw it as human speech claimed to be the word of Allah.

They were wary that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would recite to them and to the people a speech with this actual attribute: a Surah revealed "upon them" (عَلَيْهِمْ) in the sense that its content was directed toward them and aimed at them. This revealed Surah would inform them of what was in their hearts, so that what they were concealing of their disbelief and evil intentions would be exposed to the people and spread among them. This exposure is, in reality, what they feared from the revelation of the Surah.

His saying: قُلِ اسْتَهْزِؤُا إِنَّ اللهَ مُخْرِجٌ ما تَحْذَرُونَ — Say, ‘Go on mocking! Allah will surely bring to light what you fear.’ (التوبة: ٦٤). It seems the intended meaning of "mockery" (الاستهزاء) is their hypocrisy and the effects associated with it. Allah named their hypocrisy "mockery," recounting their own words when He said: "And when they meet those who believe, they say, 'We believe'; but when they are alone with their satans, they say, 'Indeed we are with you; we were only mocking'" (البقرة: ١٤). Thus, the intended meaning of mockery is the concealment of what they fear will be exposed. The command is one of "defiance" (تعجيزي) — a command intended to demonstrate the subject's inability to thwart the divine will — meaning: continue in your hypocrisy and your concealment of what you fear will emerge from you into the sight and hearing of the people, for Allah will bring that forth, strip the veil from its face, and manifest what you have hidden in your breasts.

Although the beginning of the verse mentions that they fear the revelation of such-and-such a Surah, they only feared it because of the information it contained, which they feared the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would come to know and which would become clear to the people. This is what the end of the verse mentions they fear. The speech is as if it were said: "The hypocrites fear the revelation of a Surah; say: indeed Allah is the sender of it," or: "The hypocrites fear the exposure of their inner reality and what is in their hearts; say: go on mocking, for Allah will expose that and inform of what is in your hearts."

From what has preceded, the objections raised against the verse are dismissed. First: that the hypocrites, due to their actual disbelief, did not see the Quran as speech revealed from Allah, so how can it be correct to say: "Do they fear that a Surah will be revealed against them?"

Second: Since they were not believers in reality, how can it be correct to say that a Quranic Surah was revealed "upon them" (عَلَيْهِمْ), when a Surah is only revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله or to the believers?

Third: Their fear (حذر) of the revelation of a Surah is a serious internal state within them, which is inconsistent with it being "mockery."

Fourth: The beginning of the verse mentions that they fear a Surah will be revealed, while the end says: "Allah will bring to light what you fear," which is in the sense of saying: "Allah will bring forth a Surah" or "bring forth the revelation of a Surah."

The first objection has been answered by some saying that His saying: يَحْذَرُ الْمُنافِقُونَ — The hypocrites fear — is an "imperative" (إنشاء) in the form of a "report" (خبر), meaning: "Let the hypocrites fear that a Surah will be revealed against them..."

This is weak, as there is no evidence for it at all, and the end of the verse does not harmonize with it. There is no meaning in saying: "Let the hypocrites fear such-and-such; say: go on mocking, for Allah will bring forth what you fear"—meaning what you ought to fear. This is obvious.

It has also been answered that they only manifested fear as a form of mockery, not out of serious reality. The problem with this is that it implies they were confident that the news in their hearts and the disbelief and wickedness they concealed had no way of being exposed or clarified, and that no one had a way to know of it. This is belied by many verses in the Holy Quran that recount the disbelief and wickedness they had resolved in their hearts and the deception and plotting they intended, such as the verses in Surah al-Baqarah, Surah al-Munafiqun, and others. Since they witnessed the exposure of their news and the contents of their hearts time after time, there is no meaning to them being confident that it would not be uncovered at all, nor to their manifesting fear as mockery rather than seriousness. Indeed, the Exalted said: "They take every shout to be against them" (المنافقون: ٤).

It has also been answered that most of the hypocrites were in doubt (شك) regarding the truth of the prophetic mission without being certain of its falsehood. These people allowed for the possibility of a Surah being revealed that would inform them of what was in their hearts as a rational possibility. This fear and apprehension, as they mentioned, is a natural effect of doubt and suspicion. If they were certain of the Messenger's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله falsehood, this fear would not have crossed their minds; and if they were certain of his truth, there would be no place for this fear and wariness because their hearts would be tranquil with faith.

This answer—which the majority of commentators have relied upon—though it appears to have some merit, only resolves the core of the objection if the expression in the verse were something like: "The hypocrites fear (يخاف) that a Surah will be revealed against them." Thus, they formulated the answer on the basis that "fear" (الخوف) suits doubt rather than certainty.

However, the verse expresses their state with the word "wariness" (الحذر), and reports that they "are wary" (يَحْذَرُونَ) that a Surah will be revealed... and wariness (الحذر) contains something of the meaning of "precaution" (الاحتراز) and "guarding" (الاتقاء). This is not complete except by resorting to means and methods that protect the one who is wary from what he is wary of and guards against, and shields him from an evil coming toward him from the direction of what he fears.

If it were mere doubt without witnessing any effects or being struck by something they were guarding against, then precaution and guarding would not be correct. Thus, their wariness testifies that they feared that what happened to them before—through the verses of al-Baqarah and others—would happen to them this time as well. This is the reason for their wariness, rather than mere doubt and suspicion. Therefore, the reliable answer is what we provided first.

The second objection has been answered by saying that "upon" (عَلَى) in His saying: أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ — should be sent down against them — is in the sense of "concerning" (فِي), as in His saying: "And they followed what the satans recited concerning (عَلَى) the kingdom of Solomon" (البقرة: ١٠٢). The meaning would be: the hypocrites fear that a Surah will be revealed "concerning them"—that is, regarding their affair and the clarification of their state—which would expose what is in their consciences.

The problem with this is that it would be fine were it not for His saying afterward: تُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ — informing them of what is in their hearts — as we shall clarify.

It has also been answered that the pronoun in His saying: عَلَيْهِمْ — against them — refers to the believers, not the hypocrites. The meaning would be: the hypocrites fear that a Surah will be revealed "upon the believers" that informs the hypocrites of what is in the hearts of the hypocrites, or informs the believers of what is in the hearts of the hypocrites.

This was rejected because it necessitates the "disconnection of pronouns" (تفكيك الضمائر) — the shifting of referents for pronouns within a single context. This was countered by saying that the disconnection of pronouns is not forbidden, nor is it contrary to eloquence (البلاغة) — the art of effective and persuasive speaking or writing — unless the meaning becomes incomprehensible. Some supported this answer by saying there is no disconnection of pronouns here, for it was previously mentioned that the hypocrites swear to the believers to please them, then Allah rebuked them by saying that Allah and His Messenger are more worthy of being pleased if they are believers. Thus, it was explained here by way of "resumption" (استئناف) — the beginning of a new sentence or thought that is grammatically independent of the preceding one — that they fear a Surah will be revealed upon the believers informing them of what is in their hearts, thereby nullifying their trust in them. Thus, the pronoun was returned to the believers because the context of the speech is about them, so there is no effect of disconnection.

The problem with this is that it is clear and beyond doubt that the subject of the speech in these verses, and many verses connected to them before and after, is the hypocrites. The context is that of addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and no one else. The address to the believers in His saying: "They swear by Allah to you (لكم) to please you" was a "rhetorical shift" (التفات) — a sudden change in person, number, or addressee for rhetorical effect — to draw attention to a specific purpose we alluded to, then the speech returned to its original context of addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by changing their address to his address. Thus, there is no meaning to saying that the context of the speech is about the believers.

If the context were as he mentioned, the proper speech would have been: "lest a Surah be revealed upon you (عليكم) informing you of what is in their hearts." What then is the meaning of turning to the third-person pronoun (الغيبة), when no mention of them with this description preceded in the earlier speech?

Furthermore, the claim that the verse—يَحْذَرُ الْمُنافِقُونَ—is an explanation by way of resumption for the reason they swear to the believers to please them, removes this group of verses from the independence of their original purpose which we discussed at the beginning. This would disrupt the connection and correlation apparent among the segments of the verses.

Thus, the verse—يَحْذَرُ الْمُنافِقُونَ etc.—is not an explanation for the reason of their aforementioned swearing, but a resumption driven by another purpose to which the total of the eleven verses points.

In short, the verses preceding this verse are devoid of any mention of the believers that would cause the mind to turn toward them when encountering a pronoun that could refer to them. This is the aforementioned disconnection, and it is a forbidden disconnection because it causes an ambiguity in the explanation that contradicts its eloquence.

The truth is that the pronoun in His saying: أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ — should be sent down against them — refers to the hypocrites—as previously indicated. There is no harm in calling the revelation of a Surah to clarify their state, mention their faults, and rebuke them for their hypocrisy a revelation of the Surah "upon them," while they are among the group of believers, not distinguished from them. This is similar to the expression used regarding the believers where He said: "And remember Allah’s blessing upon you, and what He has sent down to you (أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ) of the Book and wisdom, to exhort you with it" (البقرة: ٢٣١).

He, the Glorified, used a similar expression regarding the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — those to whom a scripture was revealed before Islam, specifically Jews and Christians — where He said: "The People of the Book ask you to bring down (تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ) a Book to them from the sky" (النساء: ١٥٣). And regarding the polytheists (المشركين) — those who associate partners with Allah — where He recounted their saying: "and we will not believe your ascending until you bring down (تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا) to us a book that we may read" (الإسراء: ٩٣). The attribution of the Quran's revelation "upon" the hypocrites while they are among the believers is no more remote than its attribution to the polytheists and the People of the Book. The words "descending" (النزول), "sending down" (الإنزال), and "revealing" (التنزيل) can be made transitive with "to" (إلى) with the sense of "ending at," and with "upon" (عَلَى) with the sense of "superiority" and coming (الإتيان) from above. Making them transitive with either is frequent in Quranic expressions. The meaning of the Book's revelation "to" a people or "upon" a people is its addressing their affairs and its clarification of what benefits them in their worldly life and their hereafter.

The third objection has been answered by saying that the saying of the Exalted: قُلِ اسْتَهْزِؤُا — Say, ‘Go on mocking!’ — is evidence that they were mocking through their wariness, and it was not a matter of serious wariness at all.

The problem with this is that the many verses revealed in Surah al-Baqarah, al-Nisa, and others—all of which were revealed before these verses—which brought out many of the hidden things of their hearts and exposed their secrets, indicate that this wariness was from them in reality, without mockery or sarcasm.

Furthermore, He described them in Surah al-Munafiqun with the like of His saying: "They take every shout to be against them" (المنافقون: ٤). And He said in a parable He struck for them and about them: "they put their fingers into their ears against the thunderclaps, in dread of death" (البقرة: ١٩), and it was mentioned in the following verse.

The truth is that their mockery is simply their hypocrisy and their outward saying of the opposite of what is in their hearts, as supported by the saying of the Exalted: "And when they meet those who believe, they say, 'We believe'; but when they are alone with their satans, they say, 'Indeed we are with you; we were only mocking'" (البقرة: ١٤).

The answer to the fourth objection is that the thing they were wary of in reality was the exposure of their hypocrisy and the uncovering of what was in their hearts. They only feared the revelation of the Surah for that reason. Thus, the object of wariness mentioned in the beginning of the verse and that in the end of the verse are one and the same. The meaning of His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ مُخْرِجٌ ما تَحْذَرُونَ — Allah will surely bring to light what you fear — is that He will manifest what you have hidden of hypocrisy and inform of what is in your hearts.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّما كُنَّا نَخُوضُ وَنَلْعَبُ قُلْ أَبِاللهِ وَآياتِهِ وَرَسُولِهِ كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — If you ask them, they will surely say, ‘We were only idling and playing.’ Say, ‘Were you mocking Allah, His signs, and His Apostle?’ (التوبة: ٦٥). Idling (الخوض) — according to al-Majma' — is the entry of the foot into something fluid like water or mud, then it became common until it was used for other things.

Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: Idling (الخوض) is the entering into water and passing through it; it is used metaphorically for affairs, and most of what has appeared in the Quran has appeared regarding that which is blameworthy to enter into. [End of quote].

Allah, the Glorified, did not mention the object of the question—what is the thing being asked about, which if the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله were to ask about it, he would be asking about? However, His saying: لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّما كُنَّا نَخُوضُ وَنَلْعَبُ — they will surely say, ‘We were only idling and playing’ — with its context beginning with "only" (إنما), indicates that it was an act originating from them that had some connection to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. It was a visible matter that caused suspicion toward them, and they were unable to excuse themselves from it after it became clear and was exposed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله except by saying it was only idling and playing from them, and they intended nothing else by it.

The idling and playing with which they excused themselves are among the evil deeds that people do not admit to in their normal state, especially the believers and others who outwardly profess faith, and especially if it concerns a matter relating to Allah and His Messenger. However, they found no description to describe their act to remove it from its apparent meaning except by labeling it as idling and playing.

Therefore, He commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to rebuke them for the excuse they offered, saying: قُلْ أَبِاللهِ وَآياتِهِ وَرَسُولِهِ كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — Say, ‘Were you mocking Allah, His signs, and His Apostle?’ Then He explained their act at the end of the verses by His saying: "They swear by Allah that they did not say [it], but they have surely uttered the word of unfaith and disbelieved after their Islam, and they intended what they did not attain" (التوبة: ٧٤).

It is gathered from the sum of these clues that the hypocrites had intended evil toward the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, such as an assassination or surprising him with that which would destroy him. They proceeded with what they intended and spoke some foul words at that time, but they failed in what they attempted against him, and the evil was repelled from him. The arrow did not hit its target. When their effort failed and their matter became clear, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله asked them about that and what they had attempted; they excused themselves by saying they were idling and playing. So the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله rebuked them by saying: "Were you mocking Allah, His signs, and His Apostle?" and Allah, the Glorified, rejected the excuse they offered and clarified the reality of what they intended by that.

In summary, the meaning of the verse is: I swear, if you ask them about their act that was witnessed from them—what did they intend by it?—when its appearance was that they intended a matter against you, they will surely say: "There was no evil intention, nor that which you thought, thus having a bad opinion of us. We were only idling and playing, like the idling of riders on the road, not in a serious manner but as play."

This is an excuse from them involving mockery of Allah, His signs, and His Messenger, for they admit that they did to you what they did as idling and playing; thus they mocked Allah and His Messenger. So say: "Were you mocking Allah, His signs, and His Apostle?"—meaning, do you excuse your evil act with another evil, which is mockery of Allah, His signs, and His Messenger, which is disbelief?

It is not far-fetched that the primary purpose was to state that it was mockery of the Messenger, and Allah and His signs were mentioned to indicate the meaning of mocking the Messenger: that since he was among the signs of Allah, mocking him was mocking the signs of Allah, and mocking the signs of Allah is mocking Allah the Great. Thus, mocking the Messenger of Allah is mocking Allah, His signs, and His Messenger.

His saying, the Exalted: لا تَعْتَذِرُوا قَدْ كَفَرْتُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ إِنْ نَعْفُ عَنْ طائِفَةٍ مِنْكُمْ نُعَذِّبْ طائِفَةً — Do not make excuses. You have disbelieved after your belief. If We forgive a party of you, We will punish another party. (التوبة: ٦٦). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: Circling (الطوف) is walking around a thing, and from it is "the circumambulator" (الطائف) for the one who goes around houses as a guard—until he said—and a party (الطائفة) of people is a group of them, and of a thing, it is a piece of it.

Regarding the saying of the Exalted: "Why should not a party (طائفة) from every group of them go forth to acquire a deep understanding of religion?" (التوبة: ١٢٢), some said: it may apply to one person and more. Based on this is His saying: "And if two parties (طائفتان) of the faithful..." (الحجرات: ٩), and "When two parties (طائفتان) of you were about to..." (آل عمران: ١٢٢).

The word "party" (الطائفة), if intended as a plural, is the plural of "one who circumambulates" (طائف). "And if intended as a singular, it is correct for it to be a plural used to refer to the singular, and it is correct for it to be like rawiyah (narrator) and allamah (great scholar) and the like." [End of quote].

Some have deemed it an error to say that "party" (الطائفة) can apply to one or two people just as it applies to three or more. He exaggerated in this until he considered it a mistake, but he has no evidence for what he mentioned. The root of the word does not necessitate a specific number, and its application to a piece of a thing supports its use for the singular.

His saying: لا تَعْتَذِرُوا قَدْ كَفَرْتُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ — Do not make excuses. You have disbelieved after your belief — is a prohibition against making excuses on the grounds that it is futile, as indicated by His saying: "You have disbelieved after your belief," for no benefit follows from making excuses after the judgment of their disbelief after their belief.

The intended meaning of "their belief" is the outward faith they used to profess, not the reality of faith (حقيقة الإيمان) — the true internal conviction and submission — which is from the divine guidance that is never followed by misguidance. This is supported by the saying of the Exalted at the end of these verses: "but they have surely uttered the word of unfaith and disbelieved after their Islam," thus replacing "belief" with "Islam," which is the outward profession of the two testimonies (الشهادتين).

It can be said: among the levels of faith is that which is a weak conviction and submission that is not immune to vanishing, like the faith of those in whose hearts is a sickness. Allah counted them among the believers and mentioned them with the hypocrites for their safety, and there is nothing to prevent them from being stripped of this faith.

How could it be otherwise? Allah has stripped faith from those whose faith was more firmly rooted than theirs, as He recounts in His saying: "Relate to them the story of him to whom We gave Our signs, but he stepped out of them, and Satan followed him, and he became one of the perverse. Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his [base] desire" (الأعراف: ١٧٥-١٧٦).

He also said: "Indeed those who believe, then disbelieve, then believe, then disbelieve, and then increase in unfaith..." (النساء: ١٣٧). The Holy Quran has frequently mentioned disbelief after belief, so there is no barrier to the vanishing of heart-felt conviction before its firm rooting, and it is still a conviction.

Yes, the stable faith (الإيمان المستقر) and the firmly rooted conviction (الاعتقاد الراسخ) have no way for vanishing to occur to them. The Exalted said: "Whomever Allah guides, he is the rightly guided" (الأعراف: ١٧٨), and He said: "Indeed Allah does not guide those who mislead" (النحل: ٣٧).

His saying: إِنْ نَعْفُ عَنْ طائِفَةٍ مِنْكُمْ نُعَذِّبْ طائِفَةً — If We forgive a party of you, We will punish another party — indicates that these hypocrites mentioned in the verses were numerous and many, and that the word of punishment had fallen upon them; they must be punished. Thus, if a divine forgiveness (عفو) — the waiving of punishment — were to encompass some of them for a benefit in that, the punishment would fall upon the rest. This is the meaning of the sentence: "If We forgive a party of you, We will punish another party," according to what is understood from its structure and context.

In other words, the link of necessity (اللزوم) between the condition (الشرط) and the consequence (الجزاء) by the consequence following and branching from the condition is only secondary. Its root is the consequence following here upon a matter related to the condition, which is that punishment was mandatory for the group of them; so if some of them are forgiven, the rest are designated for it without fail.

From what we have presented, the following has become clear: First, the reason for the consequence نُعَذِّبْ طائِفَةً — We will punish another party — following the condition إِنْ نَعْفُ عَنْ طائِفَةٍ — If We forgive a party — and the objection raised by some against the verse—that there is no necessary link between forgiving some and punishing others, so what is the meaning of the condition?—is dismissed.

The answer: the necessity is originally between the obligation of the punishment's descent upon the group and its descent upon some of them, then it was transferred to what is between the forgiveness of some and its descent upon others, as we established.

Second: the intended meaning of "forgiveness" (العفو) is the withholding of punishment for a benefit among the benefits of religion, not forgiveness in the sense of "pardon" (المغفرة) based on repentance (التوبة). For there is no apparent reason for saying: "If We pardon a party of you for their repentance, We will punish a party for their crime," especially since if they all repented, they would certainly not be punished.

Allah has invited them all to repent where He said at the end of the verses: "So if they repent, it will be better for them; and if they turn away, Allah will punish them with a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter" (التوبة: ٧٤).

Third: the forgiveness in the verse, and indeed the punishment mentioned in it, is forgiveness from worldly punishment and its withholding, and the same is said for the punishment. For forgiveness from the punishment of the Hereafter, as stipulated by the Quranic verses, only occurs due to repentance or intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah. Neither of them is realized in our present case. As for repentance, it has become clear that it is not intended in the verse. As for intercession, it is established by the verses of intercession that intercession is only attained in the Hereafter by a believer whose faith is pleasing, and we have completed the research on this in the first volume of the book.

Fourth: there is no barrier to the verse—meaning His saying: لا تَعْتَذِرُوا قَدْ كَفَرْتُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ إِنْ نَعْفُ عَنْ طائِفَةٍ —being a continuation of the speech of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. For the intended meaning of forgiveness and punishment is worldly punishment through political authority and its withholding, and there is no barrier to attributing them to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

However, the appearance of the following verses is that it is from the speech of Allah, the Glorified, addressing the hypocrites. Thus, it would be a rhetorical shift from addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to addressing them. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to manifest the perfection of anger and the intensity of wrath at their deed, as if the Messenger's notification and informing of the message were not sufficient; so He confronted them Himself and addressed them in His person, threatening them with a punishment that is occurring, which cannot be repelled and from which there is no escape.

His saying, the Exalted: الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالْمُنافِقاتُ بَعْضُهُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ — The hypocrites, men and women, are [all] of a piece (التوبة: ٦٧) to the end of the two verses. They mentioned that it is a resumption that addresses the state of the hypocrites in general by mentioning their general, collective attributes and defining them by them, and what Allah will recompense them with in the end of their affair. Then it addresses the state of the believers in general and defines them by their collective attributes and mentions what Allah informs them of by way of contrast to complete the division. Evidence for this comprehensive treatment is the mention of the recompense of the disbelievers along with the hypocrites in His saying: "Allah has promised the hypocrites, men and women, and the faithless..." (التوبة: ٦٨).

It appears that the verse is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for His saying in the previous verse: "If We forgive a party of you, We will punish another party," and the context of addressing the hypocrites continues and has not yet been interrupted.

The previous verse, since it indicated that He, the Exalted, does not leave the hypocrites until He punishes them for their crimes—so if He leaves some of them for a wisdom and benefit, He seizes others of them with punishment—there was a presumption that a question might be asked: "What is the reason for seizing some if others are left? Is it not like seizing a neighbor for the crime of a neighbor?" So it was answered by clarifying the cause: that the hypocrites are all "of a piece" due to their sharing in wicked attributes and deeds, and their sharing in the recompense of their deeds and the end of their state.

Perhaps He mentioned the female hypocrites with the male hypocrites, despite no prior mention of them, to indicate the perfection of union and agreement among them in their psyche, and to be an allusion that among women there are also influential parts in this corrupt and corrupting hypocritical society.

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: one should not find it strange to seize some hypocrites if others are left, because the hypocrites, men and women, are governed by a kind of psychological unity that unifies their multiplicity, so they refer back to one another. Thus, He makes them partners in attributes, deeds, and what they are recompensed with by a promise from Allah the Exalted.

So they bid what is wrong (المنكر) — that which is rejected by divine law and sound reason — and forbid what is right (المعروف) — that which is recognized as good by divine law and sound reason — and they withhold from spending in the way of Allah. In other words, they forgot Allah the Exalted by turning away from His remembrance because they are "transgressors" (فاسقون) — those who have stepped out of the bounds of obedience to Allah — having exited the garb of servitude. So Allah forgot them and did not reward them with what He rewards His servants who remember the station of their Lord.

Then He mentioned what He promised them for that, saying: وَعَدَ اللهُ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَالْمُنافِقاتِ وَالْكُفَّارَ — Allah has promised the hypocrites, men and women, and the faithless — and He joined the disbelievers with them because they are all alike — نارَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها هِيَ حَسْبُهُمْ — the fire of hell, to remain in it [forever]. That suffices them — as a recompense, it does not exceed them to anything else — وَلَعَنَهُمُ اللهُ — and Allah has cursed them — and distanced them — وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ — and for them is a lasting punishment — fixed, which will never depart from them.

Through this, it has become clear that the saying of the Exalted: نَسُوا اللهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ — They forgot Allah, so He forgot them — is an explanation for what preceded it of His saying: "They bid what is wrong and forbid what is right and keep their hands shut."

It follows from this that bidding what is right, forbidding what is wrong, and spending in the way of Allah are forms of "remembrance" (الذكر).

His saying, the Exalted: كَالَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ كانُوا أَشَدَّ مِنْكُمْ قُوَّةً وَأَكْثَرَ أَمْوالاً وَأَوْلاداً فَاسْتَمْتَعُوا بِخَلاقِهِمْ — [You are] like those who were before you: they were stronger than you in power and more affluent in possessions and children. They enjoyed their share... (التوبة: ٦٩) etc. Al-Raghib said: Share (الخلاق) is what a human acquires of virtue through his character (خلقه). The Exalted said: "and he shall have no share (خلاق) in the Hereafter" (البقرة: ١٠٢). [End of quote]. Others explained it as a general portion or lot.

The verse is a continuation of the address to the hypocrites which is in His saying: "Do not make excuses. You have disbelieved after your belief," in a single, connected context. In the verse is a comparison of the state of the hypocrites to the state of those who were before them of the disbelievers and hypocrites, and a measuring of them against them to testify thereby to what was said: that the hypocrites, men and women, are of a piece, and that they and the disbelievers all possess a single nature in turning away from the remembrance of Allah and turning toward enjoying what they were given of worldly goods—wealth and children—and idling regarding the signs of Allah, then in the nullification (حبط) — the loss of spiritual reward for deeds due to disbelief or subsequent sin — of their deeds in this world and the Hereafter, and in loss (الخسران).

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: You are like those before you; they had power, wealth, and children, indeed stronger and more numerous in that than you. They enjoyed their portion, and from this similarity it followed that you enjoyed as they enjoyed and you idled as they idled. Those—their deeds were nullified in this world and the Hereafter, and those are the losers. You also are like them in nullification and loss; therefore, He promised you the eternal Fire and cursed you.

The mention of the power of those before them being stronger and their wealth and children being more numerous is an allusion that they did not frustrate Allah thereby, nor did that repel from them the calamity of nullification and loss. So how will it be for you, when you are weaker in power and less in wealth and children?

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَأْتِهِمْ نَبَأُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعادٍ وَثَمُودَ وَقَوْمِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَأَصْحابِ مَدْيَنَ وَالْمُؤْتَفِكاتِ — Has not the news of those who were before them reached them: the people of Noah, ‘Ad and Thamud, the people of Abraham, the inhabitants of Midian, and the overturned towns? (التوبة: ٧٠). This verse is a return to the first context, which is the context of addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while assuming the absence of the hypocrites, and a reminder to them of what the Quran has recounted of the stories of past nations.

There were the people of Noah, whom Allah the Glorified encompassed with the flood; and 'Ad, who were the people of Hud, whom He destroyed with a cold, violent wind; and Thamud, who were the people of Salih, whom He punished with the earthquake; and the people of Abraham, whose king Nimrod He destroyed and from whom He stripped the blessing; and the overturned towns (المؤتفكات), which are the cities turned upside down—from i'tafakat al-ard if the land is overturned—the cities of the people of Lot, whose upper part He made their lower part.

His saying: أَتَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ — Their apostles brought them manifest proofs — meaning with clear signs, arguments, and proofs. This is a summary explanation of their news: that is, their news was that their messengers came to them with clear signs, but they denied them, so their affair ended in destruction. It was not the way of the Divine Sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the established and unchangeable laws of Allah governing the universe and human societies — to wrong them, because He clarified for them truth and falsehood, and distinguished right from wrong, and guidance from misguidance. But those peoples and nations were themselves wrongdoing by enjoying the portion of this world, idling regarding the signs of Allah, and denying His messengers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ — But the faithful, men and women, are allies of one another (التوبة: ٧١) to the end of the verse. Then Allah, the Glorified, described the state of the believers in general, in parallel to what He described of the hypocrites, saying: وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ — But the faithful, men and women, are allies of one another — to indicate thereby that despite their multiplicity and separation in terms of number and being male and female, they possess a single, harmonious being in which there is no fragmentation. Therefore, some of them take charge of the affairs of others and manage them.

Therefore, some of them bid others to what is right and some forbid others from what is wrong. For the "guardianship" (ولاية) — the authority, protection, and support — of some of the society over others is a guardianship that permeates all parts. Included in their undertaking is bidding what is right and forbidding what is wrong among themselves.

Then He described them by His saying: وَيُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ — they maintain the prayer and give the zakat — and these are the two firm pillars in the Sharia (الشريعة) — the divine law of Islam. Prayer is the pillar of acts of worship (العبادات), which are the link between Allah and His creation; and zakat is in transactions (المعاملات), which are the link between the people themselves.

Then He described them by His saying: وَيُطِيعُونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ — and obey Allah and His Apostle. He gathered in the obedience to Allah all the divine legal rulings, and gathered in the obedience to His Messenger all the "governance rulings" (الأحكام الولائية) which his Messenger issues in managing the affairs of the nation and rectifying their conditions, such as his commands in military expeditions, his judgments in cases, the execution of legal punishments (الحدود), and others.

Furthermore, obedience to the laws of Allah revealed from heaven is, from another perspective, enfolded within obedience to the Messenger, for the Messenger is the one who proclaims the truth and stands to call toward the principles of religion (أصول الدين) and its branches (فروعه).

His saying: أُولئِكَ سَيَرْحَمُهُمُ اللهُ — It is they whom Allah will soon have mercy upon — is a report of what is in the Divine Decree (القضاء الإلهي) regarding the encompassing of divine mercy for these people described by what was mentioned. In this sentence was a parallel to what was recounted regarding the hypocrites in the saying of the Exalted: "They forgot Allah, so He forgot them." It also appears that His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ — Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise — is the reason for what was mentioned of mercy. There is no barrier to His mercy due to His might, nor is there any disorder, weakness, or randomness in His wisdom.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَدَ اللهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ — Allah has promised the faithful, men and women, gardens with conduits running amidst them (التوبة: ٧٢) to the end of the verse. Eden (عدن) is a verbal noun meaning "residence" and "stability." It is said: adana bi-l-makan, meaning he resided in the place and settled there. From it is "the mine" (المعدن) for the earth in which gems and metallic minerals settle. Based on this, the meaning of "Gardens of Eden" is gardens of residence, stability, and eternity.

His saying: وَرِضْوانٌ مِنَ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ — and a pleasure from Allah is greater — meaning the pleasure of Allah, the Glorified, with them is greater than all of that—as the context suggests. "Pleasure" (رِضْوانٌ) was mentioned in the indefinite form to allude that it cannot be measured, nor can human imagination encompass it; or because any pleasure from Him, even if it were small, is greater than all of that. This is not because all of that is what branches from His pleasure and emanates from it—though it is so in itself—but because the reality of servitude (العبودية) to which the Book of Allah invites is His worship out of love for Him: not out of greed for Paradise, nor out of fear of the Fire. The greatest happiness and success for the lover is to attract the pleasure of his beloved, rather than striving to please himself.

It is as if to point to this that He concluded the verse by His saying: ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ — That is the great success. In the sentence is an indication of the meaning of "exclusivity" (الحصر), meaning that this pleasure is the reality of every great success, even the great success of the eternal Paradise; for if there were nothing of the reality of divine pleasure in the bliss of Paradise, it would be a torment, not a blessing.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ جاهِدِ الْكُفَّارَ وَالْمُنافِقِينَ وَاغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَمَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ — O Prophet! Wage jihad against the faithless and the hypocrites, and be severe with them. Their abode shall be hell, and it is an evil destination. (التوبة: ٧٣). Jihad (جهاد) against a people is the exertion of the utmost effort in resisting them. It can be with the tongue and with the hand until it ends in fighting (القتال). Its use in the Book has become common for fighting, although it is sometimes used for other than that, as in His saying: "As for those who strive (جاهَدُوا) in Our cause, We shall surely guide them to Our ways" (العنكبوت: ٦٩).

Its use for fighting the disbelievers is straightforward because they are openly defiant and schismatic. As for the hypocrites, they are those who do not manifest disbelief nor are they openly defiant; they only conceal disbelief and subvert matters through plot and deception. What then is the meaning of jihad with them in the sense of fighting and warring against them? Therefore, it may occur to the mind that the intended meaning of jihad against them is whatever the benefit (المصلحة) necessitates of exerting the utmost effort in resisting them. If the benefit necessitates, they are boycotted and not mixed with or socialized with; if the benefit necessitates, they are exhorted with the tongue; if the benefit necessitates, they are expelled and banished to another land or killed if apostasy (الردة) is proven against them, or other than that.

Perhaps this meaning—that the intended meaning of jihad in the verse is the general exertion of effort—is supported by the following of His saying: جاهِدِ الْكُفَّارَ وَالْمُنافِقِينَ — Wage jihad against the faithless and the hypocrites — with His saying: وَاغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ — and be severe with them — meaning be harsh with them and treat them with roughness.

As for His saying: وَمَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ — Their abode shall be hell, and it is an evil destination — it is conjoined to what preceded it of the command. Perhaps what made the conjunction of a "report" (إخبار) to an "imperative" (إنشاء) easier is that the previous sentence is in the sense of our saying: "Indeed these disbelievers and hypocrites are deserving of jihad." And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللهِ ما قالُوا وَلَقَدْ قالُوا كَلِمَةَ الْكُفْرِ وَكَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِسْلامِهِمْ وَهَمُّوا بِما لَمْ يَنالُوا — They swear by Allah that they did not say [it], but they have surely uttered the word of unfaith and disbelieved after their Islam, and they intended what they did not attain (التوبة: ٧٤). The context of the verse suggests that they committed an evil act and accompanied it with a word they uttered at that time, and that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله rebuked them for their word, holding them accountable. So they swore by Allah that they did not say it, as preceded in His saying: "And if you ask them, they will surely say, 'We were only idling and playing,'" to the end of the verse, for they were excusing themselves by that for their act, that it was idling and playing and nothing else.

Allah, the Glorified, belies them in both matters: As for their denial of the word, by His saying: وَلَقَدْ قالُوا كَلِمَةَ الْكُفْرِ — but they have surely uttered the word of unfaith — and He explained it secondly by His saying: وَكَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِسْلامِهِمْ — and disbelieved after their Islam — to indicate the seriousness of the word, so that disbelief after Islam follows from it.

Perhaps He said here: وَكَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِسْلامِهِمْ — and disbelieved after their Islam — while it was said previously: "You have disbelieved after your belief," because the previous saying was to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, proceeding according to the outward state of their condition, which was the faith they claimed and manifested. The second saying is from Allah, the Knower of the Unseen and the Witnessed, so He testifies that they were not believers and did not exceed the two testimonies with their tongues; thus they were Muslims, not believers, and they disbelieved by their word and exited from Islam into disbelief. In this is an allusion that their word was a word containing a rejection of the two testimonies or one of them.

Or because the first saying was in contrast to their act by which they intended to cause evil to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and an act devoid of speech which does not hit its target does not harm Islam, which is the portion of the word and the testimony; it only harms faith, which is the portion of conviction. The second saying is in contrast to the word they uttered, and it contradicts Islam, which is acquired by the word, rather than faith, which is a kind of heart-felt conviction.

As for their denial of the evil act they committed and their interpretation of it as idling and playing, it is belied by His saying: وَهَمُّوا بِما لَمْ يَنالُوا — and they intended what they did not attain.

Then He said in the position of blaming and shaming them: وَما نَقَمُوا إِلَّا أَنْ أَغْناهُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — They were only resentful because Allah and His Apostle had enriched them out of His bounty — meaning because Allah and His Messenger had enriched them. That is, the cause of this resentment of theirs was that Allah enriched them from His bounty through the spoils He provided them and the security and prosperity He spread over them, thus enabling them to generate wealth and increase property from every side. Likewise His Messenger, as he guided them to a righteous life that opened for them the doors of the blessings of heaven and earth, and he distributed the spoils among them and spread justice over them.

This is of the category of placing a thing in the place of its opposite: He placed "enrichment" (الإغناء)—which by nature is a cause for pleasure and gratitude—in the place of a cause for resentment and indignation, such as injustice and anger. If you wish, you could say: He placed "excellence" (الإحسان) in the place of "offense" (الإساءة). In this is a kind of "sarcasm" (تهكم) mixed with blame, similar to what is in the saying of the Exalted: "and make your denial of it your [only] means of livelihood?" (الواقعة: ٨٢)—meaning you make your provision a cause for denying the signs of Allah, while it is by nature a cause for thanking the blessing and being pleased with the gift. As it was said: the meaning is: and you make, instead of thanking for your provision, your denial.

The pronoun in His saying: مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — out of His bounty — refers to Allah, the Glorified. It was said in al-Majma': He did not say "out of their bounty" (من فضلهما) because one does not join the name of Allah and the name of another in a "metonymy" (الكناية) out of veneration for Allah. Therefore, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to one whom he heard saying: "Whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger has been guided, and whoever disobeys them (عصاهما) has gone astray": "What an evil orator of the people you are!" He asked: "How should I say, O Messenger of Allah?" He replied: "Say: and whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger." The same is said regarding the saying of the Glorified: "while Allah and His Apostle are worthier that they should please Him (يُرضوه)" (التوبة: ٦٢). And it was said: He did not say "out of their bounty" because the bounty of Allah is from Him and the bounty of His Messenger is from His bounty. [End of quote].

Beyond veneration, there is another matter we previously discussed in the commentary on the saying of the Exalted: "They are certainly faithless who say, 'Allah is the third [person] of a trinity'" (المائدة: ٧٣) in the sixth volume of the book: that His unity, the Exalted, is not of the genus of "numerical unity" (الوحدة العددية) such that it would be correct to compose it with the unity of another and derive a number from it.
