Then Allah, the Glorified, clarified to these hypocrites that they have, despite these destructive sins, their explicit disbelief in Allah, and their intention for what they did not attain, the opportunity to return to their Lord. He clarified the end of this repentance and the end of turning away and disregarding it, saying: فَإِنْ يَتُوبُوا يَكُ خَيْراً لَهُمْ — So if they repent, it will be better for them — because it leads to forgiveness and Paradise — وَإِنْ يَتَوَلَّوْا — and if they turn away — and disregard repentance — يُعَذِّبْهُمُ اللهُ عَذاباً أَلِيماً فِي الدُّنْيا — Allah will punish them with a painful punishment in this world — through political authority and exemplary punishment, or by inciting the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله against them, or through plot and "gradual entrapment" (الاستدراج). Even if their punishment were nothing other than that they, through their hypocrisy, oppose the system of causes based on truth and faith—so the chain of causes overtakes them, crushes them, and exposes them—that would be sufficient. Allah has said: "and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot" (التوبة: ٢٤) — وَالْآخِرَةِ — and the Hereafter — with the punishment of the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا نَصِيرٍ — and they shall have no guardian or helper on the earth — means that these people have no guardian on earth to take charge of their affair and divert the punishment from them, nor a helper to assist them and provide them with that by which they might repel the promised punishment from themselves. For the rest of the hypocrites are also of them, and the word of corruption gathers them, and their corrupt root is severed from all other cosmic causes. Thus, they have no guardian to take charge of their affair and no helper to assist them. Perhaps this sentence of the verse is an allusion to what we pointed toward regarding the meaning of worldly punishment.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَحْذَرُ الْمُنافِقُونَ أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ "The hypocrites fear lest a surah should be sent down against them..." [9:64], it is said: It was revealed concerning twelve men who stood at the mountain pass (العقبة) to assassinate the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) upon his return from Tabuk. Jibra’il informed the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of this and commanded him to send someone to them and strike the faces of their mounts.

‘Ammar was leading the mount of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and Hudhayfah was driving it. He said to Hudhayfah: "Strike the faces of their mounts," so he struck them until he drove them away. When he dismounted, he said to Hudhayfah: "Whom did you recognize of the people?" He replied: "I did not recognize any of them." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "It was so-and-so and so-and-so," until he counted them all. Hudhayfah said: "Will you not send for them and kill them?" He replied: "I dislike that the Arabs should say: 'When he triumphed with his companions, he turned to killing them.'" This is narrated from Ibn Kaysan.

It is narrated from Abu Ja‘far al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) similarly, except that he said: They conspired among themselves to kill him, and some said to others: "If he notices, we will say: 'We were only idling and playing,' and if he does not notice, we will kill him."

It is also said: A group of hypocrites said during the expedition of Tabuk: "This man thinks he will conquer the palaces and fortresses of Syria; how far-fetched, how far-fetched!" Allah informed His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of this, so he said: "Halt the caravan." He summoned them and said: "You said such and such." They replied: "O Prophet of Allah, we were only idling and playing," and they swore to that. Then the verse was revealed: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ "And if you ask them, they will surely say..." [9:65] etc. This is narrated from al-Hasan and Qatadah.

It is also said: This occurred during his return from the expedition of Tabuk to Medina. There were four or three individuals before him mocking and laughing, and one of them was laughing but not speaking. Jibra’il descended and informed the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of this. He summoned ‘Ammar ibn Yasir and said: "These people are mocking me and the Quran; Jibra’il has informed me of that. And if you ask them, they will surely say: 'We were only talking the talk of the caravan.'" ‘Ammar followed them and said: "What are you laughing at?" They said: "We are talking the talk of the caravan." ‘Ammar said: "Allah and His Messenger have spoken the truth; you have been scorched—may Allah scorch you!" They then came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) apologizing, and Allah the Exalted revealed the verses. This is narrated from al-Kalbi, ‘Ali ibn Ibrahim, and Abu Hamzah.

It is also said: A man said during the expedition of Tabuk: "I have never seen anyone with a more lying tongue or more cowardly during encounter than these," meaning the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his companions. ‘Awf ibn Malik said to him: "You have lied; rather, you are a hypocrite." He intended to inform the Messenger of Allah of this, but when he arrived, the revelation (الوحي) had preceded him. The man came apologizing, saying: "We were only idling and playing," so the verse was revealed concerning this. This is narrated from Ibn ‘Umar, Zayd ibn Aslam, and Muhammad ibn Ka‘b.

It is also said: A man from the hypocrites said: "Muhammad tells us that the camel of so-and-so is in such-and-such a valley; what does he know of the Unseen (الغيب)?" So the verse was revealed. This is narrated from Mujahid.

It is also said: It was revealed concerning ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy and his group. This is narrated from al-Dahhak.

Also in al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللهِ ما قالُوا "They swear by Allah that they did not say [it]..." [9:74], there is a difference of opinion as to whom this verse was revealed. It is said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was sitting in the shade of a tree and said: "A person will soon come to you who looks at you with the eyes of Satan." Before long, a blue-eyed man appeared. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) summoned him and said: "Why do you and your companions insult me?" The man went and brought his companions, and they swore by Allah that they had not said it. So Allah revealed this verse. This is narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas.

It is also said: The hypocrites went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Tabuk, and whenever they were alone with one another, they insulted the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his companions and slandered the religion (الدين). Hudhayfah conveyed this to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said to them: "What is this that has reached me concerning you?" They swore by Allah that they had said nothing of the sort. This is narrated from al-Dahhak.

It is also said: It was revealed concerning Jullas ibn Suwayd ibn al-Samit. This was because the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) delivered a sermon one day at Tabuk and mentioned the hypocrites, calling them an abomination (رجسا) and criticizing them. Jullas said: "By Allah, if Muhammad is truthful in what he says, then we are worse than donkeys." ‘Amir ibn Qays heard him and said: "Indeed, by Allah, Muhammad is truthful and you are worse than donkeys." When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) returned to Medina, ‘Amir ibn Qays came to him and informed him of what Jullas had said. Jullas said: "He has lied, O Messenger of Allah."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded them both to swear an oath at the pulpit (المنبر). Jullas stood at the pulpit and swore by Allah that he had not said it. Then ‘Amir stood and swore by Allah: "He did say it," then said: "O Allah, send down upon Your truthful Prophet the truth from among us." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers said: "Amen." Then Jibra’il (عليه‌السلام) descended before they dispersed with this verse until it reached: فَإِنْ يَتُوبُوا يَكُ خَيْراً لَهُمْ "So if they repent, it will be better for them" [9:74].

Jullas stood up and said: "O Messenger of Allah, I hear that Allah has offered me repentance. ‘Amir ibn Qays was truthful in what he told you; I did say it, and I seek Allah's forgiveness and repent to Him." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) accepted that from him. This is narrated from al-Kalbi, Muhammad ibn Ishaq, and Mujahid.

It is also said: It was revealed concerning ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul when he said: لَئِنْ رَجَعْنا إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ الْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا الْأَذَلَّ "If we return to the city, the mighty will surely expel the abased from it" [63:8]. This is narrated from Qatadah.

It is also said: It was revealed concerning the people of the mountain pass (أهل العقبة), for they conspired to assassinate the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at a mountain pass upon their return from Tabuk. They intended to cut the girths of his mount and then goad it with him on it. Allah informed him of this, and it was among his miracles (معجزاته), for such a thing cannot be known except through revelation (وحي) from Allah the Exalted.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) traveled through the pass, with ‘Ammar and Hudhayfah with him—one leading his camel and the other driving it. He commanded all the people to take the path through the bottom of the valley. Those who intended to kill him were twelve or fifteen men, according to the disagreement regarding it. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recognized them and named them one by one. This is narrated from al-Zajjaj, al-Waqidi, and al-Kalbi, and the story is detailed in the book of al-Waqidi.

Al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) said: Eight of them were from Quraysh and four were from the [other] Arabs.

I say: What he [the author of al-Majma‘] has mentioned from the narrations he collected and selected are recorded in the books of traditional exegesis (التفسير بالمأثور) and the compendiums of hadith (جوامع الحديث) from the books of both sects. There are other narrations he omitted, and they are indeed worthy of being omitted, so we have omitted most of them as he did.

As for the narrations he cited, none of them align with the verses except the hadith of the mountain pass (حديث العقبة), which he cited once in the commentary (تفسير) of the first verse: يَحْذَرُ الْمُنافِقُونَ أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ and once in the commentary of the verse: يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللهِ ما قالُوا.

As for the other narrations mentioned, they are merely reports containing various isolated stories and events which, if authentic and established, would be among the stories of the hypocrites without being linked to these verses. These verses, as you recognized in the previous commentary (البيان), are eleven verses connected to one another, narrated for a single purpose: to point to a story among the stories of the hypocrites in which they intended to assassinate the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and spoke at that time the word of disbelief (كلمة الكفر). Allah the Glorified intervened between them and the attainment of what they intended. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) questioned them about their affair and what they had uttered, so they offered excuses for their action, denied their speech, and swore to that, but Allah the Exalted belied them in it.

This is the summary of what appears through the verses, and among the narrations, it only aligns with those containing the story of the mountain pass in general, to the exclusion of the others.

There is no justification for relying on them [the other narrations] in the commentary of the verses except according to the method of the group [of traditionalists] who allow narrations to dominate the verses based on their content, whether the wording of the verses supports that or not—despite the gross discrepancies within them (i.e., the narrations) that necessitate suspicion toward them, as is apparent to whoever reviews them.

Furthermore, there is another flaw in the narrations: their appearance suggests the fragmentation of the verses and the scattering of some from others, with each being revealed for a different reason and pursuing a different goal. Yet you have recognized that the verses possess a single, connected context (سياق) whose nature is only to pursue a single goal.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, ‘Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from al-Kalbi: When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) returned from the expedition of Tabuk, there were three individuals before him who mocked Allah, His Messenger, and the Quran. He said: There was a man among them who did not assist them in the talk—he was traveling apart from them, called Yazid ibn Wadi‘ah—so there was revealed: إِنْ نَعْفُ عَنْ طائِفَةٍ مِنْكُمْ نُعَذِّبْ طائِفَةً "If We forgive a group of you, We will punish a group..." [9:66]. Thus, He named a "group" (طائفة) while he was one person.

I say: This is the origin of the saying of some: that the term "group" (الطائفة) is applied to one just as it is applied to many, even though the verse follows the course of an allusion (كناية) rather than a specific naming. There are many similar instances in the Quranic verses, as previously indicated.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas who said: This verse was revealed concerning a group of hypocrites from the Banu ‘Amr ibn ‘Awf—among them were Wadi‘ah ibn Thabit and a man from Ashja‘, an ally of theirs called Makhshi ibn Humayr. They were traveling with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) as he was heading to Tabuk. Some said to others: "Do you think fighting the 'Yellow Ones' [the Byzantines] is like fighting others? By Allah, it is as if we see you tomorrow being led in ropes."

Makhshi ibn Humayr said: "I wish that I could be judged such that each man of you is struck a hundred times, on the condition that we are saved from a Quran being revealed concerning us." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to ‘Ammar ibn Yasir: "Overtake the people, for they have been scorched. Ask them about what they said; if they deny and conceal it, say: 'Nay, you said such and such.'" He overtook them and spoke to them, so they came apologizing. Then Allah revealed: لا تَعْتَذِرُوا قَدْ كَفَرْتُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ إِنْ نَعْفُ عَنْ طائِفَةٍ مِنْكُمْ "Do not make excuses. You have disbelieved after your belief. If We forgive a group of you..." [9:66]. The one whom Allah forgave was Makhshi ibn Humayr; he was named ‘Abd al-Rahman and asked Allah to be killed as a martyr whose death would not be known. He was killed at al-Yamamah, and neither his death nor his killer was known, nor was any trace or sign of him seen.

I say: The story of Makhshi ibn Humayr has been reported in several narrations, but even assuming its authenticity, it does not necessitate the revelation of the verses concerning it, given the vast distance between it and the contents of the verses.

It is not mandatory for us, when we come across any story that occurred during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—whatever story it may be—to bridle a verse of the Noble Quran with it, then return to interpret the verse by the story and make it the judge over it.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas who said: "How similar is tonight to yesternight: كَالَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ كانُوا أَشَدَّ مِنْكُمْ قُوَّةً 'Like those who were before you: they were stronger than you in power...' [9:69]—until His saying—وَخُضْتُمْ كَالَّذِي خاضُوا '...and you idled as they idled.' These are the Children of Israel; we have resembled them. By Him in Whose hand is my soul, you will surely follow them—even if a man entered the hole of a lizard, you would enter it."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Majma‘ from him.

In al-Majma‘, from the Tafsir of al-Tha‘labi, from Abu Hurayrah, from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: "You will surely take as the nations before you took, arm's length by arm's length, span by span, and fathom by fathom—even if one of them entered the hole of a lizard, you would enter it." They said: "O Messenger of Allah, as Persia, Rome, and the People of the Book did?" He said: "And are the people anyone else but them?"

Also in it, from the Tafsir of al-Tha‘labi, from Hudhayfah who said: "The hypocrites who are among you today are worse than the hypocrites who were during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)." We said: "And how so?" He said: "Those used to hide their hypocrisy, while these have made it public."

In al-‘Uyun, with its chain of narration from al-Qasim ibn Muslim from his brother ‘Abd al-‘Aziz ibn Muslim, who said: I asked al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: نَسُوا اللهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ "They forgot Allah, so He forgot them" [9:67]. He said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, does not forget nor is He heedless; rather, it is the created, originated (المحدث) being that forgets and is heedless. Have you not heard Him, the Mighty and Majestic, saying: وَما كانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا '...and your Lord is not forgetful' [19:64]? Rather, He recompenses those who forgot Him and forgot the meeting of His day by making them forget themselves, as He, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَلا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللهَ فَأَنْساهُمْ أَنْفُسَهُمْ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ 'And do not be like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget themselves. It is they who are the transgressors' [59:19]. And His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: فَالْيَوْمَ نَنْساهُمْ كَما نَسُوا لِقاءَ يَوْمِهِمْ هذا 'So today We will forget them just as they forgot the meeting of this day of theirs' [7:51], meaning: We leave them just as they left the preparation for the meeting of this day of theirs."

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Jabir from Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام): نَسُوا اللهَ "They forgot Allah"—he said: "They left the obedience of Allah." فَنَسِيَهُمْ "So He forgot them"—he said: "So He left them."

In the same source, from Abu Ma‘mar al-Sa‘dani, who said: ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) said regarding His saying: نَسُوا اللهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ: "It only means that they forgot Allah in the abode of the world, so they did not act for Him in obedience, nor did they believe in Him and His Messenger; so He forgot them in the Hereafter—meaning He did not grant them any portion of His reward, so they became forgotten from [all] good."

I say: Al-Saduq narrated it in al-Ma‘ani with his chain from Abu Ma‘mar from him (عليه‌السلام).

In al-Kafi, with its chain from Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام)—in a hadith—I said: وَالْمُؤْتَفِكاتِ أَتَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ "...and the overturned towns: their apostles brought them manifest proofs" [9:70]. He said: "Those were the people of Lut; [their towns] were overturned (ائتفكت) upon them—meaning they were flipped and their upper parts became their lower parts."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain from Safwan ibn Mihran, who said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): "A Muslim woman comes to me, having known me by my work, and I know her by her Islam; she has no mahram (محرم) — a male relative whom she is forbidden to marry, so I carry her [on my mount]." He said: "Then carry her, for the believer is a mahram to the female believer." Then he recited this verse: وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ "The faithful, men and women, are allies of one another..." [9:71].

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it in his Tafsir from Safwan al-Jammal from him (عليه‌السلام).

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Thuwayr from ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام), who said: "When the people of Paradise are in Paradise, and the friend (ولي) of Allah enters his gardens and dwellings and reclines, every believer on his couch, his servants surrounding him, the fruits hanging low over him, the springs gushing around him, the rivers flowing beneath him, the carpets spread for him, the cushions set for him, and the servants bring him whatever his soul desires before he asks them for it." He said: "And the houris (الحور العين) — the pure, dark-eyed maidens of Paradise — come out to him from the gardens, and they remain in that state for as long as Allah wills."

"Then the All-Compelling (الجبار) looks upon them and says to them: 'O My friends, people of My obedience, and inhabitants of My Paradise in My proximity! Shall I inform you of something better than what you are in?' They will say: 'Our Lord, and what thing is better than what we are in—amidst what our souls desire and our eyes delight in of blessings in the proximity of the Generous?'"

He said: "Then He repeats the statement to them, and they say: 'Our Lord, yes! Then bring us something better than what we are in.' Then He, Blessed and Exalted is He, says to them: 'My pleasure (رضاي) with you and My love for you is better and greater than what you are in.'" He said: "Then they say: 'Yes, O our Lord! Your pleasure with us and Your love for us is better and more pleasant for our souls.'"

Then ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام) recited this verse: وَعَدَ اللهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها وَمَساكِنَ طَيِّبَةً فِي جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ وَرِضْوانٌ مِنَ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ "Allah has promised the faithful, men and women, gardens with conduits running amidst them, to abide in them [forever], and goodly dwellings in the Gardens of Eden. And Allah’s pleasure is greater [than all these]; that is the great success." [9:72].

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When the people of Paradise enter Paradise, Allah says: 'Do you desire anything that I may increase for you?' They say: 'O our Lord, does anything remain that You have not granted us?' He says: 'Yes, My pleasure (رضائي), so I shall never be angry with you ever again.'"

I say: This meaning is reported in many narrations from the paths of both sects.

In Jami‘ al-Jawami‘, from Abu al-Darda’ from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Eden (عدن) is the abode of Allah which no eye has seen and which has not occurred to the heart of any mortal. None shall dwell in it except three: the prophets, the truthful (الصديقون), and the martyrs (الشهداء). Allah says: 'Blessed is he who enters you.'"

I say: The specificity of the inhabitants of Paradise in the narration does not contradict their generality in the verse, due to the indication of His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ وَالشُّهَداءُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ "Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses with their Lord..." [57:19], showing that Allah the Glorified will join the generality of the believers with the truthful and the martyrs.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ جاهِدِ الْكُفَّارَ وَالْمُنافِقِينَ "O Prophet! Wage jihad against the faithless and the hypocrites..." [9:73]:

He said: My father narrated to me from Abu ‘Umayr from Abu Basir from Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام) who said: "Wage jihad against the disbelievers and the hypocrites by enjoining the obligatory duties (الفرائض)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bayhaqi narrated in Shu‘ab al-Iman from Ibn Mas‘ud who said: When يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ جاهِدِ الْكُفَّارَ وَالْمُنافِقِينَ was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to wage jihad with his hand; if he could not, then with his heart; if he could not, then with his tongue; and if he could not, then let him meet him with a frowning face.

I say: There is confusion in the narration regarding the order of its parts, as jihad with the heart comes after all else, yet it was placed in between.

Ayahs

۞ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ عَـٰهَدَ ٱللَّهَ لَئِنْ ءَاتَىٰنَا مِن فَضْلِهِۦ لَنَصَّدَّقَنَّ وَلَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٧٥)
Among them are those who made a pledge with Allah: ‘If He gives us out of His grace, we will surely give the zakat and we will surely be among the righteous.’ (75)
فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ بَخِلُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٧٦)
But when He gave them out of His grace, they begrudged it and turned away, being disregardful. (76)
فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفَاقًا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُۥ بِمَآ أَخْلَفُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا وَعَدُوهُ وَبِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْذِبُونَ (٧٧)
So He caused hypocrisy to ensue in their hearts until the day they will encounter Him, because of their going back on what they had promised Allah and because of the lies they used to tell. (77)
أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ سِرَّهُمْ وَنَجْوَىٰهُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ (٧٨)
Do they not know that Allah knows their secret [thoughts] and [hears] their secret talks, and that Allah is knower of all that is Unseen? (78)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ ٱلْمُطَّوِّعِينَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ فِى ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ إِلَّا جُهْدَهُمْ فَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنْهُمْ ۙ سَخِرَ ٱللَّهُ مِنْهُمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٧٩)
Those who blame the voluntary donors from among the faithful concerning the charities—and as for those who do not find [anything] except [what] their means [permit], they ridicule them—Allah shall put them to ridicule, and there is a painful punishment for them. (79)
ٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لَا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِن تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٨٠)
Whether you plead forgiveness for them or do not plead forgiveness for them, even if you plead forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah shall never forgive them because they defied Allah and His Apostle; and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (80)

(Commentary)

The verses mention another group of the hypocrites (المنافقين) who held back from the ruling regarding charities (الصدقات) and refused to pay the alms-tax (الزكاة). They had been poor and covenanted with Allah that if He enriched them and gave them of His grace, they would surely give charity and be among the righteous (الصالحين). Yet, when He gave them wealth, they were niggardly with it and refused to give.

The verses also mention others among the hypocrites who find fault with those believers (المؤمنين) who possess means regarding their giving of charities, and likewise, they defame those among them who possess nothing but their own effort and mock them. Allah, the Glorified, names all of these as hypocrites and decrees that they shall never be forgiven.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ عاهَدَ اللهَ لَئِنْ آتانا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ لَنَصَّدَّقَنَّ وَلَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — Among them are those who made a pledge with Allah, ‘If He gives us out of His grace, we will surely give charity and we will surely be among the righteous.’ (9:75), until the end of the two verses. Giving (الإيتاء) means bestowing. The term "giving from grace" (الإيتاء من الفضل) is frequently used for the giving of wealth. A contextual clue for this in the verse is His saying: لَنَصَّدَّقَنَّ (we will surely give charity), meaning we will give charity from the wealth He gives us, as well as the mention of being niggardly with it in the following verse.

The context implies that the speech addresses an actual event. Narrations indicate that the verses were revealed concerning Tha‘labah in a story that will be cited in the following "Discussion of Narrations," Allah the Exalted willing. The meaning of the two verses is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفاقاً فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلى يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُ — So He caused hypocrisy to ensue in their hearts until the day they will encounter Him (9:77). "Ensue" (الأعقاب) means to bequeath or result in. It is said in al-Majma‘: "He caused it to ensue for him" (أعقبه), "He made him inherit it" (أورثه), and "He led him to it" (أداه) are synonyms. Sometimes "ensue" (أعقبه) can mean "He requited him" (جازاه). This concludes the quote. It is derived from "heel" or "sequel" (العقب), meaning to bring something immediately after another.

The pronoun in His saying: فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ (He caused to ensue for them) refers back to the niggardliness (البخل) or to their action which included niggardliness. Based on this, the meaning of His saying: يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُ (the day they encounter it) would be the day they encounter the consequence of niggardliness—that is, the recompense for niggardliness—by way of a certain metaphorical attention.

It is also possible that the pronoun refers to Him, the Exalted, and the meaning of "the day they encounter Him" is the day they meet Allah, which is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), according to the well-known usage in His speech where the Day of Resurrection is called the day of meeting Allah. Or it could mean the day of death, as is apparent from His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ لَآتٍ — Whoever expects to encounter Allah [should know that] Allah’s [appointed] term will surely come (29:5).

This second interpretation is more apparent under the second possibility, as it is more intuitive to the mind to say: "They shall remain in their hypocrisy until they die," rather than saying: "They shall remain in their hypocrisy until they are resurrected," since their state does not change after death in any case.

His saying: بِما أَخْلَفُوا اللهَ ما وَعَدُوهُ وَبِما كانُوا يَكْذِبُونَ — because they failed Allah in what they had promised Him, and because they used to lie (9:77). The preposition "because of" (بـ) in both instances denotes causality (السببية). That is, this niggardliness bequeathed them hypocrisy because of the breach of promise and the persistence in lying, both of which necessitate a contradiction between their inner state and their outward appearance, which is the essence of hypocrisy (النفاق).

The meaning of the verse is: Niggardliness and the refusal to give charities bequeathed hypocrisy in their hearts that will endure and never leave them until the day of their death. This niggardliness and refusal became a cause for that because of the breach of promise to Allah and the constant persistence in lying.

Alternatively, the meaning is: Allah requited them with hypocrisy in their hearts until the day of encountering Him—which is the day of death—because they failed Him in what they promised Him and they used to lie.

The verse contains an indication, firstly: that breaching a promise and lying in speech are among the causes and signs of hypocrisy.

Secondly: that some forms of hypocrisy (النفاق) can afflict a person after they have attained faith (الإيمان), just as some forms of disbelief (الكفر) can occur after faith, which is apostasy (الردة). Allah, the Glorified, has said: ثُمَّ كانَ عاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَساؤُا السُّواى أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ وَكانُوا بِها يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was the worst [fate], for they denied the signs of Allah and used to deride them (30:10). He mentioned that wrongdoing might lead a person to deny the signs of Allah. This denial (التكذيب) might be both outward and inward, which is disbelief, or inward only, which is hypocrisy.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَعْلَمُ سِرَّهُمْ وَنَجْواهُمْ — Do they not know that Allah knows their secret and their confidential talk (9:78). Confidential talk (النجوى) is hidden speech. The interrogation is for rebuke and reprimand.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ الْمُطَّوِّعِينَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ فِي الصَّدَقاتِ وَالَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ إِلَّا جُهْدَهُمْ — Those who blame the volunteers among the faithful concerning charities, and those who find nothing [to give] but their [meager] effort (9:79). Volunteering (التطوع) is to perform an act that the soul does not dislike and does not consider burdensome. Therefore, it is mostly used for recommended acts (المندوبات), because obligatory acts (الواجبات) carry a hint of being imposed upon the soul by the fact that one is not permitted to leave them.

The contrast between "the volunteers among the believers concerning charities" and "those who find nothing but their effort" is a contextual clue that "the volunteers" refers to those who give the alms-tax (الزكاة) out of their abundance and wealth. It is as if, due to their abundance and the vastness of their wealth, they give it willingly and with desire without it being difficult for them. This is in contrast to "those who find nothing but their effort," meaning the extent of their exertion and capacity, or that which it is difficult for them to be content with giving.

His saying: الَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ (Those who blame...) is either a new sentence or a description of those mentioned in His saying: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ عاهَدَ اللهَ (Among them are those who made a pledge with Allah...). The meaning is: Those who find fault with those who volunteer charities from among the wealthy believers, and those who find no wealth except the effort of their own selves from among the destitute poor. They find fault with the givers of charity—the wealthy and the destitute, the rich and the poor—and they mock them. Allah mocks them, and for them is a painful punishment. This contains a response to their mockery and a threat of severe punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ — Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them, even if you plead for forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will never forgive them (9:80). The alternation between the command and the prohibition is a metaphor for the equality of the action and its omission—meaning the futility of the action—as previously seen in His saying: أَنْفِقُوا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً لَنْ يُتَقَبَّلَ مِنْكُمْ — Spend willingly or unwillingly, it shall never be accepted from you (9:53).

The meaning is that these hypocrites shall not attain any forgiveness from Allah, and seeking forgiveness for them or not seeking it is the same, because seeking it for them is futile and has no effect.

His saying: إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ (even if you plead for forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will never forgive them) is an emphasis of what was mentioned before regarding the futility of seeking forgiveness for them. It is a clarification that the nature of forgiveness shall never reach them at all, whether you ask for forgiveness on their behalf or do not ask, and whether the seeking of forgiveness is once or many times, few or many.

The mention of "seventy" is a metaphor for multiplicity without there being any specific significance to the number itself, such that seeking forgiveness once or twice up to seventy would be ineffective for them, but if it exceeded seventy, it would take effect. For this reason, He justified it by His saying: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ — That is because they disbelieved in Allah and His Apostle (9:80). That is, the barrier to the inclusion of forgiveness is their disbelief in Allah and His Messenger, and this barrier does not change whether forgiveness is sought or not, once or many times; they remain in their disbelief.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ — and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot (9:80) completes the preceding part. The speech is structured as a syllogistic argument (استدلال قياسي), and the implicit meaning is: They are disbelievers in Allah and His Messenger, so they are transgressors (فاسقون) who have exited the bounds of servitude to Allah, and Allah does not guide the transgressing people. However, forgiveness is a guidance to the felicity of proximity and Paradise; therefore, forgiveness shall not include them nor reach them at all.

The use of "seventy" to denote mere multiplicity without specificity is common in the language, just like the use of "one hundred" and "one thousand."

(Discussion of Narrations)

In al-Majma‘: It is said: It was revealed concerning Tha‘labah ibn Hatib, who was from the Ansar (الأنصار). He said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Pray to Allah to provide me with wealth." He replied: "O Tha‘labah, a little for which you give thanks is better than much that you cannot bear. Do you not have an excellent example in the Messenger of Allah? By Him in whose hand is my soul, if I wished for the mountains to flow with me as gold and silver, they would have flowed."

Then he came to him after that and said: "O Messenger of Allah, pray to Allah to provide me with wealth. By Him who sent you with the truth, if Allah provides me with wealth, I will surely give every rightful owner his due." So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "O Allah, provide Tha‘labah with wealth." He then acquired sheep, and they multiplied as worms multiply until the city became too narrow for him. He moved away from it and settled in one of its valleys. Then they multiplied further until he moved far from the city, and he became so occupied with them that he missed the Friday prayer (الجمعة) and the congregational prayer (الجماعة). The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent the collector to him to take the alms-tax, but he refused and was niggardly, saying: "This is nothing but a sister to the tribute (الجزية)." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Woe to Tha‘labah! Woe to Tha‘labah!" and Allah revealed the verses. This is narrated from Abu Umamah al-Bahili, and it is narrated as a marfu‘ report.

It is also said: Tha‘labah came to a gathering of the Ansar and made them witnesses, saying: "If Allah gives me of His grace, I will surely give charity from it, give every rightful owner his due, and maintain ties of kinship with it." So Allah tested him; a cousin of his died and he inherited wealth from him, but he did not fulfill what he had said, so the verses were revealed. This is from Ibn Abbas, Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, and Qatadah.

It is also said: It was revealed concerning Tha‘labah ibn Hatib and Mu‘attib ibn Qushayr, both from the Banu ‘Amr ibn ‘Awf. They said: "If Allah provides us with wealth, we will surely give charity." When Allah provided them with wealth, they were niggardly with it. This is from al-Hasan and Mujahid.

I say: The narrations they have mentioned do not contradict one another. It is possible that Tha‘labah covenanted with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding that and then made a group of the Ansar witnesses to it, and that others were with him in that, so the narrations support one another.

They are also supported by what is narrated from al-Dahhak that the verses were revealed concerning men from the hypocrites: Nabtal ibn al-Harith, Jadd ibn Qays, Tha‘labah ibn Hatib, and Mu‘attib ibn Qushayr.

As for what was narrated in al-Majma‘ from al-Kalbi: that it was revealed concerning Hatib ibn Abi Balta‘ah, who had wealth in Syria which was delayed from reaching him, and he suffered great distress because of that, so he swore that if Allah brought him that wealth, he would surely give charity; but when Allah the Exalted brought it to him, he did not do so—this is far-fetched in its application to the verses. This is because the arrival of one's own wealth to its owner is not called "giving from grace" (إيتاء من الفضل); rather, that refers to bestowing and providing.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "It is Tha‘labah ibn Hatib ibn ‘Amr ibn ‘Awf. He was in need and covenanted with Allah, but when He gave to him, he was niggardly with it."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, and al-Nasa’i narrated from Abu Hurayrah that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: آية المنافق ثلاث : إذا حدث كذب ـ وإذا وعد أخلف وإذا اؤتمن خان — "The signs of a hypocrite are three: when he speaks, he lies; when he promises, he breaks it; and when he is entrusted, he betrays."

I say: This is narrated through more than one chain from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), and some of them have been mentioned previously.

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ الْمُطَّوِّعِينَ (Those who blame the volunteers...), al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu‘aym in al-Ma‘rifah narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud who said: "When the verse of charity was revealed, we used to carry loads on our backs [to earn and give]. A man came and gave a large amount in charity, so they said: 'He is showing off.' Then Abu ‘Aqil came with half a sa‘ [of dates], and the hypocrites said: 'Allah is surely independent of this one's charity.' So there was revealed: الَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ الْمُطَّوِّعِينَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ فِي الصَّدَقاتِ ـ وَالَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ إِلَّا جُهْدَهُمْ — Those who blame the volunteers among the faithful concerning charities, and those who find nothing [to give] but their [meager] effort (9:79)."

I say: The narrations regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse are many, and the most exemplary are those we have cited. There are other narrations with similar meanings, and their apparent sense is that the verse is independent of what preceded it, starting anew in itself.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from ‘Urwah: that ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy said to his companions: "If you did not spend on Muhammad and his companions, they would have dispersed from around him," and he is the one who said: "The mightier shall surely expel the humbler from it." So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً ـ فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ (Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them...). The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I shall surely increase beyond seventy," so Allah revealed: سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ لَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ — It is the same for them whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them: Allah will never forgive them (63:6).

In the same source: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Mujahid who said: "When إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ was revealed, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I will increase beyond seventy.' Then Allah revealed in the Surah in which the hypocrites are mentioned: فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ (Allah will never forgive them)."

In the same source: Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Abbas: that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When this verse was revealed, I heard my Lord had given me a concession regarding them. By Allah, I will surely seek forgiveness more than seventy times, perhaps Allah will forgive them." So Allah said, out of His intense wrath upon them: سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ ـ لَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ (It is the same for them whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them: Allah will never forgive them. Indeed Allah does not guide the transgressing lot).

I say: There is no doubt that these verses are among those revealed in the latter period of the Prophet's (peace be upon him) era. They were preceded in revelation by the Meccan Surahs in general and certainly by most of the Medinan Surahs and verses. There is no doubt for anyone who ponders the Book of Allah that there is no hope for the salvation of the disbelievers and the hypocrites—who are even worse than them—if they die in their disbelief and hypocrisy. There is no expectation that divine forgiveness will include them, as there are many Meccan and Medinan verses that are explicit and categorical on this matter.

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is too exalted for what Allah revealed to him to be hidden from him, or for him not to trust in the eternal punishment Allah promised them—a definitive promise—such that he would hope to revoke the decreed judgment by persisting before Him, the Exalted, and entreating for their forgiveness.

Nor would it be hidden from him that the alternation in the verse is to clarify futility, and that there is no specificity to the number seventy such that he would hope for their forgiveness if he exceeded seventy.

I wonder, what does the saying of the Exalted in Surah al-Munafiqun: سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ لَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ add to the saying of the Exalted in this verse: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ? In both, Allah, the Glorified, has justified the eternal denial of forgiveness by the fact that they are transgressors and Allah does not guide the transgressing people.

In summary, these narrations and those like them are fabricated and must be discarded.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ahmad, al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Nahhas, Ibn Hibban, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu‘aym in al-Hilyah narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: I heard ‘Umar saying: "When ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy died, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was called to pray over him. He stood over him, and when he stopped, I said: 'Over the enemy of Allah, ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the one who said such-and-such, and the one who said such-and-such?' I was counting his days while the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was smiling. When I had said much, he said: 'O ‘Umar, move back from me. I have been given a choice; it was said to me: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً. If I knew that if I exceeded seventy he would be forgiven, I would have exceeded it.'"

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed over him and walked with him until he stood over his grave until he finished. I was amazed at myself and my boldness toward the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and Allah and His Messenger know best. By Allah, it was but a short time until these two verses were revealed: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ ماتَ أَبَداً وَلا تَقُمْ عَلى قَبْرِهِ — And never pray over any of them when he dies, nor stand at his grave (9:84). So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) never prayed over a hypocrite after him until Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, took his soul.

I say: His (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying in the narration: "If I knew that if I exceeded seventy..." etc., is explicit that he was despairing of forgiveness reaching him. This testifies that the meaning of his saying: "I have been given a choice; it was said to me: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ" is that Allah had alternated the matter and had not forbidden him from seeking forgiveness, not that He gave him a real choice between seeking forgiveness and not doing so, such that it would result in the effectiveness of seeking forgiveness in obtaining pardon or the hope of it.

From this, it is known that his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) seeking forgiveness for ‘Abdullah, his praying over him, and his standing at his grave—if any of that is established—none of it was for the sake of seeking forgiveness and praying for him in earnest, as will come in the narration of al-Qummi. There is further discussion regarding the narrations that will follow.

In the same source: from Ibn Abi Hatim from al-Sha‘bi that ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "I committed a slip in Islam the like of which I have never committed. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) intended to pray over ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy, so I took hold of his garment and said: 'By Allah, Allah did not command you with this. Allah has said: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ ـ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'My Lord has given me a choice, saying: Seek forgiveness for them or do not seek forgiveness for them.' Then the Messenger of Allah sat at the edge of the grave, and the people began saying to his son: 'O Hubab, do such-and-such; O Hubab, do such-and-such.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Al-Hubab is the name of a devil; you are ‘Abdullah.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ (Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them...), it was revealed when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) returned to Medina and ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy fell ill. His son, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abdullah, was a believer. He came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while his father was in the throes of death and said: "O Messenger of Allah, may my father and mother be sacrificed for you! If you do not come to my father, it will be a disgrace upon us." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered upon him while the hypocrites were with him. His son, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abdullah, said: "Seek forgiveness for him," so he sought forgiveness for him.

‘Umar said: "Did Allah not forbid you, O Messenger of Allah, from praying over anyone or seeking forgiveness for him?" The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) turned away from him, so he repeated it. He said to him: "Woe to you! I have been given a choice and I chose. Allah says: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ ـ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ."

When ‘Abdullah died, his son came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah! If you see fit to attend his funeral..." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) attended and stood at his grave. ‘Umar said to him: "O Messenger of Allah, did Allah not forbid you from ever praying over any of them who dies, or from standing at his grave?" The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Woe to you! Do you know what I said? I only said: 'O Allah, fill his grave with fire, and his belly with fire, and join him with the fire.'" Thus, there appeared from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that which he [‘Umar] did not like.

I say: There is a completion of the discussion in the narrations that will reach you under the following verses.

Ayahs

فَرِحَ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ بِمَقْعَدِهِمْ خِلَـٰفَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَرِهُوٓا۟ أَن يُجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَنفِرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَرِّ ۗ قُلْ نَارُ جَهَنَّمَ أَشَدُّ حَرًّا ۚ لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَفْقَهُونَ (٨١)
Those who were left behind boasted for sitting back against [the command of] the Apostle of Allah, and were reluctant to wage jihad with their possessions and persons in the way of Allah, and they said, ‘Do not go forth in this heat.’ Say, The fire of hell is severer in heat, should they understand. (81)
فَلْيَضْحَكُوا۟ قَلِيلًا وَلْيَبْكُوا۟ كَثِيرًا جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٨٢)
So let them laugh a little; much will they weep as a requital for what they used to earn. (82)
فَإِن رَّجَعَكَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَىٰ طَآئِفَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ فَٱسْتَـْٔذَنُوكَ لِلْخُرُوجِ فَقُل لَّن تَخْرُجُوا۟ مَعِىَ أَبَدًا وَلَن تُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ مَعِىَ عَدُوًّا ۖ إِنَّكُمْ رَضِيتُم بِٱلْقُعُودِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ فَٱقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَـٰلِفِينَ (٨٣)
If Allah brings you back [from the battlefront] to a group of them and they seek your permission to go forth, say, ‘You shall never go forth with me, and you shall not fight with me against any enemy. You were indeed pleased to sit back the first time, so sit back with those who stay behind.’ (83)
وَلَا تُصَلِّ عَلَىٰٓ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُم مَّاتَ أَبَدًا وَلَا تَقُمْ عَلَىٰ قَبْرِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٨٤)
And never pray over any of them when he dies, nor stand at his graveside. They indeed defied Allah and His Apostle and died as transgressors. (84)
وَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدُهُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٨٥)
Let not their possessions or their children impress you. Allah only desires to punish them with these in this world, and that their souls may depart while they are faithless. (85)
وَإِذَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ مَعَ رَسُولِهِ ٱسْتَـْٔذَنَكَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلطَّوْلِ مِنْهُمْ وَقَالُوا۟ ذَرْنَا نَكُن مَّعَ ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ (٨٦)
When a surah is sent down [declaring]: ‘Have faith in Allah, and wage jihad along with His Apostle, the affluent among them ask you for leave, and say, ‘Let us remain with those who sit back.’ (86)
رَضُوا۟ بِأَن يَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَوَالِفِ وَطُبِعَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ (٨٧)
They are pleased to be with those who stay back, and their hearts have been sealed. So they do not understand. (87)
لَـٰكِنِ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتُ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٨٨)
But the Apostle and the faithful who are with him wage jihad with their possessions and persons, and to such belong all the blessings, and it is they who are the felicitous. (88)
أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٨٩)
Allah has prepared for them gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great success. (89)
وَجَآءَ ٱلْمُعَذِّرُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ لِيُؤْذَنَ لَهُمْ وَقَعَدَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۚ سَيُصِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٩٠)
Some of the Bedouins who sought to be excused came, so that they may be granted leave [to stay back]; while those who lied to Allah and His Apostle sat back. Soon there shall visit the faithless among them a painful punishment. (90)
لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلضُّعَفَآءِ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرْضَىٰ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ مَا يُنفِقُونَ حَرَجٌ إِذَا نَصَحُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۚ مَا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ مِن سَبِيلٍ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٩١)
There is no blame on the weak, nor on the sick, nor on those who do not find anything to spend, so long as they are sincere to Allah and His Apostle. There is no [cause for] blaming the virtuous, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (91)
وَلَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا مَآ أَتَوْكَ لِتَحْمِلَهُمْ قُلْتَ لَآ أَجِدُ مَآ أَحْمِلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ تَوَلَّوا۟ وَّأَعْيُنُهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ حَزَنًا أَلَّا يَجِدُوا۟ مَا يُنفِقُونَ (٩٢)
Nor [is there any blame] on those to whom, when they came to you to provide them with a mount, you said, ‘I do not find any mount for you,’ and they turned back, their eyes flowing with tears, grieved because they did not find any means to spend. (92)
۞ إِنَّمَا ٱلسَّبِيلُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَـْٔذِنُونَكَ وَهُمْ أَغْنِيَآءُ ۚ رَضُوا۟ بِأَن يَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَوَالِفِ وَطَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٩٣)
The blame lies only on those who ask leave of you [to stay behind] though they are well-off. They are pleased to be with those who stay back; Allah has set a seal on their hearts, so they do not know [the outcome of their conduct]. (93)
يَعْتَذِرُونَ إِلَيْكُمْ إِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ قُل لَّا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكُمْ قَدْ نَبَّأَنَا ٱللَّهُ مِنْ أَخْبَارِكُمْ ۚ وَسَيَرَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٤)
They will offer you excuses when you return to them. Say, ‘Do not make excuses; we will never believe you. Allah has informed us of your state of affairs. Allah and His Apostle will observe your conduct, then you will be returned to the Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, and He will inform you concerning what you used to do.’ (94)
سَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ إِذَا ٱنقَلَبْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لِتُعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۖ فَأَعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ رِجْسٌ ۖ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٩٥)
They will swear to you by Allah when you return to them, that you may leave them alone. So leave them alone. They are indeed filth, and their refuge shall be hell, a requital for what they used to earn. (95)
يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ لِتَرْضَوْا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۖ فَإِن تَرْضَوْا۟ عَنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَرْضَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٩٦)
They swear to you that you may be reconciled to them. But even if you are reconciled to them, Allah shall not be reconciled to the transgressing lot. (96)

(Commentary)

The verses are capable of being connected to the verses preceding them, as they pursue a purpose followed by what came before.

His saying, the Exalted: فَرِحَ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ بِمَقْعَدِهِمْ خِلافَ رَسُولِ اللهِ — Those who were left behind rejoiced in their sitting [at home] in opposition to the Apostle of Allah (9:81). Joy (الفرح) and gladness (السرور) are the opposite of grief (الغم); they are two psychological, experiential states (حالتان نفسيتان وجدانيتان), one pleasurable and the other painful. "Those who were left behind" (المخلفون) is a passive participle (اسم مفعول) from the expression "he left him behind" (خلفه) when he leaves him after him. "Sitting" (المقعد), like "to sit" (القعود), is a verbal noun (مصدر) from "he sat, he sits" (قعد يقعد), and it is a metaphor (كناية) for not going out for jihad (الجهاد) — the striving in the way of Allah.

"Opposition" (الخلاف), like "disagreement" (المخالفة), is a verbal noun from "he opposed, he opposes" (خالف يخالف). It sometimes carries the meaning of "after" (بعد), as has been suggested, and perhaps from this is His saying: وَإِذاً لا يَلْبَثُونَ خِلافَكَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — and then they would not have stayed after you but for a little while (17:76). The linguistic expectation (قياس الكلام) was for it to be said: "in opposition to you" (خِلافَكَ), because the address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); however, it was said: "in opposition to the Apostle of Allah" (خِلافَ رَسُولِ اللهِ) to indicate that they only rejoice in opposing Allah the Magnificent, for the Apostle has no duty except the delivery of the message (البلاغ).

The meaning is: the hypocrites (المنافقون) whom you left behind rejoiced in their failure to go out with you, in opposition to you—or after you—and they were averse to striving with their wealth and their souls in the way of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لا تَنْفِرُوا فِي الْحَرِّ — and they said, ‘Do not go forth in the heat’ (9:81). They addressed others with this to discourage them from following the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and to frustrate his efforts in mobilizing the people for the expedition. Therefore, Allah the Exalted commanded him to respond to their statement by saying: قُلْ نارُ جَهَنَّمَ أَشَدُّ حَرًّا — Say, ‘The fire of hell is severer in heat’ (9:81). That is, if fleeing from the heat by sitting back saves you from it, it will not save you from that which is more severe: the fire of Hell, which is more intense in heat. Indeed, fleeing from this minor [heat] casts you into that severe [heat]. Then, He expressed with His saying: لَوْ كانُوا يَفْقَهُونَ — had they understood (9:81), using the particle "if" (لو) to denote a wish (التمني), signifying despair regarding their discernment (فقههم) and understanding.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلْيَضْحَكُوا قَلِيلاً وَلْيَبْكُوا كَثِيراً جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — So let them laugh a little, and let them weep much, as a reward for what they used to earn (9:82). This is a consequence (تفريع) of their staying behind from jihad with their wealth and souls, and their rejoicing in sitting back from this divine, primordial obligation (الفريضة الإلهية الفطرية) without which there is no happiness for man in his life.

His saying: جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — as a reward for what they used to earn (9:82), where the preposition "with" (الباء) denotes recompense (المقابلة) or causality (السببية), is evidence that the "little laughter" refers to that which is in this world out of joy for staying behind and sitting back, and the like; while the "much weeping" refers to what will be in the Hereafter in the fire of Hell, which is severer in heat. For that which the laughter and weeping are based upon is what was in the previous verse: their rejoicing in staying behind and their transition from the heat of the air to the heat of the fire of Hell.

The meaning is: It is necessary, in view of what they have done and earned, that they laugh and rejoice a little in this world, and that they weep and grieve much in the Hereafter. Thus, the command to laugh and weep is to indicate the necessity of the cause (إيجاب السبب), which is the deeds they earned for that purpose.

As for interpreting the command in His saying: فَلْيَضْحَكُوا — So let them laugh and وَلْيَبْكُوا — and let them weep as a legislative command (الأمر المولوي) to produce a legal obligation (تكليف شرعي), this is not suited by His saying: جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — as a reward for what they used to earn.

It is possible that the intended meaning is the command for both the little laughter and the much weeping to be in this world as a reward for their previous deeds; for these deeds led them to an illusory comfort for a few days—the days of their sitting back in opposition to the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—then to humiliation and ignominy before Allah, His Apostle, and the believers as long as they live in this world, and then to the severe heat of the Fire in the Hereafter after their death.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ رَجَعَكَ اللهُ إِلى طائِفَةٍ مِنْهُمْ فَاسْتَأْذَنُوكَ لِلْخُرُوجِ — So if Allah brings you back to a group of them, and they ask your permission to go forth (9:83) to the end of the verse. The meaning of "sitting back the first time" (القعود أول مرة) is the failure to go out on the first occasion they were required to do so but did not; perhaps this refers to the Battle of Tabuk, as the context (السياق) suggests.

The "those who stay behind" (الخالفين) refers to those who stay behind by nature (بحسب الطبع), such as women, children, the sick, and the chronically ill. It has also been said: those who stay behind without an excuse; and it has been said: the "stay-behinds" (الخالفون) are the people of corruption (أهل الفساد). The rest is clear.

In His saying: فَإِنْ رَجَعَكَ اللهُ إِلى طائِفَةٍ مِنْهُمْ — So if Allah brings you back to a group of them (9:83), there is an indication that this verse and those in its connected context—both preceding and following—were revealed while the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was on his journey and had not yet returned to Medina, which was his journey to Tabuk.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ ماتَ أَبَداً وَلا تَقُمْ عَلى قَبْرِهِ إِنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَماتُوا وَهُمْ فاسِقُونَ — And never pray over any of them when he dies, nor stand at his grave. They indeed disbelieved in Allah and His Apostle, and died while they were transgressors (9:84). This is a prohibition against praying for any of the hypocrites who die and against standing at their graves. The prohibition is justified by the fact that they disbelieved, transgressed, and died in their transgression (فسقهم). The futility (لغوية) of seeking forgiveness for them was similarly justified in His previous saying: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ — Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them (9:80), and likewise in His saying: سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَستَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ لَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ — It is the same for them whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them: Allah will never forgive them. Indeed Allah does not guide the transgressing lot (63:6), also citing disbelief and transgression.

What is gathered from all this is that whoever lacks faith (الإيمان) in Allah due to disbelief (الكفر) taking over his heart and encompassing it, there is no way for him to reach salvation through guidance. These three verses together reveal the futility of seeking forgiveness for the hypocrites, praying over their dead, and standing at their graves to supplicate for them.

The verse also contains an indication that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to pray over the deceased Muslims and stand at their graves to supplicate.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوالُهُمْ وَأَوْلادُهُمْ — And let not their possessions and their children impress you (9:85). Some discussion regarding this verse preceded in the commentary on verse 55 of this Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ أَنْ آمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَجاهِدُوا مَعَ رَسُولِهِ — When a surah is sent down, [saying], ‘Believe in Allah and wage jihad along with His Apostle’ (9:86) to the end of the two verses. "Affluence" (الطول) means power and blessing (النعمة). The "stay-behinds" (الخوالف) are those who remain behind (الخالفون), and the discussion regarding it is the same as before. The rest is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: لكِنِ الرَّسُولُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ جاهَدُوا بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ — But the Apostle and the faithful who are with him wage jihad with their possessions and their persons (9:88). When He censured the hypocrites in the two preceding verses for being content to stay with the "stay-behinds" and for the sealing of their hearts, He made an exception (استدرك) with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and those who believe with him—meaning the true believers whose hearts are purified from the rust of hypocrisy, as evidenced by the contrast with the hypocrites—to praise them for striving with their wealth and souls. That is, they were not content to sit back, nor were their hearts sealed; rather, they attained the happiness of life and the divine light (النور الإلهي) by which they are guided in their conduct, as He, the Exalted, said: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ — Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people (6:122).

Therefore, He followed the speech with His saying: وَأُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْخَيْراتُ وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ — for such there are all the good things, and it is they who are the felicitous (9:88). Thus, for them are all good things—as necessitated by the plural noun with the definite article—including the good life, the light of guidance, martyrdom (الشهادة), and all else that brings one closer to Allah, the Glorified. They are the felicitous (المفلحون) who attain happiness.

His saying, the Exalted: أَعَدَّ اللهُ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي — Allah has prepared for them gardens (9:89). "Preparation" (الإعداد) is to make something ready. He expressed it with "preparation" rather than "promise" because matters are judged by their ends and outcomes. If it were a promise—and it is a promise for all who believed with him—it would be a definitive decree that must be fulfilled, whether the promised ones remained in the purity of their faith and the righteousness of their deeds or changed; and Allah does not break His promise.

Quranic principles do not support this, nor does a sound primordial nature (الفطرة السليمة) accept attributing to Allah, the Glorified, that He would stamp someone with the seal of forgiveness and inevitable Paradise for a righteous deed they performed, and then leave them to do whatever they wish.

Therefore, we find Him, the Glorified, when He makes a promise, He conditions it upon a general description such as faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), with which the beautiful promise revolves, without specifying particular individuals. Otherwise, it would imply a contradiction by combining obligation (التكليف) with absolute security (التأمين), as He, the Exalted, said: وَعَدَ اللهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ جَنَّاتٍ — Allah has promised the faithful, men and women, gardens (9:72), and He, the Exalted, said: مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ — Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves—until He said—وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً — Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward (48:29).

His saying, the Exalted: وَجاءَ الْمُعَذِّرُونَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ لِيُؤْذَنَ لَهُمْ — And there came the excuse-seekers from the Bedouins that they might be granted leave (9:90). It appears that the "excuse-seekers" (المعذرون) are those who truly have an excuse, such as one who finds neither funds nor weapons, as evidenced by His saying: وَقَعَدَ الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا — while those who lied [to Allah and His Apostle] sat back (9:90). The context indicates that the speech contains a comparison of one group to the other to highlight the meanness, vileness, corruption of heart, and wretchedness of the hypocrites. For the religious obligation of jihad and the support of Allah and His Apostle moved the excuse-seekers among the Bedouins to come to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) seeking his permission, while it had no effect on these liars.

His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ عَلَى الضُّعَفاءِ وَلا عَلَى الْمَرْضى وَلا عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ ما يُنْفِقُونَ حَرَجٌ — There is no blame on the weak, nor on the sick, nor on those who do not find anything to spend (9:91). By the "weak" (الضعفاء), as indicated by the context of the verse, is meant those who have no strength for jihad by nature, such as the chronically ill; just as the "sick" (المرضى) have no strength for it due to a temporary physical ailment; and "those who do not find anything to spend" have no strength for it due to lack of wealth and the like.

From these, "straitness" (الحرج) and hardship are lifted—meaning the ruling of obligation which, if imposed, would be a burdensome ruling (حكما حرجيا), as well as the censure and punishment that follow the ruling upon the occurrence of opposition.

Allah the Exalted conditioned the lifting of straitness from them with His saying: إِذا نَصَحُوا لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ — if they are loyal to Allah and His Apostle (9:91). This refers to the censure and punishment for opposition and sitting back; for censure and punishment are only lifted from these excused ones if they are loyal to Allah and His Apostle, sincere and free from deception and betrayal, and do not act in their sitting back as the hypocrites who stayed behind act—by turning matters upside down and corrupting the hearts in the community of believers. Otherwise, they would be subject to the same censure and punishment as the hypocrites.

His saying: ما عَلَى الْمُحْسِنِينَ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ — there is no way [to blame] the virtuous (9:91) serves as the justification (التعليل) for negating straitness from the mentioned groups, provided they are loyal to Allah and His Apostle. That is because they are then "virtuous" (محسنين), and there is no way against the virtuous—meaning no path of authority over them through which they could be reached and afflicted with what they dislike.

Thus, "the way" (السبيل) is a metaphor for their being in safety from any harm that might befall them, as if they are in a fortified fortress with no path to its interior for evil to travel toward them. The sentence is general in its meaning, even if the instance of application is specific.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا عَلَى الَّذِينَ إِذا ما أَتَوْكَ لِتَحْمِلَهُمْ قُلْتَ — Nor [is there any blame] on those to whom, when they came to you that you should provide them with mounts, you said... (9:92). It is said in al-Majma': "Providing a mount" (الحمل) is giving a riding animal, whether a horse, camel, or otherwise. You say: "he carried him" (حمله يحمله حملا) when he gives him that which he may ride upon. He said:

Is there not a youth who has two shoes to carry me Upon them? For I am an old man on a journey.

He said: "Overflowing" (الفيض) is flowing from fullness, from their saying: "the vessel overflowed with what was in it." "Grief" (الحزن) is pain in the heart for the loss of something, derived from "rugged land" (حزن الأرض), which is land that is difficult to traverse. [End quote].

His saying: وَلا عَلَى الَّذِينَ — Nor on those (9:92) is a relative pronoun whose relative clause is His saying: تَوَلَّوْا — they turned back. His saying: إِذا ما أَتَوْكَ لِتَحْمِلَهُمْ — when they came to you that you should provide them with mounts is like a condition and its response, and the whole functions as an adverbial phrase (ظرف) for "they turned back" (تَوَلَّوْا). "Grief" (حزنا) is an object of purpose (مفعول له), and "that they did not find" (أَلَّا يَجِدُوا) is in the accusative case due to the removal of the preposition (بنزع الخافض).

The meaning is: and there is no straitness upon the poor who, when they came to you so that you might give them a mount to ride and provide for their other needs such as weapons and the like, you said: "I do not find that which I may carry you upon," they turned back while their eyes were filling and pouring with tears out of grief that they did not find—or because they did not find—what they could spend in the way of Allah for jihad against His enemies.

The coupling (عطف) of this category to what preceded it is the coupling of the specific to the general, out of special regard for them, because they are at the highest level of loyalty and their virtue is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا السَّبِيلُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَسْتَأْذَنُوكَ وَهُمْ أَغْنِياءُ — The way [to blame] is only against those who ask your leave [to stay behind] while they are rich (9:93). The restriction (القصر) is for isolation (للإفراد), and the meaning is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: يَعْتَذِرُونَ إِلَيْكُمْ إِذا رَجَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ — They will offer you excuses when you return to them (9:94) to the end of the verse. The plural address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and all the believers. His saying: لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكُمْ — we will never believe you (9:94) means: we will never believe you regarding the excuses you offer—based on the verb "to believe" (الإيمان) being transitive with the preposition "to" (اللام) like "with" (الباء)—or: we will never believe with a belief that benefits you—based on the "to" (اللام) being for benefit. The sentence is a justification for His saying: لا تَعْتَذِرُوا — Do not make excuses, just as His saying: قَدْ نَبَّأَنَا اللهُ مِنْ أَخْبارِكُمْ — Allah has already informed us of your state (9:94) is a justification for this sentence.

The meaning is: the hypocrites will offer you excuses when you return to them from the expedition. Say to them, O Muhammad: Do not make excuses to us, for we will never believe you in what you excuse yourselves with, because Allah has already informed us of some of your news through which your hypocrisy and your lying in your excuses become manifest. Your deeds will soon be seen with the witnessing of Allah and His Apostle, then you will be returned to Allah, who knows the Unseen and the manifest on the Day of Resurrection, and He will inform you of the realities of your deeds.

Regarding His saying: وَسَيَرَى اللهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُ — And Allah and His Apostle will see your conduct (9:94), a discussion clarifying it will follow shortly.

His saying, the Exalted: سَيَحْلِفُونَ بِاللهِ لَكُمْ إِذَا انْقَلَبْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لِتُعْرِضُوا عَنْهُمْ فَأَعْرِضُوا عَنْهُمْ — They will swear to you by Allah when you return to them so that you may leave them alone. So leave them alone (9:95). That is, so that you may turn away from them and not confront them with blame, rebuke, and what follows that. So turn away from them, not out of believing them in the excuses they swear to, but because they are a "pollution" (رجس) that should not be approached, and their abode is Hell as a reward for what they used to earn.

His saying, the Exalted: يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ لِتَرْضَوْا عَنْهُمْ فَإِنْ تَرْضَوْا عَنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَرْضى عَنِ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ — They will swear to you that you may be pleased with them. Even if you are pleased with them, Allah will not be pleased with the transgressing lot (9:96). That is, this swearing of theirs, just as it was to seek a means to turn you away from them so they might be safe from blame and rebuke, is likewise to seek a means to your pleasure with them. As for turning away, do so because they are a pollution, and it is not fitting for the purity and cleanliness of faith to be exposed to the pollution of hypocrisy and lying and the filth of disbelief and transgression. As for pleasure, know that if you are pleased with them, Allah is not pleased with them due to their transgression, and Allah is not pleased with the transgressing lot.

The meaning is that if you are pleased with them, you have been pleased with those with whom Allah is not pleased—meaning you have been pleased in opposition to Allah's pleasure. This is the most eloquent metaphor for the prohibition of being pleased with the hypocrites.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَرِحَ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ — Those who were left behind rejoiced (9:81): Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his father (peace be upon him), who said: "The Battle of Tabuk was the last expedition the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) fought, and it was the 'Expedition of Heat'—قالُوا لا تَنْفِرُوا فِي الْحَرِّ — they said, ‘Do not go forth in the heat’—and it was the 'Expedition of Hardship' (غزوة العسرة)."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas: "The Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded the people to set out with him—and that was in the summer. Some men said: 'O Apostle of Allah, the heat is severe and we cannot go out'—فلا تنفروا في الحر — so do not go forth in the heat. So Allah said: قُلْ نارُ جَهَنَّمَ أَشَدُّ حَرًّا لَوْ كانُوا يَفْقَهُونَ — Say, ‘The fire of hell is severer in heat,’ had they understood. So He commanded him to go out."

I say: The apparent meaning of the verse is that they only said it to discourage the people from going out, while the apparent meaning of the hadith is that they said it as a suggestion; thus, they do not coincide.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir narrated from Muhammad ibn Ka'b al-Qurazi and others, who said: "The Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went out in severe heat to Tabuk. A man from Banu Salimah said: 'Do not go forth in the heat.' So Allah revealed: قُلْ نارُ جَهَنَّمَ أَشَدُّ حَرًّا — Say, ‘The fire of hell is severer in heat’ (9:81)."

I say: Narrations preceded regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ ائْذَنْ لِي وَلا تَفْتِنِّي — Among them is he who says, ‘Give me leave, and do not put me into ordeal’ (9:49), stating that the one who said: لا تَنْفِرُوا فِي الْحَرِّ — Do not go forth in the heat was Jadd ibn Qays.
