In al-Durr al-Manthur as well: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ — And never pray over any of them (9:84): al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn al-Mundhir, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated from Ibn Umar, who said: "When Abd Allah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul died, his son Abd Allah came to the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asking him to give him his shirt to shroud him in, so he gave it to him. Then he asked him to pray over him, so the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stood up.

Umar ibn al-Khattab stood up and took hold of his garment and said: 'O Apostle of Allah, will you pray over him when Allah has forbidden you to pray over the hypocrites?' He said: 'My Lord has given me a choice, saying: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ ـ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ — Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them: even if you plead for forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will never forgive them (9:80), and I shall increase upon seventy.' He said: 'He is a hypocrite!' But he prayed over him. Then Allah the Exalted revealed: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ ماتَ أَبَداً وَلا تَقُمْ عَلى قَبْرِهِ — And never pray over any of them when he dies, nor stand at his grave (9:84). So he ceased praying over them."

I say: Regarding this meaning, there are other narrations recorded by the compilers of the Jami' works and narrators of hadith from Umar ibn al-Khattab, Jabir, and Qatadah. In some of them, it is mentioned that he shrouded him in his shirt, blew into his skin, and descended into his grave.

In the same source, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Nahhas, Ibn Hibban, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu'aym in al-Hilyah narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "I heard Umar saying: 'When Abd Allah ibn Ubayy died, the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was called to pray over him. He stood over him, and when he stopped, I said: "Will you pray over the enemy of Allah, Abd Allah ibn Ubayy, the one who said such-and-such and such-and-such?"—I was counting his days—while the Apostle of Allah was smiling. When I persisted, he said: "O Umar, move back from me, for I have been given a choice. It has been said to me: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً — Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them: even if you plead for forgiveness for them seventy times. If I knew that if I increased upon seventy he would be forgiven, I would increase upon it." Then the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed over him and walked with him until he stood at his grave until he finished from it.

I was amazed at myself and my boldness toward the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and Allah and His Apostle know best. By Allah, it was but a short while until these two verses were revealed: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ ماتَ أَبَداً وَلا تَقُمْ عَلى قَبْرِهِ — And never pray over any of them when he dies, nor stand at his grave. So the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) never prayed over a hypocrite after him until Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, took his soul.'"

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Sha'bi that Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "I committed a slip in Islam the like of which I have never committed. The Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) intended to pray over Abd Allah ibn Ubayy, so I took hold of his garment and said: 'By Allah, Allah has not commanded you with this! Allah has said: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ ـ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ — Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them: even if you plead for forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will never forgive them.' The Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'My Lord has given me a choice, saying: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ — Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them.'

The Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sat at the edge of the grave, and the people began saying to his son: 'O Hubab, do such-and-such; O Hubab, do such-and-such.' The Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Al-Hubab is the name of a devil; you are Abd Allah.'"

In the same source, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated from Ibn Abbas: "The son of Abd Allah ibn Ubayy was told by his father: 'Request for me a garment from the garments of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) so that you may shroud me in it, and tell him to pray over me.' He said: 'So he came to him and said: "O Apostle of Allah, you have known the nobility of Abd Allah, and he requests from you a garment of your garments to shroud him in and that you pray over him."'

Umar said: 'O Apostle of Allah, you have known Abd Allah and his hypocrisy. Will you pray over him when Allah has forbidden you to pray over him?' He said: 'And where?' He said: 'اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ ـ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ — Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them: even if you plead for forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will never forgive them.' He said: 'Then I shall increase upon seventy.' So Allah revealed: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ ماتَ أَبَداً وَلا تَقُمْ عَلى قَبْرِهِ — And never pray over any of them when he dies, nor stand at his grave (9:84). He said: 'So he sent for Umar and informed him of that.' And Allah revealed: سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ — It is the same for them whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them (63:6)."

I say: The Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) seeking forgiveness for Abd Allah ibn Ubayy and his praying over him has also been mentioned in some mursal (مرسيل) — narrations with an incomplete chain of transmission — among the Shi'a narrations, cited by al-Ayyashi and al-Qummi in their commentaries; the report of al-Qummi has preceded.

These narrations, despite the contradictions and inconsistencies they contain and the conflict between them, are clearly and undeniably refuted by the noble verses:

Firstly: Because of the clear appearance of His saying, the Exalted: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَهُمْ — Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them: even if you plead for forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will never forgive them (9:80), which clearly shows that the purpose of the verse is to state the futility of seeking forgiveness for the hypocrites, not to offer a choice. The number was mentioned for the sake of hyperbole (مبالغة الكثرة), not because of a specific property in the number seventy such that forgiveness could be hoped for with more than seventy.

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is far too exalted to be ignorant of this indication, to interpret the verse as a choice, then to say "I shall increase upon seventy," and then to be reminded by someone else of the meaning of the verse while he persists in his ignorance until Allah forbids him from the prayer and other things with another verse He reveals to him.

Furthermore, all these verses dealing with seeking forgiveness for the hypocrites and praying over them—such as His saying: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ — Whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them (9:80), His saying: سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ — It is the same for them whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them (63:6), and His saying: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ ماتَ أَبَداً — And never pray over any of them when he dies (9:84)—justify the prohibition and the futility by their disbelief and transgression. Even His saying, the Exalted, regarding the prohibition of seeking forgiveness for the polytheists: ما كانَ لِلنَّبِيِّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ يَسْتَغْفِرُوا لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَلَوْ كانُوا أُولِي قُرْبى مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ — The Prophet and the faithful may not plead for forgiveness for the polytheists, even if they should be [their] relatives, after it has become clear to them that they are the inmates of hell (9:113), prohibits seeking forgiveness while justifying it by disbelief and the eternity of the Fire. How can the permissibility of seeking forgiveness for them and praying over them be conceived alongside this?

Secondly: The context of the verses, including His saying: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ ماتَ أَبَداً — And never pray over any of them when he dies (9:84), is explicit that this verse was revealed while the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was on his journey to Tabuk and had not yet returned to Medina, which was in the year 9 AH. The death of Abd Allah ibn Ubayy occurred in Medina in the year 9 AH; all of this is accepted through transmission.

So what is the meaning of the statement in these narrations that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed over Abd Allah and stood at his grave, and then Allah revealed to him: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ ماتَ أَبَداً — And never pray over any of them when he dies (9:84)?

Even more amazing is what occurred in some of the previous narrations that Umar said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Will you pray over him when He has forbidden you from praying for the hypocrites?" and he said: "My Lord has given me a choice," then Allah revealed: وَلا تُصَلِّ عَلى أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ — And never pray over any of them (9:84).

And even more amazing is what is in the last narration regarding the revelation of His saying: سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ — It is the same for them whether you plead for forgiveness for them or do not plead for forgiveness for them (63:6). This verse is from Surah al-Munafiqun, which was revealed after the Battle of Banu al-Mustaliq in the year 5 AH, while Abd Allah ibn Ubayy was still alive then, and his saying is recounted in the Surah: لَئِنْ رَجَعْنا إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ الْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا الْأَذَلَ — ‘If we return to the city, the mightier will surely expel the abased from it’ (63:8).

Some of these narrations—and some who defend them have relied on this—claim that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only sought forgiveness and prayed over Abd Allah to win over the hearts of some hypocritical men from the Khazraj to Islam. How can that be correct? How can it be proper for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to oppose the explicit text of the verses to win over the hearts of the hypocrites and compromise with them? Allah has threatened him for such a thing with the most profound threat in His saying: إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَياةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَماتِ — then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death (17:75). Therefore, the correct view is that these narrations are fabricated (موضوعة) and must be discarded due to their opposition to the Book.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: regarding His saying: رَضُوا بِأَنْ يَكُونُوا مَعَ الْخَوالِفِ — They were content to be with those who stay behind (9:87): Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas: "Ali ibn Abi Talib went out with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) until he reached Thaniyyat al-Wada' intending for Tabuk. Ali was weeping and saying: 'Are you leaving me behind with the stay-behinds (الخوالف)?' The Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Are you not pleased to be to me as Aaron was to Moses, except for prophethood?'"

I say: The narration is reported through many chains from both sides.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Jabir, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: رَضُوا بِأَنْ يَكُونُوا مَعَ الْخَوالِفِ — They were content to be with those who stay behind (9:87), he said: "With the women."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd al-Razzaq in al-Musannaf, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas: "When the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) returned from the Battle of Tabuk and overlooked Medina, he said: 'You have left behind in Medina men who—whenever you traveled a journey, or spent any expenditure, or crossed a valley—were with you in it.' They said: 'O Apostle of Allah, how can they be with us while they are in Medina?' He said: 'An excuse detained them.'"

In al-Majma': regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ عَلَى الضُّعَفاءِ وَلا عَلَى الْمَرْضى — There is no blame on the weak, nor on the sick (9:91-92): It is said that the first verse was revealed concerning Abd Allah ibn Za'idah—who is Ibn Umm Maktum—and he was blind. He came to the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Prophet of Allah, I am a blind old man, of meager means and thin body, and I have no guide. Is there a concession for me to stay behind from jihad?" The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained silent, and then Allah revealed the verse. This is from al-Dahhak. It is also said: it was revealed concerning A'idh ibn Amr and his companions. This is from Qatadah.

The second verse was revealed concerning "the Weepers" (البكاءين), and they were seven individuals: among them were Abd al-Rahman ibn Ka'b, Ulbah ibn Zayd, and Amr ibn Tha'labah ibn Ghanmah—these were from Banu al-Najjar; Salim ibn Umayr, Harami ibn Abd Allah, Abd Allah ibn Amr ibn Awf [or] and Abd Allah ibn Mughaffal from Muzaynah. They came to the Apostle of Allah and said: "O Apostle of Allah, provide us with mounts, for we have nothing to go out upon." He said: "I do not find that which I may carry you upon." This is from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali.

It is also said: it was revealed concerning seven from various tribes who came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said to him: "Provide us with leather socks and sandals." This is from Muhammad ibn Ka'b and Ibn Ishaq.

It is also said: they were a group from Muzaynah. This is from Mujahid. It is also said: they were seven of the poor among the Ansar; when they wept, Uthman provided mounts for two of them, al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib for two, and Yamin ibn Ka'b al-Nadri for three. This is from al-Waqidi, who said: "The people at Tabuk with the Apostle of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were thirty thousand, among them ten thousand horsemen."

I say: The narrations regarding the names of "the Weepers" are severely conflicting.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, he said: "These Weepers only asked for sandals to wear."

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain from Tha'labah, from some of our companions, from Abu Abd Allah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ — Knower of the Unseen and the manifest (9:94), he said: "The Unseen (الغيب) is what has not yet been, and the manifest (الشهادة) is what has already been."

I say: This is by way of showing some instances (المصاديق), while the term is more general.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, he said: "When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrived from Tabuk, his believing companions would confront the hypocrites and annoy them. So Allah revealed: سَيَحْلِفُونَ بِاللهِ لَكُمْ إِذَا انْقَلَبْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لِتُعْرِضُوا عَنْهُمْ — They will swear to you by Allah when you return to them so that you may leave them alone (9:95) to the end of the two verses."

In al-Majma': It is said: the verses were revealed concerning Jadd ibn Qays, Mut'ib ibn Qushayr, and their companions among the hypocrites; they were eighty men. When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrived in Medina returning from Tabuk, he said: "Do not sit with them and do not speak to them." This is from Ibn Abbas.

Ayahs

ٱلْأَعْرَابُ أَشَدُّ كُفْرًا وَنِفَاقًا وَأَجْدَرُ أَلَّا يَعْلَمُوا۟ حُدُودَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٩٧)
The Bedouins are more obdurate in unfaith and hypocrisy, and more apt to be ignorant of the precepts that Allah has sent down to His Apostle, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (97)
وَمِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مَن يَتَّخِذُ مَا يُنفِقُ مَغْرَمًا وَيَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمُ ٱلدَّوَآئِرَ ۚ عَلَيْهِمْ دَآئِرَةُ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٩٨)
Among the Bedouins are those who regard what they spend as a loss, and they watch for a reversal of your fortunes. Theirs shall be an adverse turn of fortune, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (98)
وَمِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَيَتَّخِذُ مَا يُنفِقُ قُرُبَـٰتٍ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَصَلَوَٰتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ ۚ أَلَآ إِنَّهَا قُرْبَةٌ لَّهُمْ ۚ سَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٩٩)
Yet among the Bedouins are [also] those who believe in Allah and the Last Day, and regard what they spend as [a means of attaining] nearness to Allah and the blessings of the Apostle. Now, it shall indeed bring them nearness, and Allah will admit them into His mercy. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (99)
وَٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ وَٱلْأَنصَارِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُم بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ رَّضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى تَحْتَهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١٠٠)
The early vanguard of the Emigrants and the Helpers and those who followed them in virtue—Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him, and He has prepared for them gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. That is the great success. (100)
وَمِمَّنْ حَوْلَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مُنَـٰفِقُونَ ۖ وَمِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ ۖ مَرَدُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنِّفَاقِ لَا تَعْلَمُهُمْ ۖ نَحْنُ نَعْلَمُهُمْ ۚ سَنُعَذِّبُهُم مَّرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَذَابٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٠١)
There are hypocrites among the Bedouins around you and among the townspeople of Madinah, steeped in hypocrisy. You do not know them; We know them, and We will punish them twice, then they shall be consigned to a great punishment. (101)
وَءَاخَرُونَ ٱعْتَرَفُوا۟ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ خَلَطُوا۟ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا وَءَاخَرَ سَيِّئًا عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٠٢)
[There are] others who have confessed to their sins, having mixed up righteous conduct with other that was evil. Maybe Allah will accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (102)
خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ صَدَقَةً تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَتُزَكِّيهِم بِهَا وَصَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ إِنَّ صَلَوٰتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَّهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٠٣)
Take charity from their possessions to cleanse them and purify them thereby, and bless them. Indeed your blessing is a comfort to them, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (103)
أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ يَقْبَلُ ٱلتَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَأْخُذُ ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٠٤)
Do they not know that it is Allah who accepts the repentance of His servants and receives the charities, and that it is Allah who is the All-clement, the All-merciful? (104)
وَقُلِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ فَسَيَرَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۖ وَسَتُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٠٥)
And say, ‘Go on working: Allah will see your conduct, and His Apostle and the faithful [as well], and you will be returned to the Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, and He will inform you concerning what you used to do. (105)
وَءَاخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٠٦)
[There are] others waiting Allah’s edict: either He shall punish them, or turn to them clemently, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (106)

(Commentary)

The discourse continues according to the previous purpose, clarifying the state of the desert Arabs (الأعراب) regarding their disbelief, hypocrisy, and faith. Interspersed within these verses is the verse concerning alms (الصدقة).

His saying, the Exalted: الْأَعْرابُ أَشَدُّ كُفْراً وَنِفاقاً وَأَجْدَرُ أَلَّا يَعْلَمُوا حُدُودَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ — The bedouins are more obdurate in unfaith and hypocrisy, and more apt not to know the bounds of what Allah has sent down to His Apostle. (9:97). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The Arabs (العرب) are the descendants of Isma'il. The term al-A'rab (الأعراب) was originally its plural, but it became a name for the inhabitants of the desert (سكان البادية), as in: قالَتِ الْأَعْرابُ آمَنَّا — The bedouins say, ‘We have faith.’ (49:14), and: الْأَعْرابُ أَشَدُّ كُفْراً وَنِفاقاً — The bedouins are more obdurate in unfaith and hypocrisy. (9:97), and: وَمِنَ الْأَعْرابِ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ — Yet among the bedouins are [also] those who believe in Allah and the Last Day. (9:99). It is said that the plural of al-A'rab is A'arib (أعاريب); the poet said:

A'arib (أعاريب) who possess pride through falsehood, And tongues that are subtle in speech.

In common usage, the term al-A'rabi (الأعرابي) became a name for one attributed to the desert dwellers, while al-'Arabi (العربي) refers to the eloquent one. Al-I'rab (الإعراب) means clarification (البيان)." This concludes the relevant portion of his words.

The Exalted clarifies the state of the desert dwellers: that they are more intense in disbelief (الكفر) and hypocrisy (النفاق) because, due to their distance from urbanity and civilization (الحضارة) and their deprivation of the blessings of humanity—such as knowledge (العلم) and refinement (الأدب)—they are harsher and more coarse. Thus, they are more deserving and likely not to know the limits of what Allah has sent down of the foundational gnoses (المعارف الأصلية) and the legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية), including the obligations (الفرائض), the prophetic traditions (السنن), and the lawful (الحلال) and prohibited (الحرام).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ الْأَعْرابِ مَنْ يَتَّخِذُ ما يُنْفِقُ مَغْرَماً وَيَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمُ الدَّوائِرَ — Among the bedouins are those who regard what they spend as a loss, and they wait for some misfortunes to befall you. (9:98). It is stated in al-Majma': "The term al-maghram (المغرم) is the same as al-ghurm (الغرم), which is the occurrence of a financial calamity (نائبة) without any betrayal. Its root is the necessity (لزوم) of a matter; from it is His saying: إِنَّ عَذابَها كانَ غَراماً — Indeed its punishment is everlasting. (25:65). A 'devouring love' (حب غرام) is one that is constant. The term al-gharim (الغريم) — the creditor — is applied to each of two transacting parties because of the necessity of one to the other. I 'fined' him (غرمته) such-and-such means I made it a necessity upon him in his wealth." This concludes his words.

The 'turn of fortune' (الدائرة) refers to an event (الحادثة), and it is predominantly used for evil events, as if evil events circulate among people, descending upon a group each day. Thus, "waiting for turns of fortune (الدوائر) to befall the believers" means waiting for evil events to descend upon them so as to escape their authority and return to the customs of polytheism (الشرك) and misguidance (الضلال).

His saying: يَتَّخِذُ ما يُنْفِقُ مَغْرَماً — regard what they spend as a loss — means he considers the act of spending a fine (غرماً), or the wealth he spends as a loss—whether the word ma (ما) is infinitival or relative. What is meant is spending in struggle (الجهاد) or any of the paths of goodness, as has been said. It is possible that what is meant is spending specifically on alms (الصدقات), so that the discourse serves as a preamble to what will come after several verses regarding the ruling on taking alms from their wealth. This is supported by what follows in the next verse: وَيَتَّخِذُ ما يُنْفِقُ قُرُباتٍ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَصَلَواتِ الرَّسُولِ — and regard what they spend as a means of nearness to Allah and the blessings of the Apostle. (9:99), for it is like a preamble to His saying in the verse of alms: وَصَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّ صَلاتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَهُمْ — and bless them; indeed your blessing is a comfort to them. (9:103).

The meaning of the verse is: Among the desert dwellers are those who consider spending in the path of good, or specifically on alms, as a fine and a loss, and they wait for evil events to descend upon you. Upon them shall be the evil turn of fortune—either as a decree from the Exalted or a supplication against them—and Allah is All-Hearing (سميع) of words, All-Knowing (عليم) of hearts.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ الْأَعْرابِ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَيَتَّخِذُ ما يُنْفِقُ قُرُباتٍ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَصَلَواتِ الرَّسُولِ — Yet among the bedouins are [also] those who believe in Allah and the Last Day, and regard what they spend as a means of nearness to Allah and the blessings of the Apostle. (9:99). It appears that His saying: صَلَواتِ الرَّسُولِ — the blessings of the Apostle — is conjoined to ما يُنْفِقُ — what they spend, and that the pronoun in His saying: أَلا إِنَّها قُرْبَةٌ — Look! It is indeed a means of nearness for them — refers back to both the spending and the blessings of the Apostle.

The meaning of the verse is: Among the desert Arabs are those who believe in Allah, professing His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — without any polytheism, and believe in the Last Day, affirming the Reckoning (الحساب) and the Recompense (الجزاء). They regard the spending of wealth for Allah and the accompanying blessings and supplications of the Apostle for goodness and barakah as means of nearness (قربات) to Allah and ways of approaching Him. Indeed, this spending and the blessings of the Apostle are a means of nearness for them, and Allah promises them that He will admit them into His mercy, for He is All-Forgiving (غفور) of sins, Most Merciful (رحيم) to those who believe in Him and obey Him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ الْمُهاجِرِينَ وَالْأَنْصارِ وَالَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُمْ بِإِحْسانٍ — The early vanguard of the Emigrants and the Helpers and those who followed them in virtue (9:100). The famous recitation is وَالْأَنْصارِ — the Helpers — in the genitive case, conjoined to الْمُهاجِرِينَ — the Emigrants. The implied meaning is: the early vanguard of the Emigrants, the early vanguard of the Helpers, and those who followed them in virtue. Ya'qub recited وَالْأَنْصارُ in the nominative case, in which case it would refer to all the Helpers, not just the early vanguard among them.

Opinions have differed regarding who is meant by "the early vanguard" (السابقون الأولون). It has been said they are those who prayed toward both Qiblahs; it has been said they are those who gave the Pledge of Ridwan (بيعة الرضوان), which is the Pledge of Hudaybiyyah; it has been said they are specifically the people of Badr; and it has been said they are those who embraced Islam before the Hijrah. These are all views for which no linguistic evidence has been provided.

What the wording of the verse may somewhat support is that the description of the subject—the early vanguard—by one attribute after another, without mentioning specific individuals, suggests that the Hijrah and the Support (النصرة) are the two aspects in which precedence (السبق) and primacy (الأولية) were considered.

Furthermore, those conjoined to them in His saying: وَالَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُمْ بِإِحْسانٍ — and those who followed them in virtue — mentions a group characterized by "following" (الاتباع) and restricts it to being "in virtue" (بإحسان). What suits the description of following is that it succeeds the description of "precedence" (السبق) rather than "primacy" (الأولية). One does not say "the first and the follower," but rather "the predecessor (سابق) and the follower (تابع)." This is confirmed by His saying: لِلْفُقَراءِ الْمُهاجِرِينَ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ وَأَمْوالِهِمْ — [They are also] for the poor Emigrants who have been expelled from their homes and [deprived of] their possessions until He says: وَالَّذِينَ تَبَوَّؤُا الدَّارَ وَالْإِيمانَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ — [They are also for] those who were settled in the land and [abided] in faith before them until He says: وَالَّذِينَ جاؤُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ : رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لَنا وَلِإِخْوانِنَا الَّذِينَ سَبَقُونا بِالْإِيمانِ — And [also for] those who came after them, who say, ‘Our Lord, forgive us and our brethren who preceded us in faith’ (59:8-10).

Thus, the "predecessors" (السابقون) are those who preceded in faith among the Muslims from the dawn of Islam until the Day of Resurrection.

Since precedence (السبق)—and its opposites, succeeding (اللحوق) and following (الاتباع)—are relative matters, necessitating that the Muslims of every era are predecessors in faith relative to those after them, just as they are successors relative to those before them, the term "predecessors" (السابقون) was restricted by His saying "the early" (الأولون) to indicate that the intended predecessors are the first generation among them.

When Allah, the Glorified, mentioned the third of the three categories with His saying: وَالَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُمْ بِإِحْسانٍ — and those who followed them in virtue — and did not restrict it to the followers of one era over another, nor described them with advancement or primacy, and it was inclusive of all who follow the early vanguard, it follows that the believers (excluding the hypocrites) from the day of the Mission until the Day of Resurrection are classified in the verse into three categories: the early vanguard of the Emigrants, the early vanguard of the Helpers, and those who followed them in virtue. The first two categories lack the attribute of "following"; rather, they are two leaders (إمامان) followed by others, while the third category is not followed except relatively.

This is an excellent proof that the "early vanguard" are those who established the foundation of the religion and raised its pillars before its structure was fortified and its banners fluttered. One group did so by believing, joining the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), enduring trials and torture, and leaving their homes and wealth by migrating to Abyssinia and Medina. The other group did so by believing and supporting the Messenger, providing him refuge, and sheltering those believers who migrated to them, defending the religion before the major battles occurred.

This applies to those who believed in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) before the Hijrah and then migrated before the Battle of Badr—from which the triumph of Islam over disbelief began—or those who believed in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), gave him refuge, and prepared to support him when he migrated to Medina.

Furthermore, in His saying: وَالَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُمْ بِإِحْسانٍ — and those who followed them in virtue — He restricted their following to being "in virtue" (بإحسان). He did not mean "following in doing good"—as in the predecessors being doers of good and the followers following them in their good deeds and emulating them therein (taking the preposition bi to mean in)—nor did He mean "following by means of virtue" (taking the bi for causality or instrumentality). Rather, "virtue" (الإحسان) was mentioned in an indefinite form (منكراً), and it is most appropriate for the bi to denote accompaniment (المصاحبة). Thus, the meaning is that the following should be coupled with a certain type of virtue and accompanied by it; in other words, virtue is an attribute of the following itself.

We find that the Exalted, in His Book, does not censure "following" except when it is based on ignorance or whim (الهوى), such as the polytheists following their fathers, or the People of the Book following their rabbis, monks, and ancestors out of whim, or following whim itself or following Satan. Whoever follows any of these has "followed poorly" (أساء في الاتباع). But whoever follows the truth, not out of a whim related to persons or otherwise, has "followed virtuously" (أحسن في الاتباع). The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَداهُمُ اللهُ — those who listen to the word [of Allah] and follow the best [interpretation] of it. It is they whom Allah has guided (39:18). Part of "virtue in following" is the perfect correspondence of the follower's action to the action of the one followed, while its opposite is "evil in following."

Thus, it appears that the meaning of "those who followed them in virtue" is that they follow them with a type of virtue in the act of following itself, which is that the following be based on truth—following them because the truth is with them—which ultimately returns to following the truth in reality, as opposed to following them out of a whim for them or in the act of following them, as well as maintaining correspondence.

This is what appears from the meaning of "following in virtue." As for what has been mentioned—that it means the following is accompanied by virtue in the follower's actions, such that he performs righteous deeds and good acts—this does not fully harmonize with the indefinite form (التنكير) indicating a specific type of virtue. Even if we were to accept it, there is no escape from restricting it to what we mentioned, for following the truth and in the truth necessitates performing good, righteous deeds, but not vice versa, and this is evident.

In summary, the verse divides the believers of this community (الأمة) into three categories: two categories are the early vanguard of the Emigrants and the Helpers, and the third category consists of those who followed them in virtue.

From what has preceded, several points have become clear: First: The verse praises the first two categories for their precedence in faith and their advancement in establishing the core of the religion and raising its foundation, favoring them over others as the context implies.

Second: The word "from" (مِنَ) in His saying: مِنَ الْمُهاجِرِينَ وَالْأَنْصارِ — of the Emigrants and the Helpers — is partitive (تبعيضية), not explicative (بيانية), due to the aforementioned reason for their merit, and because the verse mentions that Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him. The Quran itself mentions that among them are those in whose hearts is a sickness, those who listen to the hypocrites, those whom it calls "transgressors" (فاسقاً), and those from whose actions the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) disassociated himself. There is no meaning to Allah being pleased with such people, for Allah is not pleased with transgressing people.

Third: The judgment of merit and the pleasure of Allah, the Glorified, in the verse is restricted by faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), as the context dictates. The verse praises the believers in a context where it censures the hypocrites for their disbelief and evil deeds. This is indicated by all other instances where Allah praises them or mentions them favorably and gives them a beautiful promise; He has restricted all of that to faith and righteous deeds, such as His saying: لِلْفُقَراءِ الْمُهاجِرِينَ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ وَأَمْوالِهِمْ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلاً مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْواناً وَيَنْصُرُونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ — [They are also] for the poor Emigrants who have been expelled from their homes and [deprived of] their possessions, seeking Allah’s grace and pleasure, and helping Allah and His Apostle until the end of the three verses (59:8).

And His saying regarding what He narrated of the angels' supplication for them: وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَحْمَةً وَعِلْماً فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ رَبَّنا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدْتَهُمْ وَمَنْ صَلَحَ مِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَأَزْواجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ — and plead for forgiveness for the faithful: ‘Our Lord! You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge. So forgive those who repent and follow Your way, and save them from the punishment of hell. Our Lord! Admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them, along with whoever is righteous among their forebears, their spouses, and their descendants’ (40:7-8).

And His saying: مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ — Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves until He says: وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً — Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward (48:29).

And His saying: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ — As for those who have faith and whose descendants followed them in faith, We will unite their descendants with them, and We will not stint anything of their conduct. Every man is a hostage to what he has earned (52:21). Note the placement of His saying: بِإِيمانٍ — in faith — and His saying: كُلُّ امْرِئٍ — Every man... etc.

If the judgment in the verse were not restricted by the condition of faith and righteous deeds, and they were pleasing to Allah and forgiven whether they did good or evil, were God-conscious or transgressed, it would be an explicit contradiction of His saying: فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَرْضى عَنِ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ — for Allah is not pleased with the transgressing folk (9:96), and His saying: وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ — and Allah does not guide the transgressing folk (9:80), and His saying: وَاللهُ لا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ — and Allah does not love the wrongdoers (3:57), among many other verses indicating directly or by implication that Allah is not pleased with the wrongdoer, the transgressor, or anyone who does not obey Him in a command or prohibition. These verses are not of the type that accept restriction (التقييد) or abrogation (النسخ). Similarly, His saying addressing the believers: لَيْسَ بِأَمانِيِّكُمْ وَلا أَمانِيِّ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً يُجْزَ بِهِ — It will be neither according to your fancies nor according to the fancies of the People of the Book: whoever commits an evil shall be requited for it (4:123).

Furthermore, the consequence of not restricting the judgment in this verse would be the restriction of all verses indicating recompense and containing warnings and threats. These are numerous verses; restricting them would disrupt the system of promise and threat, invalidate most of the rulings and laws, and nullify wisdom. There is no difference in this whether we say "from" (مِنَ) is partitive and the merit is for some of the Emigrants and Helpers, or explicative and the merit and divine pleasure are for all of them; this is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ — Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him (9:100). Pleasure (الرضى) from us is the soul's agreement with an action without opposition or resistance. It is said: "He was pleased with such-and-such," meaning he agreed with it and did not refrain from it. it is realized by the absence of dislike for it, whether he loves it or neither loves nor dislikes it. Thus, the servant's pleasure with Allah is that he does not dislike anything Allah wills, nor loves anything Allah hates. This is only realized if he is pleased with His decree (قضاء) and what appears of His creative acts (أفعاله التكوينية), as well as His ruling and what He wills from him legislatively. In other words, when he submits to Him in creation and legislation, which is Islam and submission (التسليم) to Allah, the Glorified.

This itself is another witness to what preceded: that the judgment in the verse is restricted by faith and righteous deeds, meaning that Allah, the Glorified, only praises those among the Emigrants, Helpers, and Followers who believed in Him and acted righteously, and He informs of His pleasure with them and His preparation for them of gardens beneath which rivers flow.

The meaning of the verse is not that anyone to whom the label "Emigrant," "Helper," or "Follower" applies is someone with whom Allah is pleased with a pleasure after which there is never any wrath, and for whom He has made forgiveness and Paradise mandatory, whether he does good or evil thereafter, or is God-conscious or transgresses.

As for His pleasure (رضاه), it is among His active attributes (أوصافه الفعلية), not His essential ones (الذاتية). He, the Exalted, is not described in His essence by that which would make Him subject to change and alteration, such that a state of wrath would occur to Him when He is disobeyed, then pleasure when one repents to Him. Rather, He is pleased or angry in the sense that He treats His servant with the treatment of one who is pleased—by sending down mercy and granting blessings—or the treatment of one who is angry—by withholding mercy and imposing vengeance and punishment.

Therefore, it is possible for pleasure to occur and then change to wrath, or vice versa. However, it is apparent from the context of the verse that the intended pleasure is that after which there is no wrath, for it is a judgment predicated on the nature of the best of the community—among their predecessors and followers in faith and righteous deeds. This is a matter in which time has no involvement, such that it would be correct to assume wrath after pleasure. This is unlike His saying: لَقَدْ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ — Allah was certainly pleased with the faithful when they swore allegiance to you under the tree (48:18), for that is a pleasure restricted to a specific time, which allows for the assumption of wrath after it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِمَّنْ حَوْلَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ مُنافِقُونَ وَمِنْ أَهْلِ الْمَدِينَةِ — Among the bedouins who are around you there are hypocrites, and [also] among the people of Madinah (9:101). "Around" (حول) something refers to what is adjacent to it in space from its sides; it is an adverb of place. Al-marad (المرد) means insolence and exiting from obedience, as well as practicing and training in evil. This is the meaning suitable for His saying in the verse: مَرَدُوا عَلَى النِّفاقِ — who are habituated to hypocrisy — meaning they have become practiced in it and exercised it until they became accustomed to it.

The meaning of the verse is: Among those around you or around Medina of the desert-dwelling Arabs, there are hypocrites who have become practiced in hypocrisy; and among the people of Medina also are hypocrites accustomed to hypocrisy. You do not know them, O Muhammad; We know them. We shall punish them twice, then they shall be consigned to a great punishment.

Opinions have differed regarding what is meant by "punishing them twice." What are the two times? It has been said: once in this world through captivity and killing and the like, and once by the punishment of the grave. It has been said: in this world by taking the Zakat, and in the Hereafter by the punishment of the grave. It has been said: by hunger twice. It has been said: once at the time of death and once in the grave. It has been said: by the carrying out of legal punishments (الحدود) and the punishment of the grave. It has been said: once by scandal in this world and once by punishment in the grave. Other things have been said as well. There is no evidence for any of these views, though if one must choose, the first is the most plausible.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ خَلَطُوا عَمَلاً صالِحاً وَآخَرَ سَيِّئاً — And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins: they have mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil (9:102). That is, among the desert Arabs is another group of sinners who are not hypocrites like the others; rather, they confessed to their sins. They have a righteous deed and another evil one, having mixed this with that. It is hoped that Allah will turn to them in forgiveness, for Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.

In His saying: عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ — Maybe Allah will accept their repentance — there is the creation of hope in their souls so that they remain between fear and hope, without being overwhelmed by despair and despondency. In His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — Indeed Allah is All-forgiving, All-merciful — the side of hope is given preponderance.

His saying, the Exalted: خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَتُزَكِّيهِمْ بِها وَصَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّ صَلاتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَهُمْ وَاللهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ — Take charity from their possessions to cleanse them and purify them thereby, and bless them. Indeed your blessing is a comfort to them, and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing (9:103). Cleansing (التطهير) is the removal of dirt and filth from a thing so its existence becomes pure and ready for growth, development, and the appearance of its effects and blessings. Purification (التزكية) is its growth and the granting of maturity to it through the attachment of good things and the appearance of blessings, like a tree when its excess branches are cut, increasing its beautiful growth and the quality of its fruit. Thus, the combination of cleansing and purification in the verse is a subtle expression.

His saying: خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً — Take charity from their possessions — is a command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to take alms from the people's wealth. He did not say "from their wealth" (singular) so as to indicate that it is taken from various types of wealth, which are the two currencies: gold and silver; the three types of livestock: camels, cows, and sheep; and the four crops: wheat, barley, dates, and raisins.

His saying: تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَتُزَكِّيهِمْ بِها — to cleanse them and purify them thereby — is addressed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and is not a description of the state of the alms. The proof for this is the pronoun "thereby" (بها) referring back to the alms. That is: Take, O Muhammad, from the types of their wealth an alms by which you cleanse them and you purify them through that alms—that is, through the taking of it.

His saying: وَصَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ — and bless them — the blessing upon them is the supplication (الدعاء) for them. The context implies it is a supplication for them and their wealth for goodness and barakah, which is what is preserved from the Sunnah of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); he used to pray for the giver of Zakat and for his wealth for goodness and barakah.

His saying: إِنَّ صَلاتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَهُمْ — Indeed your blessing is a comfort to them — "comfort" (السكن) is that in which a thing finds rest. What is meant is that their souls find rest in your supplication and trust in it. This is a type of gratitude for their striving for Allah, just as His saying at the end of the verse: وَاللهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ — and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing — is a comfort in which the souls of the legally responsible (المكلفين) find rest among those who hear the verse or recite it.

The verse contains the ruling on the financial Zakat (الزكاة المالية), which is one of the pillars of the Sharia and the religion, as is apparent from the verse itself. This has been explained by numerous narrations from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) and others.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ وَيَأْخُذُ الصَّدَقاتِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ — Do they not know that it is Allah who accepts the repentance of His servants and receives the charities, and that it is Allah who is the All-clement, the All-merciful? (9:104). This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) intended to motivate people to give Zakat.

This is because they only give alms for Allah, and they only deliver it to the Messenger or his agent and collector insofar as he is commanded by Allah to take it. Thus, giving to him is giving to Allah, and his taking is a taking from Allah. So Allah, the Glorified, is the one who receives it in reality. He, the Exalted, has said in similar instances: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُبايِعُونَكَ إِنَّما يُبايِعُونَ اللهَ يَدُ اللهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ — Indeed those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah: the hand of Allah is above their hands (48:10), and He said: وَما رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ رَمى — and you did not throw when you threw, rather it was Allah who threw (8:17), and He said as a general statement: مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ — Whoever obeys the Apostle certainly obeys Allah (4:80).

When people are reminded with the likes of His saying: أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ — Do they not know that it is Allah... — their desires are stirred and they long to transact with their Lord, to shake His hand and for their hands to touch His hand—He is transcendent above the accidents of bodies and exalted above the involvement of temporal events.

The pairing of alms with repentance is because repentance cleanses and giving alms cleanses. Thus, giving alms is a financial repentance, just as repentance is like alms in actions and movements. Therefore, He conjoined to the beginning of the verse His saying at the end: وَأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ — and that it is Allah who is the All-clement, the All-merciful — mentioning His servants with His two names, the All-Clement (التواب) and the Most Merciful (الرحيم), and combining within them repentance and the giving of alms.

It has become clear from the verse that giving alms and paying Zakat is a type of repentance.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقُلِ اعْمَلُوا فَسَيَرَى اللهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ — And say, ‘Go on working: Allah will behold your conduct, and His Apostle and the faithful [as well]’ (9:105). The verse, based on its apparent connection to what precedes it, seems to address the believers, driving and inciting them to give alms.

However, its wording is general; there is no evidence to restrict its address to the almsgivers among the believers, nor to the general body of believers. Rather, it includes every person of action among the people—disbelievers, hypocrites, and believers—or at the very least, it includes both hypocrites and believers together.

However, the parallel to this verse which preceded—namely His saying in the context of the discourse on the hypocrites: وَسَيَرَى اللهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلى عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — Allah and His Apostle will behold your conduct, then you will be returned to the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, and He will inform you of what you used to do (9:94)—where He mentioned Allah and His Messenger in beholding their conduct but did not mention the believers, is not without a hint that the address in the verse we are currently discussing is specifically for the believers. For when one verse is joined to the other, it occurs to the mind that the reality of the hypocrites' actions—meaning their intentions behind their actions—since they were hidden from the masses of people, are only known by Allah and His Messenger through revelation from Allah. As for the believers, the realities of their actions—meaning their intentions, effects, and the benefits branching from them, such as the spread of God-consciousness, the reform of the affairs of Islamic society, the aiding of the poor in their livelihoods, and the Zakat of wealth and its growth—are known by Allah and His Messenger and witnessed by the believers among themselves.

But the appearance of actions with the realities of their effects and their general benefits or harms in the environment of their existence, their transformation into their likes, and their manifestation in their various stages time after time and era after era, is something that is not exclusive to the action of one group over another, nor is witnessing them and being affected by them exclusive to one group over another.

If the meaning of the believers seeing the actions of the doers were the appearance of their effects and results—in other words, the appearance of the actions themselves in the garb of their results to them—then this witnessing would not be exclusive to one group over another, nor to the action of one group over another. Why then would the actions be seen by the believers and not by the hypocrites when they are members of the same society? And why would the actions of the hypocrites not be witnessed by the believers when they were formed in their society and intermingled with their actions?

This, along with the specific context in the verse, brings to mind another meaning. For His saying: وَسَتُرَدُّونَ إِلى عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — then you will be returned to the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, and He will inform you of what you used to do — indicates, first, that His saying: فَسَيَرَى اللهُ عَمَلَكُمْ — Allah will behold your conduct — refers to what is before the Resurrection, which is this world, because of His saying: وَسَتُرَدُّونَ — then you will be returned — for it points to the Day of Resurrection, and what precedes it is this world.

Second: They will only be made aware of the reality of their actions on the Day of Resurrection; as for before that, they only see their outward appearance. We have pointed out this meaning repeatedly in our previous discussions. Since their knowledge of the realities of their actions is limited to His informing them of it on the Day of Judgment, and He mentioned the beholding of Allah, His Messenger, and the believers of their actions before the Day of Resurrection in this world—and Allah is mentioned alongside the Messenger and others while He is All-Knowing of their realities and He may reveal them to His Prophet—the meaning was the witnessing by Allah, the Glorified, His Messenger, and the believers of the reality of their actions. The "believers" intended here are the witnesses of actions (شهداء الأعمال) among them, not the general body of believers, as indicated by the likes of His saying: وَكَذلِكَ جَعَلْناكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطاً لِتَكُونُوا شُهَداءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيداً — Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses to the people, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you (2:143). The discussion on this has already passed in the first volume of this book.

Accordingly, the meaning of the verse is: Say, O Muhammad, "Do whatever you wish of actions, good or evil, for Allah, the Glorified, will witness the reality of your conduct, and His Messenger and the believers—who are the witnesses of actions—will witness it too. Then you will be returned to Allah, the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, on the Day of Resurrection, and He will show you the reality of your conduct."

In other words: Whatever action you perform, good or evil, its reality is seen and witnessed by Allah, the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, then by His Messenger and the believers in this world, then by your own selves, O assembly of doers, on the Day of Resurrection.

Thus, the verse is intended to urge people to monitor their actions by reminding them that their actions, good or evil, have realities that are not hidden by any veil, and that they have overseers and witnesses who will become aware of them and see their realities—namely the Messenger of Allah and the witnesses of actions among the believers—and Allah is behind them, encompassing all. He sees them and they see them. Then Allah, the Glorified, will uncover the veil from them on the Day of Resurrection for the doers themselves, as He said: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — ‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have suspended your veil from you, and so your sight is sharp today’ (50:22). There is a vast difference between a person performing an action in private where no one sees him, and performing that same action in the midst of a crowd of onlookers in public while he sees that it is so.

This is regarding the verse we are currently in. As for the previous verse: يَعْتَذِرُونَ إِلَيْكُمْ إِذا رَجَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ قُلْ لا تَعْتَذِرُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكُمْ قَدْ نَبَّأَنَا اللهُ مِنْ أَخْبارِكُمْ وَسَيَرَى اللهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلى عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — They will offer you excuses when you return to them. Say, ‘Do not offer excuses; we will not believe you. Allah has already informed us of your state. Allah and His Apostle will behold your conduct, then you will be returned to the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, and He will inform you of what you used to do’ (9:94)—the address in it is directed toward specific individuals among the hypocrites. In it, Allah commands His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to reject their excuses and mentions to them first that Allah has informed them—meaning the Prophet and those believers with him in the army of Islam—of their news through the revelation of these verses that narrate the news of the hypocrites and uncover their evil deeds.

Then He mentions to them that the reality of their actions is not hidden from Allah, the Glorified, nor concealed from Him, nor from His Messenger alone, and there was no one with him of the witnesses of actions. Then Allah will uncover to them their own selves regarding the reality of their actions on the Day of Resurrection.

This is the difference between the two verses despite their similarity in outward context, where in the verse we are in, He mentioned: "Allah, His Messenger, and the believers," while in the previous verse He mentioned: "Allah and His Messenger," and limited it to that. This is what reflection on the meaning of the verse yields. Whoever is not satisfied with this and is not pleased until he forms an outward meaning for the verse, let him say that His mentioning "Allah and His Messenger" in the address to the hypocrites is because they only intend to plot against Allah and His Messenger and have no concern for the believers. As for His mentioning "Allah, His Messenger, and the believers" in the general address, the purpose is to incite them toward righteous deeds in the presence of the righteous assembly, and He did not pay heed to the state of others among the disbelievers and hypocrites. So reflect.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ — [There are] others waiting for Allah’s decree: either He shall punish them, or He shall turn to them in mercy, and Allah is All-knowing, All-wise (9:106). Al-irja' (الإرجاء) is delay. The verse is conjoined to His saying: وَآخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ — And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins. The meaning of their being "deferred to the command of Allah" is that they have no cause that would give preponderance for them to the side of punishment or the side of forgiveness; thus, their affair returns to the command of Allah—whatever He wills and intends for them is what shall be carried out regarding them.

This verse applies in itself to the "oppressed" (المستضعفين) who are like an intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — between the doers of good and the doers of evil, even if it is reported in the occasions of revelation (شأن النزول) that the verse was revealed concerning the three who were left behind and then repented, so Allah sent down their repentance to His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as will come, if Allah wills.

In any case, the verse conceals what the outcome of their affair will be and keeps it in its ambiguity, even in what it concluded with of the two Noble Names: the All-Knowing (العليم) and the All-Wise (الحكيم), which indicate that Allah, the Glorified, judges concerning them according to what His knowledge and wisdom necessitate. This is unlike what concluded His saying: وَآخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ — And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins — where He said: عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — Maybe Allah will accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is All-forgiving, All-merciful.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Dawud ibn al-Husayn from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: I asked him about the saying of Allah: وَمِنَ الْأَعْرابِ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ ـ وَيَتَّخِذُ ما يُنْفِقُ قُرُباتٍ عِنْدَ اللهِ — Yet among the bedouins are [also] those who believe in Allah and the Last Day, and regard what they spend as a means of nearness to Allah. Will He reward them for it? He said: "Yes."

In the same source, from Abu 'Amr al-Zubayri from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Indeed, Allah established a race among the believers just as a race is established among horses on the day of a wager."

I said: "Inform me of what Allah has urged the believer regarding the race toward faith." He said: "The saying of Allah, the Exalted: سابِقُوا إِلى مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ ـ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُها كَعَرْضِ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ ـ أُعِدَّتْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ — Take the lead towards forgiveness from your Lord and a paradise as vast as the heavens and the earth, prepared for those who have faith in Allah and His apostles (57:21). And He said: السَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: they are the ones brought near [to Allah] (56:10-11).

And He said: وَالسَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ الْمُهاجِرِينَ وَالْأَنْصارِ ـ وَالَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُمْ بِإِحْسانٍ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ — The early vanguard of the Emigrants and the Helpers and those who followed them in virtue—Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him (9:100). So He began with the early Emigrants according to the degree of their precedence, then He followed with the Helpers, then He placed third the Followers and commanded them with virtue. Thus, He placed every group according to the measure of their degrees and stations with Him."

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Malik ibn Anas from Abu Salih from Ibn 'Abbas, he said: "وَالسَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ — The early vanguard — was revealed concerning the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him). He is the most preceding of all people in faith; he prayed toward the two Qiblahs, gave the two pledges—the Pledge of Badr and the Pledge of Ridwan—and migrated the two migrations: with Ja'far from Mecca to Abyssinia, and from Abyssinia to Medina."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from the path of al-Awza'i: Yahya ibn Kathir, al-Qasim, Makhul, 'Abdah ibn Abi Lubabah, and Hassan ibn 'Atiyyah narrated to me that they heard a group of the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: When this verse was revealed: وَالسَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ — The early vanguard — until His saying: وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ — and they are well-pleased with Him, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "This is for my entire community, and after pleasure there is no wrath."

I say: Its meaning is that those with whom Allah is pleased and who are pleased with Him are those whom the verse has gathered, not that the verse indicates His pleasure with the entire community, for this is something the Book refutes by definitive contradiction. Likewise, his saying: "and after pleasure there is no wrath"—his meaning is that after the pleasure mentioned in the verse there is no wrath, as we established previously; it does not mean that after absolute pleasure from Allah there is no wrath, for that is something that is not sound at all.

In the same source, Abu al-Shaykh and Ibn 'Asakir extracted from Abu Sakhr Humayd ibn Ziyad, who said: I said to Muhammad ibn Ka'b al-Qurazi: "Inform me about the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and I only intend the trials (الفتن)." He said: "Indeed, Allah has forgiven all the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and made Paradise mandatory for them in His Book, the virtuous among them and the sinful." I said: "And in which place has Allah made Paradise mandatory for them in His Book?" He said: "Do you not read: وَالسَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ — The early vanguard — the verse? He made Paradise and pleasure mandatory for all the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and He stipulated a condition upon the Followers that He did not stipulate for them."

I said: "And what did He stipulate upon them?" He said: "He stipulated upon them that they follow them in virtue. He says: they emulate them in their virtuous deeds, and they do not emulate them in anything else." Abu Sakhr said: "By Allah, it was as if I had never read it before that, and I did not know its interpretation until Muhammad ibn Ka'b recited it to me."

I say: He—as you see—concedes that in their actions there are virtuous and evil ones, obedience and transgression, yet Allah was pleased with them in all of that and forgave them, so He does not requite them for an evil deed with an evil one. This is what we mentioned in the preceding commentary: that its consequence is the contradiction of many Quranic verses indicating that Allah is not pleased with the transgressing and wrongdoing people, and that He does not love them nor guide them. It also restricts many more verses than that—which are the majority of Quranic verses—indicating the generality of the recompense of a good deed with good and an evil deed with evil without a restrictor, and upon which the verses of command and prohibition—which are the verses of rulings in their entirety—depend.

If the meaning of the verse were what he mentioned, the companions—with their pure Arabic nature and their proximity to the time of Prophethood and the descent of revelation—would have been more worthy of understanding that from the verse. Had they understood that from it, they would not have treated one another in the manner that sound transmission has recorded.

How can they all be realized by the content of His saying: رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ — Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him — and understand that from it, and then some of them not be pleased with others when Allah is pleased with them? For the one who is pleased with Allah is pleased with what Allah is pleased with. This problem is not repelled by the tradition of their ijtihad (independent legal reasoning), for even if that were conceded, it would be an excuse in the station of action, not a justification for combining two contradictory attributes existentially: namely, pleasure with Allah and lack of pleasure with what Allah is pleased with. The discussion is lengthy.

In the same source, Abu 'Ubayd, Sunayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Habib al-Shahid from 'Amr ibn 'Amir al-Ansari: that 'Umar ibn al-Khattab read: "The early vanguard of the Emigrants and the Helpers who followed them in virtue," so he recited "the Helpers" in the nominative case and did not include the "and" (الواو) in "those who" (الذين). Zayd ibn Thabit said to him: "And those who" (والذين). 'Umar said: "Those who" (الذين). Zayd said: "The Commander of the Faithful knows best." 'Umar said: "Bring me Ubayy ibn Ka'b." He came to him and he asked him about that. Ubayy said: "وَالَّذِينَ — And those who." 'Umar said: "Then yes, indeed, we follow Ubayy."

I say: The consequence of 'Umar's recitation is the restriction of the Emigrants to what is contained in His saying: وَالسَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ — The early vanguard — of merit, and another merit, which is their being followed by the Helpers, as the following tradition indicates.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Abu al-Shaykh extracted from Muhammad ibn Ka'b al-Qurazi, who said: 'Umar passed by a man reciting: وَالسَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ الْمُهاجِرِينَ وَالْأَنْصارِ — The early vanguard of the Emigrants and the Helpers. 'Umar took him by the hand and said: "Who taught you to recite this?" He said: "Ubayy ibn Ka'b." He said: "Do not leave me until I take you to him." When he came to him, 'Umar said: "Did you teach this man to recite this verse like this?" He said: "Yes." He said: "And did you hear it from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)?" He said: "Yes." He said: "I used to think that we had been raised to a height that no one after us would reach."

Ubayy said: "The confirmation of that is in the beginning of Surah al-Jumu'ah: وَآخَرِينَ مِنْهُمْ لَمَّا يَلْحَقُوا بِهِمْ — and others from among them who have not yet joined them (62:3), and in Surah al-Hashr: وَالَّذِينَ جاؤُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ ـ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لَنا وَلِإِخْوانِنَا الَّذِينَ سَبَقُونا بِالْإِيمانِ — And [also for] those who came after them, who say, ‘Our Lord, forgive us and our brethren who preceded us in faith’ (59:10), and in al-Anfal: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْ بَعْدُ وَهاجَرُوا وَجاهَدُوا مَعَكُمْ فَأُولئِكَ مِنْكُمْ — Those who believed afterwards and migrated and waged jihad with you, they are of you (8:75)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Musa ibn Bakr from a man, he said: Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "الذين خَلَطُوا عَمَلاً صالِحاً وَآخَرَ سَيِّئاً — those who have mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil — those are a believing people who commit sins within their faith—which the believers find fault with and dislike—so those, it is hoped that Allah will turn to them in forgiveness."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it from Zurarah from him (peace be upon him), except that it has "sinners" (مذنبون) in place of "believers" (مؤمنون).

In al-Majma': regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَآخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ — And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins — the verse. Abu Hamzah al-Thumali said: "It has reached us that they were three individuals from the Helpers: Abu Kinanah ibn 'Abd al-Mundhir, Tha'labah ibn Wadi'ah, and Aws ibn Hudham. They stayed behind from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) when he set out for Tabuk. When they heard what Allah had revealed concerning those who stayed behind from His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), they were certain of destruction. They bound themselves to the pillars of the mosque and remained so until the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrived. He asked about them, and it was mentioned to him that they had sworn not to untie themselves until the Messenger of Allah untied them. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'And I swear that I will not be the first to untie them unless I am commanded regarding them with a command.'"

When there was revealed: عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ — Maybe Allah will accept their repentance, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) went to them and untied them. They went and brought their wealth to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "This is our wealth that kept us behind from you, so take it and give it in charity on our behalf." He said: "I have not been commanded regarding it." Then there was revealed: خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً — Take charity from their possessions — the verses.

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur, with differences among them regarding the names of the men. In them, the verse of alms was revealed specifically regarding their wealth, but this is weakened by the abundance of narrations stating that the verse was revealed regarding the obligatory Zakat.

In the same source, it is narrated from Abu Ja'far al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "It was revealed concerning Abu Lubabah," and he did not mention anyone else with him. The reason for its revelation concerning him was what occurred from him regarding Banu Qurayzah, when he said: "If you submit to his judgment, it is slaughter."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from 'Abdullah ibn Sinan, he said: Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "When this verse was revealed: خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَتُزَكِّيهِمْ بِها — Take charity from their possessions to cleanse them and purify them thereby, it was revealed in the month of Ramadan. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ordered his caller, who called out among the people: 'Indeed, Allah has made Zakat obligatory upon you just as He made prayer obligatory upon you.' So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made it obligatory upon them from gold and silver; and He made alms obligatory from camels, cows, and sheep, and from wheat, barley, dates, and raisins. He called out to them with that in the month of Ramadan, and He excused them from everything else."

He said: "Then He did not make it obligatory for anything of their wealth until a year had passed from the following year. They fasted and broke their fast, then he ordered his caller, who called out among the Muslims: 'O Muslims, purify your wealth, and your prayer will be accepted.' Then he dispatched the collectors of alms and the collectors of land tax (الطسوق)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from 'Abdullah ibn Abi Awfa, who said: "When alms were brought to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he would say: 'O Allah, bless the family of so-and-so.' My father brought him his alms, and he said: 'O Allah, bless the family of Abu Awfa.'"

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from al-Saduq with his chain of transmission from Sulayman ibn Mehran from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَيَأْخُذُ الصَّدَقاتِ — and receives the charities. He said: "He accepts it from its people and rewards for it."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Malik ibn 'Atiyyah from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: 'Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) said: "I have guaranteed upon my Lord that alms do not fall into the hand of the servant until they fall into the hand of the Lord, and that is His saying: هُوَ يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ وَيَأْخُذُ الصَّدَقاتِ — it is He who accepts the repentance of His servants and receives the charities."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), 'Ali, Abu Ja'far, and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In Basa'ir al-Darajat, with its chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: I asked whether actions are presented to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He said: "There is no doubt in it." He said: "Have you seen the saying of Allah: اعْمَلُوا فَسَيَرَى اللهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ — Go on working: Allah will behold your conduct, and His Apostle and the faithful [as well]?" He said: "Allah has witnesses among His creation."

I say: There are numerous and abundant narrations with this meaning narrated in the Shi'a collections from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام). In most of them, it is stated that "the faithful" (المؤمنون) in the verse are the Imams, and their application to the interpretation we provided is evident.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah: وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللهِ — [There are] others waiting for Allah’s decree. He said: "A people who were polytheists and killed the likes of Hamzah and Ja'far—and their likes among the Muslims. Then they entered Islam, professed the oneness of Allah, and left polytheism, but faith did not become known to their hearts such that they would be among the believers for whom Paradise is mandatory; nor were they in their rejection such that they would be disbelievers for whom the Fire is mandatory. So they are in that state, deferred to the command of Allah: either He shall punish them, or He shall turn to them in mercy."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it in his Tafsir from Zurarah from him (peace be upon him), and there are other narrations with this meaning.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Humran, he said: I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the "oppressed" (المستضعفين). He said: "They are neither believers nor disbelievers; they are those deferred to the command of Allah."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from 'Ikrimah: regarding His saying: وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللهِ — [There are] others waiting for Allah’s decree. He said: "They are the three who were left behind."

I say: The like of this is narrated from Mujahid and Qatadah, and that their names were Hilal ibn Umayyah, Murarah ibn al-Rabi', and Ka'b ibn Malik from the Aws and Khazraj. However, their story does not apply to this verse, and it will come, if Allah wills.

(A Discourse on Zakat and Other Alms)

Social and economic studies, and other related research, have today placed the need of society—insofar as it is a society—for wealth that is specific to it and spent to meet its general needs, in the category of self-evident truths that no doubter doubts and no suspicion enters. Many social and economic issues—including this issue—were among those that the general public in past ages was oblivious to, only sensing them with a general primordial intuition (شعوراً فطرياً إجمالياً). Today, they are among the basics known to both the elite and the common folk.

However, Islam—according to what it has clarified of the psychology of society and its identity, and what it has legislated of financial rulings pertaining to it, and the systems and laws it has arranged in its aspects and cores—has the upper hand in this.

The Holy Quran has clarified that social interaction (الاجتماع) molds a new form from the elements of the individuals who come together, resulting in a new, living identity: society (المجتمع). This society possesses existence, a lifespan, life, death, consciousness, will, weakness, strength, accountability (التكليف), virtue, vice, felicity, and wretchedness, similar or analogous to those of the individual human being. Many Quranic verses have been revealed in explanation of all this, which we have repeatedly pointed out during previous discussions.

The Islamic Law (الشريعة) has allocated a portion of the benefits and profits of wealth for society, such as the obligatory charity (الصدقة الواجبة) which is the poor-rate (الزكاة) — the mandatory religious tax on specific types of wealth to be distributed among the needy — and the one-fifth (الخمس) — the twenty percent tax levied on spoils of war, minerals, and surplus income — from the spoils of war and the like. In this, Islam did not introduce an unprecedented innovation, for the laws and codes preceding it, such as the Code of Hammurabi and the laws of Ancient Rome, contained similar provisions. Indeed, all national traditions in any era, and among any group, have never been devoid of considering a financial dimension for their society; for society, in whatever form it takes, senses a financial need for its sustenance and growth.

However, the Islamic Law is distinguished from other traditions and codes by several matters that must be carefully contemplated to grasp its true objective and its sound perspective in legislation. These are:

First: It restricted the imposition of this type of financial obligation to the very being and emergence of ownership (الملك) as it comes into existence, and did not exceed that.
