In other words, whenever wealth is generated in any circumstance—such as a harvest resulting from agriculture, a profit returning from trade, or the like—Islam immediately intervenes and designates a portion of it as the property of society, while the remaining portions are the property of the one who provided the capital or the labor, for example. The individual is then only required to return the society’s property—which is that specific portion—to it.

In fact, it might be inferred from verses such as His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ لَكُم مَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا "It is He who created for you all that is in the earth" (2:29), and His saying: وَلَا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهَاءَ أَمْوَالَكُمُ الَّتِي جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ قِيَامًا "Do not give the feeble-minded your property, which Allah has assigned to you for support" (4:5), that newly generated wealth belongs in its entirety to society at the moment of its emergence. Then, a portion of it is specialized for the individual whom we call the owner (المالك) or the worker (العامل), while a portion—namely the portion of the poor-rate (الزكاة) or the one-fifth (الخمس)—remains in the ownership of society as it originally was. Thus, the individual owner is an owner in longitudinal succession (في طول) to the primary owner, which is society. Some discussion on this preceded in the commentary (تفسير) of these two verses.

In short, the financial rights established by the Law, such as the poor-rate (الزكاة) and the one-fifth (الخمس), were only placed upon newly generated wealth at the moment of its emergence. Thus, it made society a partner with the individual from the very start. Thereafter, the individual is free to use his specific wealth as he wishes for his legitimate purposes without anyone objecting, unless society is suddenly beset by general dangers that necessitate spending some of the capital for the sake of preserving its life—such as an attacking enemy seeking to destroy "crops and progeny," or a general famine that "spares nothing and leaves nothing behind."

As for financial levies related to persons (poll taxes), estates, real estate, or commercial goods upon the fulfillment of certain conditions or in specific states—such as the tithe (العشر) taken at border outposts and the like—Islam does not recognize this. Rather, it considers it a form of usurpation (الغصب) and an injustice (الظلم) that imposes a restriction on the owner's freedom regarding his property.

In reality, society does not take from the individual except its own wealth, which attaches to the spoil or profit at its first emergence, sharing with the individual in his ownership in a manner explained in detail in Islamic Jurisprudence (الفقه). However, once ownership is established and settled for its owner, no objector has the right to challenge the owner in any state or under any condition that would cause his hand to be restricted or his freedom to vanish.

Second: Islam considers the state of individuals regarding the wealth specific to society just as it considers the state of society itself. In fact, what appears from its perspective is that the welfare of individuals often prevails over the welfare of the collective. For it designates eight categories for the portions of the poor-rate (الزكاة), of which only one portion is specialized for "the way of Allah" (سبيل الله), while the remaining portions are for individuals, such as the poor (الفقراء), the needy (المساكين), the collectors (العاملين), those whose hearts are to be reconciled (المؤلفة قلوبهم), and others. Regarding the one-fifth (الخمس), it is divided into six portions; only one portion is designated for Allah, the Glorified, while the rest are for the Messenger, the near of kin (ذي القربى), the orphans (اليتامى), the needy (المساكين), and the wayfarer (ابن السبيل).

This is because the individual is the sole element for the formation of society. Eliminating class distinctions—which is one of the fundamental principles of the Islamic program—and establishing balance and equilibrium between the different forces of society, as well as stabilizing moderation in its trajectory through its pillars and parts, cannot be achieved except by reforming the state of the parts—meaning the individuals—and bringing their conditions closer to one another.

As for restricting the wealth of society to being spent only on creating general might, collective decorations, erecting lofty fortified palaces, and grand luxurious buildings, while leaving the strong and the weak, or the rich and the poor, in their respective states—only increasing in distance from each other every day—long and definitive experience has shown that this neither fends off a disaster nor provides any significant benefit.

Third: An individual Muslim has the right to personally distribute the obligatory financial right due from him, such as the poor-rate (الزكاة), to some of the eligible recipients, like the poor and the needy, without necessarily handing it over to the ruler (ولي الأمر) or his agent, who would then return it to those entitled to it.

This is a type of independent respect (الاحترام الاستقلالي) that Islam has accorded to the individuals of its society, similar to the granting of protection (إعطاء الذمة) which every individual Muslim has the right to extend to whichever combatant disbelievers (الكفار المحاربين) he wishes, and neither the Muslims nor their ruler has the right to revoke that.

Yes, if the ruler (ولي الأمر) perceives in a particular instance that the interest of Islam and the Muslims lies in the contrary, he may forbid that, and it then becomes obligatory to refrain from it due to the obligation of obeying him.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مَسْجِدًا ضِرَارًا وَكُفْرًا وَتَفْرِيقًۢا بَيْنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَإِرْصَادًا لِّمَنْ حَارَبَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَلَيَحْلِفُنَّ إِنْ أَرَدْنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (١٠٧)
As for those who took to a mosque for sabotage and for defiance, and to cause division among the faithful, and for the purpose of ambush [used] by those who have fought Allah and His Apostle before—they will surely swear, ‘We desired nothing but good,’ and Allah bears witness that they are indeed liars. (107)
لَا تَقُمْ فِيهِ أَبَدًا ۚ لَّمَسْجِدٌ أُسِّسَ عَلَى ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ مِنْ أَوَّلِ يَوْمٍ أَحَقُّ أَن تَقُومَ فِيهِ ۚ فِيهِ رِجَالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَن يَتَطَهَّرُوا۟ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُطَّهِّرِينَ (١٠٨)
Do not stand in it ever! A mosque founded on Godwariness from the [very] first day is worthier that you stand in it [for prayer]. Therein are men who love to keep pure, and Allah loves those who keep pure. (108)
أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَـٰنَهُۥ عَلَىٰ تَقْوَىٰ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنٍ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَـٰنَهُۥ عَلَىٰ شَفَا جُرُفٍ هَارٍ فَٱنْهَارَ بِهِۦ فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٠٩)
Is he who founds his building on Godwariness and [the pursuit of Allah’s] pleasure better-off or he who founds his building on the brink of a collapsing bank which collapses with him into the fire of hell? Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (109)
لَا يَزَالُ بُنْيَـٰنُهُمُ ٱلَّذِى بَنَوْا۟ رِيبَةً فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَّآ أَن تَقَطَّعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (١١٠)
The building they have built will never cease to be [a source of] disquiet in their hearts until their hearts are cut into pieces, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (110)

(Commentary)

The verses mention another group of the hypocrites (المنافقون) who built the Mosque of Harm (مسجد الضرار), contrasting their condition with that of a group of believers (المؤمنون) who built a mosque based on the godwariness (تقوى) of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مَسْجِداً ضِراراً وَكُفْراً — As for those who built a mosque to cause harm and for unfaith (9:107), until the end of the verse. Harm (الضرار) and inflicting harm (المضارة) refer to the act of causing injury. Lying in wait (الإرصاد) means taking up a position of observation, waiting, and monitoring.

Regarding His saying: وَالَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مَسْجِداً ضِراراً — As for those who built a mosque to cause harm, if these verses were revealed along with the preceding verses concerning the hypocrites, then the conjunction links them to the previously mentioned groups of hypocrites identified by His saying: "And among them..." (ومنهم), meaning: "And among them are those who built a mosque to cause harm."

However, if they were revealed independently, then the "and" (الواو) is initial (استئنافية), and the phrase الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا — those who built — is the subject (مبتدأ), while its predicate (خبر) is His saying: لا تَقُمْ فِيهِ أَبَداً — Never stand in it (9:108). This latter view can also be applied to the first assumption. Grammarians have mentioned other possibilities for the syntax (إعراب) of the verse, but they are not free from affectation (تكلف), so we have omitted them.

Allah has clarified the purpose of this group of hypocrites in building this mosque: to cause harm to others, to manifest disbelief (الكفر), to create division among the believers, and to provide a place of ambush for those who had previously fought Allah and His Messenger. These mentioned purposes are specific and linked to a particular event. According to the consensus of the transmitters of reports (أهل النقل), it is as follows:

A group from the tribe of Banu ‘Amr ibn ‘Awf built the Mosque of Quba and asked the Prophet to pray in it, which he did. A group from Banu Ghanm ibn ‘Awf, who were hypocrites, envied them. Consequently, they built a mosque next to the Mosque of Quba to cause harm, to divert the believers from it, and to wait for Abu ‘Amir the Monk (الراهب). He had promised them that he would bring an army from the Romans to expel the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from Medina, and he commanded them to prepare for battle alongside them.

When they finished building the mosque, they came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while he was preparing for the expedition to Tabuk. They asked him to come and pray in it and to supplicate for blessings for them. He promised them he would do so after finishing the matter of Tabuk and returning to Medina. Then these verses were revealed.

Thus, their mosque was intended to harm the Mosque of Quba, to manifest disbelief in Allah and His Messenger, to divide the believers who gathered at Quba, and to serve as an ambush for Abu ‘Amir the Monk, who had fought Allah and His Messenger previously. Allah, the Glorified, informed about them that they would surely swear: "We intended nothing by building this mosque but the best deed," which was to facilitate matters for the believers by increasing the number of places where Allah is worshipped. However, the Exalted testified to their lying by His saying: وَلَيَحْلِفُنَّ إِنْ أَرَدْنا إِلَّا الْحُسْنى وَاللهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ — They will surely swear, ‘We desired nothing but good,’ but Allah bears witness that they are indeed liars (9:107).

His saying, the Exalted: لا تَقُمْ فِيهِ أَبَداً — Never stand in it (9:108), until the end of the verse. He began by forbidding the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from ever standing [in prayer] within it. Then He mentioned the Mosque of Quba, preferring standing in it after praising it with His saying: لَمَسْجِدٌ أُسِّسَ عَلَى التَّقْوى مِنْ أَوَّلِ يَوْمٍ أَحَقُّ أَنْ تَقُومَ فِيهِ — A mosque founded on God-wariness from the first day is worthier that you should stand in it (9:108). He praised it because of the good intention of its founders from the very first day, and based upon this, He established the preference of standing in it over standing in the Mosque of Harm.

Although the sentence does not explicitly restrict standing only to the Mosque of Quba—since He used the expression "worthier" (أحق)—the preceding prohibition against standing in the Mosque of Harm necessitates this restriction. His saying, the Exalted: فِيهِ رِجالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يَتَطَهَّرُوا — In it are men who love to keep pure (9:108), serves as the justification (تعليل) for the aforementioned preference. His saying: وَاللهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُطَّهِّرِينَ — and Allah loves those who keep pure (9:108), completes this justification. This is the evidence that the intended meaning of لَمَسْجِدٌ أُسِّسَ — a mosque founded — is the Mosque of Quba, not the Prophet's Mosque or any other.

The meaning of the verse is: Never stand—meaning for prayer—in the Mosque of Harm. I swear, the Mosque of Quba, which is a mosque founded upon the godwariness (تقوى) of Allah from the first day, is worthier and more appropriate for you to stand in for prayer. This is because within it are men who love to purify themselves from sins, or from impurities (الأرجاس) and ritual defilements (الأحداث), and Allah loves those who keep pure; therefore, you should stand among them.

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: لَمَسْجِدٌ أُسِّسَ — a mosque founded — functions as the justification for the preference of one mosque over the other, while His saying: فِيهِ رِجالٌ — In it are men — conveys the preference of its people over the people of the other. His subsequent saying: أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيانَهُ — Is he who founded his building... (9:109) — clarifies this second preference.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيانَهُ عَلى تَقْوى مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْوانٍ خَيْرٌ — Is he who founded his building on God-wariness and [the pursuit of] Allah’s pleasure better... (9:109), until the end of the verse. The brink (شفا) of a well is its edge. The bank (جرف) of a valley is its side that has been hollowed out at its base by water. A thing is collapsing (هار) when it is about to fall; it is also said هار by metathesis (القلب), and أنهار means it collapsed or crumbled due to softness. Thus, His saying: عَلى شَفا جُرُفٍ هارٍ فَانْهارَ بِهِ فِي نارِ جَهَنَّمَ — on the brink of a collapsing bank, so that it collapsed with him into the fire of hell (9:109), is an imaginary metaphor (استعارة تخييلية). In it, He likened their state to one who builds a structure on the very edge of a valley's brink, having no confidence in its stability or endurance, so it falls along with the structure built upon it. Since its foundation was over Gehenna (جهنم), it fell into its fire. This is in contrast to one who founds his building upon godwariness (تقوى) from Allah and His pleasure (رضوان)—meaning he proceeds in his life upon the guarding against Allah's punishment and the seeking of His pleasure.

The apparent context suggests that His saying: أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيانَهُ عَلى تَقْوى — Is he who founded his building on God-wariness — and His saying: أَمْ مَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيانَهُ عَلى شَفا جُرُفٍ — or he who founded his building on the brink of a bank — are two parables (مثلان). They represent the foundation of the lives of the believers and the hypocrites, which is the religion (الدين) and the path they follow. The religion of the believer is the godwariness of Allah and the seeking of His pleasure based on certainty (اليقين) in Him, while the religion of the hypocrite is built upon instability and doubt (الشك).

For this reason, Allah the Exalted followed it with further clarification, saying: لا يَزالُ بُنْيانُهُمُ — Their building will not cease — meaning the hypocrites — الَّذِي بَنَوْا رِيبَةً — which they have built, [to be] a source of disquiet — and doubt — فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ — in their hearts (9:110), never reaching the stage of certainty, إِلَّا أَنْ تَقَطَّعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ — unless their hearts are cut into pieces (9:110), such that the doubt vanishes with the vanishing of the hearts themselves. وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ — And Allah is all-knowing, all-wise (9:110); therefore, He humbles these and elevates those.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, it is stated: The commentators said: The Banu ‘Amr ibn ‘Awf established the Mosque of Quba and sent for the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to come to them. He came and prayed in it. A group of hypocrites from the Banu Ghanm ibn ‘Awf envied them and said: "We shall build a mosque and pray in it, and we will not attend the congregation of Muhammad." They were twelve men—some say fifteen—including Tha‘labah ibn Hatib, Mu‘attib ibn Qushayr, and Nabtal ibn al-Harith. So they built a mosque next to the Mosque of Quba.

When they had built it, they came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while he was preparing for Tabuk and said: "O Messenger of Allah, we have built a mosque for the infirm, the needy, and for rainy and wintry nights. We would love for you to come and pray in it for us and supplicate for blessings." He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: "I am on the verge of a journey. If we return, we shall come to you, if Allah wills, and pray for you in it." When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله returned from Tabuk, the verse regarding the mosque was revealed to him.

He said: Upon his return from Tabuk, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله dispatched ‘Asim ibn ‘Awf al-‘Ajlani and Malik ibn al-Dukhshum—Malik was from the Banu ‘Amr ibn ‘Awf—and said to them: "Go to this mosque whose people are wrongdoers (الظالم), demolish it, and burn it." It is also narrated that he sent ‘Ammar ibn Yasir and Wahshi, and they burned it. He then ordered that it be turned into a refuse pit where carcasses are thrown.

I say: In the narration of al-Qummi, it is mentioned that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent Malik ibn Dukhshum al-Khuza‘i and ‘Amir ibn ‘Adi, the brother of Banu ‘Amr ibn ‘Awf, for this purpose. Malik came and said to ‘Amir: "Wait for me until I bring fire from my house." He went in, brought fire, and ignited palm branches. Then he set the mosque on fire, and the people dispersed. Zayd ibn Harithah sat there until the structure was burned, and then he ordered its walls to be demolished.

The story is narrated through many chains from the Sunni sources (طرق أهل السنة), and the narrations are similar, though there is some variation in the names of those whom the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله dispatched.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Ishaq who said: Those who built the Mosque of Harm were twelve men: Khidham ibn Khalid ibn ‘Ubayd ibn Zayd, Tha‘labah ibn Hatib, Hilal ibn Umayyah, Mu‘attib ibn Qushayr, Abu Habibah ibn al-Az‘ar, ‘Abbad ibn Hunayf, Jariyah ibn ‘Amir and his two sons Mujammi‘ and Zayd, Nabtal ibn al-Harith, Bakhdaj ibn ‘Uthman (1), and Wadi‘ah ibn Thabit.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying: وَإِرْصاداً لِمَنْ حارَبَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ — and as an ambush for those who had fought Allah and His Apostle (9:107), he said: This refers to Abu ‘Amir the Monk. He said: Part of his story was that he had practiced monkery during the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) and wore haircloth. When the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله came to Medina, he envied him and incited the confederates (الأحزاب) against him. After the conquest of Mecca, he fled to al-Ta’if. When the people of al-Ta’if embraced Islam, he joined the Levant (الشام) and went to the Romans where he became a Christian. He was the father of Hanzalah, "the one washed by the angels" (غسيل الملائكة), who was killed alongside the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله on the day of Uhud; he was in a state of ritual impurity (جنب), so the angels washed him.

The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله named Abu ‘Amir "the Transgressor" (الفاسق). He had sent word to the hypocrites: "Prepare yourselves and build a mosque, for I am going to Caesar and will bring soldiers from him to expel Muhammad from Medina." These hypocrites were expecting Abu ‘Amir to come to them, but he died before reaching the King of the Romans.

I say: There are several narrations to this effect.

(1) In the Sirah: Bijad ibn ‘Uthman, which is the correct name.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from al-Halabi, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: I asked him about the mosque founded upon godwariness (التقوى). He replied: "The Mosque of Quba."

I say: al-‘Ayyashi narrated it in his Tafsir, and this meaning is also narrated in al-Kafi with its chain from Mu‘awiyah ibn ‘Ammar from him (peace be upon him).

It has been narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through several chains from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he said: "It is this mosque of mine." However, this contradicts the apparent meaning of the verse, especially His saying: فِيهِ رِجالٌ — In it are men — for the discourse is set in a comparison between the two mosques: the Mosque of Quba and the Mosque of Harm, and a comparison between their respective people. No such purpose relates to the Prophet's Mosque صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from al-Halabi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: I asked him about the saying of Allah: فِيهِ رِجالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يَتَطَهَّرُوا — In it are men who love to keep pure. He said: "Those who love to keep pure through the cleanliness of ablution (الوضوء)—which is cleaning oneself with water (الاستنجاء بالماء)." He said: "This was revealed concerning the people of Quba."

In al-Majma‘, regarding the verse, he said: They love to purify themselves with water from excrement and urine. This is what is narrated from the two Masters: al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them). It is narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he said to the people of Quba: "What do you do in your purification, for Allah the Exalted has praised you excellently?" They said: "We wash the trace of excrement." He said: "Then Allah revealed concerning you: وَاللهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُطَّهِّرِينَ — and Allah loves those who keep pure."

In the same source: Regarding the recitation of His saying: إِلَّا أَنْ تَقَطَّعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ — unless their hearts are cut into pieces. Ya‘qub and Sahl recited it as "إلى أن" (until), treating it as a preposition. This is also the recitation of al-Hasan, Qatadah, al-Jahdari, and a group; it was narrated by al-Barqi from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱشْتَرَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلَهُم بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ ۚ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَقْتُلُونَ وَيُقْتَلُونَ ۖ وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَٱلْإِنجِيلِ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ۚ وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِعَهْدِهِۦ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَٱسْتَبْشِرُوا۟ بِبَيْعِكُمُ ٱلَّذِى بَايَعْتُم بِهِۦ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١١١)
Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful their souls and their possessions for paradise to be theirs: they fight in the way of Allah, kill, and are killed. A promise binding upon Him in the Torah and the Evangel and the Quran. And who is truer to his promise than Allah? So rejoice in the bargain you have made with Him, and that is the great success. (111)
ٱلتَّـٰٓئِبُونَ ٱلْعَـٰبِدُونَ ٱلْحَـٰمِدُونَ ٱلسَّـٰٓئِحُونَ ٱلرَّٰكِعُونَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدُونَ ٱلْـَٔامِرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَٱلنَّاهُونَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظُونَ لِحُدُودِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١١٢)
[The faithful are] penitent, devout, celebrators of Allah’s praise, wayfarers, who bow [and] prostrate [in prayer], bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and keep Allah’s bounds—and give good news to the faithful. (112)
مَا كَانَ لِلنَّبِىِّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن يَسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَلَوْ كَانُوٓا۟ أُو۟لِى قُرْبَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١١٣)
The Prophet and the faithful may not plead for the forgiveness of the polytheists, even if they should be [their] relatives, after it has become clear to them that they will be the inmates of hell. (113)
وَمَا كَانَ ٱسْتِغْفَارُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَن مَّوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَهَآ إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُۥٓ أَنَّهُۥ عَدُوٌّ لِّلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ ۚ إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَأَوَّٰهٌ حَلِيمٌ (١١٤)
Abraham’s pleading forgiveness for his father was only to fulfill a promise he had made him. So when it became manifest to him that he was an enemy of God, he repudiated him. Indeed Abraham was most plaintive and forbearing. (114)
وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْمًۢا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَىٰهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يُبَيِّنَ لَهُم مَّا يَتَّقُونَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (١١٥)
Allah does not lead any people astray after He has guided them until He has made clear to them what they should beware of. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (115)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۚ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (١١٦)
Indeed to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He gives life and brings death. And besides Allah you do not have any guardian or helper. (116)
لَّقَد تَّابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ وَٱلْأَنصَارِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُ فِى سَاعَةِ ٱلْعُسْرَةِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا كَادَ يَزِيغُ قُلُوبُ فَرِيقٍ مِّنْهُمْ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِهِمْ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١٧)
Certainly Allah turned clemently to the Prophet and the Emigrants and the Helpers, who followed him in the hour of difficulty, after the hearts of a part of them were about to swerve. Then He turned clemently to them—indeed, He is most kind and merciful to them (117)
وَعَلَى ٱلثَّلَـٰثَةِ ٱلَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا۟ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَن لَّا مَلْجَأَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّآ إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١١٨)
—and to the three who were left behind. When the earth became narrow for them with [all] its expanse, and their own souls weighed heavily on them, and they knew that there was no refuge from Allah except in Him, then He turned clemently toward them so that they might be penitent. Indeed Allah is the All-clement, the All-merciful. (118)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (١١٩)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and be with the Truthful. (119)
مَا كَانَ لِأَهْلِ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ أَن يَتَخَلَّفُوا۟ عَن رَّسُولِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَرْغَبُوا۟ بِأَنفُسِهِمْ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ لَا يُصِيبُهُمْ ظَمَأٌ وَلَا نَصَبٌ وَلَا مَخْمَصَةٌ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَطَـُٔونَ مَوْطِئًا يَغِيظُ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَلَا يَنَالُونَ مِنْ عَدُوٍّ نَّيْلًا إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُم بِهِۦ عَمَلٌ صَـٰلِحٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٢٠)
It is not fitting for the people of Madinah and the Bedouins around them to hang back behind the Apostle of Allah and prefer their own lives to his life. That is because they neither experience any thirst, nor fatigue, nor hunger, in the way of Allah, nor do they tread any ground enraging the faithless, nor do they gain any ground against an enemy but a righteous deed is written for them on its account. Indeed Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous. (120)
وَلَا يُنفِقُونَ نَفَقَةً صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً وَلَا يَقْطَعُونَ وَادِيًا إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُمْ لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٢١)
And neither do they incur any expense, big or small, nor do they cross any valley, but it is written to their account, so that Allah may reward them by the best of what they used to do. (121)
۞ وَمَا كَانَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ لِيَنفِرُوا۟ كَآفَّةً ۚ فَلَوْلَا نَفَرَ مِن كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ طَآئِفَةٌ لِّيَتَفَقَّهُوا۟ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَلِيُنذِرُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ إِذَا رَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَحْذَرُونَ (١٢٢)
Yet it is not for the faithful to go forth en masse. But why should not there a group from each of their sections go forth to become learned in religion, and to warn their people when they return to them, so that they may beware? (122)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يَلُونَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْكُفَّارِ وَلْيَجِدُوا۟ فِيكُمْ غِلْظَةً ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٢٣)
O you who have faith! Fight the faithless who are in your vicinity, and let them find severity in you, and know that Allah is with the Godwary. (123)

(Commentary)

These verses address various purposes, yet they are united by a single objective linked to the theme of the preceding verses. Since those verses discussed fighting (القتال), some of these praise the believers (المؤمنين) and grant them a beautiful promise for their struggle in the way of Allah (الجهاد في سبيل الله). Others prohibit showing affection toward the polytheists (المشركين) or seeking forgiveness for them. Some verses indicate Allah’s turning in mercy (توبته) toward the three who stayed behind from the expedition of Tabuk. Others mandate that the people of Medina and the Bedouins (الأعراب) around them must go forth with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) whenever he intends to go out for battle and must not remain behind. Some verses enjoin that a group of people must remain behind to gain a deep understanding of the religion (التفقه في الدين) and then convey it to their people when they return to them. Finally, some verses decree fighting those disbelievers (الكفار) who are adjacent to the lands of Islam.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ اشْتَرى مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوالَهُمْ بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ — Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful their souls and their possessions for paradise to be theirs (9:111), until the end of the verse. Buying (الاشتراء) is the acceptance of the sold commodity (العين المبيعة) in exchange for the transfer of the price (الثمن) in a transaction.

In this verse, Allah, the Glorified, mentions His definitive promise of Paradise for those who struggle in the way of Allah with their lives and wealth. He mentions that He has recorded this in the Torah (التوراة) and the Gospel (الإنجيل) just as He mentions it in the Quran (القرآن).

He, the Glorified, has cast this in the mold of a parable (التمثيل), depicting it as a sale. He has made Himself the Buyer (المشتري), the believers the sellers (البائعين), their souls and wealth the commodity and the sold item (المبيع), Paradise the price (الثمن), and the Torah, the Gospel, and the Quran the contract (السند) for the transaction. This is an exquisite parable. He then gives glad tidings to the believers regarding this sale of theirs and congratulates them on the great success.

His saying, the Exalted: التَّائِبُونَ الْعابِدُونَ الْحامِدُونَ السَّائِحُونَ — [The faithful are] those who repent, those who worship, those who celebrate His praise, those who go forth [for jihad or to seek knowledge] (9:112), until the end of the verse. He, the Glorified, describes the believers with their most beautiful attributes. These attributes are in the nominative case by way of ellipsis (القطع), meaning: "The believers are those who repent, those who worship, etc." They are the repentant (التائبون) because of their return from other than Allah to Allah, the Glorified. They are the worshippers (العابدون) of Him, worshipping Him with their tongues by praising Him with beautiful commendation (الحامدون), with their feet by traveling and moving (السائحون) from one religious center or mosque of Allah to another, and with their bodies by bowing (الراكعون) and prostrating (الساجدون) to Him.

This is their state in private (الانفراد). As for their state in society (الاجتماع), they are those who enjoin the good (الآمرون بالمعروف) within the religious tradition and forbid the evil (الناهون عن المنكر) therein. Furthermore, they are the keepers of the bounds of Allah (الحافظون لحدود الله), not transgressing them in their states of solitude or social interaction, in their private or public lives. Then He commands the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to give them glad tidings, though Allah Himself had already given them glad tidings in the previous verse; in this, there is a degree of perfect emphasis (التأكيد) that is beyond measure.

From what we have established, several points become clear: First: The reason for the sequence of the attributes He enumerated for them. He began with their attributes as individuals—repentance, worship, travel, bowing, and prostration. Then He mentioned the specific attribute arising from their faith as a community—enjoining the good and forbidding the evil. He concluded with the beautiful attribute they possess in both their individual and social states—their keeping of the bounds of Allah. The expression "keeping" (الحفظ), in addition to indicating non-transgression, signifies vigilance (الرقوب) and profound concern.

Second: The meaning of "traveling" (السياحة)—which literally means journeying through the land—is, according to what best fits the context of the sequence, journeying to the places of Allah’s remembrance and worship, such as mosques. As for the views that "traveling" refers to fasting, or journeying through the land to take heed of the wonders of Allah’s power and what befell past nations as told by their dwellings and ruins, or traveling specifically to seek knowledge or to seek hadith, these are unsound views.

As for the first view (fasting), there is absolutely no linguistic evidence for it. As for the other views, although recommendation (الندب) might be derived from verses such as His saying: أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ — Have they not traveled through the land and seen how was the fate of those who were before them? (40:82), and His saying: فَلَوْ لا نَفَرَ مِنْ كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِنْهُمْ طائِفَةٌ لِيَتَفَقَّهُوا فِي الدِّينِ — ...Why should not there go forth a group from every section of them to become learned in religion... (9:122), intending them by the word السَّائِحُونَ would disrupt the excellence of the arrangement among these ordered attributes.

Third: These noble attributes are those by which the faith of a believer is perfected, necessitating the definitive promise of Paradise and followed by the divine and prophetic glad tidings. They are inseparable from fulfilling the right of Allah (حق الله) and entail Allah’s fulfillment of the right He has placed upon Himself.

His saying, the Exalted: ما كانَ لِلنَّبِيِّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ يَسْتَغْفِرُوا لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَلَوْ كانُوا أُولِي قُرْبى — The Prophet and the faithful may not plead for the forgiveness of the polytheists, even if they should be [their] relatives (9:113), until the end of the two verses. The meaning of the verse is clear. However, when He mentioned in the second verse—which explains the reason for Ibrahim’s seeking forgiveness for his father despite his being a disbeliever—that he disassociated from him after it became clear to him that he was an enemy of Allah, it indicated that the realization that the polytheists are the inhabitants of the Hellfire only guides to the impermissibility of seeking forgiveness because it is inseparable from their being enemies of Allah. Thus, when it became clear to the Prophet and the believers that the polytheists are enemies of Allah, it revealed to them a necessary judgment: the impermissibility of seeking forgiveness, as it would be a futile act (لغوا) yielding no result. The humility of faith (خضوع الإيمان) prevents a servant from engaging in futility before the Presence of Majesty.

This is because one may suppose Allah to be an enemy to a servant, displeased with him due to a shortcoming on his part or an evil deed; in such a case, considering the vastness of Allah’s mercy, it is permissible to seek forgiveness and mercy for him if the servant is humble and not arrogant. But if the servant is assumed to be an enemy of Allah, warring against Him, arrogant and haughty—like the masters of denial and stubbornness among the polytheists—then clear reason dictates that neither the intercession (الشفاعة) of a request nor seeking forgiveness will benefit him unless he repents, returns to Allah, sheds his arrogance and stubbornness, and clothes himself in the garment of humility and wretchedness. There is no meaning in asking for mercy and forgiveness for one who refuses to accept it, nor in seeking a gift for one who does not humble himself to receive it, except as a mockery of the station of Lordship (الربوبية) and a play with the station of servitude (العبودية), which is impermissible by the necessity of the judgment of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being.

In the verse, the negation of permissibility is expressed by the negation of the right (الحق), as evidenced by His saying: ما كانَ لِلنَّبِيِّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — meaning they did not possess the right to seek forgiveness after such and such became clear to them. It has already been mentioned in the commentary on His saying: ما كانَ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَنْ يَعْمُرُوا مَساجِدَ اللهِ — The polytheists may not maintain Allah’s mosques (9:17), that the judgment of permissibility in the Law (الشرع) is preceded by the establishment of a right.

The meaning is that after it became manifest and clear to the Prophet and the believers, through Allah’s clarification, that the polytheists are enemies of Allah and eternal in the Fire, they had no right by which they could seek forgiveness for the polytheists, even if they were their near relatives. As for Ibrahim’s seeking forgiveness for his polytheist father, it was because he thought he was not a stubborn enemy of Allah, even if he was a polytheist; so he showed him affection through a promise he had made to him and sought forgiveness for him. But when it became clear to him that he was an enemy of Allah, stubborn in his polytheism and misguidance, he disassociated from him.

His saying: إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ لَأَوَّاهٌ حَلِيمٌ — Indeed Abraham was very tender-hearted and forbearing (9:114), is a justification for Ibrahim’s promise and his seeking forgiveness for his father: he bore his father’s harshness and gave him a good promise because he was forbearing (حليم), and he sought forgiveness for him because he was tender-hearted (أواه). The awwāh (الأواه) is one who sighs much out of fear of his Lord and hope in Him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْماً بَعْدَ إِذْ هَداهُمْ حَتَّى يُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ ما يَتَّقُونَ — Allah does not lead a people astray after He has guided them until He makes clear to them what they should beware of (9:115), until the end of the two verses. These two verses are connected to the two preceding them, which were aimed at prohibiting seeking forgiveness for the polytheists.

As for the first verse, namely His saying: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُضِلَ etc., it contains a threat to the believers of being led into misguidance (الإضلال) after guidance (الهداية) if they do not beware of what Allah has made clear to them to beware of and avoid. In terms of how it applies to the context, it means that since the polytheists are enemies of Allah, it is not permissible to seek forgiveness for them or show them affection; the believers must beware of that, otherwise, it is misguidance after guidance. You should recall what we presented in the commentary on His saying: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ — ...Today the faithless have despaired of your religion. So do not fear them, but fear Me... (5:3) in the fifth volume of this book, and in the commentary on the verses regarding the guardianship (ولاية) of the polytheists and the People of the Book occurring in the previous Surahs.

The verse is, in a way, similar in meaning to His saying: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى قَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ — That is because Allah never changes a blessing He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls (8:53), and other similar verses. All of them proclaim that it is the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) that His blessing and guidance continue for a servant until the servant himself changes what is within him through ingratitude and transgression, whereupon Allah strips him of the blessing and guidance.

As for the second verse, namely His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا نَصِيرٍ — Indeed to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He gives life and brings death. And besides Allah you do not have any guardian or helper (9:116), its conclusion is an explanation of the reason for the preceding judgment—the prohibition of taking the enemies of Allah as guardians or the obligation of disassociating from them. This is because there is no true guardian (ولي) or helper (نصير) except Allah, the Glorified. He has made this clear to the believers, so they must, by the dictate of their faith, restrict their guardianship to Him, the Exalted, or to those of His friends (أوليائه) whom He has permitted to be taken as guardians; they have no right to exceed that by taking His enemies as guardians, whoever they may be.

The beginning of the verse is an explanation of the cause of this cause: that Allah, the Glorified, is the One who owns everything, and in His hand are death and life; thus, to Him belongs the governance of every affair. He is the Guardian, and there is no guardian besides Him.

It has become clear from the generality of the explanation and the reasoning in these four verses that the judgment is general: the obligation of disassociation (التبري) or the prohibition of guardianship (التولي) toward the enemies of Allah, whether that guardianship is through seeking forgiveness or otherwise, and whether the enemy is a polytheist, a disbeliever, a hypocrite, or others among the people of innovation (أهل البدع) who disbelieve in Allah’s signs or persist in certain major sins, such as the usurer who is at war with Allah and His Messenger.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ تابَ اللهُ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ وَالْمُهاجِرِينَ وَالْأَنْصارِ الَّذِينَ — Allah has certainly turned [mercifully] to the Prophet and the Migrants and the Helpers who... (9:117), until the end of the two verses. The hour (الساعة) is a measure of time; thus, "the hour of difficulty" (ساعة العسرة) is the time in which life becomes difficult because a person is afflicted with that which makes living hard, such as thirst, hunger, intense heat, or otherwise. Deviation (الزيغ) is the departure from the path and swerving from the truth. The attribution of deviation to the hearts, the mention of the hour of difficulty, and other indications from the context of the speech prove that what is meant by deviation is the refusal to comply with the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) command and departing from his obedience by being sluggish in going out for Jihad or returning to the homelands by cutting the journey short, out of distress from the hardship and difficulty they faced in their march.

"Staying behind" (التخليف)—according to al-Majma‘—is the delaying of a thing from one who has passed; as for delaying a thing from yourself in a place, it is not takhliif. It is derived from the back (الخلف), which is the opposite of the front; it is said "he left him behind" (خلفه), meaning he placed him behind him, so he is "one left behind" (مخلف). End quote. Ample (الرحب) is the spaciousness that is the opposite of narrowness. "With all its ampleness" (بما رحبت) means "with its spaciousness," where mā is an infinitive particle (مصدرية).

Although each of the two verses looks toward a different aspect—the first explains the turning in mercy toward the Prophet, the Migrants (المهاجرين), and the Helpers (الأنصار), and the second explains the turning in mercy toward the three who were left behind—and despite the fact that the type of mercy shown to the people of the two verses is different (as the people of the first verse, or some of them, were turned to by Allah without having committed a sin, while the people of the second verse were turned to while being disobedient sinners), nevertheless, the context indicates that they are aimed at a single purpose. They are connected as a single discourse explaining His turning in mercy toward the Prophet, the Migrants, the Helpers, and the three who were left behind. Evidence for this is His saying: لَقَدْ تابَ اللهُ عَلَى النَّبِيِ until He said: وَعَلَى الثَّلاثَةِ etc. Thus, the second verse is not independent of the first in terms of wording, even if it is independent in meaning. This necessitates that they were revealed together and that a specific purpose is attached to this connection and blending.

Perhaps the primary purpose was to explain Allah’s turning in mercy toward those three who were left behind, and the mention of His turning toward the Migrants, the Helpers, and even the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was joined to it to soothe their hearts by mixing them with others, removing their distinctness from the rest of the people, and erasing the trace of that from them, so that everyone returns to a single description: that Allah turned to them by His mercy. Thus, they are equal in this regard, without some being raised above others or some being lowered below others.

Through this, the subtle point (النكتة) in the repetition of the mention of "turning in mercy" (التوبة) in the two verses becomes clear. Allah, the Glorified, begins by mentioning His turning toward the Prophet, the Migrants, and the Helpers, then says: ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ (then He turned to them), and toward the three who were left behind, then says: ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوا (then He turned to them so that they might repent). This is only because the speech is structured on the method of brevity followed by detail (الإجمال والتفصيل); His turning toward everyone was mentioned briefly, then the state of each of the two groups was pointed out separately, and at that point, the specific turning in mercy for each was mentioned.

If each of the two verses had an independent purpose without being gathered by a unifying objective, it would have been a repetition without a manifest subtle point.

Furthermore, in the first verse, there is a clear indication that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had no sin in that, nor any deviation, nor was his heart about to deviate. The speech contains praise for the Migrants and the Helpers for following the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); thus, his heart did not deviate, nor was it about to deviate, such that he became the followed one to be emulated. Were it not for the purpose we mentioned, there would be no manifest reason for mentioning him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) along with the others mentioned.

The meaning of the verse thus boils down to: Allah—He has sworn to this—has turned and returned with His mercy toward the Prophet, the Migrants, the Helpers, and the three who were left behind. As for His turning and returning with mercy toward the Migrants and the Helpers, it is because they followed the Prophet in the hour of difficulty and its time—which was the days of their march to Tabuk—they followed him after the hearts of a group of them were about to deviate and swerve from the truth by abandoning the departure or abandoning the march; so after they followed him, Allah turned to them; indeed, He is Most Kind and Merciful to them.

As for the three who were left behind, their affair reached the point where the earth became narrow for them despite its ampleness and vastness—and that was because the people did not associate with them or speak to them, even their own families, so they found no companion to be comforted by—and their own souls became narrow for them—due to the continuous grief upon them—and they became certain that there is no refuge from Allah except in Him, through repentance and turning back (الإنابة). When all that occurred, Allah turned to them, inclined toward them, and returned with His mercy to them so that they might repent to Him and He might accept their repentance. Indeed, He is the Oft-Returning (التواب) — the one who frequently returns to His servants with guidance and the success to repent, and then with the acceptance of that repentance — and the Most Merciful (الرحيم) to the believers.

From all this, several points have become clear: First: The meaning of the "turning" (التوبة) toward the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is purely the return to him with mercy. Part of returning to him with mercy is returning to his community with mercy; thus, the turning toward them is a turning toward him, for he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is the intermediary in the descent of good things and blessings upon his community.

Also, it is part of Allah’s grace upon His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that whenever He mentions his community or those with him with goodness, He singles him out from among them and begins the speech with his mention as an honor for him, as in His saying: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ — The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful [as well]... (2:285), and His saying: ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — Then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the faithful... (9:26), and His saying: لكِنِ الرَّسُولُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ جاهَدُوا — But the Apostle and the faithful who are with him wage jihad... (9:88), among other instances.

Second: What is meant by the second and third mentions of "turning" in His saying: ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ in both places is the detailing of what He mentioned briefly in His saying: لَقَدْ تابَ اللهُ.

Third: The meaning of "turning" in His saying: ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ in both places is His, the Exalted, return to them with guidance toward good and with success (التوفيق). We have mentioned repeatedly in previous discussions that the servant’s repentance is flanked by two turnings from the Lord, the Exalted: He returns to him with success and the bestowal of the mercy of guidance—which is the first turning from Him—then the servant is guided to seek forgiveness—which is his repentance—then He, the Exalted, returns to him by accepting his repentance and forgiving his sins—which is the second turning from Him, the Exalted.

The evidence that this is what is meant in both places is: in the first verse, because He did not mention any sin of theirs for which they should seek forgiveness such that His turning toward them would be a turning of acceptance; rather, He mentioned that the deviation of the hearts of some of them was expected, which suits the first turning from Him, the Exalted, rather than the second. In the second verse, because He mentioned after it His saying: لِيَتُوبُوا (so that they might repent), which is seeking forgiveness, making it the goal of His, the Exalted, turning; thus, His turning before their repentance is none other than the first turning from Him.

This is perhaps supported by His saying in the context of justifying His turning toward them: إِنَّهُ بِهِمْ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ — Indeed He is most kind and merciful to them (9:117), where He did not mention any of His names that linguistically indicate the acceptance of their repentance, just as He did not mention a repentance from them in the sense of seeking forgiveness.

Fourth: What is meant by His saying in the second verse: لِيَتُوبُوا is the repentance of the three who were left behind, which resulted from Allah’s first turning toward them. The meaning is: then Allah turned toward the three so that the three might repent, and then He would turn toward them and forgive them; indeed, He is the Oft-Returning, the Most Merciful.

If you say: Then the verse does not indicate the acceptance of their repentance, and this contradicts the necessity established through transmission (النقل) that the verse was revealed regarding their repentance.

I say: The story is established through transmission, but there is no explicit indication in the wording of the verse except that the verse indicates it through its context. He said in the brief statement: لَقَدْ تابَ اللهُ, which is more general in its absolute sense than turning in the sense of success or in the sense of acceptance. Likewise, His saying afterward: إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ — Indeed Allah is the Oft-returning, the All-merciful (9:118), especially considering the context of restriction (الحصر) in the sentence looking toward His saying: وَظَنُّوا أَنْ لا مَلْجَأَ مِنَ اللهِ إِلَّا إِلَيْهِ — ...and they knew for certain that there is no refuge from Allah except in Him... (9:118). If they proceeded to repent to take a refuge from Allah where they would be safe, and Allah had guided them to it through the turning, and they repented, then it is impossible that Allah would turn them away from His door disappointed while He is the Oft-Returning, the Most Merciful. How could that be consistent? And He is the One who says—exalted is the Speaker: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ فَأُولئِكَ يَتُوبُ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ — Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance and then repent promptly. It is such whose repentance Allah will accept... (4:17).

It has been said: The meaning of ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوا is "then Allah made repentance easy for them so that they might repent." This is weak. Even weaker is the view of those who said: The meaning of "turning" in لِيَتُوبُوا is returning to their original state before the sin. Even weaker is the view of others: "The pronoun in لِيَتُوبُوا refers to the believers, and the meaning is: then He turned toward the three and sent down their repentance to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) so that the believers might repent of their sins, knowing that Allah is the Accepter of repentance."

Fifth: "Certainty" (الظن) in the verse conveys the meaning of knowledge (العلم), not due to a linguistic indication but due to the specific context.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ — O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and be with the truthful (9:119). Truthfulness (الصدق) is originally the correspondence of speech and report to external reality. A person is described as truthful if his report corresponds to reality. Then, since each of belief (الاعتقاد) and resolve (العزم)—the will—is considered a "word," the meaning of truthfulness was expanded: a person is considered truthful if his report corresponds to reality, truthful if he acts according to what he believes, and truthful if he performs what he intends and resolves upon with earnestness.

The absolute nature of the command for godwariness (التقوى), the absolute mention of "the truthful" (الصادقين), and the absolute command to be with them—and "being with" (المعية) is companionship in action, which is following (الاتباع)—indicates that what is meant by truthfulness is its broad, general meaning rather than the specific one.

Thus, the verse commands the believers to be godwary and to follow the truthful in their words and deeds. This is different from the command to be characterized by their attribute, for that would be "being of them" (الكون منهم) rather than "being with them" (الكون معهم), as is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: ما كانَ لِأَهْلِ الْمَدِينَةِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُمْ مِنَ الْأعرابِ — It is not fitting for the people of Madinah and the Bedouins around them... (9:120), until the end of the two verses. Desire (الرغبة) is a specific psychological inclination. Desiring in a thing (الرغبة في الشيء) is the inclination toward it to seek a benefit in it. Desiring away from a thing (الرغبة عن الشيء) is the inclination away from it by abandoning it. The particle bā’ is for causality (السببية). Thus, His saying: وَلا يَرْغَبُوا بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ means: they have no right to occupy themselves with their own lives away from his life, abandoning him at the dangers of battles and in the fatigue and hardship of travels, while they sit to enjoy the pleasures of life. Thirst (الظمأ) is thirst, fatigue (النصب) is tiredness, hunger (المخمصة) is starvation, rage (الغيظ) is the most intense anger, and a place of treading (الموطأ) is the land that is trodden by feet.

The verse strips the people of Medina and the Bedouins around it of the right to stay behind from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Then it mentions that Allah has countered this stripping of right from them by writing for them—for every affliction that befalls them in Jihad, such as hunger, thirst, and fatigue, and for every land they tread upon whereby they enrage the disbelievers, or any gain they obtain from them—a righteous deed (عملا صالحا); for they are doers of good (محسنون), and Allah does not waste the reward of the doers of good. This is the meaning of His saying: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ لا يُصِيبُهُمْ ظَمَأٌ etc.

Then He mentioned that their expenditures, whether small and easy or large and significant, as well as every valley they cross, are written for them and preserved for their sake, so that they may be rewarded with the best reward.

His saying: لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ اللهُ أَحْسَنَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — ...that Allah may reward them with the best of what they used to do (9:121), is a goal related to His saying: كُتِبَ لَهُمْ (it was written for them); i.e., the goal of this writing is that He rewards them for the best of their deeds. The reward for the best of deeds was specifically mentioned because the desire of the worker is focused upon it, or because the reward for the best of them entails the reward for others, or because what is meant by the best of deeds is Jihad in the way of Allah, as it is the most difficult of them and the establishment of the religious call is achieved through it.

There is another meaning here: that the reward for a deed is, in reality, the deed itself returning to Allah; thus, the best reward is the best deed. So, rewarding with the best of deeds is in the sense of rewarding with the best reward. Another meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, forgives their evil deeds that are mixed with their good deeds and veils the aspects of their deficiency, so the deed becomes "best" after it was "good," and then He rewards them with the best of what they used to do. Understand this. Perhaps the two meanings return to a single one.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ لِيَنْفِرُوا كَافَّةً فَلَوْ لا نَفَرَ مِنْ كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِنْهُمْ طائِفَةٌ لِيَتَفَقَّهُوا فِي الدِّينِ — The faithful should not go forth all together [to jihad]. Why should not there go forth a group from every section of them to become learned in religion... (9:122). The context indicates that what is meant by His saying: لِيَنْفِرُوا كَافَّةً is to go forth and go out for Jihad all together. His saying: فِرْقَةٍ مِنْهُمْ, the pronoun refers to the believers who are not to go forth all together. This necessitates that the going forth (النفر) to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) be from them.

Thus, the verse prohibits the believers of other lands—besides the city of the Messenger—from going forth to Jihad all together; rather, it urges that a group from them go forth to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to gain a deep understanding of the religion, while others go forth to Jihad.

Most suitable to this meaning is that the pronoun in His saying رَجَعُوا (they return) refers to the group gaining understanding, and in His saying: إِلَيْهِمْ (to them) to their people. The meaning is: when these who have gained understanding return to their people. The reverse is also possible: when their people return from Jihad to this group after their gaining of understanding and their return to their homelands.

The meaning of the verse is: It is not permissible for the believers of the lands to go out to Jihad all together. Why then did not a group from every section of the believers go forth and go out to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to realize understanding (الفقه) and comprehension in the religion, so they may act upon it for themselves and warn their people—by spreading the religious sciences and mentioning the consequences of opposing its principles and branches—when this group returns to them, so that they may perhaps be wary and godwary?

From here, several points become clear: First: What is meant by "gaining understanding" (التفقه) is the comprehension of all religious sciences, including principles (أصول) and branches (فروع), not specifically the practical laws, which is the technical term for "jurisprudence" (الفقه) among the scholars of the Law. The evidence for this is His saying: لِيُنْذِرُوا قَوْمَهُمْ (that they may warn their people), for that is a matter that is only completed by gaining understanding of the entire religion, as is manifest.

Second: Going forth to Jihad is waived for the students of religious knowledge, by the indication of the verse.

Third: All other possible meanings mentioned regarding the verse are far from the context, such as the view of some that what is meant by لِيَنْفِرُوا كَافَّةً is their going forth to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to gain understanding; or the view of some regarding فَلَوْ لا نَفَرَ that it means "to Jihad," and what is meant by لِيَتَفَقَّهُوا is those who remain behind, who then warn their people who went forth to Jihad when they return to those who stayed behind. These and similar views are far-fetched meanings, and there is no benefit in addressing them or elaborating on their discussion.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ يَلُونَكُمْ مِنَ الْكُفَّارِ وَلْيَجِدُوا فِيكُمْ غِلْظَةً وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الْمُتَّقِينَ — O you who have faith! Fight the faithless who are in your vicinity, and let them find severity in you, and know that Allah is with the God-conscious (9:123). This is a command for general Jihad through which Islam expands until it spreads throughout the world. For the fighting of every group of believers against those disbelievers adjacent to them does not end except with the expansion of Islam—an expansion through the establishment of its authority over the world and its encompassing of all people.

What is meant by His saying: وَلْيَجِدُوا فِيكُمْ غِلْظَةً (and let them find severity in you) is firmness for the sake of Allah. It does not mean coarseness, rudeness, ill-temper, hard-heartedness, or cruelty; all religious principles condemn and find such things ugly. The tone of the verses of Jihad prohibits every transgression, aggression, and cruelty, as mentioned in Surah al-Baqarah.

In His saying: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الْمُتَّقِينَ (and know that Allah is with the God-conscious), there is a divine promise of victory on the condition of godwariness (التقوى). Its meaning boils down to guiding them to always be watchful of themselves, mindful of the station of their Lord toward them—that He is with them and is their Guardian; thus, they are the superior ones if they are godwary.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah, who said: "This verse was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while he was in the mosque: إِنَّ اللهَ اشْتَرى مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ (Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful their souls). The people in the mosque cried 'Allahu Akbar.' Then a man from the Helpers (الأنصار) approached, folding the ends of his cloak over his shoulder, and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, was this verse revealed?' He said: 'Yes.' The Ansari said: 'A profitable sale! We shall not seek to cancel it, nor shall we accept its cancellation.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Sama‘ah, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Abbad al-Basri met Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) on the way to Mecca and said to him: 'O Ali ibn al-Husayn, you have left Jihad and its difficulty and turned to Hajj and its ease, while Allah says: إِنَّ اللهَ اشْتَرى (Indeed Allah has bought) etc.' Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) replied: 'If we see those who possess these attributes, then Jihad with them is better than Hajj.'" I say: He (peace be upon him) means the attributes in the second verse: التَّائِبُونَ الْعابِدُونَ (those who repent, those who worship) etc.

From the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "The traveling (سياحة) of my community is in the mosques." I say: It is also narrated from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "The travelers (السائحون) are the fasters." And from Abu Umamah from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "The traveling of my community is Jihad in the way of Allah." The discussion regarding this has already preceded.

In al-Majma‘: "التائبين العابدين" (the repentant, the worshippers) until the end—with the letter yā’—from Abu Ja‘far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying: ما كانَ لِلنَّبِيِّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ يَسْتَغْفِرُوا لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il narrated from Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib, from his father, who said: "When death approached Abu Talib, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered upon him—while Abu Jahl and Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah were with him—and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'O uncle, say: There is no god but Allah, that I may plead for you with it before Allah.' Abu Jahl and Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah said: 'O Abu Talib, do you desire [to turn] away from the creed of Abdul-Muttalib?' The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) continued to offer it to him, while Abu Jahl and Abdullah assisted him (يعانوانه) with that statement. The last thing Abu Talib said to them was: '[I am] upon the creed of Abdul-Muttalib,' and he refused to say: 'There is no god but Allah.' The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I shall certainly seek forgiveness for you as long as I am not forbidden from it.' Then was revealed: ما كانَ لِلنَّبِيِّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ يَسْتَغْفِرُوا لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ (The Prophet and the faithful may not plead for the forgiveness of the polytheists). And Allah revealed regarding Abu Talib, saying to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): إِنَّكَ لا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ (You cannot guide whomever you wish, but Allah guides whomever He wishes)."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning from the Sunni paths. In some of them, it is said that when the Muslims saw the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) seeking forgiveness for his uncle while he was a polytheist, they sought forgiveness for their polytheist fathers, so the verse was revealed. However, the narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) are in agreement that he was a Muslim who did not manifest his Islam so that he could protect the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). In what is narrated through authentic transmission of his poetry, there is much that indicates his monotheism (توحيده) and his belief in the Prophethood; we have already presented a portion of it.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Zurarah, from Abu Ja‘far, who said: "The awwāh (الأواه) is the one who supplicates (الدعاء)."

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْماً: "It is said: A group of Muslims died upon Islam before the obligatory duties (الفرائض) were revealed. The Muslims said: 'O Messenger of Allah, our Muslim brothers died before the obligatory duties; what is their status?' So there was revealed: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْماً (Allah does not lead a people astray) etc." Narrated from al-Hasan.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding the verse: "It was revealed when they took ransom from the polytheists on the day of the captives (i.e., the day of Badr). He said: 'It was not for you to take it until permission was given to you—but Allah would not punish a people for a sin they committed until He makes clear to them what they should beware of.' He said: 'Until He forbids them before that.'" I say: The apparent sense of the two narrations is that they are instances of application (التطبيق) rather than "occasion of revelation" (شأن النزول) in its technical sense. The connection of the verse to the two verses before it and its inclusion in their context is manifest, as has been clarified.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Hamzah ibn Muhammad al-Tayyar, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), regarding the saying of Allah: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْماً ـ بَعْدَ إِذْ هَداهُمْ حَتَّى يُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ ما يَتَّقُونَ: "He said: 'He makes known to them what pleases Him and what angers Him.'" The tradition continues. I say: It was also narrated from Abd al-A‘la from him (peace be upon him), and al-Barqi also narrated it in al-Mahasin.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: "لقد تاب الله بالنبي على المهاجرين والأنصار ـ الذين اتبعوه في ساعة العسرة" (Allah has certainly turned [mercifully] by the Prophet to the Migrants and the Helpers who followed him in the hour of difficulty). Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "Thus was it revealed—and they were Abu Dharr, Abu Khaythamah, and Umayr ibn Wahb—those who stayed behind and then caught up with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)." I say: We extracted this from a long tradition cited by al-Qummi in his Tafsir regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَرادُوا الْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا لَهُ عُدَّةً — Had they desired to go forth, they would have made some preparation for it... (9:46). The reading "بالنبي" (by the Prophet) was also narrated in al-Majma‘ from him and from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him).

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying: وَعَلَى الثَّلاثَةِ الَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا (and to the three who were left behind): "Ali ibn al-Husayn Zayn al-Abidin, Muhammad ibn Ali al-Baqir, Ja‘far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq (peace be upon them), and Abu Abd al-Rahman al-Sulami read it as: خالفوا (they opposed)."

In the same source, regarding His saying: لَقَدْ تابَ اللهُ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ وَالْمُهاجِرِينَ وَالْأَنْصارِ: "It was revealed regarding the expedition of Tabuk and the difficulty that befell the Muslims therein—to the point that a group intended to return, then the grace of Allah, the Glorified, reached them. Al-Hasan said: 'Ten of the Muslims would go out with one camel which they would take turns riding; a man would ride for an hour, then dismount and his companion would ride likewise. Their provisions were worm-eaten barley, maggoty dates, and rancid fat. A group of them would go out with a few dates between them; when hunger overcame one of them, he would take a date and chew it until he tasted its flavor, then give it to his companion who would suck it, then drink a gulp of water over it, and so on until it reached the last of them, and nothing remained of the date but the pit.'"

In the same source, regarding His saying: وَعَلَى الثَّلاثَةِ الَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا: "It was revealed concerning Ka‘b ibn Malik, Murarah ibn al-Rabi‘, and Hilal ibn Umayyah. This was because they stayed behind from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and did not go out with him, not out of hypocrisy but out of sluggishness. Then they regretted it. When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrived in Medina, they came to him and apologized, but the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not speak to them and instructed the Muslims that no one should speak to them. So the people shunned them, even the children. Their wives came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, shall we separate from them?' He said: 'No, but they must not approach you.'

Medina became narrow for them, so they went out to the mountain peaks. Their families would bring them food but would not speak to them. They said to one another: 'The people have shunned us, and no one speaks to us; why do we not also shun one another?' So they separated, and no two of them remained together. They stayed in that state for fifty days, beseeching Allah, the Exalted, and repenting to Him. Then Allah, the Exalted, accepted their repentance and revealed this verse regarding them." I say: The story has already preceded in a long tradition we quoted from the Tafsir of al-Qummi under verse 46 of this Surah, and the story is narrated through many paths.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Shahrashub, from the Tafsir of Abu Yusuf ibn Ya‘qub ibn Sufyan: Malik ibn Anas narrated to us from Nafi‘, from Ibn Umar, who said: "يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ (O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah): Allah commanded the Companions to fear Allah. Then He said: وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ (and be with the truthful), meaning with Muhammad and his Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them)." I say: There are many narrations with this meaning from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn Abbas, and also from Ibn Asakir from Abu Ja‘far regarding His saying: وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ, both said: "With Ali ibn Abi Talib."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Ya‘qub ibn Shu‘ayb, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'If an event happens to the Imam, what should the people do?' He said: 'Where is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَلَوْ لا نَفَرَ مِنْ كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِنْهُمْ طائِفَةٌ لِيَتَفَقَّهُوا فِي الدِّينِ ـ وَلِيُنْذِرُوا قَوْمَهُمْ إِذا رَجَعُوا إِلَيْهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَحْذَرُونَ (Why should not there go forth a group from every section of them to become learned in religion...)?' He said: 'They are excused as long as they are in search, and those who are waiting for them are excused until their companions return to them.'" I say: There are many narrations with this meaning from the Imams (peace be upon them), and it is among the things that indicate that what is meant by "gaining understanding" (التفقه) in the verse is broader than learning jurisprudence in the technical sense used today.

Know that there are other views regarding the occasions of revelation for some of the preceding verses which we have omitted due to their manifest weakness and frailty.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ فَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زَادَتْهُ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنًا ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَزَادَتْهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَهُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (١٢٤)
Whenever a surah is sent down, there are some of them who say, ‘Which of you did it increase in faith?’ As for those who have faith, it increases them in faith, and they rejoice. (124)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ فَزَادَتْهُمْ رِجْسًا إِلَىٰ رِجْسِهِمْ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (١٢٥)
But as for those in whose heart is a sickness, it only adds defilement to their defilement, and they die while they are faithless. (125)
أَوَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّهُمْ يُفْتَنُونَ فِى كُلِّ عَامٍ مَّرَّةً أَوْ مَرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ لَا يَتُوبُونَ وَلَا هُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٢٦)
Do they not see that they are tried once or twice every year? Yet they neither repent, nor do they take admonition. (126)
وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ نَّظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ هَلْ يَرَىٰكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ ثُمَّ ٱنصَرَفُوا۟ ۚ صَرَفَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُم بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ (١٢٧)
Whenever a surah is sent down they look at one another: ‘Is anybody observing you?’ Then they slip away. Allah has turned aside their hearts, for they are a people who do not understand. (127)
لَقَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ مَا عَنِتُّمْ حَرِيصٌ عَلَيْكُم بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٢٨)
There has certainly come to you an apostle from among yourselves. Grievous to him is your distress; he has deep concern for you, and is most kind and merciful to the faithful. (128)
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُلْ حَسْبِىَ ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ ۖ وَهُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (١٢٩)
But if they turn their backs [on you], say, ‘Allah is sufficient for me. There is no god except Him. In Him I have put my trust and He is the Lord of the Great Throne.’ (129)

(Commentary)

These are the verses that conclude the verses of Bara’ah (Repudiation). They mention the state of the believers (المؤمنين) and the hypocrites (المنافقين) upon witnessing the revelation of the Quranic chapters (السور). Through this, one of the signs (أمارات) of hypocrisy (النفاق) is established, by which the hypocrite is distinguished from the believer. This sign is their saying when the Quran is revealed: "Which of you has this increased in faith?" and their looking at one another, saying: "Does anyone see you?"

In these verses, Allah, the Exalted, describes His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with a description that draws the hearts of the believers toward him, and He commands him to rely (التوكل) upon Him if they turn away from him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زادَتْهُ هذِهِ إِيماناً "Whenever a surah is sent down, there are some of them who say, ‘Which of you did this increase in faith?’" until the end of the two verses. The manner of the question in their saying: "Does anyone see you?" indicates that the questioner is not free from something [of doubt] in his heart. For this question is naturally the question of one who finds no trace in his heart from the revelation of the Quran. It is as if he believes that the hearts of others are like his own regarding what they receive; thus, he searches for anyone whose heart was affected by the revelation of the Quran. It is as if he perceives that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) claims the Quran rectifies every heart, whether it is prepared and disposed for rectification or not, while he does not believe this. Whenever a new chapter is recited to him and he finds in his heart neither humility (خشوع) before Allah nor an inclination or affection toward the Truth (الحق), his doubt increases. This prompts him to ask others present at the time of revelation about it, until he settles in his doubt and increases in the firmness of his hypocrisy (النفاق).

In short, the question is that of one whose heart is not free from hypocrisy.

Allah, the Glorified, has detailed the matter of hearts and distinguished between the hearts of the believers and those in whose hearts is a sickness (مرض). He said: فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "As for those who have faith," and they are those whose hearts are free from hypocrisy and innocent of sickness, and they possess certainty (يقين) in their religion by the context of the contrast. فَزادَتْهُمْ "it increases them"—the revealed chapter— إِيماناً "in faith." For by illuminating the soil of the heart with the light of its guidance (هدايتها), it necessitates the intensification of the light of faith within it; this is an increase in quality (الكيف). Furthermore, by containing new insights and realities from the Quranic knowledge and Divine truths, and by spreading the light of faith in them over the heart, it necessitates the addition of new faith upon the previous faith; this is an increase in quantity (الكمية). The attribution of the increase in faith to the chapter is of the nature of attribution to apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرة). In any case, the chapter increases the believers in faith, whereby their breasts are expanded and their faces brighten with joy: وَهُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ "and they rejoice."

وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ "But as for those in whose hearts is a sickness," and they are the people of doubt and hypocrisy, فَزادَتْهُمْ رِجْساً إِلَى رِجْسِهِمْ "it only adds defilement to their defilement," meaning a new misguidance (ضلال) added to their old misguidance. Allah, the Glorified, has named misguidance "defilement" (رجسا) in His saying: وَمَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً كَأَنَّما يَصَّعَّدُ فِي السَّماءِ كَذلِكَ يَجْعَلُ اللهُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ "and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened as if he were climbing into the sky. Thus does Allah lay defilement on those who do not have faith" (Al-An'am: 125).
